topics.title: Concept of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Concept of Quality – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality** is the **degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements** (ISO 9000:2015 definition adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus).

In the context of **Shipbuilding Quality Assurance**, *Quality* refers to the conformance of a vessel, its components, and its fabrication processes to:

- **Class rules** (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS)
- **Customer-specified technical specifications**
- **Regulatory standards** (SOLAS, MARPOL, IMO)
- **Statutory maritime safety codes**

The term "Object" in ISO 9000 is broad — it can mean a **product, service, process, person, resource, system, or organization**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The "Cruise Ship Coffee Cup" Test:**
> Imagine you are a passenger on a cruise ship. You order a cup of coffee.
> - The cup arrives **hot, full, in a clean mug, served by a smiling steward, exactly as you ordered** → that is **Quality**.
> - The cup arrives cold, half-full, in a chipped mug, dropped on the table by a distracted steward → that is **Non-Conformance (NC)**.

**In shipbuilding terms:**
A ship block that meets the **dimensional tolerance (within ±2 mm)**, has **approved welding procedures (WPS)**, passes **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)**, and is **delivered on time** = **Quality block**.

A block that is **out of tolerance, has weld defects, fails NDT, or is delayed** = **Non-Conforming block** — regardless of how beautifully it is painted!

## 1.3 Quality vs. Grade – A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Quality | Grade |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Degree of conformance to **requirements** | Degree of **relative features/characteristics** in a product |
| Scale | **High / Low** (binary fitness for use) | **High / Medium / Low** (categorical ranking) |
| Focus | **Uniformity & Reliability** | **Features & Performance level** |
| Example in shipbuilding | A steel plate meeting ABS Grade A chemistry is **quality steel** | A **VLCC** (Very Large Crude Carrier) is a **higher-grade** ship than a coaster |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students write "Quality = Excellence" — this is **WRONG**. Quality is **fitness for use**, not luxury. A low-cost fishing boat perfectly suited to a coastal fisherman's needs is **high quality**, even if it lacks luxury features of a yacht.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024

- **Requirement** – Need or expectation that is stated, generally implied, or obligatory.
- **Customer Satisfaction** – Customer's perception of the degree to which their requirements have been fulfilled.
- **Capability** – Ability of an object (process, system) to realize a product that will fulfill requirements.
- **Conformance** – Fulfillment of a requirement.
- **Non-conformance (NC)** – Non-fulfillment of a requirement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Foundational Pillars of "Quality Thinking"

Quality Assurance in shipbuilding rests on five logically sequential pillars (remember the acronym **P-D-C-S-I**):

1. **P – Plan Quality**  
   Establish quality objectives, identify applicable codes (ABS, DNV, IRS), define inspection test plans (ITP), and write WPS/PQR.

2. **D – Define Standards & Tolerances**  
   Classify tolerance bands. Typical shipyard dimensional tolerance: **±2 mm** for plate flatness, **±3 mm** for line correctness, **±5 mm** for block squareness.

3. **C – Control Processes (QA/QC)**  
   In-process inspection, monitoring welding parameters, statistical process control (SPC) on robot welding lines.

4. **S – Supervise Outputs (Inspection & NDT)**  
   Visual inspection, ultrasonic testing (UT), radiographic testing (RT), magnetic particle testing (MPT), dye penetrant testing (DPT).

5. **I – Improve Continuously (Kaizen)**  
   Corrective Action Requests (CAR), root-cause analysis (RCA), and 8D reports.

## 2.2 The Three Universal Quality Goals (Crosby's Philosophy)

Philip B. Crosby, in his book *"Quality is Free" (1979)*, defined quality as **"conformance to requirements"**. His three universal quality goals are:

| # | Goal | Shipbuilding Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prevention** (not inspection) | Pre-weld checklists rather than post-weld re-work |
| 2 | **Zero Defects** performance standard | First-time-right welding, dimensional accuracy |
| 3 | **Measurement of Quality Cost** (COPQ) | Tracking rework man-hours, scrap steel tonnage |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Internal + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs |
| **Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$)** | $C_{IF} = S_{Scrap} + C_{Rework} + C_{Re-inspect} + C_{Downtime}$ | Incurred **before** delivery |
| **External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$)** | $C_{EF} = C_{Warranty} + C_{Penalty} + C_{Recall}$ | Incurred **after** delivery |
| **Prevention Cost ($C_P$)** | $C_P = C_{Training} + C_{Design} + C_{QA\text{-}planning}$ | Proactive investment |
| **Appraisal Cost ($C_A$)** | $C_A = C_{Inspection} + C_{Testing} + C_{Audit}$ | Cost of measuring quality |
| **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passed first time}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation |
| **Six Sigma Level** | $3.4 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$ | Industry gold standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX rule used:** Absolute values / modulus bars in formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid` — never as the vertical pipe `|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table. Example: $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ is safe, whereas $C_p = \dfrac{\mid \text{USL} - \text{LSL} \mid}{6\sigma}$ must be written as $C_p = \dfrac{\vert \text{USL} - \text{LSL} \vert}{6\sigma}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding

| Application | How Quality Concept is Used |
|---|---|
| **Steel plate procurement** | Verifying Mill Test Certificate (MTC) against ABS/IRS grade specifications |
| **Welding procedure qualification** | WPS/PQR conforms to ASME Section IX or ISO 15614 |
| **Outfitting installation** | Hull-to-pipe alignment tolerance = ±2 mm |
| **Coating & corrosion protection** | DFT (Dry Film Thickness) measurement meets NACE/SSPC standards |
| **Sea trials** | Performance of main engine, steering gear, and auxiliaries against contract specs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Questions on *"Difference between Quality Control, Quality Assurance, and Quality Management"* appear in **almost every university examination**. Memorize the distinction using the **PDCA cycle ownership**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Walkthrough

A KTU numerical problem is essentially: *"Given the data below, calculate the total COPQ and identify the largest contributor."*

**Given Data (Hypothetical Shipyard XYZ):**

| Cost Element | Amount (₹ Lakhs/year) |
|---|---|
| Training of welders (Prevention, $C_P$) | 8 |
| Inspection & NDT (Appraisal, $C_A$) | 12 |
| Scrap steel (Internal Failure, $C_{IF}$) | 25 |
| Rework man-hours (Internal Failure, $C_{IF}$) | 30 |
| Warranty claims (External Failure, $C_{EF}$) | 15 |
| Customer penalties for late delivery (External Failure, $C_{EF}$) | 10 |

**Step 1 — Aggregate the Prevention cost:**

$$C_P = 8 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate the Appraisal cost:**

$$C_A = 12 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate the Internal Failure cost:**

$$C_{IF} = 25 + 30 = 55 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate the External Failure cost:**

$$C_{EF} = 15 + 10 = 25 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the master COPQ equation:**

$$\text{COPQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\text{COPQ} = 8 + 12 + 55 + 25$$

**Step 7 — Final summation:**

$$\boxed{\text{COPQ} = 100 \text{ Lakhs/annum}}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the dominant contributor:**

$$\text{Largest contributor} = C_{IF} = 55 \text{ Lakhs} \;(55\%)$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** Internal failure costs dominate. Management should **increase Prevention cost (better training, robust WPS)** to drive down the $C_{IF}$ component — a classic application of **Crosby's "Quality is Free" thesis**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities) Calculation

**Given:** During outfitting of a VLCC, **45 defects** were observed across **15,000 outfitting operations**, with each operation having **3 distinct quality checkpoints**.

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Opportunities (O):**

$$O = 15000 \times 3 = 45000$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DPMO formula:**

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values ($D = 45$, $U = 15000$):**

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^{6}}{15000 \times 3}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify denominator:**

$$= \dfrac{45 \times 10^{6}}{45000}$$

**Step 5 — Perform division:**

$$= \dfrac{45 \times 10^{6}}{4.5 \times 10^{4}} = 10 \times 10^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 1000 \text{ defects per million opportunities}}$$

**Step 7 — Sigma-level interpretation (using standard conversion table):**

| DPMO | Sigma Level | Process Capability |
|---|---|---|
| 1000 | **4.6 σ** | Better than industry average, below Six Sigma |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — First Pass Yield (FPY) Analysis

**Given:** In a panel line producing 200 hull panels/month, **170** pass dimensional and NDT inspection on the **first attempt**. The remaining 30 are reworked and then pass.

**Step 1 — Write the FPY formula:**

$$\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first time}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{FPY} = \dfrac{170}{200} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute ratio:**

$$= 0.85 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{FPY} = 85\%}$$

**Engineering Insight:** An FPY of 85% means **15% of panels require rework** — a critical KPI in modern shipbuilding, where leading Korean and Japanese yards target **FPY ≥ 95%**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Concept of Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concept of Quality] --> B[Definition Layer]
    A --> C[Dimensions of Quality]
    A --> D[Cost of Quality]
    A --> E[Quality Grade Distinction]
    A --> F[Quality Tools]

    B --> B1[ISO 9000 Definition]
    B --> B2[Crosby Conformance to Requirements]
    B --> B3[Juran Fitness for Use]

    C --> C1[Performance]
    C --> C2[Reliability]
    C --> C3[Durability]
    C --> C4[Conformance]
    C --> C5[Serviceability]
    C --> C6[Aesthetics]
    C --> C7[Perceived Quality]

    D --> D1[Prevention Cost CP]
    D --> D2[Appraisal Cost CA]
    D --> D3[Internal Failure Cost CIF]
    D --> D4[External Failure Cost CEF]

    E --> E1[Quality Uniformity vs Grade Features]
    E --> E2[Class Rules Reference]

    F --> F1[PDCA Cycle Deming]
    F --> F2[Seven QC Tools]
    F --> F3[Six Sigma DMAIC]
    F --> F4[Root Cause Analysis 8D]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Quality Management Decision Flow in Shipbuilding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Contract Received] --> A2[Identify Class Rules and Customer Specs]
    A2 --> A3[Develop Quality Plan and ITP]
    A3 --> A4{QA or QC Decision Point}
    A4 --> A5[Preventive Measures Training and WPS]
    A4 --> A6[Appraisal Measures NDT and Visual Inspection]
    A5 --> A7[Process Execution Welding and Outfitting]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8{Conformance Check}
    A8 --> A9[Pass and Move to Next Stage]
    A8 --> A10[Failure Detected]
    A10 --> A11[Root Cause Analysis]
    A11 --> A12[Corrective Action 8D Report]
    A12 --> A13[Feedback into Prevention Layer]
    A13 --> A5
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cost of Quality Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TotalCOPQ[Total Cost of Poor Quality COPQ]
        direction LR
        subgraph ConformanceCosts[Conformance Costs]
            P1[Prevention Cost CP]
            A1[Appraisal Cost CA]
        end
        subgraph NonConformanceCosts[Non Conformance Costs]
            IF1[Internal Failure Cost CIF]
            EF1[External Failure Cost CEF]
        end
        P1 --> Sum1
        A1 --> Sum1
        IF1 --> Sum2
        EF1 --> Sum2
        Sum1 --> TotalCOPQ
        Sum2 --> TotalCOPQ
    end
    TotalCOPQ --> KPI[Quality KPI Dashboard]
    KPI --> K1[DPMO]
    KPI --> K2[FPY Percent]
    KPI --> K3[Sigma Level]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** In Mermaid labels, only plain uppercase text is used (e.g., `PREVENTION COST CP`) — no bold `**` markers or HTML inside quoted node labels — to guarantee clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the term **"Quality"** as per ISO 9000:2015. How is it different from **"Grade"**?

**Model Answer:**

> **Quality** is the **degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements** (ISO 9000:2015). It is a measure of **conformance** to specifications.

**Difference between Quality and Grade:**

| Parameter | Quality | Grade |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Degree of conformance to requirements | Degree of relative features / characteristics |
| Scale | High / Low (fitness for use) | High / Medium / Low (ranking) |
| Orientation | **Uniformity & reliability** | **Performance & features** |
| Shipbuilding example | A steel plate meeting ABS-A chemistry specs = **High Quality** | A VLCC vs. a general cargo ship = different **Grades** |
| Nature | Relative to requirements | Relative to comparison class |

> **Conclusion:** A product can be **high quality but low grade** (e.g., a no-frills fishing boat perfectly built), or **low quality but high grade** (a luxury yacht with multiple defects).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** List the **four cost components** of the Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) and give **one example** of each from a shipbuilding context.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Cost Component | Symbol | Shipbuilding Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prevention Cost** | $C_P$ | Welders' training, QA/ITP preparation, WPS documentation |
| 2 | **Appraisal Cost** | $C_A$ | Ultrasonic testing of butt welds, dimensional survey of hull blocks |
| 3 | **Internal Failure Cost** | $C_{IF}$ | Rework of defective fillet welds, scrap of out-of-tolerance plates |
| 4 | **External Failure Cost** | $C_{EF}$ | Warranty claim after delivery, penalty for late delivery, dry-dock repair |

**Master Formula:**

$$\text{COPQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q:** Discuss in detail the **concept of Quality**, its **evolution** (Inspection → QC → QA → TQM), and the **dimensions of Quality** with reference to shipbuilding.

#### Part (a) — Evolution of Quality Thought (7 Marks)

The evolution of quality management can be represented in **four eras**:

| Era | Period | Concept | Focus | Shipbuilding Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. **Inspection** | Pre-1900 | Sort good from bad | Product-by-product check | Visual check of finished hull |
| 2. **Quality Control (QC)** | 1920s–1950s | Statistical control | Sampling, SQC, control charts | Sampling of welds using MIL-STD tables |
| 3. **Quality Assurance (QA)** | 1960s–1980s | System-based | Process orientation, documentation | ISO 9001 QMS in shipyards |
| 4. **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | 1980s–Present | People-based | Customer focus, continuous improvement | Six Sigma, Lean in Korean yards |

**Definitions:**

- **Inspection:** Measuring, examining, testing, and gauging one or more characteristics of a product and comparing to specified requirements.
- **Quality Control (QC):** Operational techniques and activities used to fulfill requirements for quality. Focuses on **product conformity**.
- **Quality Assurance (QA):** Part of quality management focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled. Focuses on **process adequacy**.
- **Total Quality Management (TQM):** A management philosophy centred on **customer satisfaction**, **employee involvement**, and **continuous improvement** (Kaizen).

#### Part (b) — Dimensions of Quality (7 Marks) — Garvin's 8 Dimensions applied to a Ship:

| # | Dimension | Definition | Shipbuilding Illustration |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics | Speed, deadweight, fuel consumption of vessel |
| 2 | **Features** | Secondary characteristics | Bow thrusters, automation systems |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of main engine |
| 4 | **Conformance** | Meeting established standards | Compliance with SOLAS, MARPOL, Class rules |
| 5 | **Durability** | Useful life under normal use | Hull fatigue life (typically 25–30 years) |
| 6 | **Serviceability** | Ease & speed of repair | Modular engine parts, accessibility for crew |
| 7 | **Aesthetics** | Sensory appeal | Hull form, painting finish, interior décor |
| 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Customer's subjective perception | Yard's brand reputation (Hyundai HI vs. unknown yard) |

> **Conclusion:** Modern shipbuilding demands all 8 dimensions — **Conformance** is the non-negotiable baseline, while **Perceived Quality** drives commercial competitiveness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q:** With neat derivations, explain the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** concept. A shipyard incurs the following annual costs: Training ₹10 L, Inspection ₹14 L, Scrap ₹20 L, Rework ₹28 L, Warranty ₹12 L, Late delivery penalty ₹6 L. Calculate the total COPQ and recommend a reduction strategy.

#### Part (a) — Concept and Derivation of COPQ (7 Marks)

**Concept:**
COPQ quantifies the **total financial loss** a shipyard suffers due to poor quality. It is the **sum of all costs** that would **disappear** if quality were perfect.

**Master Equation:**

$$\text{COPQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

**Component equations:**

$$C_{IF} = C_{Scrap} + C_{Rework} + C_{Re-inspect} + C_{Downtime}$$

$$C_{EF} = C_{Warranty} + C_{Penalty} + C_{Recall} + C_{Loss\text{ of goodwill}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify and aggregate Prevention cost:**

$$C_P = 10 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 2 — Identify and aggregate Appraisal cost:**

$$C_A = 14 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — Identify and aggregate Internal Failure cost (Scrap + Rework):**

$$C_{IF} = 20 + 28 = 48 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 4 — Identify and aggregate External Failure cost (Warranty + Penalty):**

$$C_{EF} = 12 + 6 = 18 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 5 — Apply master equation:**

$$\text{COPQ} = 10 + 14 + 48 + 18$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\text{COPQ} = 90 \text{ Lakhs/annum}}$$

**Step 7 — Contribution analysis:**

$$C_P = 11.1\% \quad C_A = 15.6\% \quad C_{IF} = 53.3\% \quad C_{EF} = 20.0\%$$

#### Part (b) — Reduction Strategy (7 Marks)

| Strategy | Action | Expected Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Increase $C_P$** | Welders' re-certification, mock-up trials, WPS library | Reduce $C_{IF}$ by 30–50% |
| **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** | Implement 8D reports for all NCs | Eliminate repeat defects |
| **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** | Control charts on welding parameters | Early detection of drift |
| **Supplier QA** | Incoming MTC verification, audits | Reduce scrap at source |
| **Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing)** | Jigs and fixtures for panel assembly | Reduce dimensional rework |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Condition monitoring of CNC machines | Reduce downtime-related defects |

> **Conclusion:** Per **Crosby's theorem**, every **₹1 spent on prevention saves ₹10 in failure costs**. The yard should target a **30% increase in $C_P$** to **halve $C_{IF}$**, achieving net savings of approximately **₹15–20 Lakhs/year**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (from KTU valuation key analysis):**
> 1. **Writing "Quality = Best" or "Quality = Excellence"** → Lose 1 mark immediately. Correct term is **"Conformance to Requirements"**.
> 2. **Confusing QA and QC** → QA is **process-oriented**; QC is **product-oriented**. Mixing them = –2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting external failure cost** → Many students miss warranty and penalty components in COPQ numerics. Always read the question carefully.
> 4. **Skipping contribution % analysis** → Examiners award bonus marks for converting raw numbers to **percentage contribution** of each cost element.
> 5. **Not relating to shipbuilding** → Generic textbook answers lose marks in KTU 2024 scheme. Always use **welding, NDT, hull, sea trial, class rules** as examples.
> 6. **Wrong units in COPQ** → Use **₹ Lakhs/year** consistently; do not mix ₹ and Rs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality** = **Conformance to Requirements** (ISO 9000:2015 / Crosby). **NOT** = Excellence.
- **Grade** is a **ranking concept**; **Quality** is a **conformance concept** — they are **independent**.
- **Evolution Path:** Inspection → QC → QA → TQM → Six Sigma.
- **Garvin's 8 Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- **COPQ Master Formula:**  
  $\text{COPQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$
- **Juran's Quality Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement.
- **Deming's PDCA:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (14 points).
- **Crosby's Absolutes of Quality Management:** Prevention, Zero Defects, Do it right first time, Measurement of COPQ.
- **DPMO** quantifies defects; **FPY** quantifies first-time-right units; **$C_p$** quantifies process capability.
- **Shipbuilding linkages:** Class rules (ABS / DNV / IRS), SOLAS, MARPOL, WPS, PQR, NDT (UT, RT, MPT, DPT), ITP.
- **Cost dominance in shipyards:** Typically $C_{IF}$ (rework + scrap) is the **largest** COPQ component.
- **Golden rule:** Investment in **Prevention** yields **multi-fold returns** by reducing failure costs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ Quality Characteristics in Shipbuilding — A KTU 2024 Premium Study Module

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1 – Quality Characteristics)**
> Quality Characteristics form the **measurable, observable, and verifiable attributes** of a ship, its subsystems, and the shipbuilding process that collectively determine its conformance to contractual, regulatory, and classification-society requirements. This topic is a **direct CO1 (Understand QA fundamentals) and CO2 (Apply QA tools in shipbuilding)** linkage.

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Quality Characteristic (QC)** is defined by **ISO 9000:2015** as an *inherent feature of an object* (product, process, system, or combination thereof) that relates to a requirement.

In the **shipbuilding domain**, a Quality Characteristic is any **distinguishing feature** of a ship, its structural members, machinery, electrical installations, or construction process that can be:
- **Quantitatively measured** (e.g., plate thickness in mm, weld penetration depth)
- **Qualitatively assessed** (e.g., surface finish, weld bead appearance)
- **Functionally tested** (e.g., turning circle diameter, fuel consumption rate)
- **Statistically verified** (e.g., tolerance bands, defect density per square meter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Examiner Focus):**
> A *Quality Characteristic* is an **inherent property** (built-in by design), not an *assigned* characteristic. For example, the *speed of a ship* is a quality characteristic, but *the paint color chosen by the buyer* is an assigned characteristic.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship is Like a Human Body"

Imagine a ship is like a human body. Just as a doctor checks:
- **Heart rate** → Ship's **propulsion RPM**
- **Blood pressure** → **Hull structural stress limits**
- **Body temperature** → **Engine exhaust temperature**
- **Vision clarity** → **Radar/ECDIS performance**
- **Stamina** → **Endurance / range at sea**

Each of these "vital parameters" is a **Quality Characteristic** — a feature that must fall within a defined **acceptance range** for the entity (ship) to be considered "healthy" and fit for purpose. If even one goes out of range, the entire system's quality is compromised.

## 1.3 Categorical Foundation — The Six Classical Quality Characteristics

Drawing from **Juran's Quality Handbook** and the **ISO 9000 framework**, all quality characteristics can be taxonomized into **six functional categories**:

| Sl. | Category | Shipbuilding-Specific Example | Engineering Unit / Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Physical Characteristics** | Hull plate thickness, steel grade, propeller mass | mm, kg, MPa |
| 2 | **Performance Characteristics** | Top speed, fuel consumption, cargo capacity | knots, tonnes/day |
| 3 | **Sensory Characteristics** | Paint gloss, surface roughness of hull, cabin noise | dB, RA (µm) |
| 4 | **Time-Related Characteristics** | Service life, MTBF of engine, overhaul interval | years, hours |
| 5 | **Ergonomic Characteristics** | Bridge layout visibility, crew cabin comfort, vibration level | ISO 2631, subjective rating |
| 6 | **Functional Characteristics** | Manoeuvrability, emergency response time, loading rate | degrees/min, sec |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Fact for KTU:** Always remember the **"3-S" rule** of Quality Characteristics: **Specification → Standard → Satisfaction**. A characteristic must be **specifiable** (definable), **standardizable** (measurable against a norm), and **satisfaction-linked** (impacts the end user).

## 1.4 Quality Characteristics vs. Quality Parameters vs. Quality Indicators

These three terms are **frequently confused** in KTU answers. Master the distinction:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  QUALITY CHARACTERISTIC  →  WHAT is being measured (feature) │
│  Example: "Hull plate tensile strength"                      │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  QUALITY PARAMETER        →  HOW it is quantified (value)    │
│  Example: "490 N/mm² minimum yield strength"                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  QUALITY INDICATOR        →  PERFORMANCE proxy (KPI)         │
│  Example: "Defect density per 100 m² of weld"                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Do NOT write "Quality Characteristics are the same as Quality Standards." Characteristics are **features of the product**; Standards are the **external references** (e.g., ISO, IACS UR) that define acceptable values for those features.

## 1.5 Why Quality Characteristics Matter in Shipbuilding

A modern ship comprises **~2 million individual parts** and **~500+ km of welding**. Without a structured identification of quality characteristics:
- Inspection would be **ad-hoc** and inconsistent
- Defect liability and warranty disputes become unresolvable
- Class society certification (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS, BV) cannot be granted
- Customer requirements (e.g., naval vs. commercial) cannot be mapped to deliverables

Therefore, the **first step of any QA program in shipbuilding is the systematic decomposition of the ship into a Quality Characteristic Tree (QCT)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Quality Characteristic Framework

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Quality Characteristics

In shipbuilding QA, quality characteristics are organized in a **four-tier hierarchy**, aligning with the WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) of the vessel:

```
TIER 1: Ship-Level QC          → "Is the ship fit for purpose?"
TIER 2: System-Level QC        → "Does each system perform?"
TIER 3: Subsystem/Component QC → "Does each part meet specs?"
TIER 4: Process QC             → "Was the manufacturing process controlled?"
```

### Detailed Breakdown of Each Tier

**Tier 1 — Ship-Level Quality Characteristics** (Composite Indicators)
- **Seaworthiness Index** (stability + buoyancy + strength)
- **Mission Effectiveness Ratio** (availability × capability)
- **Life-Cycle Cost Efficiency** (LCC per nautical mile)
- **Environmental Compliance Score** (IMO MARPOL adherence)

**Tier 2 — System-Level Quality Characteristics**
- **Hull Structural System**: scantling accuracy, corrosion margin
- **Propulsion System**: shaft alignment, fuel consumption curve
- **Electrical System**: power quality (THD < 5%), insulation resistance
- **Navigation & Communication System**: GMDSS compliance, ECDIS accuracy
- **Auxiliary Systems**: HVAC, bilge, fire-fighting response time

**Tier 3 — Subsystem/Component Quality Characteristics**
- *Hull plate* → thickness tolerance, surface profile, material grade
- *Welded joint* → penetration, porosity, undercut, hardness
- *Diesel engine* → BHP, BSFC (brake specific fuel consumption), emission level
- *Pump* → flow rate (m³/hr), head (m), NPSH margin

**Tier 4 — Process Quality Characteristics**
- *Welding process* → preheat temperature, interpass temp, arc energy
- *Surface preparation* → Sa 2.5 (ISO 8501-1) standard of cleanliness
- *Pipe bending* → ovality < 8%, wall thinning < 12.5%
- *Machining* → surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm, dimensional tolerance ±0.05 mm

## 2.2 The Three Domains of Quality Characteristics

| Domain | Definition | Shipbuilding Examples | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Product (Output) QC** | Features of the finished ship/component | Hull geometry, paint thickness | Dimensional inspection, NDT |
| **Process (Transformation) QC** | Features of the production activity | Weld heat input, cure time of coating | Process parameter logging |
| **System (Organizational) QC** | Features of the management system | Audit non-conformance rate, NCR closure time | ISO 9001 audit, KPI dashboard |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since Quality Characteristics is fundamentally a **classification topic**, the "formulas" below are **quantitative metrics** used to measure, monitor, and control each characteristic. Memorize these for the **Part A (3-mark) numerical questions** in KTU ESE.

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbolic Form | Application in Shipbuilding | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Measures whether welding process variation fits within spec | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Defects Per Unit (DPU)** | $DPU = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | Defects per 100 m of weld seam | defects/unit |
| 3 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | Six-Sigma quality benchmarking | ppm |
| 4 | **Yield Strength Margin** | $\sigma_{margin} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{applied}} \times 100$ | Hull plate safety factor check | % |
| 5 | **Weld Penetration Ratio** | $P_{ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Penetration}}{\text{Theoretical Penetration}} \times 100$ | Fillet/T-joint weld QC | % |
| 6 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Pipe spool fabrication efficiency | % |
| 7 | **Rolled Steel Cost Per Tonne** | $C_{RT} = \dfrac{C_{steel}}{\eta_{yield}}$ | Cutting & forming yield tracking | ₹/tonne |
| 8 | **Tolerance Stack-Up** | $T_{stack} = \sqrt{\sum t_i^{2}}$ | Block assembly dimensional accuracy | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Note:** $\sigma$ (sigma) is the standard deviation of the process. USL and LSL denote the **Upper** and **Lower Specification Limits** of the Quality Characteristic. $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the **KTU-cited benchmark** for a "capable" shipbuilding process.

## 2.4 The Quality Characteristic Tree (QCT) — Construction Logic

The QCT is the **principal deliverable** of QC identification in Module 1. Construction follows a **5-step recursive decomposition**:

1. **Define Mission** — What is the ship built for? (cargo, naval, passenger, fishing)
2. **Identify Customer Requirements (CRs)** — "Voice of Customer" (VoC) capture
3. **Translate CRs → Engineering QCs** — "Voice of Engineer" (VoE) interpretation
4. **Sub-divide QCs → Sub-characteristics → Metrics** — Hierarchical tree
5. **Assign Measurement Methods & Acceptance Criteria** — Where/how it is verified

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "MDTMA":** *Mission → Decompose → Translate → Measure → Audit*. This five-letter acronym captures the QCT-building workflow.

## 2.5 Standards Framework Governing Quality Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Name the standards that govern Quality Characteristics in shipbuilding."* Memorize this list:

- **ISO 9000:2015** — Vocabulary of quality (defines "characteristic")
- **ISO 9001:2015** — Requirements for the QMS that controls QCs
- **ISO 8402 (legacy)** — Earlier definitions superseded by ISO 9000:2015
- **IACS Unified Requirements (UR)** — Hull, machinery, electrical class rules
- **SOLAS (IMO)** — Safety-of-life characteristics (fire, life-saving)
- **MARPOL (IMO)** — Environmental QC (emission, discharge)
- **Classification Society Rules** — Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS, BV
- **ASTM / EN Standards** — Material testing QCs
- **AWS D1.1 / D1.6** — Welding QCs (US-based)
- **ISO 5817** — Weld quality levels (B, C, D)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The identification of quality characteristics is the **upstream foundation** for:
- **Quality Planning (QP)** — What to inspect, when, and how
- **Inspection & Test Plan (ITP)** — Translated directly from QCT
- **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** — "House of Quality" linkage
- **Failure Mode & Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Each QC becomes a potential failure mode
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — Control charts monitor the QC over time
- **Six-Sigma DMAIC** — Define (the QC) → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — From Concept to Application

## 3.1 Worked Example: Identifying the Quality Characteristics of a Bulk Carrier Hull

> **Scenario:** You are appointed as the **QA Engineer** for a 75,000 DWT bulk carrier under construction at a shipyard. The hull is 220 m long, single-island superstructure, with 6 cargo holds. Demonstrate how you would systematically identify the **Quality Characteristics** of the hull.

### Step 1: Establish the Mission & Operational Envelope

```
Mission: Transport dry bulk cargo (iron ore, coal, grain)
Routes: India → Far East, Australia → China
Design life: 25 years
Operational env.: Tropical, sea state 5 max
```

**Explanation:** Mission defines the **boundary conditions** for ALL downstream QCs. Without this step, you would be measuring the wrong things.

### Step 2: Decompose the Hull into a Tree Structure

$$\text{Hull} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Hull Form} \\ \text{Hull Structural Members} \\ \text{Hull Openings} \\ \text{Hull Coating} \\ \text{Hull Fittings} \end{cases}$$

Each branch further decomposes (recursive), for example:
$$\text{Hull Structural Members} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Shell Plating} \\ \text{Deck Plating} \\ \text{Bottom Plating} \\ \text{Longitudinals} \\ \text{Transverse Frames} \\ \text{Bulkheads} \end{cases}$$

### Step 3: Assign Quality Characteristics to Each Leaf Node

| Component | Quality Characteristic | Target Value | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shell Plate AH36 | Yield Strength | $\geq 355$ N/mm² | IACS UR S11 |
| Shell Plate | Thickness Tolerance | $-0.0$ mm to $+0.4$ mm | Class CSR Rules |
| Weld (Fillet T-joint) | Leg Length | $7 \pm 1$ mm | AWS D1.1 |
| Weld | Visual Defects | Level B (ISO 5817) | ISO 5817 |
| Hull Form | Length Overall (LOA) | $220.000 \pm 0.300$ m | Class Specification |
| Block Alignment | Butt Joint Offset | $\leq 0.15 \times t$ | IACS UR Z17 |
| Coating (Antifouling) | DFT (Dry Film Thickness) | $75 \pm 15$ µm | ISO 19840 |
| Surface Prep | Cleanliness Grade | Sa 2.5 | ISO 8501-1 |

### Step 4: Verification Method Selection

For each QC, a **measurement technique** is chosen:

```
┌─────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┐
│ Quality Characteristic  │ Verification Method              │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│ Plate thickness         │ Ultrasonic Thickness Gauge (UTG) │
│ Yield strength          │ Tensile Test (coupon)            │
│ Weld penetration        │ Radiographic Testing (RT)        │
│ Hull form accuracy      │ Total Station / Laser Scan       │
│ Coating DFT             │ Elcometer (magnetic induction)   │
│ Surface cleanliness     │ Visual + ISO 8501-1 comparator   │
└─────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation:** The choice of verification method must consider:
- **Accuracy** required (e.g., $\pm 0.05$ mm needs digital UTG, not manual calliper)
- **Destructiveness** (RT vs. UT vs. visual)
- **Speed & Cost** (visual = cheap, RT = expensive)
- **Class society acceptance** of the method

### Step 5: Statistical Process Capability Analysis (Cpk)

Suppose the **welding process** for the bulk carrier has:
- **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** = 8 mm (leg length)
- **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)** = 6 mm
- **Process Mean** ($\bar{X}$) = 7.0 mm
- **Standard Deviation** ($\sigma$) = 0.5 mm

The process capability is computed in two steps:

**Step 5a — Process Capability Index ($C_p$):**

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_p = \dfrac{8 - 6}{6 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{2.0}{3.0} = 0.667$$

**Step 5b — Critical Process Capability ($C_{pk}$), accounting for centering:**

$$C_{pk_{upper}} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{8 - 7.0}{3 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.667$$

$$C_{pk_{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{7.0 - 6}{3 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.667$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.667, 0.667) = 0.667$$

**Interpretation for KTU Answer:**

$$\text{If } C_{pk} < 1.00 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Process is INCAPABLE — unacceptable}$$

$$\text{If } 1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Marginally capable}$$

$$\text{If } 1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Capable (KTU benchmark)}$$

$$\text{If } C_{pk} \geq 1.67 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Highly capable (Six-Sigma class)}$$

**Conclusion:** Our weld process with $C_{pk} = 0.667$ is **NOT capable**. Action: Reduce $\sigma$ (process variation) or re-center the mean before production. This is a classic **KTU Part B 14-mark problem**.

### Step 6: Document the Quality Characteristic Tree in the ITP

The ITP (Inspection & Test Plan) is a **tabular document** that lists:
- Each QC (from the QCT)
- The acceptance criteria
- The verification method
- The frequency of inspection (e.g., 100%, 10%, AQL sampling)
- The responsible party (yard, class surveyor, owner rep)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the QCT directly **drives every other QA document** in the shipyard — ITP, NCR procedure, audit checklist, FMEA register, and SPC plan. This is why Module 1 is the **foundation stone** of the entire course.

## 3.2 Mapping to Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — "House of Quality"

The QC framework is the **technical row** of the **House of Quality (HoQ)**:

```
                    Technical QCs (WHATs) →
Customer Reqs ↓    Speed  Payload  Range  Cost  Safety  Comfort
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Fast transit        ●●○     ○       ●●     ○    ○        ○
High payload         ○     ●●●      ○     ○●   ○        ○
Long range           ○      ○      ●●●    ○    ○        ○
Low fuel cost        ●●     ○      ●●     ●●●  ○        ○
Crew safety          ○      ○       ○     ○   ●●●     ●●●
Comfort              ○      ○       ○     ○    ●      ●●●
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

The ●●○ symbols denote **strength of correlation** (strong, medium, weak). This is the **engineering link** between the customer's "voice" and the design team's measurable characteristics.

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, ESE Module Choice Type]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)**

> **Q(A).** (a) Explain the **hierarchical decomposition** of quality characteristics in a shipbuilding project, from ship-level to process-level. Illustrate with **one example** from the **hull structural system**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) Compute the **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** and **Critical Process Capability ($C_{pk}$)** for a **pipe-bending process** in your shipyard with the following data:
> - Inner diameter specification: **150 mm $\pm$ 0.5 mm**
> - Process mean: **150.1 mm**
> - Process standard deviation ($\sigma$): **0.18 mm**
>
> Comment on the **capability** of the process and suggest **two corrective actions** if it is found inadequate. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Hierarchical decomposition of QCs in shipbuilding:**

The QA framework organizes quality characteristics in **four tiers**, mirroring the ship's WBS (Work Breakdown Structure).

**Tier 1 — Ship Level (1 mark):** Composite characteristics that reflect the entire vessel's fitness. Examples: seaworthiness index, mission readiness, LCC efficiency.

**Tier 2 — System Level (1 mark):** Major ship systems. In the hull structural system, the relevant sub-systems are: shell plating, deck plating, bulkheads, frames, and openings.

**Tier 3 — Subsystem / Component Level (1 mark):** Specific parts. For example, *shell plate* has QCs such as thickness, material grade, surface profile, flatness, and edge preparation.

**Tier 4 — Process Level (1 mark):** Manufacturing operations. For a shell plate, the cutting process has QCs such as: kerf width, bevel angle (for welding), cutting surface roughness, and thermal distortion.

**Worked example — Hull plate thickness (3 marks):**
- **Ship-level QC:** Overall hull structural integrity (objective: zero catastrophic failure).
- **System-level QC:** Shell plating system contributes to longitudinal strength.
- **Component-level QC:** Individual AH36 plate of 22 mm nominal thickness.
- **Process-level QC:** Plasma cutting produces ±0.5 mm tolerance; rolling produces flatness ≤ 2 mm/m.

| Tier | QC of "Hull Plate Thickness" | Typical Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| Ship | Hull structural index | Class CSR |
| System | Shell plating contribution to section modulus | ± 3% of design |
| Component | Plate thickness at point of measurement | $-0.0$ / $+0.4$ mm |
| Process | Plasma cut dimensional accuracy | $\pm 0.5$ mm |

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Specification: $150 \pm 0.5$ mm → $USL = 150.5$ mm, $LSL = 149.5$ mm
- $\bar{X} = 150.1$ mm
- $\sigma = 0.18$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$ (2 marks):**

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_p = \dfrac{150.5 - 149.5}{6 \times 0.18} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.08} = 0.926$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk_{upper}}$ (1 mark):**

$$C_{pk_{upper}} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{150.5 - 150.1}{3 \times 0.18} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.54} = 0.741$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk_{lower}}$ (1 mark):**

$$C_{pk_{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{150.1 - 149.5}{3 \times 0.18} = \dfrac{0.6}{0.54} = 1.111$$

**Step 4 — Overall $C_{pk}$ (1 mark):**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.741, 1.111) = 0.741$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation & Corrective Action (2 marks):**

- $C_{pk} = 0.741 < 1.00$ → **Process is NOT capable.**
- $C_p = 0.926 < 1.00$ → **Process spread exceeds specification width.**
- The process is also **off-center** (the upper capability is the limiting one), meaning output is drifting toward the USL.

**Two Corrective Actions:**
1. **Reduce variation:** Re-calibrate the CNC pipe bender, replace worn mandrel, use statistical process control (X-bar R chart) to monitor.
2. **Re-center the process:** Adjust the CNC offset to bring $\bar{X}$ closer to 150.0 mm (the target), then re-verify $C_{pk}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ⚠️**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Reporting $C_{pk} = 0.926$ (confusing it with $C_p$). Always compute **BOTH** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to take the **minimum** of upper and lower $C_{pk}$. The overall $C_{pk}$ is the smaller one.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Stating "process is capable" when $C_{pk} < 1.00$. KTU benchmark for "capable" is $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.
> - **Unit error (–0.5 mark):** Mixing up USL/LSL — USL is the **upper** (numerically larger) limit.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2 — Internal Choice)**

> **Q(B).** (a) Discuss the **classification of quality characteristics** into **physical, performance, sensory, time-related, ergonomic, and functional** domains. Provide **two shipbuilding examples** for **each** domain. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) With a neat **block diagram**, explain the **Quality Function Deployment (QFD) House of Quality** methodology. Show how **customer requirements** are translated into **technical quality characteristics** for a **container ship**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks — 1 mark per domain + 0.17 each example)**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Quality characteristics, as classified in Juran's Quality Handbook and ISO 9000, fall into **six functional categories** that exhaustively describe the inherent properties of a product or service.

**Domain 1 — Physical Characteristics (1 mark):** Features measurable by direct physical or geometric means.
- *Example 1:* Hull plate thickness (mm)
- *Example 2:* Steel density (kg/m³)

**Domain 2 — Performance Characteristics (1 mark):** Features that quantify the operational capability.
- *Example 1:* Top speed of a frigate in knots
- *Example 2:* Cargo pumping rate of an oil tanker in m³/hr

**Domain 3 — Sensory Characteristics (1 mark):** Features perceived by human senses.
- *Example 1:* Surface roughness of the bridge (RA in µm)
- *Example 2:* Engine room noise level (dB)

**Domain 4 — Time-Related Characteristics (1 mark):** Features that evolve over time.
- *Example 1:* Fatigue life of welded joints (cycles)
- *Example 2:* MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) of the main engine (hours)

**Domain 5 — Ergonomic Characteristics (1 mark):** Features related to human-system interaction.
- *Example 1:* Bridge visibility cone (360° unobstructed, ISO 8468)
- *Example 2:* Vibration dose value at crew seats (ISO 2631)

**Domain 6 — Functional Characteristics (1 mark):** Features that determine the operational utility of a system.
- *Example 1:* Turning circle diameter at design speed
- *Example 2:* Emergency stop response time of the main engine

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**QFD Definition (1 mark):** Quality Function Deployment is a structured planning methodology that translates **Customer Requirements (CRs)** into **Technical Characteristics (TCs)** through a matrix known as the **House of Quality (HoQ)**. Developed by Yoji Akao (Japan, 1966).

**Components of the House of Quality (4 marks — block diagram is mandatory):**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ROOF MATRIX (Inter-Technical Correlations)                 │
│  +────────────────+────────────────+──────────────+         │
│  │ TECHNICAL QCs  │                │  IMPORTANCE  │         │
│  │  → → → → → →   │  CORRELATION   │   WEIGHT     │         │
│  │ Speed  Payload │  MATRIX        │  (per CR)    │         │
│  │ Range  Cost    │  (CRs × TCs)   │              │         │
│  │ Safety Comfort │                │              │         │
│  +────────────────+────────────────+──────────────+         │
│  │ CUSTOMER       │   ●●○ symbols: │  TECHNICAL   │         │
│  │ REQUIREMENTS   │   Strong/Med/  │  TARGETS     │         │
│  │ (Voice of      │   Weak         │  & UNITS     │         │
│  │  Customer)     │   correlation  │              │         │
│  │ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓    │                │              │         │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Translation of CRs → TCs for a container ship (2 marks):**

| Customer Requirement (VoC) | Technical Quality Characteristic (VoE) | Target |
|---|---|---|
| Fast transit (reduce ETA) | Top speed of vessel | $\geq 24$ knots |
| Maximum TEU capacity | Container slot count | $\geq 8{,}000$ TEU |
| Long ocean range | Fuel bunker capacity | $\geq 5{,}000$ tonnes |
| Low slot cost | Fuel consumption per nautical mile | $\leq 90$ g/kWh |
| Cargo safety (heavy weather) | Hull scantling safety factor | $\geq 1.5$ |
| Crew comfort on long voyages | Habitability index (ISO 8468) | Class B+ |

**Conclusion:** The QFD outputs a **prioritized list of technical QCs** with measurable targets, which directly feed into the design specification, ITP, and quality plan of the shipyard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ⚠️**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Drawing the House of Quality **without the ROOF matrix** (inter-TC correlations). The roof is mandatory for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Writing only "customer requirements" without translating them into **measurable** TCs. KTU requires units and target values.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing **"WHATs" (CRs)** with **"HOWs" (TCs)**. A CR like "fast transit" is a WHAT; a TC like "top speed" is a HOW.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1, Quality Characteristics**

- ✅ **Definition (ISO 9000:2015):** Quality Characteristic = *inherent feature* of an object related to a requirement.
- ✅ **Six Domains (Juran's taxonomy):** Physical, Performance, Sensory, Time-related, Ergonomic, Functional.
- ✅ **Three-Tier Confusion Trap:** QC (feature) ≠ Quality Parameter (value) ≠ Quality Indicator (KPI).
- ✅ **Four-Tier Hierarchy:** Ship-level → System-level → Component-level → Process-level.
- ✅ **5-Step QCT Construction:** "MDTMA" → Mission → Decompose → Translate → Measure → Audit.
- ✅ **Capability Benchmarks:** $C_p < 1$ (incapable), $1 \leq C_p < 1.33$ (marginal), $C_p \geq 1.33$ (capable), $C_p \geq 1.67$ (Six-Sigma class).
- ✅ **Mandatory Standards:** ISO 9000:2015, ISO 9001:2015, IACS UR, SOLAS, MARPOL, AWS D1.1, ISO 5817, ISO 8501-1.
- ✅ **QFD — House of Quality:** Translates CRs (WHATs) into TCs (HOWs) via a correlation matrix with a roof of inter-TC correlations.
- ✅ **Process Capability Formula:** $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ and $C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$.
- ✅ **Shipbuilding QC Examples to Memorize:**
  - Hull plate thickness tolerance: $-0.0$ / $+0.4$ mm
  - Weld visual: ISO 5817 Level B
  - Surface prep: ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5
  - Coating DFT: per ISO 19840
  - Plate yield: AH36 ≥ 355 N/mm²
- ✅ **Common KTU Valuation Traps:** Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$, omitting the ROOF matrix in QFD, mixing up WHATs and HOWs, forgetting units in formulas.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Quality Characteristics | KTU 2024 Scheme | PENST862**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Standards # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Quality Standards A **Quality Standard** is a formally documented set of requirements, specifications, guidelines, or characteristics that a product, process, or service must consistently satisfy to demonstrate its fitness for purpose and ensure stakeholder confidence. In the context of shipbuilding, quality standards establish the engineering, procedural, and managerial benchmarks against which every hull plate, weld, piping system, electrical installation, and documentation deliverable is measured. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Ready):** > *"Quality Standards are authoritative reference documents — issued by international bodies (ISO, IEC), classification societies (IACS), flag administrations, and industry consortia — that codify the minimum technical, procedural, and management criteria required to assure that a ship, its components, and the processes used to build it, satisfy safety, performance, environmental, and statutory requirements throughout the vessel's lifecycle."* The standards landscape in shipbuilding is multi-layered: | Layer | Examples | Governing Body | |---|---|---| | **International Management** | ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001 | ISO (Geneva) | | **Maritime Statutory** | SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines | IMO (London) | | **Classification** | LR Rules, DNV Rules, ABS Rules, BV Rules | IACS Members | | **Welding & Fabrication** | ISO 3834, EN 1090, AWS D1.1 | ISO / CEN / AWS | | **NDT Competence** | ISO 9712 | ISO | | **Laboratory & Testing** | ISO/IEC 17025 | ISO/IEC | | **Audit Methodology** | ISO 19011 | ISO | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Shipyard Recipe Book" Imagine a master shipwright in the year 1820. His "quality standard" was the **master's eye** — judgment passed down orally from a generation of shipwrights to apprentices. There was no written benchmark. A new apprentice might over-weld a joint by 30 %, or under-treat a timber, and no one could quantify the deviation. Now imagine the modern shipyard. Every welder follows **WPS (Welding Procedure Specifications)** that list exact current, voltage, travel speed, and preheat temperature. Every inspector refers to **ISO 9712** to certify that the NDT operator is competent. Every project manager references **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.1** to plan controlled production. > [!NOTE] > **The Analogy:** A quality standard is to a shipyard what a **building code (NBC) plus a master recipe book plus a medical licensing exam** is to a hospital. It tells the builder *what* to achieve, *how* to verify, and *who* is authorised to certify. ## 1.3 Why Quality Standards Are Non-Negotiable in Shipbuilding Shipbuilding differs fundamentally from mass manufacturing. Each vessel is essentially a **one-off mega-structure** assembled in open yards, exposed to weather, and constructed from millions of parts sourced globally. The consequences of failure are catastrophic (loss of life, environmental disaster, asset loss). Standards therefore serve **five irreducible purposes**: 1. **Safety Assurance** — Prevent structural, mechanical, and electrical failures at sea. 2. **Inter-operability** — Ensure components from different makers fit and function together. 3. **Statutory Compliance** — Meet flag-state and international maritime law (SOLAS, MARPOL). 4. **Risk Transfer** — Insurance underwriters and P&I clubs require certified compliance. 5. **Continuous Improvement** — Provide a measurable baseline for kaizen / Six Sigma projects. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 explicitly requires students to identify the major international and maritime quality standards. Memorise the ISO 9000 **family** structure and the **seven QMS principles** — these are guaranteed 7-to-14 mark questions in the ESE. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The ISO 9000 Family — The Backbone of Modern Quality Management The **ISO 9000 family** is the world's most widely implemented quality framework, with over **1.3 million certified organisations** in 190+ countries. It is built around three core documents: ### 2.1.1 The Three Pillars | Standard | Full Title | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **ISO 9000:2015** | Quality management systems — Fundamentals and vocabulary | Defines the **terminology** and **fundamental concepts** (no certification possible against this alone). | | **ISO 9001:2015** | Quality management systems — Requirements | The **certifiable** standard. Specifies the requirements a QMS must meet. | | **ISO 9004:2018** | Quality management — Quality of an organisation | Guidance for **sustained success**; goes beyond ISO 9001. | Supporting standards include **ISO 19011** (auditing), **ISO 31000** (risk management), and **ISO 10005** (quality plans). ## 2.2 ISO 9001:2015 — Structure and Core Philosophy ### 2.2.1 The High-Level Structure (HLS) Since 2015, all ISO management system standards share a **10-clause High-Level Structure (HLS)**. This allows seamless integration of QMS, EMS (ISO 14001), and OH&S (ISO 45001) into a single Integrated Management System (IMS). | Clause | Title | Focus | |---|---|---| | 1 | Scope | Boundaries of the QMS | | 2 | Normative references | ISO 9000 | | 3 | Terms and definitions | Vocabulary | | 4 | Context of the organisation | Internal/external issues, interested parties | | 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment, policy, roles | | 6 | Planning | Risks, opportunities, QMS objectives | | 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information | | 8 | Operation | Operational planning, control, design, production | | 9 | Performance evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review | | 10 | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement | ### 2.2.2 The Seven Quality Management Principles (QMPs) These principles form the philosophical foundation of ISO 9001:2015: 1. **Customer Focus** — Primary focus on meeting customer and applicable regulatory requirements. 2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction. 3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered, engaged people at all levels. 4. **Process Approach** — Consistent and predictable results are achieved more efficiently when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes. 5. **Improvement** — Successful organisations have an ongoing focus on improvement. 6. **Evidence-based Decision Making** — Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data and information. 7. **Relationship Management** — Managing relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners, regulators). ## 2.3 PDCA Cycle and the Process Approach ISO 9001:2015 embeds the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA / Deming Cycle)** at two levels: - **PDCA at the QMS level** (Clauses 4–10 overall structure). - **PDCA at the process level** (each individual process in the shipyard). $$\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Output Achieved (Conforming Product)}}{\text{Input Applied (Resources + Specs)}}$$ The risk-based thinking introduced in ISO 9001:2015 replaced the preventive-action concept of the 2008 version. Risks and opportunities are identified at the **planning stage (Clause 6.1)** rather than reactively. ## 2.4 Maritime-Specific Standards — The Shipbuilding Quality Stack Beyond ISO 9001, a shipyard must satisfy a hierarchy of sector-specific standards. ### 2.4.1 Classification Society Rules (IACS) **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** is the body of 12 member classification societies (LR, DNV, ABS, BV, CCS, CRS, IRS, KR, ClassNK, PRS, RINA, RS). Each society publishes **Rules and Regulations** for hull, machinery, electrical systems, and materials. While each society's rules differ in detail, IACS maintains **Unified Requirements (UR)** to harmonise them. | Society | Abbreviation | Headquarters | |---|---|---| | Lloyd's Register | LR | London, UK | | DNV (now DNV) | DNV | Høvik, Norway | | American Bureau of Shipping | ABS | Houston, USA | | Bureau Veritas | BV | Paris, France | | Nippon Kaiji Kyokai | ClassNK | Tokyo, Japan | | Registro Italiano Navale | RINA | Genoa, Italy | | Korean Register | KR | Busan, South Korea | ### 2.4.2 Welding and Fabrication Standards | Standard | Title | Relevance to Shipbuilding | |---|---|---| | **ISO 3834-1 to -5** | Quality requirements for fusion welding of metallic materials | Defines **four quality levels** (Comprehensive, Standard, Elementary, Pristine) that classification societies map to weld class. | | **EN 1090-1 / -2** | Execution of steel structures and aluminium structures | CE-marking of structural components; Execution Class EXC1–EXC4. | | **AWS D1.1** | Structural Welding Code — Steel | Widely used in US-built vessels. | | **ASME Section IX** | Welding and Brazing Qualifications | Mandatory for pressure vessel / boiler welds. | ### 2.4.3 NDT Competence Standards | Standard | Title | |---|---| | **ISO 9712** | Non-destructive testing — Qualification and certification of NDT personnel | | **ISO 17025** | General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories | | **ASTM E-543** | Standard practice for agencies performing NDT | ### 2.4.4 Other Critical Standards | Standard | Scope | |---|---| | **ISO 14001:2015** | Environmental Management Systems (EMS) | | **ISO 45001:2018** | Occupational Health & Safety | | **ISO 19011:2018** | Guidelines for auditing management systems | | **ISO 31000:2018** | Risk management — Guidelines | | **SOLAS** (IMO) | International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea | | **MARPOL** (IMO) | Prevention of pollution from ships | | **ISO 9001:2015 / IACS UR Z17** | Procedural requirements for service suppliers | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarises key formulae, ratios, and structural mappings essential to quality standard analysis. | Concept | Formula / Mapping | Application | |---|---|---| | Conformity Rate | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{conforming}}}{N_{\text{total inspected}}} \times 100\%$ | Weld/inspection KPIs | | First Pass Yield | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{N_{\text{passed first time}}}{N_{\text{total started}}}$ | Process capability | | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | Six Sigma ($O$ = opportunities, $U$ = units, $D$ = defects) | | Process Capability | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensional control of plates | | Process Performance | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Centred vs off-centre processes | | PDCA Coverage | $\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 1$ where $w_i$ are P, D, C, A weights | Audit scoring | | Cost of Poor Quality | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | Quality economics | | ISO 3834 Quality Levels | ISO 3834-2 $\rightarrow$ 3834-3 $\rightarrow$ 3834-4 $\rightarrow$ 3834-5 | Comprehensive $\rightarrow$ Pristine | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Quality Standards in Engineering Quality standards are not bureaucratic paperwork. Their engineering utility is concrete: - **Design Optimisation** — ISO 9001 Clause 8.3 mandates Design and Development verification, which forces shipyards to perform FEM analysis, model tests, and prototype trials. - **Supply Chain Integration** — IACS UR Z17 and ISO 9001 certification are prerequisites for a supplier to be on a shipyard's Approved Vendor List (AVL). - **Liability Mitigation** — In maritime disputes, ISO 9001 / ISO 3834 / ISO 9712 certification records are legal evidence of "due diligence." - **Continuous Improvement** — The PDCA cycle drives down weld repair rates (typical reduction from 8 % to <2 % in three years). - **Cross-yard Mobility** — A welder qualified to ISO 9606-1 can work at any IACS yard globally without requalification. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Standards to Shipyard Activities The following matrix maps each shipbuilding activity to the primary quality standards that govern it. This is **directly examinable** in KTU Part B questions. | Shipyard Activity | Primary Standard | Secondary / Supporting | Verification Output | |---|---|---|---| | Hull plate cutting & forming | ISO 9001 Cl. 8.5, EN 1090-2 | Class Society Rules | Dimensional report, NDT of edges | | Welding procedure approval | ISO 15614-1, ASME IX | ISO 3834 | WPS, PQR, WPQ | | Welder qualification | ISO 9606-1 | AWS D1.1 | Welder certificate | | NDT inspection | ISO 9712 | ASTM E-543 | NDT report (RT, UT, MT, PT) | | Painting & corrosion protection | ISO 12944 | NACE / SSPC standards | DFT measurement, adhesion test | | Pipe fabrication | ASME B31.1 / B31.3 | Class Society piping rules | Hydrostatic test certificate | | Electrical installation | IEC 60092 | Class rules | Insulation resistance, continuity | | Machinery installation | ISO 9001 Cl. 8.5 | Maker's manuals, Class rules | Performance test (dock & sea trials) | | Document control | ISO 9001 Cl. 7.5 | IMO resolution A.601(15) | Document register | | Internal audit | ISO 19011 | ISO 9001 Cl. 9.2 | Audit report, NCRs | | Management review | ISO 9001 Cl. 9.3 | ISO 9004 | Meeting minutes, action plan | | Risk management | ISO 31000 | Class-specific risk UR | Risk register | ## 3.2 Derivation — The Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Decomposition The Cost of Poor Quality is a foundational quality economics metric. Let us derive it step by step. ### Step 1 — Define Quality Cost Categories Industry-standard categorisation (per Juran / Feigenbaum): $$C_{\text{P}} = \text{Prevention Costs (training, planning, quality system)}$$ $$C_{\text{A}} = \text{Appraisal Costs (inspection, testing, audits)}$$ $$C_{\text{IF}} = \text{Internal Failure Costs (rework, scrap, reinspection)}$$ $$C_{\text{EF}} = \text{External Failure Costs (warranty, recall, litigation)}$$ ### Step 2 — Total COPQ Aggregation $$\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{P}} + C_{\text{A}} + C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}}$$ ### Step 3 — Normalised COPQ (per vessel) For a shipyard delivering $N$ vessels per year, with revenue $R_i$ per vessel: $$\text{COPQ}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i}$$ Typical shipbuilding benchmark: **2 % to 5 % of turnover**. World-class yards target <2 %. ### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example Suppose a shipyard: - Spends **₹50 lakh** on prevention (training, FMEA). - Spends **₹80 lakh** on appraisal (NDT, third-party inspection). - Incurs **₹120 lakh** in rework and scrap. - Incurs **₹50 lakh** in warranty / claim settlements. - Annual revenue = **₹100 crore**. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} &= 50 + 80 + 120 + 50 \\ &= 300 \text{ lakh} \\ &= \text{₹}3 \text{ crore} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COPQ}_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{3 \text{ crore}}{100 \text{ crore}} \\ &= 0.03 = 3\% \end{aligned} $$ This 3 % falls within the **typical shipbuilding benchmark range (2 %–5 %)**. Improvement actions such as Six Sigma can target a 1 % reduction, yielding **₹1 crore savings** annually — directly justifying QA investment. ## 3.3 Derivation — Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ for Plate Flatness Consider a 12 mm hull plate with specification **Flatness ≤ 4 mm over 2 m span**. The standard deviation from process data is $\sigma = 0.8$ mm and the mean deviation is $\mu = 1.6$ mm. ### Step 1 — Identify Specification Limits $$\text{USL} = +4 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{LSL} = -4 \text{ mm (symmetric tolerance)}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute One-sided Indices $$ \begin{aligned} C_{p,\text{upper}} &= \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{4 - 1.6}{3 \times 0.8} = \frac{2.4}{2.4} = 1.00 \\ C_{p,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{1.6 - (-4)}{3 \times 0.8} = \frac{5.6}{2.4} = 2.33 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk} &= \min(C_{p,\text{upper}},\ C_{p,\text{lower}}) \\ &= \min(1.00,\ 2.33) \\ &= 1.00 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation - $C_{pk} < 1.00$: Process **not capable** (high rejection rate). - $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$: Process **marginally capable**. - $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$: Process **capable**. - $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$: Process **highly capable** (Six Sigma territory). A $C_{pk} = 1.00$ indicates the process is right at the edge — corrective action (machine recalibration, operator retraining) is required to lift $C_{pk}$ to at least 1.33. ## 3.4 Derivation — Risk-Based Thinking Mapping (ISO 9001 Cl. 6.1) Modern quality management uses **risk = likelihood × severity**. In a shipyard: $$ \text{Risk Score (R)} = L \times S $$ Where: - $L$ = Likelihood (1–5 scale: 1 rare, 5 frequent). - $S$ = Severity (1–5 scale: 1 negligible, 5 catastrophic — loss of life / vessel loss). A corrective action priority matrix: | Risk Score $R$ | Action | |---|---| | 1 – 4 | Accept; monitor | | 5 – 9 | Mitigate within 30 days | | 10 – 15 | Mitigate immediately; stop process if needed | | 16 – 25 | Halt activity; management review | ### Worked Example A gas shielded FCAW process on the bulbous bow shows porosity trending up. Assessment: - $L = 4$ (frequent occurrence in trial coupons). - $S = 5$ (porous critical-path weld could lead to structural failure). - $R = 4 \times 5 = 20$ → **Halt activity; management review**. This triggers ISO 9001 Cl. 10.2 (nonconformity and corrective action). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The ISO 9000 Family — Structural Map The following Mermaid block illustrates the relationship between the core and supporting ISO standards relevant to shipbuilding. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["ISO 9000 FAMILY OF STANDARDS"] nodeB["ISO 9000:2015
Fundamentals and Vocabulary"] nodeC["ISO 9001:2015
Requirements for QMS
Certifiable"] nodeD["ISO 9004:2018
Sustained Success"] nodeE["ISO 19011:2018
Auditing Management Systems"] nodeF["ISO 31000:2018
Risk Management"] nodeG["ISO 10005:2018
Quality Plans"] nodeH["ISO 14001:2015
Environmental Management"] nodeI["ISO 45001:2018
Occupational Health and Safety"] nodeJ["IACS Unified Requirements
Class Rules"] nodeK["ISO 3834
Welding Quality"] nodeL["ISO 9712
NDT Personnel Competence"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG nodeC --- nodeH nodeC --- nodeI nodeC --- nodeJ nodeC --- nodeK nodeC --- nodeL style nodeA fill:#0B3D91,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#C8102E,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 The PDCA (Deming) Cycle Applied to a Shipyard Weld Process ```mermaid graph LR nodeP["PLAN
Clause 6
WPS Development
Risk Identification"] nodeD["DO
Clause 8
Welding Trial
Production Welding"] nodeC["CHECK
Clause 9
NDT Inspection
Dimensional Check"] nodeA["ACT
Clause 10
Rework / Correction
WPS Revision"] nodeP ==> nodeD nodeD ==> nodeC nodeC ==> nodeA nodeA ==> nodeP style nodeP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style nodeD fill:#7CFC00,stroke:#000 style nodeC fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 The Multi-Layer Standard Stack in Shipbuilding ```mermaid graph TD layer1["Layer 1 International Treaty
SOLAS MARPOL Load Lines"] layer2["Layer 2 Classification Society Rules
LR DNV ABS BV ClassNK RINA"] layer3["Layer 3 Management Systems
ISO 9001 ISO 14001 ISO 45001"] layer4["Layer 4 Technical Standards
ISO 3834 EN 1090 AWS ASME IEC"] layer5["Layer 5 Competence Standards
ISO 9712 ISO 9606 ISO 17025"] layer6["Layer 6 Audit and Risk
ISO 19011 ISO 31000"] layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layer4 --> layer5 --> layer6 style layer1 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff layer2 fill:#B22222,stroke:#000,color:#fff style layer3 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000 style layer4 fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#fff style layer5 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style layer6 fill:#4B0082,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 ISO 9001:2015 Clause Flow — QMS Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD cl4["Clause 4
Context of Organisation"] cl5["Clause 5
Leadership"] cl6["Clause 6
Planning"] cl7["Clause 7
Support"] cl8["Clause 8

Performance Evaluation"] cl10["Clause 10

Quality control across this single activity involves seven interacting functions. ```mermaid graph LR blkA["Design Office
Releases Drawings and WPS"] blkB["Production Planning
Issues Block Erection Plan"] blkC["Surveyor IACS Member
Class Attendance Points"] blkD["Welding Foreman
Allocates Qualified Welders"] blkE["NDT Operator ISO 9712 Level 2
Performs RT UT MT PT"] blkF["QA Documentation Cell
Compiles Traceability Dossier"] blkG["Client Representative
Witnesses Key Tests"] blkA --> blkD blkB --> blkD blkD --> blkE blkE --> blkF blkC --> blkE blkG --> blkF blkF --> blkH["Block Released to Next Stage
Painting Outfitting"] style blkA fill:#4682B4,color:#fff style blkB fill:#5F9EA0,color:#fff style blkC fill:#8B008B,color:#fff style blkD fill:#B8860B,color:#fff style blkE fill:#DC143C,color:#fff style blkF fill:#2E8B57,color:#fff style blkG fill:#FF8C00,color:#fff style blkH fill:#000080,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern Note (2024 Scheme):** Each Part A carries 3 marks. Answers should be crisp, definition-style, and limited to 80–120 words. ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a quality standard. List any four international quality standards applicable to shipbuilding.** **Model Answer:** A quality standard is an authoritative document that specifies the requirements, specifications, or criteria a product, process, or service must satisfy to demonstrate fitness for purpose. In shipbuilding, quality standards establish engineering, procedural, and managerial benchmarks. Four international quality standards applicable to shipbuilding are: 1. **ISO 9001:2015** — Quality Management Systems. 2. **ISO 3834** — Quality requirements for fusion welding. 3. **ISO 9712** — Competence qualification of NDT personnel. 4. **ISO 14001:2015** — Environmental Management Systems. *(Any other relevant standard such as ISO 45001, ISO 19011, IACS UR, or class rules may also be listed.)* ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the seven Quality Management Principles of ISO 9001:2015 in brief.** **Model Answer:** The seven QMPs of ISO 9001:2015 are: 1. **Customer focus** — Understanding and meeting customer needs. 2. **Leadership** — Establishing direction and unity of purpose. 3. **Engagement of people** — Empowering competent personnel at all levels. 4. **Process approach** — Managing activities as interrelated processes. 5. **Improvement** — Ongoing focus on continual improvement. 6. **Evidence-based decision making** — Decisions grounded in data analysis. 7. **Relationship management** — Managing relationships with interested parties (suppliers, regulators). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern Note (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means students answer either Option A **or** Option B in full. ### QUESTION A (Option A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Discuss the ISO 9000 family of standards in detail with a focus on ISO 9001:2015. Explain its High-Level Structure and the concept of risk-based thinking. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The ISO 9000 family is a globally recognised suite of standards published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) for Quality Management Systems (QMS). It comprises three core documents — **ISO 9000** (vocabulary), **ISO 9001** (requirements), and **ISO 9004** (sustained success) — supported by sector-specific guidance documents. **ISO 9001:2015 High-Level Structure (HLS):** Since 2015, all ISO management system standards share a 10-clause structure: - Clauses **1–3** set the scope, normative references, and definitions. - Clause **4** determines the **context** of the organisation (internal/external issues, scope of QMS, processes). - Clause **5** establishes **leadership** and top management commitment. - Clause **6** covers **planning** of risks, opportunities, and quality objectives. - Clause **7** addresses **support** (resources, competence, communication, documented information). - Clause **8** governs **operation** (planning, product/service requirements, design, production, control of nonconforming outputs). - Clause **9** deals with **performance evaluation** (monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review). - Clause **10** ensures **improvement** (nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement). **Risk-Based Thinking:** ISO 9001:2015 replaced the standalone "preventive action" clause with risk-based thinking integrated into the entire QMS. The organisation must identify risks and opportunities (Cl. 6.1), evaluate their potential effect on conformity of products and services, and plan actions to address them. This proactive approach minimises the likelihood of nonconformities and maximises opportunity capture. Tools such as FMEA, SWOT, and risk matrices (likelihood × severity) are used to operationalise risk thinking in a shipyard. **(b) Explain the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle with reference to its application in a shipyard's welding quality control process. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **PDCA cycle** is a four-stage iterative management method attributed to Walter Shewhart and popularised by W. Edwards Deming. ISO 9001:2015 mandates PDCA at both the overall QMS level and at each individual process level. **Application in Shipyard Welding QC:** | Stage | Welding QC Activity | |---|---| | **PLAN** | Develop the Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) per **ISO 15614-1**. Identify risks (porosity, lack of fusion). Define inspection criteria per **ISO 3834**. Train welders (ISO 9606). | | **DO** | Execute production welding using qualified welders. Record parameters (current, voltage, travel speed). Maintain traceability of electrodes and base materials. | | **CHECK** | Perform NDT (RT, UT, MT, PT) per **ISO 9712** certified operators. Measure weld dimensions against WPS tolerance. Compare against acceptance criteria (Class Rules). | | **ACT** | Issue Non-Conformance Reports (NCR) for failures. Implement corrective actions (rework, retraining, WPS revision). Feed lessons learnt into the next PLAN cycle. | **Outcome:** This iterative cycle drives **continual improvement**, reduces weld repair rates, and ensures that weld quality moves systematically toward world-class benchmarks. In a mature shipyard, this cycle is executed at the project, block, panel, and joint levels. --- ### QUESTION B (Option B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain the role of classification societies and IACS in shipbuilding quality assurance. List at least five major classification societies. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A **classification society** is a non-governmental organisation that establishes and applies technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of ships and offshore structures. Classification is **not statutory** (that is the role of the flag state), but in practice it is mandatory because most flag states delegate technical surveys to classification societies. **Functions of a Classification Society:** 1.
