Develop and publish **Rules and Regulations** for hull, machinery, and electrical systems. 2. Conduct **plan approval** during the design stage. 3. Perform **surveys during construction** (attendance at fabrication, welding, erection, launching). 4. Issue **Classification Certificates** (e.g., Hull, Machinery, Cargo Ship Safety Construction). 5. Maintain the **Register Book** of classed vessels. 6. Investigate marine casualties and issue findings. **IACS — International Association of Classification Societies:** Founded in 1969, IACS is the umbrella body of major classification societies. Its roles include: - Issuing **Unified Requirements (UR)** and **Procedural Requirements (PR)** to harmonise rules across members. - Acting as a recognised organisation (RO) before the IMO. - Promoting quality and consistency of classification services. **Five Major Classification Societies:** 1. **Lloyd's Register (LR)** — UK. 2. **DNV (Det Norske Veritas)** — Norway. 3. **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** — USA. 4. **Bureau Veritas (BV)** — France. 5. **Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (ClassNK)** — Japan. *(Other members include RINA, KR, IRS, CCS, CRS, PRS, RS.)* **(b) Describe the salient requirements of ISO 3834 and ISO 9712. Why are they critical for shipbuilding? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **ISO 3834 — Quality Requirements for Fusion Welding of Metallic Materials:** ISO 3834 is a five-part international standard specifying quality requirements for fusion welding in metallic materials. It establishes four quality levels mapped to the criticality of welds: | Part | Title | Use Case | |---|---|---| | ISO 3834-1 | Criteria for selection of appropriate level | Decision framework | | ISO 3834-2 | **Comprehensive** quality requirements | Critical structural welds (hatch corners, side shell) | | ISO 3834-3 | **Standard** quality requirements | Secondary structural welds | | ISO 3834-4 | **Elementary** requirements | Non-critical welds | | ISO 3834-5 | **Pristine** requirements | Where higher than comprehensive is required | ISO 3834 covers welding coordination personnel, WPS qualification (per ISO 15614 series), welder qualification (ISO 9606), production control, inspection, and testing. **ISO 9712 — Qualification and Certification of NDT Personnel:** ISO 9712 is the international standard for the qualification and certification of personnel performing non-destructive testing. It defines: - **Three levels of certification**: Level 1 (operate equipment), Level 2 (perform and supervise), Level 3 (authorise procedures, train/audit). - Required training hours and examination modules (RT, UT, MT, PT, ET, VT). - Validity periods (typically 5 years for Level 1/2, 10 years for Level 3) and re-certification requirements. - Industrial sector endorsements (e.g., **sector a: pre-/in-service welding inspection**). **Why They Are Critical for Shipbuilding:** 1. **Safety** — Welds in ships carry fatigue and fracture-critical loads; substandard welds can cause catastrophic hull failure (e.g., *MV Prestige* 2002). 2. **Class Rule Compliance** — All IACS member societies require compliance with ISO 3834 and ISO 9712 as a condition of class. 3. **Legal Defence** — In casualty investigations, certified welders and NDT operators provide legal evidence of due diligence. 4. **Global Mobility of Workforce** — ISO 9606 + ISO 9712 certifications are portable across yards worldwide. 5. **Insurance and P&I Acceptance** — Underwriters require ISO-compliant QA records to provide cover. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse ISO 9000 (vocabulary) with ISO 9001 (certifiable).** Marks are routinely lost by students writing "9000" when they mean "9001". > 2. **PDCA is not just for QMS.** Examiners expect explicit linking of each PDCA stage to a *specific shipyard activity*. > 3. **Always cite the standard number correctly** — say "ISO 9001:2015", not just "ISO 9001". The year denotes the revision. > 4. **Classification Society ≠ IMO.** IMO writes conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL); classification societies write technical rules. Both are needed. > 5. **Seven QMPs of ISO 9001:2015 are mandatory recall items.** Forgetting any one of them costs at least 1 mark per answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Standards** - [ ] A **Quality Standard** is a documented benchmark for product, process, or service fitness for purpose. - [ ] The **ISO 9000 family** comprises ISO 9000, 9001, and 9004, supported by ISO 19011, 31000, 10005, etc. - [ ] **ISO 9001:2015** is the only certifiable QMS standard in the family. - [ ] ISO 9001:2015 uses a **10-clause High-Level Structure (HLS)** shared with ISO 14001 and ISO 45001. - [ ] The **seven Quality Management Principles** are: Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement of People, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-based Decision Making, Relationship Management. - [ ] **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is embedded at QMS level and at each individual process level. - [ ] **Risk-based thinking** replaced preventive action in ISO 9001:2015 (Cl. 6.1). - [ ] **IACS** is the International Association of Classification Societies with 12 member societies. - [ ] Major classification societies: **LR, DNV, ABS, BV, ClassNK, RINA, KR** (among 12 members). - [ ] **ISO 3834** specifies four welding quality levels (Comprehensive, Standard, Elementary, Pristine). - [ ] **ISO 9712** governs NDT personnel qualification across three levels. - [ ] **ISO 9606** governs welder qualification; **ISO 15614** governs WPS qualification. - [ ] **ISO 14001:2015** = EMS; **ISO 45001:2018** = OH&S; **ISO 19011:2018** = Auditing. - [ ] **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. - [ ] **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the threshold for a capable process in shipbuilding QC. - [ ] Class Rules + IMO Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL) + ISO Management Standards + Technical Standards (ISO 3834, EN 1090) + Competence Standards (ISO 9712, ISO 9606) + Audit/Risk (ISO 19011, ISO 31000) form the **six-layer shipbuilding quality stack**. - [ ] Remember the **2015 revision** for ISO 9001 and ISO 14001; the **2018 revision** for ISO 9004, ISO 19011, and ISO 45001 (note: ISO 45001:2018, not :2015). - [ ] Typical shipbuilding COPQ benchmark = **2 % to 5 %** of annual turnover.

topics.title: Quality Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Cost # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Cost (Cost of Quality - COQ)** is defined as the total expenditure incurred by an organization in ensuring that products and services conform to specified quality requirements, including the costs associated with preventing, detecting, and rectifying non-conformities. In the context of shipbuilding, it encompasses all monetary outlays related to designing, producing, inspecting, repairing, and warranting a vessel against established classification society and customer standards. The term **"Cost of Quality" (COQ)** is somewhat counterintuitive because it does *not* refer to the cost of producing a high-quality product; rather, it refers to the **total cost** incurred due to quality-related activities—both conformance and non-conformance activities. The KTU 2024 Scheme adopts the classical **Joseph Juran** and **Philip Crosby** framework for Quality Cost analysis, which categorizes quality costs into four distinct families: 1. **Prevention Costs** – Expenditures to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place. 2. **Appraisal Costs** – Expenditures to *detect* defects through inspection and testing. 3. **Internal Failure Costs** – Costs incurred when defects are discovered *before* delivery to the customer. 4. **External Failure Costs** – Costs incurred when defects are discovered *after* delivery, in service. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you own a fishing boat (a small ship!). You have two choices for keeping it sea-worthy: > - **Option A (Prevention + Appraisal):** Pay for regular engine servicing, anti-fouling paint, and pre-voyage inspections. > - **Option B (Failure Costs):** Wait for the engine to break down mid-sea, pay for emergency towing, repair the hull, and compensate fishermen for lost catch. > > The *smart* boat owner spends more on Option A, because every rupee saved on prevention causes fifty rupees of failure cost later. **Quality Cost** is exactly this trade-off—capturing every rupee spent on *preventing* problems versus every rupee spent on *fixing* them. The goal of quality cost analysis is to **shift the spending toward prevention**, where the marginal cost of avoiding a defect is far less than the marginal cost of repairing it. In a shipyard, this translates as follows: - A shipyard that **spends heavily on prevention** (welding procedure qualification, ISO 9001 implementation, skilled workforce training) will see **dramatically lower failure costs** (fewer weld rework hours, less steel scrap, fewer customer warranty claims). - Conversely, a yard that **neglects prevention** and relies only on appraisal (NDT after welding) ends up reworking huge volumes of defective welds—an extremely expensive proposition given the labour intensity of shipbuilding. ## 1.3 Standard Quality Cost Metrics The following industry-standard ratios are bolded as they are universally cited in KTU board examinations: - **Prevention Cost Ratio (PCR):** Percentage of total quality cost spent on prevention activities. - **Appraisal-to-Failure Cost Ratio (A/F Ratio):** Indicator of an organization's quality maturity. - **Cost of Quality as a Percentage of Sales (COQ%):** Benchmark metric for performance tracking. - **Failure Cost Ratio (FCR):** Proportion of total quality cost attributed to failures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Quality Cost is a *planning* and *control* tool—not a mere accounting exercise. It allows shipyard managers to identify the **Pareto-significant** quality cost elements (typically rework in welding and piping accounts for 60–80 % of internal failure costs in Indian shipyards) and to justify investment in prevention programmes. ## 1.4 The "Iceberg" Model of Quality Cost A widely taught conceptual model in KTU Module 1 is the **Quality Cost Iceberg**: | Visible (Above Water) | Hidden (Below Water) | |---|---| | Scrap, Rework, Warranty Claims | Lost Customer Goodwill | | Inspection Costs | Lost Productivity | | Testing Costs | Lost Market Share | | Rejection Costs | Schedule Slippage | | Concessions & Deviations | Engineering Investigation Time | Most shipyard managers see only the visible costs and fail to allocate budgets for the hidden costs. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *identify* and *rank* these categories. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The P-A-F Model (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) The P-A-F Model is the foundational taxonomy of Quality Cost. Each category is further subdivided into shipbuilding-relevant sub-elements: ### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (P) Expenditures incurred to *prevent defects from occurring* in the first place. **Typical Shipbuilding Sub-Elements:** - Quality planning and procedure writing (Welding Procedure Specifications, Quality Plans). - Training and certification of welders, fitters, NDT operators. - Design reviews and Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA). - Supplier qualification and material certification audits. - Calibration of measuring instruments. - Quality Management System (QMS) implementation and audits (ISO 9001). - Process capability studies. ### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (A) Expenditures incurred to *detect defects* through inspection, testing, and measurement. **Typical Shipbuilding Sub-Elements:** - Incoming material inspection (steel plates, pipes, fittings). - In-process inspection (weld visual examination, dimensional checks). - Non-Destructive Testing (NDT): Ultrasonic Testing (UT), Radiographic Testing (RT), Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT), Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT). - Hydrostatic and pneumatic testing of pressure vessels and piping. - Sea trials and harbour acceptance tests. - Inspection equipment depreciation. - Inspector salaries and third-party survey fees (Classification Society surveyors). ### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (IF) Costs incurred when defects are discovered **before** delivery to the customer—i.e., inside the shipyard. **Typical Shipbuilding Sub-Elements:** - **Rework** of defective welds (the single largest quality cost in shipbuilding). - **Scrap** of steel plates, pipes, and castings. - **Re-inspection** after rework. - Material wastage due to design changes or wrong fabrication. - Schedule delays causing contractual penalties. - Investigation of root causes (corrective action costs). ### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (EF) Costs incurred when defects are discovered **after delivery**, i.e., during the ship's operational life. **Typical Shipbuilding Sub-Elements:** - Warranty claims and post-delivery rectifications. - Hull damage repair costs during guarantee period. - Litigation and legal settlements. - Loss of goodwill and future order cancellations. - Cost of towing/repair at sea due to early-life failures. - Insurance premium increases. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes every key equation and relationship required to solve KTU 2024 Scheme Quality Cost numerical problems. | # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$ | Total Quality Cost | INR / USD per vessel | | 2 | $COC = P + A$ | Cost of Conformance | INR | | 3 | $CONC = IF + EF$ | Cost of Non-Conformance | INR | | 4 | $TQC = COC + CONC$ | Restatement of (1) | INR | | 5 | $COQ\% = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100$ | Quality Cost Intensity | Percentage | | 6 | $PCR = \dfrac{P}{TQC} \times 100$ | Prevention Cost Ratio | Percentage | | 7 | $FCR = \dfrac{IF + EF}{TQC} \times 100$ | Failure Cost Ratio | Percentage | | 8 | $A/F = \dfrac{A}{IF + EF}$ | Appraisal-to-Failure Ratio | Dimensionless | | 9 | $Q_{opt} : \dfrac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 0$ | Optimal Quality Level (Economic Quality) | Quality units | | 10 | $EF_{per\,vessel} = \dfrac{EF_{total}}{N_{vessels}}$ | Per-Vessel External Failure Cost | INR / vessel | | 11 | $IF_{per\,Tonne} = \dfrac{IF_{total}}{\text{Steel Tonnage}}$ | Internal Failure Cost per Tonne (shipbuilding specific) | INR / Tonne | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions for Use in KTU Exams:** > - All costs are positive semi-definite: $P, A, IF, EF \geq 0$. > - In mature shipyards, typical ranges: $P \approx 5$–$10\%$, $A \approx 20$–$30\%$, $IF \approx 40$–$60\%$, $EF \approx 5$–$15\%$ of $TQC$. > - In best-in-class shipyards (Japan, South Korea), $P \approx 15$–$25\%$ and $EF < 5\%$—reflecting the maturity of their QMS. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Quality Cost Analysis Quality cost analysis is not a one-time accounting exercise; it is a **continuous improvement engine** with the following engineering utilities in shipbuilding: 1. **Identify Pareto-Dominant Cost Elements:** In almost every Indian shipyard audit, **weld rework** and **piping rework** account for over 60 % of internal failure costs. Once identified, these become targets for Kaizen/Lean projects. 2. **Justify Capital Expenditure on Prevention:** Showing management that "₹1 lakh spent on welder training saves ₹10 lakh in rework" unlocks budgets for training, automation, and digitalization. 3. **Benchmark Across Yards:** Standardized COQ% allows comparison between shipyards in the same corporate group or against international competitors. 4. **Drive ISO 9001 / IACS QSCS Compliance:** Classification societies such as Lloyd's Register and DNV increasingly demand documented COQ trends as part of supplier qualification audits. 5. **Set Quality Objectives (QOs):** Provides a quantitative basis for the "measurable objectives" required by ISO 9001 Clause 6.2. ## 2.4 Cost of Conformance vs Cost of Non-Conformance (The Core Trade-Off) > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Concept:** As an organization invests more in Prevention (P), failure costs fall faster than prevention costs rise—**up to a point**. Beyond that point, additional prevention spending yields diminishing returns. The **minimum of the Total Quality Cost curve** represents the *economic* or *optimal* quality level. Mathematically, this is expressed as: $$ \dfrac{dP}{dQ} > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{d(IF + EF)}{dQ} < 0 $$ Where $Q$ is the quality level. The optimum occurs when the marginal cost of additional prevention equals the marginal savings in failure cost. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Quality Cost Model This derivation is a **KTU favourite** and frequently appears for 7–14 marks. We will derive the expression for the *minimum Total Quality Cost* as a function of quality level $Q$. ### 3.1.1 Assumptions 1. Quality level $Q$ is a continuous variable measured as a percentage (e.g., 95 %, 99 %, 99.9 %). 2. Prevention cost $P(Q)$ is a **monotonically increasing, convex** function of $Q$. We assume a linear approximation for tractability: $$ P(Q) = k_1 Q + c_1 $$ where $k_1$ and $c_1$ are positive constants determined empirically. 3. Appraisal cost $A(Q)$ is approximately constant for moderate ranges of $Q$: $$ A(Q) = k_2 $$ 4. Internal failure cost $IF(Q)$ is a **monotonically decreasing, convex** function of $Q$. We assume: $$ IF(Q) = k_3 (100 - Q)^2 $$ 5. External failure cost $EF(Q)$ is approximately: $$ EF(Q) = k_4 (100 - Q) $$ ### 3.1.2 Derivation **Step 1 — Compose the Total Quality Cost:** $$ TQC(Q) = P(Q) + A(Q) + IF(Q) + EF(Q) $$ Substituting the assumed functions: $$ TQC(Q) = (k_1 Q + c_1) + k_2 + k_3 (100 - Q)^2 + k_4 (100 - Q) $$ **Step 2 — Expand the squared term:** $$ TQC(Q) = k_1 Q + c_1 + k_2 + k_3 (10000 - 200Q + Q^2) + 100 k_4 - k_4 Q $$ **Step 3 — Group by powers of $Q$:** $$ TQC(Q) = k_3 Q^2 + (k_1 - 200 k_3 - k_4) Q + (c_1 + k_2 + 10000 k_3 + 100 k_4) $$ **Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $Q$ to find the minimum:** $$ \dfrac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 2 k_3 Q + (k_1 - 200 k_3 - k_4) $$ **Step 5 — Set the derivative to zero for the optimum:** $$ 2 k_3 Q_{opt} + (k_1 - 200 k_3 - k_4) = 0 $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $Q_{opt}$:** $$ 2 k_3 Q_{opt} = 200 k_3 + k_4 - k_1 $$ $$ Q_{opt} = \dfrac{200 k_3 + k_4 - k_1}{2 k_3} $$ $$ Q_{opt} = 100 + \dfrac{k_4 - k_1}{2 k_3} $$ **Step 7 — Second-derivative test for minimum:** $$ \dfrac{d^2(TQC)}{dQ^2} = 2 k_3 > 0 \quad (\text{since } k_3 > 0) $$ Since the second derivative is positive, the critical point is indeed a **minimum**. **Step 8 — Minimum Total Quality Cost:** Substituting $Q_{opt}$ back: $$ TQC_{min} = c_1 + k_2 + 10000 k_3 + 100 k_4 - \dfrac{(k_4 - k_1)^2}{4 k_3} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for Shipbuilding:** The optimal quality level $Q_{opt}$ depends on the *relative magnitudes* of prevention efficiency ($k_1$) and failure-cost elasticity ($k_3, k_4$). In shipyards where rework is very expensive (large $k_3$), the optimum shifts toward higher $Q_{opt}$, justifying greater investment in prevention. Conversely, when prevention is inherently costly (e.g., exotic NDT methods), $k_1$ is large and the optimum shifts to a more moderate $Q_{opt}$. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem Statement (Adapted for KTU 2024 Scheme):** A Cochin-based shipyard records the following annual quality costs for a single Panamax bulk carrier: | Category | Amount (₹ Lakhs) | |---|---| | Welder Training and Certification | 12 | | WPS Preparation and Design Review | 8 | | NDT (UT, RT, MPT) | 45 | | Inspection Salaries | 30 | | Weld Rework | 120 | | Steel Scrap | 25 | | Schedule Delay Penalties | 18 | | Warranty Repairs (post-delivery) | 40 | **Required:** (a) Classify each item into P, A, IF, or EF. *(4 marks)* (b) Calculate Total Quality Cost, Cost of Conformance, Cost of Non-Conformance, COQ% (assume contract value ₹ 50 Crore), PCR, and FCR. *(7 marks)* (c) Recommend two prevention activities to reduce failure costs. *(3 marks)* ### 3.2.1 Solution **(a) Classification:** - Welder Training and Certification → **Prevention (P)** - WPS Preparation and Design Review → **Prevention (P)** - NDT (UT, RT, MPT) → **Appraisal (A)** - Inspection Salaries → **Appraisal (A)** - Weld Rework → **Internal Failure (IF)** - Steel Scrap → **Internal Failure (IF)** - Schedule Delay Penalties → **Internal Failure (IF)** - Warranty Repairs (post-delivery) → **External Failure (EF)** **(b) Calculations:** **Step 1 — Sum Prevention costs:** $$ P = 12 + 8 = 20 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 2 — Sum Appraisal costs:** $$ A = 45 + 30 = 75 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 3 — Sum Internal Failure costs:** $$ IF = 120 + 25 + 18 = 163 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 4 — Sum External Failure costs:** $$ EF = 40 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 5 — Total Quality Cost:** $$ TQC = P + A + IF + EF = 20 + 75 + 163 + 40 = 298 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 6 — Cost of Conformance:** $$ COC = P + A = 20 + 75 = 95 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 7 — Cost of Non-Conformance:** $$ CONC = IF + EF = 163 + 40 = 203 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 8 — COQ as a Percentage of Sales:** $$ COQ\% = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Contract Value}} \times 100 = \dfrac{298}{5000} \times 100 = 5.96\% $$ **Step 9 — Prevention Cost Ratio:** $$ PCR = \dfrac{P}{TQC} \times 100 = \dfrac{20}{298} \times 100 \approx 6.71\% $$ **Step 10 — Failure Cost Ratio:** $$ FCR = \dfrac{IF + EF}{TQC} \times 100 = \dfrac{203}{298} \times 100 \approx 68.12\% $$ **(c) Recommendations:** 1. **Invest in Automated Welding (e.g., Robotic SAW/GMAW)** to reduce human-error-driven weld defects, which are the dominant source of rework. 2. **Implement Statistical Process Control (SPC) on fit-up dimensions** to catch tolerance excursions *before* welding commences, eliminating rework at source. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Quality Cost Maturity Levels | Maturity Level | P (%) | A (%) | IF (%) | EF (%) | Strategic Diagnosis | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Level 1 — Reactive** | 5 | 15 | 55 | 25 | Fire-fighting; high warranty exposure. | | **Level 2 — Awakening** | 10 | 25 | 50 | 15 | Awareness rising; some prevention programmes exist. | | **Level 3 — Proactive** | 20 | 30 | 40 | 10 | Investments in prevention yielding returns. | | **Level 4 — World-Class** | 30 | 35 | 30 | 5 | Six Sigma / Lean maturity; minimal failures. | | **Level 5 — Benchmark** | 40 | 35 | 22 | 3 | Continuous innovation; near-zero defects. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Interpretation:** The Indian shipbuilding industry predominantly operates at Level 2–3, while leading Korean and Japanese yards operate at Level 4–5. Movement up this maturity ladder is a multi-year journey requiring leadership commitment, employee training, and digitalization (Industry 4.0). ## 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis: Effect of Doubling Prevention Budget A KTU favourite problem: *What happens to TQC if prevention spending is doubled?* **Step 1 — Initial state:** $$ TQC_1 = P + A + IF + EF $$ **Step 2 — Double prevention:** $$ P' = 2P $$ **Step 3 — Empirical rule of thumb (cross-industry):** A 10 % increase in prevention cost typically reduces internal failure cost by **15–25 %** in labour-intensive industries like shipbuilding. For doubling, we assume: $$ IF' = IF \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.70 \, IF $$ (assuming a conservative 30 % reduction for doubling prevention). **Step 4 — New Total Quality Cost:** $$ TQC_2 = 2P + A + 0.70\, IF + EF $$ **Step 5 — Net change:** $$ \Delta TQC = TQC_2 - TQC_1 = P - 0.30\, IF $$ $$ \Delta TQC = P - 0.30 \times 163 = 20 - 48.9 = -28.9 \text{ Lakhs} $$ Since $\Delta TQC < 0$, doubling prevention yields a **net saving of ₹ 28.9 Lakhs**, even before considering the qualitative benefits of reduced warranty exposure and improved customer goodwill. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PAF Model Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeQMS[Quality Management System
ISO 9001 / IACS QSCS] --> nodeP[Prevention Costs] nodeQMS --> nodeA[Appraisal Costs] nodeQMS --> nodeIF[Internal Failure Costs] nodeQMS --> nodeEF[External Failure Costs] nodeP --> nodeP1[Welder Training] nodeP --> nodeP2[WPS Preparation] nodeP --> nodeP3[Design Review] nodeP --> nodeP4[Supplier Audit] nodeP --> nodeP5[SPC Studies] nodeA --> nodeA1[NDT Inspection] nodeA --> nodeA2[Dimensional Check] nodeA --> nodeA3[Hydrostatic Test] nodeA --> nodeA4[Sea Trials] nodeIF --> nodeIF1[Weld Rework] nodeIF --> nodeIF2[Steel Scrap] nodeIF --> nodeIF3[Re Inspection] nodeIF --> nodeIF4[Schedule Slippage] nodeEF --> nodeEF1[Warranty Claims] nodeEF --> nodeEF2[Litigation] nodeEF --> nodeEF3[Customer Compensation] nodeEF --> nodeEF4[Reputation Loss] nodeIF1 --> nodeReduce[Total Quality Cost Minimization] nodeEF1 --> nodeReduce nodeP1 --> nodeReduce nodeA1 --> nodeReduce nodeReduce --> nodeGoal[Optimal Quality Level Q-opt] ``` ## 4.2 Cost Flow Block Diagram (Quality Cost as a System) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeInput[Shipyard Inputs
Steel Labour Energy] --> nodeProcess[Shipbuilding Process] nodeProcess --> nodeOutput[Vessel Delivery] nodeInvest[Prevention Investment
P rupees] --> nodeProcess nodeInspect[Appraisal Activity
A rupees] --> nodeProcess nodeProcess --> nodeDefect{Defect Found?} nodeDefect --> nodeYesRework[Internal Failure IF rupees
Rework Scrap Re Inspect] nodeDefect --> nodeNoPass[Pass] nodeYesRework --> nodeReCheck{Re Check OK?} nodeReCheck --> nodeYesFinal[Pass] nodeReCheck --> nodeNoReject[Scrap] nodeOutput --> nodeInService[In Service] nodeInService --> nodeFieldFailure{Field Failure?} nodeFieldFailure --> nodeYesClaim[External Failure EF rupees
Warranty Litigation Reputation] nodeFieldFailure --> nodeNoGood[Customer Satisfied] nodePass --> nodeOutput nodeYesFinal --> nodeOutput ``` ## 4.3 Quality Cost Reporting Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeData[Raw Quality Cost Data
From SAP / ERP] --> nodeClassify[Classification Engine
P A IF EF] nodeClassify --> nodeAggregate[Aggregation by Project and Cost Centre] nodeAggregate --> nodeReport[Monthly COQ Report] nodeReport --> nodeMgmt[Shipyard Management Review] nodeMgmt --> nodeDecision[Strategic Decisions] nodeDecision --> nodeAction[Action Plans Training CAPA] nodeAction --> nodeProcess[Updated Process] nodeProcess --> nodeData ``` ## 4.4 Maturity Ladder Schematic (Movement Across Levels) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeL1[Level 1 Reactive
P 5 A 15 IF 55 EF 25] --> nodeL2[Level 2 Awakening
P 10 A 25 IF 50 EF 15] nodeL2 --> nodeL3[Level 3 Proactive
P 20 A 30 IF 40 EF 10] nodeL3 --> nodeL4[Level 4 World Class
P 30 A 35 IF 30 EF 5] nodeL4 --> nodeL5[Level 5 Benchmark
P 40 A 35 IF 22 EF 3] nodeL1 --> nodeTools1[Tools 5 Why Pareto Chart] nodeL2 --> nodeTools2[Tools ISO 9001 FMEA] nodeL3 --> nodeTools3[Tools SPC DOE] nodeL4 --> nodeTools4[Tools Lean Six Sigma] nodeL5 --> nodeTools5[Tools Industry 4 0 Digital Twin] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Cost of Quality". List the four categories of quality cost as per the PAF model.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure that its products and services conform to quality requirements. It includes the cost of preventing defects, detecting defects, and the costs arising from defective products. **Four Categories (2 Marks):** 1. **Prevention Costs (P):** Costs incurred to prevent defects (e.g., training, quality planning). 2. **Appraisal Costs (A):** Costs incurred to detect defects (e.g., inspection, NDT). 3. **Internal Failure Costs (IF):** Costs of defects found before delivery (e.g., rework, scrap). 4. **External Failure Costs (EF):** Costs of defects found after delivery (e.g., warranty, claims). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Distinguish between Cost of Conformance and Cost of Non-Conformance. Give two shipbuilding examples for each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Cost of Conformance (COC) (1 Mark):** Costs incurred to *meet* quality requirements—both prevention and appraisal costs. **Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC) (1 Mark):** Costs incurred due to *failure* to meet quality requirements—both internal and external failure costs. **Shipbuilding Examples (1 Mark):** - *COC Examples:* Welder training (Prevention); Ultrasonic testing of welds (Appraisal). - *CONC Examples:* Weld rework before launch (Internal Failure); Hull crack repair under guarantee (External Failure). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** A Kochi shipyard constructing offshore patrol vessels compiles the following annual quality cost data: | S.No. | Cost Item | Amount (₹ in Lakhs) | |---|---|---| | 1 | Weld procedure qualification and WPS preparation | 15 | | 2 | Welder training and certification | 10 | | 3 | Steel plate incoming inspection | 22 | | 4 | Radiographic testing of welds | 38 | | 5 | Weld rework | 95 | | 6 | Steel scrap due to cutting errors | 18 | | 7 | Engine room piping rework | 42 | | 8 | Post-delivery propeller shaft repair | 25 | | 9 | Customer compensation for delayed delivery | 30 | | 10 | Design review meetings | 8 | **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify each cost item into P, A, IF, or EF and compute the Total Quality Cost, Cost of Conformance, Cost of Non-Conformance, and the Failure Cost Ratio. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the shipyard doubles its prevention budget and this reduces internal failure costs by 25 %, calculate the new Total Quality Cost and comment on whether the investment is justified. --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Classification and Calculations (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Classify each item (3 Marks):** | S.No. | Cost Item | Classification | |---|---|---| | 1 | WPS preparation | Prevention (P) | | 2 | Welder training | Prevention (P) | | 3 | Steel plate inspection | Appraisal (A) | | 4 | Radiographic testing | Appraisal (A) | | 5 | Weld rework | Internal Failure (IF) | | 6 | Steel scrap | Internal Failure (IF) | | 7 | Piping rework | Internal Failure (IF) | | 8 | Propeller shaft repair | External Failure (EF) | | 9 | Customer compensation | External Failure (EF) | | 10 | Design review | Prevention (P) | **Step 2 — Compute aggregates (2 Marks):** $$ P = 15 + 10 + 8 = 33 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ A = 22 + 38 = 60 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ IF = 95 + 18 + 42 = 155 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ EF = 25 + 30 = 55 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the required metrics (2 Marks):** $$ TQC = 33 + 60 + 155 + 55 = 303 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ COC = 33 + 60 = 93 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ CONC = 155 + 55 = 210 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ FCR = \dfrac{IF + EF}{TQC} \times 100 = \dfrac{210}{303} \times 100 \approx 69.31\% $$ --- **(b) New TQC after doubling Prevention (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — New prevention cost:** $$ P' = 2 \times 33 = 66 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 2 — Reduction in IF (25 %):** $$ IF' = 155 \times (1 - 0.25) = 155 \times 0.75 = 116.25 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 3 — New TQC:** $$ TQC' = P' + A + IF' + EF = 66 + 60 + 116.25 + 55 = 297.25 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 4 — Net change:** $$ \Delta TQC = 297.25 - 303 = -5.75 \text{ Lakhs} $$ **Step 5 — Comment (2 Marks):** Yes, the investment is justified. Although the savings of ₹ 5.75 Lakhs appear modest in monetary terms, this calculation excludes: - The *qualitative* benefits of reduced warranty exposure. - Improved customer goodwill and higher probability of repeat orders. - Lower schedule delays and associated contractual penalties. - Reduced stress on supervisory staff and improved morale. Therefore, doubling the prevention budget is **strategically justified**, even if the immediate financial saving is marginal. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the "Iceberg Model" of quality cost with reference to shipbuilding. Discuss why managers often underestimate the true cost of poor quality. **(b) [7 Marks]** The following data pertains to two shipyards (Yard X and Yard Y) producing the same class of vessel: | Cost Element | Yard X (₹ Lakhs) | Yard Y (₹ Lakhs) | |---|---|---| | Prevention | 40 | 15 | | Appraisal | 70 | 35 | | Internal Failure | 80 | 110 | | External Failure | 10 | 40 | | Contract Value | 6000 | 6000 | Compare the two yards using COQ%, PCR, FCR, and the A/F Ratio. Identify which yard is more quality-mature and justify your conclusion. --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) The Iceberg Model (7 Marks):** **Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):** The Iceberg Model, attributed to *John Seddon* and popularized in quality literature, illustrates that the visible portion of quality costs (above water) represents only a small fraction of the true cost of poor quality. The bulk of quality-related costs lie hidden below the surface. **Visible Costs (Above Water) – Examples in Shipbuilding (2 Marks):** - Scrap steel plates and pipes - Visible weld rework - Inspection costs - Warranty repairs **Hidden Costs (Below Water) – Examples in Shipbuilding (2 Marks):** - Lost customer goodwill and future order cancellations - Schedule slippage cascading through the entire build plan - Loss of management credibility with classification societies - Engineering time spent on investigations - Reduced workforce morale **Why Managers Underestimate (Squeeze in for 2 Marks within section):** 1. **Accounting systems** typically capture only direct, visible costs and classify hidden costs as "overhead." 2. **Lack of measurement culture** in shipyards—many indirect costs are never quantified. 3. **Optimism bias** among managers who assume their yard is "above average." 4. **Cross-functional cost diffusion**—a delay caused by welding may be charged to "project overhead" rather than to "quality failure." --- **(b) Comparative Analysis (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Yard X Metrics (3 Marks):** $$ TQC_X = 40 + 70 + 80 + 10 = 200 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ COQ\%_X = \dfrac{200}{6000} \times 100 = 3.33\% $$ $$ PCR_X = \dfrac{40}{200} \times 100 = 20\% $$ $$ FCR_X = \dfrac{80 + 10}{200} \times 100 = 45\% $$ $$ A/F_X = \dfrac{70}{80 + 10} = \dfrac{70}{90} \approx 0.778 $$ **Step 2 — Yard Y Metrics (2 Marks):** $$ TQC_Y = 15 + 35 + 110 + 40 = 200 \text{ Lakhs} $$ $$ COQ\%_Y = \dfrac{200}{6000} \times 100 = 3.33\% $$ $$ PCR_Y = \dfrac{15}{200} \times 100 = 7.5\% $$ $$ FCR_Y = \dfrac{110 + 40}{200} \times 100 = 75\% $$ $$ A/F_Y = \dfrac{35}{110 + 40} = \dfrac{35}{150} \approx 0.233 $$ **Step 3 — Comparative Table (1 Mark):** | Metric | Yard X | Yard Y | Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | COQ% | 3.33 % | 3.33 % | Identical total spend | | PCR | 20 % | 7.5 % | Yard X invests 2.7× more in prevention | | FCR | 45 % | 75 % | Yard Y has 30 % more failure costs | | A/F | 0.778 | 0.233 | Yard X is 3.3× more mature | **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 Mark):** **Yard X is more quality-mature.** Although both yards spend an identical absolute amount on quality (₹ 200 Lakhs), Yard X's strategic emphasis on **prevention** (PCR = 20 %) yields dramatically lower failure costs (FCR = 45 %), whereas Yard Y's neglect of prevention (PCR = 7.5 %) results in three-quarters of its quality spend being wasted on failure recovery. Yard X follows the strategic direction recommended by **Deming's 85/15 Rule** and **Crosby's Zero Defects philosophy**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing Prevention with Appraisal:** Students frequently classify routine inspection as "Prevention." Inspection is *appraisal*—it detects defects but does not prevent them. Prevention = activities that *eliminate* the cause of defects. > 2. **Forgetting Customer Compensation as External Failure:** Many students miss that customer compensation for late delivery is an external failure cost because it reflects failure to meet contractual quality/delivery promises. > 3. **Ignoring EF in early-stage calculations:** Always check whether the problem statement includes post-delivery data; if so, EF must be accounted for. > 4. **Mixing up COC and CONC:** Memorize the mnemonic: *Conformance* = "Conforming to specs" = P + A. *Non-Conformance* = "Not conforming" = IF + EF. > 5. **Not computing PCR and FCR:** Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for these ratios. Always include them in your answer. > 6. **Calculation errors:** When asked to compute *new* TQC after a prevention change, students often incorrectly *subtract* instead of *add*. Write the equation explicitly: $TQC_{new} = P_{new} + A + IF_{new} + EF$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Quality Cost** - **Definition:** Quality Cost (COQ) is the totality of expenditure on conformance plus non-conformance activities. - **PAF Model:** Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure — the four canonical categories. - **Key Equations:** - $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$ - $COC = P + A$; $CONC = IF + EF$ - $COQ\% = TQC / \text{Sales} \times 100$ - $PCR = P / TQC \times 100$ - $FCR = (IF + EF) / TQC \times 100$ - $A/F = A / (IF + EF)$ - **Optimal Quality Level:** Occurs where marginal prevention cost equals marginal failure cost reduction. - **Iceberg Model:** Visible costs are minor; hidden costs (loss of goodwill, schedule slippage) are major. - **Maturity Ladder:** Movement from Reactive → World-Class requires shifting spend from failure to prevention. - **Shipbuilding Specifics:** - Weld rework is the single largest internal failure cost element. - Hydrostatic testing and sea trials are major appraisal activities. - Warranty repair and customer compensation are key external failure costs. - **Strategic Principle (Deming):** Higher prevention investment reduces failure costs faster than it raises prevention costs — up to the optimal quality level. - **Crosby's Insight:** "Quality is free." The cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure recovery. - **Classification Trick:** Inspection = Appraisal; Training = Prevention; Rework = Internal Failure; Warranty = External Failure. - **Reporting:** COQ must be tracked monthly, per project, per cost centre, with Pareto analysis on the dominant cost element.

topics.title: Concept of Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Concept of Quality Control (QC)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quality Control (QC)** is the operational, shop-floor oriented set of activities and techniques used to fulfil the requirements for quality. It is a **product-oriented**, **reactive**, and **inspection-driven** discipline whose primary goal is to identify defects in finished products, intermediate components, and raw materials, and to ensure that only conforming items are released for use, assembly, or delivery.

As per the **ISO 9000:2015** family of standards (which is the internationally accepted backbone adopted by KTU 2024 scheme for engineering quality education), quality control is formally defined as:

> *"Part of quality management focused on fulfilling quality requirements."*

In the context of **shipbuilding**, Quality Control covers the inspection, measurement, and testing of:
- Steel plates and rolled sections (dimensional and chemical)
- Welded joints (visual, NDT — radiographic, ultrasonic, magnetic particle)
- Hull lines and offsets
- Pipe spools and machinery components
- Outfit items, electrical systems, and coatings

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Quality Control as a "Gate Inspector"

Imagine a **highway toll booth** on a busy national highway. Cars (raw materials, sub-assemblies) arrive at the gate. The toll inspector (the QC inspector) checks **each vehicle** — does it meet road-safety rules? Are brakes working? Are headlights fine? If **yes** → the car is allowed to pass (released for next process). If **no** → the car is diverted for repair or rejection.

The gate inspector does **not** design the car, **does not** fix the factory that produced a faulty model, and **does not** plan the highway. He only **inspects, sorts, and decides**. That is exactly what Quality Control does in a shipyard — it inspects every plate, weld, pipe, and component against drawings and specifications and decides *accept / rework / reject*.

### 1.3 Core Terminology in **Bold**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions Every KTU Student Must Memorise:**
>
> - **Quality (Q)** — The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfils requirements (ISO 9000:2015).
> - **Quality Control (QC)** — The operational techniques and activities used to detect and correct defects in the *product* itself.
> - **Quality Assurance (QA)** — All the planned and systematic activities implemented within the *quality system* that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements.
> - **Quality Management (QM)** — Coordinated activities to direct and control an organisation with regard to quality (umbrella term covering QA + QC + policy).
> - **Quality System (QS)** — The organisational structure, responsibilities, procedures, processes, and resources for implementing quality management.
> - **Defect** — The non-fulfilment of a requirement related to an intended or specified use.
> - **Conformance** — The fulfilment of a specified requirement.
> - **Inspection** — The process of measuring, examining, testing, and gauging one or more characteristics of a product and comparing the results with specified requirements.

### 1.4 QC in the Shipbuilding Value Chain

In shipbuilding, QC is not a single department — it is a **process woven across every block of the ship**. From the moment a steel coil enters the stockyard to the sea-trial of the finished vessel, Quality Control is the silent watchdog.

| Shipyard Stage | Typical QC Activity | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Steel receipt | Dimensional, mill-cert, chemical check | IACS UR W, Class rules |
| Cutting & forming | Plate nesting, edge preparation check | Workshop drawings |
| Welding | Visual + NDT (RT/UT/MPI/DPI) | AWS D1.1, IACS UR W11/W13 |
| Block assembly | Alignment, squareness, tolerance | Class tolerance tables |
| Erection on berth | Hull line, block level, fairing | Ship specification |
| Outfitting | Pipe pressure test, cable continuity | Maker's trials, Class |
| Pre-launch & launch | Inclining experiment, leak test | SOLAS, Load Line |
| Sea trial | Performance, noise, vibration | Contract specification |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight (2024 Scheme):** Examiners frequently ask students to *distinguish between Quality Control and Quality Assurance*. Always remember — QC is **product-focused, detective, and corrective**; QA is **process-focused, preventive, and systemic**. This is the single most asked 3-mark question in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — The QC Operating Framework

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Quality Control

Quality Control in any engineering industry — shipbuilding included — rests on **three interlinked pillars**:

1. **Specification (the "what")** — A clear, measurable description of what is required. In shipbuilding this is the *approved-for-construction drawing*, the *Class rule citation*, the *welding procedure specification (WPS)*, or the *paint specification*.
2. **Inspection (the "how we check")** — The actual measurement, visual examination, or non-destructive test that compares reality against specification.
3. **Disposition (the "what next")** — The decision: *Accept*, *Rework*, *Repair*, or *Reject* (often abbreviated A/R/R/R in yard QC reports).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The QC Equation (Conceptual):**
>
> $$\text{QC Effectiveness} = f(\text{Clear Specification},\ \text{Calibrated Inspection},\ \text{Timely Disposition})$$
>
> If any of the three terms is zero, QC fails — even with the best inspectors and most expensive equipment.

## 2.2 Principles of Quality Control (Juran's QC Trilogy adapted for KTU)

Quality Control is governed by **three universal planning principles** which every KTU 2024 scheme student is expected to know:

- **Quality Planning** — Identify the customers (internal and external), their requirements, and develop processes to meet them.
- **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs goals, take corrective action on the difference.
- **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure to continuously improve processes and reduce chronic waste.

In shipbuilding, this maps to:

| Juran Principle | Shipyard Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Quality Planning | Tender stage — class rule study, WPS development |
| Quality Control | Stage inspection, NDT, dimensional surveys |
| Quality Improvement | Lessons learned database, weld defect trend analysis |

## 2.3 Inspection Types Used in Quality Control

Quality Control uses **four categories of inspection**, depending on when and where it is done:

- **Source Inspection** — Performed at the supplier's premises before despatch (e.g., vendor inspection of a marine engine).
- **Receiving Inspection** — Done at the shipyard on incoming material (steel plates, bought-out equipment).
- **In-Process Inspection** — Conducted during fabrication at sub-stages (after tack welding, after fit-up, before final weld).
- **Final Inspection** — Carried out on the finished product before handover (sea trial, dock trial).

## 2.4 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — The Quantitative Backbone

Modern QC is not guesswork. It is built on **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** introduced by **Walter A. Shewhart** in 1924 at Bell Labs. SQC gives QC its mathematical teeth.

> [!NOTE]
> **SQC divides naturally into two branches:**
>
> 1. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — Uses *control charts* to monitor a *process* while it is running. Goal: detect when the process drifts out of statistical control.
> 2. **Acceptance Sampling** — Uses *sampling plans* (e.g., MIL-STD-105E / ISO 2859-1 / MIL-STD-1916) to decide whether a *lot* of items should be accepted or rejected.

### 2.4.1 The Normal Distribution Foundation

Most QC statistical theory assumes the measured quality characteristic follows a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \ \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

where:
- $\mu$ = process mean (target)
- $\sigma$ = process standard deviation (spread)
- $x$ = measured value

Within the $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ band, **99.73 %** of all measurements lie. This 3-sigma rule is the heart of Shewhart control charts.

### 2.4.2 Control Limits — The Heart of an SPC Chart

For an **$\bar{X}$ (X-bar) chart** monitoring the subgroup means:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \,\bar{R}$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \,\bar{R}$$

where:
- $\bar{\bar{X}}$ = grand average of subgroup means
- $\bar{R}$ = average range of subgroups
- $A_2$ = control-chart constant (depends on subgroup size $n$; for $n=5$, $A_2 = 0.577$)

For an **R-chart** (monitoring variability):

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \,\bar{R}, \qquad \text{CL}_R = \bar{R}, \qquad \text{LCL}_R = D_3 \,\bar{R}$$

For $n=5$: $D_4 = 2.114$, $D_3 = 0$.

### 2.4.3 Process Capability Indices

These tell us *whether the process can meet the specification*, independently of statistical control.

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

Interpretation (industry standard):
- $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ → process capable
- $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ → excellent (six-sigma territory)

### 2.4.4 Acceptance Sampling — Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve

In shipbuilding, **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** sampling is used for bought-out items. For a single-sampling plan with sample size $n$ and acceptance number $c$, the probability of acceptance is:

$$P_a = \sum_{k=0}^{c} \binom{n}{k} p^{\,k} (1-p)^{\,n-k}$$

where $p$ is the true fraction defective in the lot. Plotting $P_a$ vs $p$ gives the **OC curve** — the QC engineer's "decision graph".

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Use in Shipbuilding QC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Normal distribution | Modelling dimensional variation |
| 2 | $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit, X-bar chart | Monitoring weld leg-length |
| 3 | $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit, X-bar chart | Monitoring plate thickness |
| 4 | $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Control limits, R-chart | Monitoring variability |
| 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability | Block squareness, alignment |
| 6 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process capability index (centred) | Pipe diameter, bolt torque |
| 7 | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{c}\binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$ | Probability of acceptance | AQL lot inspection |
| 8 | $\text{AOQL} \approx 0.7\,p^*$ | Average outgoing quality limit | Vendor QC audit |
| 9 | $\bar{R} = d_2\,\sigma$ | Average range to std-dev conversion | Estimating $\sigma$ from data |
| 10 | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Standard error of mean | Subgroup sampling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall:** Always use $\vert\ \vert$ or `abs()` notation in tables instead of single vertical pipes `\|` — they break markdown rendering. In this cheat-sheet table, $\vert \text{USL} - \mu\vert$ would be written in your notebook using brackets such as $\vert\ \vert$.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of QC in Shipbuilding

A modern ship is a **5-million-part floating assembly**. Without QC, the **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** and **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** requirements cannot be evidenced. Practical consequences of QC failures include:

- **Hull failures** — brittle fractures at low temperatures (e.g., the *Liberty ship* failures of WWII, traced to poor steel chemistry and no QC).
- **Weld defects** — cracks, lack of fusion, porosity → fatigue → catastrophic fracture in service.
- **Class detention** — Failure of Class survey leads to commercial loss; reinsurance rates rise.
- **Off-hire / downtime** — Poor outfitting QC causes machinery breakdowns at sea.

Thus QC is not a paperwork exercise — it is the **economic and safety backbone** of every ship.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section rigorously derives the QC control limits, process capability indices, and acceptance-sampling probabilities that KTU 2024 examiners expect in 14-mark problems.

## 3.1 Derivation — Shewhart Control Limits from the 3-$\sigma$ Principle

**Premise:** Subgroup measurements are independent, identically distributed (iid) and follow $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 1.** For a subgroup of size $n$, the sample mean $\bar{X}$ follows a normal distribution with:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

**Step 2.** The 3-$\sigma$ band around the true mean of $\bar{X}$ gives the **theoretical control limits**:

$$\mu_{\bar{X}} \pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu \pm \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3.** In practice we do not know $\mu$ and $\sigma$. We **estimate** them from $k$ subgroups of size $n$:

$$\hat{\mu} = \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\bar{X}_i$$

**Step 4.** To estimate $\sigma$ from subgroup ranges $R_i$:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant (for $n=5$, $d_2 = 2.326$).

**Step 5.** Substitute into the 3-$\sigma$ limit, and combine the constants $\dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$ into the single tabulated constant $A_2$:

$$\frac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}} = A_2$$

**Step 6.** Final working formula (the one you use in the exam):

$$\boxed{\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \qquad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}}$$

Similarly for the R-chart:

$$\boxed{\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \qquad \text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}}$$

with $D_4 = 1 + \dfrac{3\,d_3}{d_2}$ and $D_3 = 1 - \dfrac{3\,d_3}{d_2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation — Process Capability Indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

**Premise:** A stable (in statistical control) process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ produces output between $\mu - 3\sigma$ and $\mu + 3\sigma$ virtually always (99.73 %).

**Step 1.** The **natural tolerance** of the process is therefore $6\sigma$ (spread of $\pm 3\sigma$ about the mean).

**Step 2.** The **specification tolerance** is the spread the *customer* allows: $\text{USL} - \text{LSL}$.

**Step 3.** Define $C_p$ as the ratio of specification tolerance to natural tolerance:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

This measures *potential* capability assuming the process is perfectly centred.

**Step 4.** Real processes are rarely centred. Define the off-centring term:

$$k = \frac{2\mu - (\text{USL} + \text{LSL})}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}$$

**Step 5.** The one-sided capability indices are:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 6.** Take the minimum (worst side) as $C_{pk}$:

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}) = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)}$$

Alternative compact form:

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - \vert k\vert)$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Acceptance Sampling Probability $P_a$ for Binomial Lot Inspection

**Premise:** Lot of $N$ items, of which $Np$ are defective. A sample of $n$ is drawn. The lot is accepted if the sample contains at most $c$ defectives.

**Step 1.** Probability that **exactly** $k$ of the $n$ sampled items are defective (hypergeometric, exact):

$$P(X = k) = \frac{\binom{Np}{k}\binom{N-Np}{n-k}}{\binom{N}{n}}$$

**Step 2.** For large $N$ relative to $n$ (typical case, $N/n \ge 10$), the hypergeometric reduces to the **binomial approximation**:

$$P(X = k) \approx \binom{n}{k} p^{\,k} (1-p)^{\,n-k}$$

**Step 3.** The probability of acceptance is the cumulative sum up to $c$:

$$\boxed{P_a = \sum_{k=0}^{c}\binom{n}{k}p^{\,k}(1-p)^{\,n-k}}$$

**Step 4.** Two extreme cases give intuition:
- $p = 0$: $P_a = 1$ (perfect lot always accepted).
- $p = 1$: $P_a = 0$ if $c = 0$, else $P_a = 1$ if $n \le c$.

**Step 5.** The Producer's Risk $\alpha$ is the probability of rejecting a *good* lot ($p = p_1$ = AQL):

$$\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$$

The Consumer's Risk $\beta$ is the probability of accepting a *bad* lot ($p = p_2$ = LTPD):

$$\beta = P_a(p_2)$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem.** A shipyard monitors the *plate-thickness deviation* (mm from nominal) of 25-mm hull plates. Five subgroups of size $n = 5$ are taken; their means $\bar{X}_i$ and ranges $R_i$ are:

| Subgroup $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}_i$ | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.12 | 0.18 | 0.20 |
| $R_i$ | 0.08 | 0.10 | 0.09 | 0.11 | 0.12 |

**Specification:** USL = +0.40 mm, LSL = −0.40 mm. The process is centred. Compute the control limits, estimate $\sigma$, and find $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

**Solution — Step 1: Grand mean and average range**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{0.10 + 0.15 + 0.12 + 0.18 + 0.20}{5} = \frac{0.75}{5} = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.08 + 0.10 + 0.09 + 0.11 + 0.12}{5} = \frac{0.50}{5} = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Control chart constants for $n = 5$**

From the standard SPC table: $A_2 = 0.577$, $d_2 = 2.326$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $D_3 = 0$.

**Solution — Step 3: X-bar chart limits**

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 0.15 + (0.577)(0.10) = 0.15 + 0.0577 = 0.2077 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 0.15 - (0.577)(0.10) = 0.15 - 0.0577 = 0.0923 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

**Solution — Step 4: R-chart limits**

$$\text{UCL}_R = (2.114)(0.10) = 0.2114 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = (0)(0.10) = 0 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_R = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Solution — Step 5: Estimate of $\sigma$**

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.10}{2.326} = 0.0430 \text{ mm}$$

**Solution — Step 6: Process capability indices**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{0.40 - (-0.40)}{6 \times 0.0430} = \frac{0.80}{0.258} = 3.10$$

Since the process is centred ($\mu = \bar{\bar{X}} = 0.15 \neq 0$ mid-spec), we still compute $C_{pk}$:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{0.40 - 0.15}{3 \times 0.0430} = \frac{0.25}{0.129} = 1.94$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{0.15 - (-0.40)}{0.129} = \frac{0.55}{0.129} = 4.26$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.94,\ 4.26) = 1.94$$

**Conclusion:** Both $C_p = 3.10$ and $C_{pk} = 1.94$ exceed **1.33**, so the plate-thickness process is highly capable and well-suited to shipbuilding tolerance needs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Quality Control Operating Cycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: Specification & Drawings"] --> nodeB["PLAN: Define CTQ and Inspection Plan"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MEASURE: Collect Dimensional and NDT Data"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ANALYSE: Compute Control Limits and Capability"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"In Statistical Control?"}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF{"Capability Cpk >= 1.33?"}
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG["ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS"]
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeH["ACCEPT and RELEASE"]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeI["CORRECTIVE ACTION on PROCESS"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Corrective Action Plan"]
    nodeJ --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeH --> nodeK["FEEDBACK LOOP to QA System"]
    nodeK --> nodeB
```

**Reading the diagram.** Each node is a step in the **PDCA-style Quality Control loop** (Plan-Do-Check-Act). Data flows clockwise; failure branches re-enter the measurement stage for re-evaluation. The feedback loop to *QA System* ensures QC findings drive systemic improvement — bridging Section 1's QC/QA distinction.

## 4.2 QC vs QA — Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQM["QUALITY MANAGEMENT (QM) Umbrella"] --> nodeQA["QUALITY ASSURANCE (QA) Process Focused Preventive Systemic"]
    nodeQM --> nodeQC["QUALITY CONTROL (QC) Product Focused Detective Reactive"]
    nodeQA --> nodeSOP["Procedures, Audits, Training, Document Control"]
    nodeQC --> nodeInsp["Inspection, Measurement, Testing, Sampling"]
    nodeSOP --> nodeShip["SHIPBUILDING DELIVERABLES"]
    nodeInsp --> nodeShip
```

**Interpretation.** QA creates the **system** that prevents defects; QC executes the **tests** that catch defects. Both are children of Quality Management, and both ultimately feed the same physical deliverable — the ship.

## 4.3 Hierarchy of QC Documentation in a Modern Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop["Quality Manual (ISO 9001)"] --> nodeProc["Procedures QA-QP series"]
    nodeProc --> nodeWPS["WPS / WPQR Welding Docs"]
    nodeProc --> nodeITP["Inspection and Test Plans"]
    nodeProc --> nodeNCR["NCR and CAR Records"]
    nodeWPS --> nodeInsp["Stage Inspection Sheets"]
    nodeITP --> nodeInsp
    nodeInsp --> nodeCert["Class and Maker Certificates"]
    nodeNCR --> nodeCert
```

This diagram mirrors the typical **Class-society audit trail** that every ship must carry in its *Construction File* before delivery.

## 4.4 Inspection Sequence Along the Shipbuilding Production Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSteel["Steel Receipt"] --> nodeCut["Cutting and Forming"]
    nodeCut --> nodeFit["Fit-up"]
    nodeFit --> nodeWeld["Welding"]
    nodeWeld --> nodeNDT["NDT Stage Inspection"]
    nodeNDT --> nodeBlock["Block Assembly"]
    nodeBlock --> nodeErect["Berth Erection"]
    nodeErect --> nodeOut["Outfitting"]
    nodeOut --> nodeLaunch["Launch and Mooring Trial"]
    nodeLaunch --> nodeSea["Sea Trial"]
    nodeSea --> nodeDel["Delivery to Owner"]
```

Every arrow in this diagram represents **at least one QC hold-point** in a real shipyard's ITP (Inspection and Test Plan). KTU examiners love asking students to mark such points on a flow diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Quality Control. How does it differ from Quality Assurance? [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Quality Control (QC)** is the operational part of quality management that focuses on *fulfilling quality requirements* by detecting and correcting defects in the product through inspection, measurement, and testing (ISO 9000:2015).

**Quality Assurance (QA)**, in contrast, is the *systematic, planned part* of quality management that provides confidence that the product or service will satisfy quality requirements — it focuses on the **process** that produces the product.

| Aspect | Quality Control | Quality Assurance |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Product | Process / System |
| Nature | Reactive / Detective | Preventive |
| Tools | Inspection, gauges, NDT | Audits, procedures, training |
| Responsibility | QC inspector / line | QA manager / entire org |
| Question answered | "Is this part good?" | "Are we building it the right way?" |

In shipbuilding, **QC** checks the weld, plate, pipe; **QA** builds the WPS, training matrix, audit schedule. *(3 marks: definition 1, contrast table 1, shipbuilding example 1.)*

### Q2. List the four types of inspection used in shipbuilding Quality Control. Give one example of each. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

1. **Source Inspection** — Performed at the supplier's works. *Example*: Witnessing the manufacturer's pressure test of a marine boiler before despatch.
2. **Receiving Inspection** — Done at the shipyard on incoming material. *Example*: Dimensional check of steel plates against mill certificate.
3. **In-Process Inspection** — Conducted during fabrication. *Example*: Visual + dye-penetrant inspection of a butt-weld before the next pass.
4. **Final Inspection** — Performed on the completed product. *Example*: Inclining experiment and dock trial before delivery.

*(3 marks: list 1, examples 2.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. A choice of two questions is offered; only **one** is to be attempted.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the concept of Statistical Quality Control (SQC). Describe the construction of an $\bar{X}$-chart with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

**Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the application of statistical methods to monitor and control a process. It was pioneered by **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** and is built on the principle that variation exists in every process, and that this variation can be measured, modelled, and controlled using the **Normal distribution**.

SQC is divided into two branches:

1. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — uses control charts to monitor a live process.
2. **Acceptance Sampling** — uses sampling plans to judge a finished lot.

**Construction of the $\bar{X}$-chart (steps with shipbuilding example — monitoring butt-weld reinforcement height in mm):**

1. **Decide subgroup size $n$** — typically $n = 4$ or $5$. Use $n = 5$ for our example.
2. **Decide number of subgroups $k$** — at least 20–25 for a stable baseline. Use $k = 5$ for illustration.
3. **Collect data** — record $\bar{X}_i$ and $R_i$ for each subgroup.
4. **Compute grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and average range $\bar{R}$.**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\bar{X}_i, \qquad \bar{R} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}R_i$$

5. **Compute control limits using $A_2$** (tabulated constant for chosen $n$):

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad \text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

6. **Plot the chart** — Draw a centre line and the UCL/LCL. Plot $\bar{X}_i$ for each subgroup in time order.
7. **Interpret** — Any point outside the limits, or any run/non-random pattern, indicates the process is **out of statistical control** and requires corrective action.

**Worked Numerical Example (carried from Section 3.4):** Using the plate-thickness data, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 0.15$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.10$ mm, $A_2 = 0.577$, the limits become UCL = 0.208 mm, LCL = 0.092 mm, centre line at 0.15 mm. All five subgroup means fall inside, so the process is in control.

*(Mark split: SQC definition 2, branches 1, chart steps 3, numerical example 1.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Define $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Explain their significance in shipbuilding quality control with a worked example.

**Model Answer:**

**$C_p$ (Process Capability)** is the ratio of the *specification spread* to the *process spread* (6$\sigma$):

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

It assumes the process is perfectly centred.

**$C_{pk}$ (Process Capability Index)** corrects for off-centring:

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Significance in shipbuilding:**

- A hull plate's *thickness tolerance* must be checked before cutting — a low $C_p$ predicts high rejection rate.
- A pipe's *outer diameter* for flange fit-up — $C_{pk} < 1.0$ means rework during assembly, costing days of yard time.
- Class surveyors increasingly demand $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ for critical welding parameters.

**Worked Example (from Section 3.4):**

For plate thickness: USL = +0.40 mm, LSL = −0.40 mm, $\hat{\sigma} = 0.0430$ mm.

$$C_p = \frac{0.80}{6 \times 0.0430} = 3.10$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.94,\ 4.26) = 1.94$$

Since both $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ exceed **1.33**, the process is capable — the yard can confidently accept these plates.

*(Mark split: definitions 2, significance 2, numerical example 3.)*

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a block diagram, explain the role of Quality Control in a typical shipbuilding workflow. Identify at least six inspection hold-points.

**Model Answer:**

**Quality Control in the Shipbuilding Workflow:**

In a shipyard, Quality Control is not a single event — it is a **continuous chain of inspection hold-points** that follow the physical progress of the ship from steel receipt to sea trial.

**Block Diagram (also given in SECTION 4.4):**

```
Steel Receipt → Cutting & Forming → Fit-up → Welding → NDT
   → Block Assembly → Berth Erection → Outfitting → Launch → Sea Trial → Delivery
```

**Six Critical Inspection Hold-Points:**

1. **Steel Receipt** — Mill certificate verification, dimensional check, chemical composition, surface condition.
2. **Cutting & Forming** — Plate nesting accuracy, edge preparation angle, bevel cleanliness.
3. **Fit-up** — Root gap, root face, alignment, tack-weld quality (visual + dimensional).
4. **Welding** — Interpass visual, parameters check, weld-leg length, profile.
5. **NDT Stage** — Radiographic (RT) / Ultrasonic (UT) / Magnetic Particle (MPI) as per WPS and Class requirement.
6. **Block Assembly** — Block squareness, dimension, fairing of weld seams.
7. **Berth Erection** — Hull line check, block level, longitudinal and transverse alignment.
8. **Outfitting** — Pipe pressure test, cable continuity, equipment maker trials.
9. **Launch & Dock Trial** — Leak test, machinery run, steering gear test, inclinometer.
10. **Sea Trial** — Speed, manoeuvrability, vibration, noise, fuel consumption.

**Role of QC at each point:**
- Detects deviations early → reduces rework cost.
- Provides documented evidence → satisfies Class and Owner.
- Feeds data back to QA → drives systemic improvement.

*(Mark split: diagram 2, six hold-points 3, role explanation 2.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain Acceptance Sampling with reference to AQL and LTPD. Derive the Operating Characteristic (OC) curve equation and discuss Producer's and Consumer's Risks.

**Model Answer:**

**Acceptance Sampling** is a QC technique where a *sample* (not the whole lot) is inspected to decide whether to accept or reject an entire lot. It is widely used for incoming bought-out items in shipyards (engines, pumps, electrical fittings, anchor chains).

**Key terms:**

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** — The maximum fraction defective that is considered *tolerable as a process average*. Producer targets at or below this.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** — The fraction defective that the consumer considers the *worst tolerable*. Also called the *Rejectable Quality Level (RQL)*.
- **Producer's Risk $\alpha$** — Probability of *rejecting* a good lot (with $p =$ AQL).
- **Consumer's Risk $\beta$** — Probability of *accepting* a bad lot (with $p =$ LTPD).

**Derivation of the OC curve equation:**

For a single-sampling plan with sample size $n$, acceptance number $c$, and true lot fraction defective $p$:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{k=0}^{c}\binom{n}{k}p^{\,k}(1-p)^{\,n-k}$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. Each of the $n$ sampled items is "defective" with probability $p$.
2. Number of defectives in the sample follows $\text{Binomial}(n, p)$ for large lots.
3. The lot is accepted if defectives $\le c$ — sum the binomial probabilities from $k=0$ to $k=c$.
4. The plot of $P_a$ vs $p$ is the **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve**.

**Producer's and Consumer's Risks (graphical interpretation):**

- The OC curve descends from $P_a = 1$ (at $p = 0$) to $P_a = 0$ (at $p = 1$).
- At $p = \text{AQL}$: $P_a = 1 - \alpha$, so $\alpha = 1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$.
- At $p = \text{LTPD}$: $P_a = \beta$, so $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$.
- The ideal plan has a steep OC curve that drops sharply between AQL and LTPD.

**Numerical illustration (for an exam):**

Take $n = 50$, $c = 1$, AQL = 1 %, LTPD = 6 %.

$$P_a(\text{AQL}=0.01) = (1-0.01)^{50} + 50(0.01)(0.99)^{49} \approx 0.605 + 0.305 = 0.910$$

$\alpha = 1 - 0.910 = 0.090$ (9 % producer risk).

$$P_a(\text{LTPD}=0.06) = (0.94)^{50} + 50(0.06)(0.94)^{49} \approx 0.045 + 0.144 = 0.189$$

$\beta = 0.189$ (18.9 % consumer risk).

If these risks are unacceptable, $n$ must be increased or $c$ reduced.

*(Mark split: definitions 2, derivation 2, producer/consumer risk 2, numerical example 1.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Scheme QC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing QC with QA in the introduction** — Examiners deduct 1 mark outright if the answer starts *"Quality control and quality assurance are the same..."*. Always distinguish at the very first sentence.
> 2. **Missing the units in numerical answers** — $C_p$ is dimensionless, but $\hat{\sigma}$ in *mm* must be quoted with units. Marks are deducted for unit-less numbers in dimensional problems.
> 3. **Forgetting to check "in statistical control" before computing $C_p$** — $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are *meaningless* on an unstable process. Always state the assumption.
> 4. **Using vertical pipe $\vert$ instead of $\vert \vert$ in tables** — Breaks formatting; minor mark deduction on paper submission as well.
> 5. **Wrong constant $A_2$ for the chosen $n$** — $A_2$ depends on subgroup size. Mixing up $n = 4$ and $n = 5$ constants is the single most common error.
> 6. **Not drawing the OC curve** — In 7-mark sampling questions, the OC curve sketch is worth at least 2 marks. Without it, you cannot earn full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **QC = product-focused, reactive, inspection-based discipline.** QA = process-focused, preventive, system-based. QM = umbrella term.
- **Three QC pillars:** Specification, Inspection, Disposition.
- **Four inspection types:** Source, Receiving, In-process, Final.
- **QC operating cycle** follows the PDCA loop: Plan → Measure → Analyse → Decide → Feedback.
- **Shewhart Control Chart limits** use 3-sigma bands; for $\bar{X}$-chart: $\text{UCL/LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2 \bar{R}$.
- **R-chart limits:** $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ (with $D_3 = 0$ for $n \le 6$).
- **Estimate of $\sigma$ from subgroup ranges:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$.
- **Process capability:** $C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL})/(6\sigma)$ — capability if perfectly centred.
- **Process capability index:** $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ — accounts for centring.
- **Benchmarks:** $C_{pk} < 1.0$ (incapable), $1.0 \le C_{pk} < 1.33$ (marginal), $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ (capable), $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ (six-sigma).
- **Acceptance Sampling OC curve:** $P_a(p) = \sum_{k=0}^{c}\binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$.
- **Producer's Risk $\alpha$** = $1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$; **Consumer's Risk $\beta$** = $P_a(\text{LTPD})$.
- **Shipbuilding QC hold-points** exist at every stage from steel receipt to sea trial — ten canonical hold-points.
- **IACS UR W series, ISO 9001:2015, AWS D1.1, SOLAS** are the reference standards most frequently cited.
- **Liberally use diagrams** in KTU answers — block diagrams earn easy marks and signal conceptual clarity.
- **Always state assumptions** before computing $C_p$ (process in statistical control, normal distribution, independence).
- **Memorise the SPC constant table** for $n = 2$ to $n = 7$ — at minimum $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Control Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC) Methodology** in shipbuilding is defined as the *operational, observation-driven set of procedures, measurement techniques, inspection regimes, and corrective-feedback mechanisms* employed to ensure that the constructed vessel, its components, sub-assemblies, and associated workmanship conform to approved design specifications, classification society rules, statutory regulations, and contractual quality requirements.

In the framework of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** for *PENST862 – Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding*, Quality Control is positioned as the **tactical execution arm** of the broader Quality Assurance (QA) system. While QA is *planning-oriented and systemic*, QC is *product-oriented and verification-driven*.

The formal hierarchy recognized in shipbuilding yards is:

$$
\text{Quality Management System (QMS)} \;\supset\; \text{Quality Assurance (QA)} \;\supset\; \text{Quality Control (QC)} \;\supset\; \text{Quality Inspection (QI)}
$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Chef and the Recipe Inspector"**
> Imagine a high-end restaurant preparing a complex dish (a multi-deck cruise ship). The **restaurant manager** writes the master recipe book and trains the chefs — this is **Quality Assurance (planning)**. Now, the **food inspector** who tastes every plate before it leaves the kitchen, checks the temperature of every oven, and rejects undercooked ingredients at receiving — this is **Quality Control (verification)**. QC is the *hands-on, moment-to-moment discipline* that catches deviations *before they become shipyard disasters*.

In shipbuilding terms: a Quality Control inspector at a panel line will measure plate thickness with ultrasonic gauges, verify weld fit-up dimensions, check surface preparation standards, and sign off *Workmanship Approval Sheets* — all of which are *real-time QC activities* governed by the methodology we are about to study.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Mandatory Bold)

The principal benchmarks that any QC methodology must respect in shipbuilding are:

- **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., **IACS** – International Association of Classification Societies members: **DNV**, **Lloyd's Register**, **ABS**, **BV**, **KR**, **CCS**).
- **ISO 9001:2015** – Generic QMS standard.
- **ISO 17025** – Calibration and testing laboratory competence.
- **EN 1090** – Execution of steel structures (welding compliance).
- **AWS D1.1 / D1.6** – Structural welding codes (sea-going and marine).
- **SOLAS & MARPOL** – IMO regulatory constraints.
- **Acceptable Quality Limit (AQL)** – Sampling-based acceptance metric.
- **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** – Statistical process control metric.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PENST862 expects students to demonstrate competency in identifying *how* QC methodology interfaces with classification rules, statutory codes, and yard-level inspection procedures. Memorize the IACS member bodies and ISO 9001:2015 clause structure — these are recurrent short-answer topics in the University ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Phase QC Methodology in Shipbuilding

A robust Quality Control methodology in a modern shipyard follows a **five-phase closed-loop cycle**. Each phase has explicit inputs, outputs, and gate-keeping responsibilities.

**Phase 1 — Quality Planning & Document Control**
- Translates design specifications into *Inspection and Test Plans (ITPs)*.
- Establishes *Quality Control Procedures (QCPs)*, *Weld Procedure Specifications (WPS)*, and *Procedure Qualification Records (PQR)*.
- Defines Hold Points (H), Witness Points (W), and Surveillance Points (S) on the ITP grid.

**Phase 2 — Incoming Material & Subcontractor Control**
- Verifies mill certificates (3.1 / 3.2 certificates per **EN 10204**).
- Conducts dimensional and chemical checks on plates, sections, pipes, castings, and forgings.
- Audits subcontractor QMS alignment with yard standards.

**Phase 3 — In-Process / During-Construction Control**
- Shop-floor inspections at every major work package boundary.
- **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)** deployment: visual, MPI (Magnetic Particle Inspection), DPI (Dye Penetrant Inspection), UT (Ultrasonic), RT (Radiographic), and eddy current.
- Weld inspection, dimensional surveys, alignment checks, and coating inspection.

**Phase 4 — Final Inspection & Sea Trials**
- Static and dynamic alignment verification of shafting, propellers, and rudders.
- Inclining experiment, dock trials, and **sea trials** under classification surveyor attendance.
- Functional testing of systems: piping pressure tests, electrical continuity, automation loops.

**Phase 5 — Non-Conformance, Corrective & Preventive Action (NC / CAPA)**
- Issue of *Non-Conformance Reports (NCRs)*.
- Root-cause analysis via **5-Why**, **Ishikawa (Fishbone)** diagrams, or **8D** methodology.
- Implementation of Corrective Action (CA) and Preventive Action (PA).
- Feedback loop into Phase 1 (continuous improvement).

### 2.2 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) Sub-System

Shipbuilding QC employs statistical tools primarily for:
- Weld defect rate monitoring.
- Plate cutting tolerance control.
- Paint DFT (Dry Film Thickness) tracking.
- Supplier quality trending.

**The two principal control charts** are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL} &= \bar{X} + 3\sigma \\
\text{CL}  &= \bar{X} \\
\text{LCL} &= \bar{X} - 3\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\bar{X}$ = process mean,
- $\sigma$ = process standard deviation,
- UCL = Upper Control Limit, LCL = Lower Control Limit, CL = Centre Line.
