For attribute data (defective / not-defective), the **p-chart** is used:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_p &= \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} \\
\text{LCL}_p &= \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\bar{p}$ is the average fraction defective and $n$ is the sample size.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **high-yield reference** for KTU University ESE questions on this topic. Students writing Part A (3-mark) answers should reproduce the **bold** entries verbatim.

| Concept / Quantity | Governing Relationship | Typical Numerical Range in Shipbuilding | SI / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\geq 1.33$ (capable), $\geq 1.67$ (highly capable) | Dimensionless |
| Control Limit (X-bar) | $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$ | $3\sigma$ corresponds to **99.73%** coverage | Same as variable |
| p-Chart Control Limit | $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | Used for weld defect rate trending | Fraction |
| Six-Sigma Defect Rate | $3.4$ DPMO = $0.00034\%$ defects | Target in advanced shipyards | ppm / DPMO |
| Sampling Plan (AQL) | $\text{AQL} \leq 1.0\%$ for critical welds | Per **ISO 2859-1** | Percentage |
| First-Time-Yield (FTY) | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units inspected}}$ | Yard benchmark: $85\% - 95\%$ | Percentage |
| Rolled-Throughput-Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_i$ | Aggregated over all stages | Percentage |
| Paint DFT Tolerance | $\text{Actual DFT} = \text{DFT}_{\text{nominal}} \pm 10\%$ | Per **PSPC** (IMO) for ballast tanks | Microns ($\mu m$) |
| Weld Porosity Limit | $\text{Porosity Area} \leq 2\%$ of weld cross-section | Per **AWS D1.1** Table 8.1 | Percentage |
| En 10204 Certificate Type | 3.1 (mill issued) / 3.2 (independent witnessed) | Mandatory for hull plates | Categorical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering:** The **$C_{pk}$ index** is increasingly used in robotic panel-line yards (e.g., Korean and Japanese mega-yards) to validate that automated welding stations are *capable* of meeting the $\pm 1$ mm plate-positioning tolerance required for one-side welding (OSW) processes. Shipowners increasingly demand **Six-Sigma capability demonstration** for engine-room piping spools before accepting them into outfitting blocks.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Five-Phase Loop

Each phase exists because shipbuilding is a *one-of-a-kind, low-volume, high-complexity* manufacturing system:
- A single Panamax bulker has over **400 km of welds**.
- A single VLCC has over **1,500 km of welds**.
- Roughly **70% of in-service hull failures** are attributable to weld-related defects.

Hence, *Phase 3 (in-process)* and *Phase 5 (NC/CAPA)* are non-negotiable — they are the statistical and engineering firewalls protecting the asset from catastrophic failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: $C_{pk}$ from First Principles

The **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** is derived under the assumption that the process output $X$ follows a normal distribution:

$$
X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\;\sigma^2)
$$

The bilateral specification limits (LSL = Lower, USL = Upper) define the engineering tolerance window. The proportion of in-spec output is given by the probability integral over the tolerance band. We define two one-sided capability indices first.

**Step 1** — Define the one-sided indices.

$$
C_{pU} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pL} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 2** — Geometric interpretation: the numerator is the distance from the process mean to the specification edge, expressed in **three-sigma units**. This is because the normal distribution has $99.73\%$ of its area within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

**Step 3** — The combined capability index is the *minimum* of the two one-sided indices, because the *worse* side dominates the defect rate:

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pU},\;C_{pL})
$$

**Step 4** — Substitute the expressions:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 5** — Decision thresholds (industry-accepted, used in classification audits):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} < 1.00 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Process is NOT capable} \\
1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Marginally capable} \\
1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Capable} \\
C_{pk} \geq 1.67 \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Highly capable (Six-Sigma territory)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example (Numerical Evaluation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A robotic welding station in a panel line is required to deposit a fillet weld leg length of **$L = 8.0 \pm 0.5$ mm**. Over a 30-day campaign, 100 readings yielded $\mu = 7.92$ mm and $\sigma = 0.18$ mm. Compute $C_{pk}$ and interpret.

**Step 1** — Identify specification limits.
- $\text{USL} = 8.5$ mm, $\text{LSL} = 7.5$ mm.

**Step 2** — Calculate $C_{pU}$.

$$
C_{pU} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{8.5 - 7.92}{3 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.58}{0.54} = 1.074
$$

**Step 3** — Calculate $C_{pL}$.

$$
C_{pL} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{7.92 - 7.5}{3 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.42}{0.54} = 0.778
$$

**Step 4** — Take the minimum.

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.074,\;0.778) = 0.778
$$

**Step 5** — Interpretation. Since $C_{pk} = 0.778 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT capable**. The lower specification limit is being violated statistically. Corrective action required: re-calibrate the robot, re-train operator, or replace consumables.

### 3.3 Derivation: p-Chart Lower Control Limit

For a yard monitoring the **weld defect rate** in a block fabrication zone, with $\bar{p} = 0.04$ (4% defectives) and sample size $n = 50$ welds per sub-block:

**Step 1** — Compute the standard error of the proportion.

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.04 \times 0.96}{50}} = \sqrt{0.000768} = 0.02771
$$

**Step 2** — Compute $\text{UCL}_p$.

$$
\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p = 0.04 + 3(0.02771) = 0.04 + 0.08313 = 0.12313
$$

**Step 3** — Compute $\text{LCL}_p$.

$$
\text{LCL}_p = \bar{p} - 3\sigma_p = 0.04 - 0.08313 = -0.04313
$$

**Step 4** — Since LCL is negative, set $\text{LCL}_p = 0$ (negative defect rates are physically impossible).

**Step 5** — If a sample yields a defective fraction above 0.12313, the process is *out of statistical control* and a CAPA cycle must be initiated.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — QC Methodology Tools vs. Application Stage

| QC Tool | Plan | Incoming | In-Process | Final | NC / CAPA |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Inspection & Test Plan (ITP) | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — |
| Control Chart (X-bar, p) | — | — | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| Pareto Analysis | — | — | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| Ishikawa (Fishbone) | — | — | — | — | ✔ |
| FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis) | ✔ | — | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| 5-Why Analysis | — | — | — | — | ✔ |
| 8D Report | — | — | — | — | ✔ |
| NDT (UT, RT, MPI, DPI) | — | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — |
| Dimensional Survey (laser, total station) | — | — | ✔ | ✔ | — |
| Material Certificate Verification (EN 10204) | — | ✔ | — | — | — |
| Sea Trial Performance Test | — | — | — | ✔ | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the QC Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Specifications and Class Rules] --> B[Phase 1: Quality Planning]
    B --> B1[Inspection and Test Plan ITP]
    B --> B2[Weld Procedure Specification WPS]
    B --> B3[Quality Control Procedure QCP]
    B1 --> C[Phase 2: Incoming Material Control]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Mill Certificate Verification EN10204]
    C --> C2[Dimensional and Chemical Check]
    C1 --> D[Phase 3: In-Process Inspection]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Visual and Dimensional Inspection]
    D --> D2[Non Destructive Testing NDT]
    D --> D3[Weld Fit-up and Procedure Compliance]
    D1 --> E[Phase 4: Final Inspection and Trials]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Dock Trial]
    E --> E2[Sea Trial under Surveyor Attendance]
    E1 --> F[Phase 5: Non Conformance and CAPA]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Non Conformance Report NCR]
    F --> F2[Root Cause Analysis 5-Why or 8D]
    F --> F3[Corrective and Preventive Action]
    F3 --> B
    F1 --> G[Classification Society Sign-off and Delivery]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Closed-Loop NC / CAPA Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Defect Detected at Inspection] --> N2[Issue Non Conformance Report]
    N2 --> N3{Rework Possible?}
    N3 -- Yes --> N4[Rework and Re-inspect]
    N3 -- No --> N5[Scrap or Concession Request]
    N4 --> N6[Root Cause Analysis]
    N5 --> N6
    N6 --> N7[Implement Corrective Action]
    N7 --> N8[Implement Preventive Action]
    N8 --> N9[Update ITP WPS and Training]
    N9 --> N10[Close NCR and Audit Effectiveness]
    N10 --> N11[Lessons Learned Database]
    N11 --> N1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: SQC Layer in the QC Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Process Data Acquisition] --> S1[Variable Data]
    S0 --> S2[Attribute Data]
    S1 --> S3[X-bar and R Chart]
    S1 --> S4[Cpk Computation]
    S2 --> S5[p Chart or c Chart]
    S3 --> S6{In Control?}
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Continue Process]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Trigger CAPA Cycle]
    S8 --> S9[Update Control Limits and Specifications]
    S9 --> S0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> *Define Quality Control (QC) in the context of shipbuilding. How does it differ from Quality Assurance (QA)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Quality Control in shipbuilding is the operational set of inspection, testing, and measurement activities performed to verify that the constructed vessel and its components conform to approved design specifications, classification society rules (e.g., IACS members), and contractual quality requirements.
- **(1 mark)** QC is *product-oriented* and focuses on defect detection through inspection, NDT, and dimensional verification.
- **(1 mark)** QA, in contrast, is *system- and process-oriented*, covering the entire quality management system — planning, documentation, training, and audits. The distinction: *QA plans the system; QC verifies the product*.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
> *List any three Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods commonly deployed in ship hull construction and state one typical application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Visual Testing (VT):** Inspection of weld surface for undercut, crater cracks, and surface porosity in every pass.
- **(1 mark)** **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI):** Detection of surface and near-surface cracks in ferromagnetic welds of high-strength hull steel.
- **(1 mark)** **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** Volumetric examination of butt-welded joints in critical zones (e.g., sheer strake, bilge strake) to detect slag, lack of fusion, and laminations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain the **five-phase Quality Control methodology** adopted in a modern shipyard, with a clear description of each phase and its key deliverables. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the role of the **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** as the central document of the QC methodology. Include the meaning of Hold (H), Witness (W), and Surveillance (S) points. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five-Phase QC Methodology (7 marks):**

1. **(1.5 marks) Phase 1 — Quality Planning:** Translates design and class rules into actionable documents: ITP, WPS, PQR, and yard-level QCPs. Defines inspection hold points and acceptance criteria.
2. **(1.5 marks) Phase 2 — Incoming Material Control:** Verification of EN 10204 certificates (3.1/3.2), dimensional checks on plates/sections/pipes, and subcontractor QMS audits. Rejects out-of-spec material *before* it enters the production line.
3. **(1.5 marks) Phase 3 — In-Process Control:** Shop-floor inspection at every major work-package boundary. NDT (VT, MPI, DPI, UT, RT), weld fit-up and procedure compliance, alignment surveys, and surface preparation checks.
4. **(1 mark) Phase 4 — Final Inspection & Trials:** Pressure tests, electrical continuity, alignment of shafting, dock trials, and sea trials under classification surveyor attendance (e.g., ABS, DNV, LR).
5. **(1.5 marks) Phase 5 — NC / CAPA:** Issue of NCR, root cause analysis (5-Why, Ishikawa, 8D), corrective and preventive action, and feedback into Phase 1 — closing the loop for continuous improvement.

**(b) ITP — Central QC Document (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** The ITP is a matrix document listing every inspection activity, the responsible party (yard, class surveyor, owner, or flag-state inspector), the timing, the acceptance criteria, and the verification method.
- **(2 marks)** **Hold Point (H):** Mandatory stop — work cannot proceed beyond this point until the inspector releases the hold. Example: fit-up inspection before welding commences.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Witness Point (W):** Inspector must be given prior notice; if not present within the notice window, work may proceed. Example: final NDT of a major erection joint.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Surveillance Point (S):** Routine monitoring without mandatory hold. Example: ambient temperature check during welding.

> **Q3B. [Alternative Choice — Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** sub-system used in shipbuilding, with derivations of the X-bar control chart limits and the p-chart control limits. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A robotic welding station must achieve a fillet weld leg length of **$8.0 \pm 0.5$ mm**. Statistical sampling of 100 welds gives $\mu = 7.92$ mm and $\sigma = 0.18$ mm. Compute $C_{pk}$ and comment on the capability of the process. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) SQC Sub-System (7 marks):**

- **(1.5 marks)** SQC uses statistical sampling and control charts to distinguish *common-cause* (inherent) variation from *special-cause* (assignable) variation. It is the analytical backbone of Phase 3 and Phase 5 of the QC methodology.
- **(2 marks)** **X-bar Chart Limits:** Derived from the normal distribution such that $99.73\%$ of the process output lies within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$:
$$
\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma, \qquad \text{CL} = \bar{X}, \qquad \text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma
$$
- **(2 marks)** **p-Chart Limits:** Used for attribute (defective/non-defective) data with sample size $n$:
$$
\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}, \qquad \text{LCL}_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
$$
- **(1.5 marks)** Out-of-control points trigger immediate CAPA. In shipbuilding, p-charts are typically applied to *weld defect rate*, *dimensional rejection rate*, and *coating DFT compliance rate*.

**(b) $C_{pk}$ Computation (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** $\text{USL} = 8.5$ mm, $\text{LSL} = 7.5$ mm.
- **(2 marks)** $C_{pU} = \dfrac{8.5 - 7.92}{3 \times 0.18} = \dfrac{0.58}{0.54} = 1.074$.
- **(2 marks)** $C_{pL} = \dfrac{7.92 - 7.5}{3 \times 0.18} = \dfrac{0.42}{0.54} = 0.778$.
- **(1 mark)** $C_{pk} = \min(1.074, 0.778) = 0.778$.
- **(1 mark)** **Comment:** Since $C_{pk} = 0.778 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT capable**. The lower specification limit is the limiting factor. Corrective action is mandatory — re-calibrate the robot, replace welding consumables, and re-train the operator.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Question:**
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ assumes the process is centred on the midpoint of the specification window. $C_{pk}$ accounts for off-centring. Examiners will deduct **2 marks** if you compute $C_p$ instead of $C_{pk}$ in a 14-mark numerical question.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\min()$ function:** Always take the minimum of $C_{pU}$ and $C_{pL}$. Many students average them — this is **conceptually wrong** and will cost a full mark on the interpretation.
> 3. **Misinterpreting control limits:** The $3\sigma$ rule means $99.73\%$ in-control, **not** $100\%$. Examiners expect this percentage in your answer.
> 4. **Skipping units in ITP description:** Always specify the *responsibility code* (Yard / Class / Owner / Flag) when listing ITP hold/witness/surveillance points. Omission is a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Quality Control Methodology (Module 1)**

- **Definition Anchor:** Quality Control = *operational, observation-driven verification* of product conformance; it is the *tactical arm* of Quality Assurance.
- **Five-Phase Loop:** Plan → Incoming → In-Process → Final → NC/CAPA → *(back to Plan)*.
- **ITP Vocabulary:** **H** = Hold (mandatory stop), **W** = Witness (notice-based), **S** = Surveillance (routine). Memorize the meaning of each — a 3-mark question every KTU cycle tests this.
- **Control Chart Anchors:** X-bar uses $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$ (variable data); p-chart uses $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ (attribute data).
- **$3\sigma$ Coverage:** $99.73\%$ of in-control process output lies within the control limits.
- **$C_{pk}$ Decision Thresholds:** $< 1.00$ (not capable), $\geq 1.33$ (capable), $\geq 1.67$ (highly capable / Six-Sigma).
- **AQL Benchmark:** $\leq 1.0\%$ for critical welds (per ISO 2859-1).
- **EN 10204 Certificate Types:** 3.1 (mill-issued) vs 3.2 (independent witnessed) — both mandatory for hull steel.
- **NDT Methods Mapped to Application:** VT (every pass), MPI (ferritic steel welds), DPI (austenitic stainless, non-magnetic), UT (volumetric butt welds), RT (critical thick-plate joints).
- **Regulatory Anchors:** **IACS** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, KR, CCS); **SOLAS**, **MARPOL**, **AWS D1.1**, **EN 1090**, **ISO 9001:2015**.
- **CAPA Tools:** 5-Why, Ishikawa (Fishbone), 8D, Pareto, FMEA.
- **Numerical Habit:** In every KTU 14-mark numerical, write the formula, substitute numerical values with units, and conclude with a one-sentence engineering interpretation — this is the KTU valuation key.
- **One-Sentence Differentiation:** *QA = "Are we building it right?"* (system); *QC = "Did we build it right?"* (product).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Philosophy and Management Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Philosophy and Management Strategies # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Quality Philosophy — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Philosophy** is the foundational set of beliefs, values, attitudes, and managerial doctrines that an organization adopts toward achieving excellence in its products, services, and processes. In the shipbuilding context (as defined under KTU 2024 syllabus for PENST862), it represents the *intellectual and cultural bedrock* upon which all Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) activities are constructed. In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terms, **Quality Philosophy** refers to the integrated body of thought contributed by quality gurus (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum, Taguchi, etc.) that guides an enterprise — particularly a shipyard — toward **customer satisfaction, defect prevention, continuous improvement, and statutorily compliant seaworthy construction** in accordance with IACS (International Association of Classification Societies), SOLAS, and ISO 9001:2015 frameworks. ### 1.1.1 Key Definitions (Board-Ready) | Term | Definition | KTU 2024 Significance | |---|---|---| | **Quality** | The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfills requirements (ISO 9000:2015) | Foundational definition used in Part A questions | | **Grade** | A category assigned to products having the same functional use but different technical characteristics | Often confused with quality in Part B answers | | **Quality Policy** | A formal statement of an organization's overall intentions and direction with regard to quality | Mandatory under ISO 9001 Clause 5.2 | | **Quality Management** | Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to quality | Umbrella term covering QA + QC + QM | ## 1.2 Quality Management Strategies — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Quality Management Strategy** is the *long-term, organization-wide plan* that translates quality philosophy into actionable systems, methodologies, and measurable outcomes. It encompasses TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, Kaizen, Quality Circles, and ISO-based compliance frameworks. In shipbuilding, these strategies directly influence: - Hull structural integrity (DNV, ABS, LR class rules) - Welding quality (AWS D1.1, ISO 3834) - Outfitting precision - On-time delivery (OTD) and budget adherence ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shipyard as a Hospital" > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a shipyard as a **hospital operating theater**. > - The **ship's hull** is the *patient*. > - The **welder/fitter** is the *surgeon*. > - The **Quality Manual** is the *medical protocol book*. > - The **QA Inspector** is the *anesthesiologist monitoring vitals in real-time*. > - The **QC Inspector** is the *pathologist performing post-op tests*. > > If the *surgeon* does not follow sterile protocol (philosophy missing), no amount of post-op testing (strategy) can save the patient. **Quality must be built-in, not inspected-in.** This is the central tenet of all modern quality philosophies in shipbuilding. ## 1.4 Core Metrics and Standard Constants The following bold-highlighted metrics are universally accepted in shipbuilding QA practice: - **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — typically 5%–15% of total project revenue in shipyards - **Defect Density (DD)** — defects per standard unit (e.g., per 100 m of weld) - **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — proportion of products meeting specs without rework - **Six Sigma Level** — **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** - **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** — usually benchmarked above **90%** for shipowners - **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** — Target ≥ **95%** in modern shipyards > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Board Examiners expect students to cite the standard benchmark values (e.g., Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO) with units. Always pair a metric with its unit. ## 1.5 Why Quality Philosophy Matters in Shipbuilding A single undetected weld defect can lead to: - Catastrophic hull failure (referenced in marine casualty databases) - Total project loss (one defective block can scrap weeks of work) - Class society non-conformance, halting vessel delivery - Reputational damage affecting future contracts Hence, shipbuilding is a **quality-critical, low-margin, schedule-driven** industry where philosophy-driven prevention is more economical than detection-based correction. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Evolution of Quality Philosophy — A Structured Breakdown The chronological evolution of quality thought is a recurring 14-mark essay topic in KTU Module 1. Memorize the four eras: ### Era 1 — Inspection Era (Pre-1920s) - Focus: **Detection** through end-product inspection - Pioneered by **Frederick Taylor** (scientific management) - Shipyard analogy: Inspecting the finished hull for cracks after launch (too late!) ### Era 2 — Quality Control Era (1920s–1950s) - Introduction of **Shewhart's Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** charts (1924) - Use of control charts for **welder qualification monitoring** - Walter Shewhart's *PDCA cycle* seeds the modern management philosophy ### Era 3 — Quality Assurance Era (1950s–1970s) - **Armand Feigenbaum** coins **Total Quality Control (TQC)** in 1956 - Quality becomes a *system-wide* responsibility, not just the QC department - **Joseph Juran** publishes the *Juran Trilogy* (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) ### Era 4 — Strategic Quality Management Era (1980s–Present) - **W. Edwards Deming** — 14 Points for Management, *System of Profound Knowledge* - **Philip Crosby** — *Zero Defects*, *Quality is Free* - **Kaoru Ishikawa** — *Quality Circles*, *Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagrams* - **Genichi Taguchi** — *Loss Function*, *Robust Design* - **Total Quality Management (TQM)** emerges as the umbrella framework ## 2.2 The Quality Gurus — KTU High-Yield Summary > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently ask **"Compare and contrast the contributions of Deming, Juran, and Crosby"** for 7 marks. Use this comparison matrix: | Quality Guru | Core Philosophy | Key Contribution | Famous Quote (Memorize) | |---|---|---|---| | **W. Edwards Deming** | Systemic management, statistical process control | 14 Points, PDCA Cycle, System of Profound Knowledge | *"In God we trust; all others must bring data."* | | **Joseph M. Juran** | Customer-focused quality, planning-driven | Juran Trilogy (QP, QC, QI); Pareto Principle (80/20) | *"Quality is fitness for use."* | | **Philip B. Crosby** | Conformance to requirements, prevention | Zero Defects; Quality is Free; 4 Absolutes of Quality | *"The cost of quality is the price of non-conformance."* | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total organizational involvement | Total Quality Control (TQC) — precursor to TQM | *"Quality is everyone's job."* | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Bottom-up, human-centered quality | Quality Circles; Fishbone Diagram; Company-Wide QC | *"Next to the worker, the foreman is most important to QC."* | | **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss minimization, off-target cost | Quality Loss Function; Robust Design | *"The customer is not a target; the customer is a partner."* | | **Walter A. Shewhart** | Statistical foundation | Control Charts, PDCA origin | *"Data is not information; information is not knowledge."* | ## 2.3 The Juran Trilogy — Detailed Theoretical Framework The **Juran Trilogy** is the most-tested management strategy in KTU 2024 papers. It consists of three managerial processes: ### Step 1 — Quality Planning (QP) - Identify customers and their needs - Develop product features that respond to those needs - Establish quality goals and specifications - *Shipbuilding example:* Setting weld acceptance criteria per AWS D1.1 before block fabrication begins ### Step 2 — Quality Control (QC) - Evaluate actual performance against goals - Compare actual vs. planned performance - Act on the difference (corrective action) - *Shipbuilding example:* Monitoring weld penetration via NDT (ultrasonic testing) and comparing to spec ### Step 3 — Quality Improvement (QI) - Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement - Identify specific improvement projects - Establish project teams, provide resources, training, motivation - *Shipbuilding example:* Reducing rework rates on curved plate sections through jig redesign ## 2.4 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality This is a frequent **3-mark short-answer** question: 1. **Quality = Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness") 2. **Prevention, not inspection** is the system of quality 3. **Zero Defects** performance standard 4. **Measure quality by the price of non-conformance** (PONC) ## 2.5 Deming's 14 Points (Condensed for KTU Board Exam) Memorize at least these 5 critical points (full 14 expected for 14-mark essays): - **Point 1:** Create constancy of purpose toward improvement - **Point 2:** Adopt the new philosophy (we can no longer live with commonly accepted levels of mistakes) - **Point 3:** Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality (build quality in) - **Point 5:** Improve constantly every process (Kaizen) - **Point 13:** Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement ## 2.6 Taguchi Loss Function — Mathematical Foundation > [!NOTE] > The Taguchi Loss Function is the most analytically rigorous concept in Module 1 and frequently appears as a 14-mark problem in KTU ESE. The **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** quantifies the economic loss caused by deviation from the target value, even when the product is *within specification*. This is the opposite of the traditional "go/no-go" inspection view. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > This table consolidates **all equations** required for Module 1 problem-solving. Exam hall: reproduce this verbatim where applicable. | \# | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Taguchi Quality Loss Function** | $L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2}$ | $L$ = Loss in currency, $k$ = quality loss coefficient, $x$ = actual value, $T$ = target value | | 2 | **Quality Loss Coefficient (k)** | $k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}}$ | $A_{0}$ = average loss at functional tolerance $\Delta_{0}$ | | 3 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = P_{c} + A_{c} + IF_{c} + EF_{c}$ | Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure costs | | 4 | **Cost of Quality as % of Revenue** | $\%\text{CoQ} = \dfrac{\text{CoQ}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | Industry benchmark: **5%–15%** | | 5 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \cdot O}$ | $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit | | 6 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min \!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Shipbuilding target: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for critical welds | | 7 | **Six Sigma Level** | $\sigma_{L} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} + 1.5\sigma_{shift}$ | Target: **3.4 DPMO** at $6\sigma$ | | 8 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passed first time}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100$ | Target: $\geq 95\%$ for ship blocks | | 9 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | For multi-stage shipbuilding processes | | 10 | **Pareto (80/20) Cumulative %** | $\%_{i} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_{j}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{j}} \times 100$ | Used to prioritize quality problems | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility In a Korean or Japanese shipyard: - **Deming's PDCA** is operationalized through the *Production Planning Sheet* updated daily - **Juran Trilogy** maps onto the three ISO 9001:2015 clauses: Context (QP), Operational Planning (QC), Performance Evaluation (QI) - **Taguchi Loss Function** is used to assess plating thickness deviation — out-of-spec thinner plates still incur corrosion-related economic loss proportional to $(x - T)^{2}$ This philosophy-driven framework is what enables global shipbuilders (Hyundai HI, Samsung HI, Mitsubishi HI) to deliver LNG carriers and VLCCs on schedule while meeting class society stringent tolerances. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Taguchi Quality Loss Function (Board-Level Problem) **Problem (KTU-style):** A shipyard manufactures propeller shaft journals. Target diameter $T = 200.00$ mm. Functional tolerance $\Delta_{0} = 0.50$ mm. When deviation equals $\Delta_{0}$, average economic loss to shipowner due to bearing wear is $A_{0} = \text{Rs. } 8{,}000$ per shaft. Calculate the loss for a shaft with actual diameter $x = 200.30$ mm. Also compute the loss coefficient $k$. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Establish the loss coefficient $k$** $$k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}}$$ Substituting $A_{0} = 8000$ and $\Delta_{0} = 0.50$: $$k = \frac{8000}{(0.50)^{2}} = \frac{8000}{0.25} = 32{,}000 \;\; \text{Rs. per mm}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the deviation from target** $$x - T = 200.30 - 200.00 = 0.30 \;\; \text{mm}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Taguchi Loss Function** $$L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2}$$ $$L(x) = 32{,}000 \times (0.30)^{2}$$ $$L(x) = 32{,}000 \times 0.09$$ $$\boxed{L(x) = \text{Rs. } 2{,}880 \;\; \text{per shaft}}$$ **Interpretation:** Although the shaft is *within* the conventional tolerance band ($\pm 0.50$ mm), the shipowner already suffers a **Rs. 2,880** economic loss per shaft due to deviation from target. This is the radical insight of Taguchi's philosophy. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost of Quality (PAF Model) with Worked Example **Problem:** A shipyard's annual QA budget reveals: - Prevention costs $P_{c}$ = Rs. 12,00,000 - Appraisal costs $A_{c}$ = Rs. 18,00,000 - Internal failure costs $IF_{c}$ = Rs. 25,00,000 - External failure costs $EF_{c}$ = Rs. 15,00,000 Calculate: (a) Total CoQ, (b) CoQ as % of annual revenue of Rs. 7 Crore, (c) Prevention-to-Failure cost ratio. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step (a) — Total CoQ** $$\text{CoQ} = P_{c} + A_{c} + IF_{c} + EF_{c}$$ $$\text{CoQ} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\text{CoQ} = \text{Rs. } 70{,}00{,}000}$$ **Step (b) — CoQ as percentage of revenue** $$\%\text{CoQ} = \frac{70{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\%\text{CoQ} = 10\%}$$ This falls within the healthy shipbuilding industry range of **5%–15%**. **Step (c) — Prevention-to-Failure ratio (PFR)** Total failure cost: $$F_{c} = IF_{c} + EF_{c} = 25{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}00{,}000$$ Prevention cost: $P_{c} = 12{,}00{,}000$ $$\text{PFR} = \frac{P_{c}}{F_{c}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{\text{PFR} = 0.30 \;\; (\text{or } 30\%)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Juran's optimal guideline:** PFR should approach **1.0 (100%)** as the yard matures. A PFR of 0.30 indicates under-investment in prevention. KTU examiners award marks for *interpreting* PFR, not just calculating it. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DPMO and Six Sigma Level Calculation **Problem:** A shipyard produces 5,000 steel panels per quarter. Each panel has 10 critical welding opportunities. During QA inspection, 250 defects are recorded. Compute DPMO and identify the corresponding sigma level. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Total opportunities** $$N \cdot O = 5000 \times 10 = 50{,}000$$ **Step 2 — DPMO** $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \cdot O} = \frac{250 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{50{,}000}$$ $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{250 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{4}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 5000}$$ **Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Sigma Level (using standard Six Sigma conversion table)** | DPMO | Sigma Level | |---|---| | 3.4 | 6.0 | | 233 | 5.0 | | 6,210 | 4.0 | | **66,807** | 3.0 | For **DPMO = 5000**, by interpolation: $$\sigma \approx 4.0 + \frac{6210 - 5000}{6210 - 233} \times (5 - 4)$$ $$\sigma \approx 4.0 + \frac{1210}{5977} \times 1.0 \approx 4.0 + 0.20$$ $$\boxed{\sigma \approx 4.20 \;\; (\text{Four Sigma Level})}$$ The shipyard is operating at ~4.2σ — competent, but world-class shipbuilders target ≥ 5σ for critical safety welds. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) for a Welding Process **Problem:** A robotic welding station specifies bead width as $\text{USL} = 12.0$ mm and $\text{LSL} = 10.0$ mm. Sample mean $\mu = 10.8$ mm, sample standard deviation $\sigma = 0.30$ mm. Compute $C_{pk}$ and assess capability. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute upper-side capability** $$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.0 - 10.8}{3 \times 0.30} = \frac{1.2}{0.9}$$ $$C_{pu} = 1.333$$ **Step 2 — Compute lower-side capability** $$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.8 - 10.0}{3 \times 0.30} = \frac{0.8}{0.9}$$ $$C_{pl} = 0.889$$ **Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ as the minimum** $$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\; C_{pl}) = \min(1.333,\; 0.889)$$ $$\boxed{C_{pk} = 0.889}$$ **Interpretation Table:** | $C_{pk}$ Value | Process Status | KTU Board Statement | |---|---|---| | $\geq 1.67$ | World-class (Six Sigma) | Excellent; exceeds IACS expectations | | $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | Capable | **Minimum acceptable for shipbuilding** | | $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | Marginally capable | Tight control required | | $< 1.00$ | Not capable | Process re-engineering mandated | > [!WARNING] > Our $C_{pk} = 0.889$ is **below 1.00**, indicating the welding station is *not capable*. The lower specification limit side (10.0 mm) is the binding constraint. Corrective action: recalibrate the robot torch, retrain welders, or shift the process mean toward the centerline. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Philosophy to Shipbuilding Application | Philosophy Element | Strategic Deployment | Measurable Outcome | |---|---|---| | Deming's PDCA | Block fabrication cycle review | Cycle time reduction | | Juran Trilogy | ISO 9001:2015 QMS structure | Audit closure rate | | Crosby Zero Defects | Welder qualification threshold | Defect Density per 100 m weld | | Ishikawa Fishbone | Root cause analysis of weld porosity | NCR closure turnaround | | Taguchi Loss Function | Plate thickness tolerance economics | PONC in Rs. | | Feigenbaum TQC | Cross-functional QA teams | First Pass Yield (FPY) | | Quality Circles | Shop-floor worker involvement | Suggestions per worker/year | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution of Quality Philosophy — Timeline Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Inspection Era
Pre 1920s
Detection Focused] --> A2[Quality Control Era
1920s to 1950s
Statistical Methods] A2 --> A3[Quality Assurance Era
1950s to 1970s
System Wide QC] A3 --> A4[Strategic Quality Era
1980s to Present
TQM and Beyond] B1[Frederick Taylor] -.-> A1 B2[Walter Shewhart
Control Charts] -.-> A2 B3[Armand Feigenbaum
Total Quality Control] -.-> A3 B4[Deming Juran Crosby
Ishikawa Taguchi] -.-> A4 C1[Craft Based Inspection] -.-> A1 C2[SPC and Sampling] -.-> A2 C3[QMS Standards ISO] -.-> A3 C4[TQM Six Sigma Lean] -.-> A4 ``` ## 4.2 Juran Trilogy Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD JT1[Customer Identification] --> JT2[Quality Planning] JT2 --> JT3[Product and Process Design] JT3 --> JT4[Quality Goals Set] JT4 --> JT5[Quality Control] JT5 --> JT6[Measure Actual Performance] JT6 --> JT7[Compare with Goals] JT7 --> JT8{Corrective
Action Needed} JT8 -- Yes --> JT9[Act on Difference] JT8 -- No --> JT10[Document and Monitor] JT9 --> JT11[Quality Improvement] JT10 --> JT11 JT11 --> JT12[Infrastructure for CI] JT12 --> JT13[Identify Improvement Projects] JT13 --> JT14[Project Teams Mobilized] JT14 --> JT2 ``` ## 4.3 Taguchi Loss Function Visualization — Loss vs Deviation ```mermaid flowchart LR D1[Target T
Zero Loss] --> D2[Deviation x minus T] D2 --> D3[Quadratic Loss L equals k times x minus T squared] D3 --> D4[Loss Increases with Deviation] D4 --> D5[Specification Limits USL LSL
Loss Still Non Zero] D5 --> D6[Continuous Improvement
Reduces Variance] D6 --> D1 ``` ## 4.4 Cost of Quality (PAF Model) — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Prevention Costs
Training, Planning, Design Review] A[Appraisal Costs
Inspection, Testing, NDT] IF[Internal Failure
Rework, Scrap, Reinspection] EF[External Failure
Warranty, Recall, Class Penalties] P --> SUM[Total Cost of Quality CoQ] A --> SUM IF --> SUM EF --> SUM P -.-> R1[Investment] A -.-> R2[Detection] IF -.-> R3[Correction] EF -.-> R4[Damage Control] SUM --> OUT[Optimal CoQ Region
Where Total Cost is Minimized] style P fill:#cce5ff style A fill:#cce5ff style IF fill:#ffcccc style EF fill:#ffcccc style SUM fill:#fff2cc style OUT fill:#d5e8d4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the PAF Diagram (KTU Board Favourite):** As prevention spending increases (left → right), failure costs decrease (right → left). The *optimal CoQ* is the minimum of the total cost curve. This is the economic justification for Deming's *Point 3: Cease dependence on inspection*. ## 4.5 Quality Management Strategy Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP[Quality Philosophy
Beliefs and Values] TOP --> MID[Quality Management Strategies
TQM Six Sigma Lean Kaizen] MID --> IMPL[Implementation Tools
PDCA Fishbone Pareto FMEA SPC] IMPL --> OUTCOME[Shipbuilding Outcomes
Class Approval OTD above 95 percent
FPY above 95 percent
CoQ 5 to 15 percent] style TOP fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style MID fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style IMPL fill:#9dc3e6 style OUTCOME fill:#d5e8d4 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part A expects **2–3 sentence crisp answers with diagram support** where applicable. Each below provides the exact model answer a Board examiner expects. ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **"Define Quality Philosophy. List any four quality gurus and state one core contribution of each."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** > Quality Philosophy is the set of beliefs, values, and managerial doctrines that guide an organization toward excellence in its products, services, and processes. In shipbuilding, it forms the cultural foundation for all QA/QC activities. > > Four quality gurus and their core contributions: > 1. **W. Edwards Deming** — 14 Points for Management and the PDCA cycle > 2. **Joseph M. Juran** — Juran Trilogy (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) > 3. **Philip B. Crosby** — Zero Defects concept and the principle *Quality is Free* > 4. **Kaoru Ishikawa** — Quality Circles and the Fishbone (cause-and-effect) diagram *(Award 1 mark for definition; ½ mark each for guru + contribution.)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style] **"State the four Absolutes of Quality as proposed by Philip Crosby."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** > The four Absolutes of Quality proposed by **Philip B. Crosby** are: > 1. **Quality is defined as conformance to requirements**, not as "goodness" or "elegance." > 2. **The system for causing quality is prevention, not inspection.** > 3. **The performance standard for quality must be Zero Defects**, not "acceptable quality levels." > 4. **The measurement of quality is the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**, not indices. *(Award ¾ mark per absolute; 3 marks total.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks. Examiners expect *diagrams + bullet structure + numerical examples*. --- ### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **Juran Trilogy** of quality management in detail. How is it applicable in a shipbuilding organization? *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A shipyard produces 8,000 steel brackets per quarter. Each bracket has 8 critical dimension opportunities. During QA inspection, 400 defects are identified. Calculate the **DPMO** and identify the **sigma level**. Comment on whether the process is acceptable for shipbuilding class requirements. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Answer — Part (a) **Juran Trilogy** consists of three managerial processes essential for breakthrough quality management: **1. Quality Planning (QP):** - Identify the customers (shipowners, classification societies, end-users) - Determine customer needs (seaworthiness, payload capacity, fuel efficiency) - Develop product features that satisfy those needs - Establish quality goals and develop processes to meet them - *Shipbuilding application:* Before commencing hull block fabrication, the yard identifies all stakeholders, derives class-rule requirements, sets dimensional tolerances, and plans the welding procedure specification (WPS). **2. Quality Control (QC):** - Evaluate actual quality performance - Compare actual performance against quality goals - Act on the difference (corrective/preventive action) - *Shipbuilding application:* Daily monitoring of weld quality through NDT (ultrasonic, radiographic), comparing measured results against WPS, and triggering rework when deviations occur. **3. Quality Improvement (QI):** - Establish the infrastructure needed for continuous improvement - Identify specific improvement projects (cost, rework, cycle time) - Establish project teams, allocate resources, provide training and motivation - *Shipbuilding application:* Dedicated Kaizen teams reducing plate-cutting waste through nesting optimization. **Conclusion:** The Juran Trilogy provides shipyards a *systematic lifecycle framework* ensuring quality is embedded from design to delivery, which is essential for IACS class certification and customer satisfaction. #### Model Answer — Part (b) **Given:** - Units produced per quarter, $N = 8000$ - Opportunities per unit, $O = 8$ - Total defects observed, $D = 400$ **Step 1 — Total Opportunities** $$N \times O = 8000 \times 8 = 64{,}000 \;\; \text{opportunities}$$ **Step 2 — DPMO Calculation** $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O} = \frac{400 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{64{,}000}$$ $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{4 \times 10^{8}}{6.4 \times 10^{4}} = 6250 \;\; \text{DPMO}$$ **Step 3 — Sigma Level Identification** From standard conversion tables: - 5σ = 233 DPMO - 4σ = 6,210 DPMO - 3σ = 66,807 DPMO By interpolation between 4σ and 5σ: $$\sigma \approx 4.0 + \frac{6210 - 6250}{6210 - 233} \times (5 - 4)$$ $$\sigma \approx 4.0 + \frac{-40}{5977} \approx 3.99 \;\; (\text{approximately } 4\sigma)$$ **Step 4 — Assessment for Shipbuilding** A 4σ process yields **6,210 DPMO**, which translates to ~99.38% yield. For shipbuilding: - Critical safety welds require ≥ **5σ** capability - The current process is **borderline acceptable** for non-critical brackets but **unacceptable** for hull-integrity components **Recommendation:** Implement Six Sigma DMAIC methodology to lift capability toward 5σ. --- ### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Internal Choice Alternative] **(a)** Discuss the **evolution of quality philosophy** from the Inspection Era to the Strategic Quality Management Era. Highlight the contributions of any four quality gurus. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A shipyard's propeller shaft production line has: - Specification: Target diameter $T = 250.00$ mm - Functional tolerance: $\Delta_{0} = 0.40$ mm - Average loss at functional tolerance: $A_{0} = \text{Rs. } 6{,}400$ - Actual diameter of one shaft: $x = 250.25$ mm Compute: (i) the Taguchi loss coefficient $k$; (ii) the economic loss $L(x)$ for the actual shaft; (iii) the loss if diameter were exactly at target $T = 250.00$ mm. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Answer — Part (a) **Evolution of Quality Philosophy:** | Era | Period | Focus | Key Contributor | |---|---|---|---| | Inspection | Pre-1920s | Detection, sorting | Frederick Taylor | | Quality Control | 1920s–1950s | Statistical control | Walter Shewhart | | Quality Assurance | 1950s–1970s | System-wide QC | Armand Feigenbaum | | Strategic QM | 1980s–Present | TQM, continuous improvement | Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Taguchi | **Contribution of Four Gurus:** 1. **W. Edwards Deming** introduced the *14 Points for Management* and the *PDCA cycle*, emphasizing that quality must be built into the system rather than inspected at the end. His *System of Profound Knowledge* integrates systems thinking, variation, psychology, and epistemology. 2. **Joseph M. Juran** developed the *Juran Trilogy* (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement), providing a universal management framework applicable across industries including shipbuilding. 3. **Philip B. Crosby** championed *Zero Defects* philosophy and the concept of *Quality is Free*. He argued that the cost of prevention is always lower than the cost of failure, a principle particularly relevant in shipbuilding where rework costs are enormous. 4. **Kaoru Ishikawa** introduced *Quality Circles* as a mechanism for shop-floor worker participation, and the *Fishbone Diagram* as a structured root-cause analysis tool widely used in shipyard NCR investigations. **Conclusion:** The evolution reflects a paradigm shift from *detection → prevention → participation → strategy*. #### Model Answer — Part (b) **Given:** - Target $T = 250.00$ mm - Functional tolerance $\Delta_{0} = 0.40$ mm - Average loss $A_{0} = \text{Rs. } 6{,}400$ - Actual $x = 250.25$ mm **Step (i) — Loss Coefficient $k$** $$k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} = \frac{6400}{(0.40)^{2}} = \frac{6400}{0.16}$$ $$\boxed{k = \text{Rs. } 40{,}000 \;\; \text{per mm}^{2}}$$ **Step (ii) — Loss at Actual Diameter** Deviation: $x - T = 250.25 - 250.00 = 0.25$ mm $$L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2} = 40{,}000 \times (0.25)^{2}$$ $$L(x) = 40{,}000 \times 0.0625$$ $$\boxed{L(x) = \text{Rs. } 2{,}500 \;\; \text{per shaft}}$$ **Step (iii) — Loss at Target Diameter** Deviation: $x - T = 0$ $$L(T) = k \cdot 0^{2} = 0$$ $$\boxed{L(T) = \text{Rs. } 0 \;\; \text{(No loss at target)}}$$ **Engineering Insight:** A shaft *within* conventional tolerance still imposes a Rs. 2,500 economic loss. This validates Taguchi's central argument: **specifications are economic fences, not quality fences**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 1:** > 1. **Confusing Quality with Grade** — Grade is a *categorization* for different functional uses; quality is *conformance to requirements*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this interchange. > 2. **Citing Crosby's "Quality is Free" without the qualifying clause** — Crosby meant quality *prevention* is free compared to failure costs. Always qualify. > 3. **Using the wrong formula direction in Taguchi QLF** — Some students write $L(x) = k \cdot (T - x)^{2}$; while mathematically equivalent due to squaring, board convention prefers $(x - T)^{2}$. > 4. **Forgetting the $\times 1{,}000{,}000$ multiplier in DPMO** — This is the single most common computational error. Lose 1 mark. > 5. **Writing Juran Trilogy as PDCA instead of QP, QC, QI** — PDCA is Deming's cycle, not Juran's trilogy. Confusing these costs 1–2 marks. > 6. **No units in numerical answers** — Always pair numerical values with units (Rs., mm, %, DPMO). Examiners actively hunt for missing units. > 7. **Writing essays without diagrams** — KTU's 2024 OBE expects *visual structure*. A flowchart or table in long answers typically earns 1–2 grace marks. > 8. **Skipping the interpretation step** — After computing $C_{pk}$ or DPMO, you *must* state whether the value is acceptable for shipbuilding. Marks are reserved for the interpretive statement. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Quality Philosophy & Management Strategies** - ☐ **Quality Philosophy** = cultural foundation; **Quality Management Strategy** = tactical deployment - ☐ Four eras of quality: **Inspection → QC → QA → Strategic QM** - ☐ Seven gurus to memorize: **Shewhart, Deming, Juran, Crosby, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Taguchi** - ☐ **Deming** = 14 Points + PDCA + Statistical variation - ☐ **Juran Trilogy** = QP + QC + QI (NOT PDCA — common confusion) - ☐ **Crosby's 4 Absolutes** = Conformance + Prevention + Zero Defects + PONC - ☐ **Ishikawa** = Quality Circles + Fishbone Diagram (cause-and-effect) - ☐ **Feigenbaum** = TQC (precursor to TQM) and "Quality is everyone's job" - ☐ **Taguchi Loss Function**: $L(x) = k(x - T)^{2}$, where $k = A_{0}/\Delta_{0}^{2}$ - ☐ **PAF Model** CoQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure - ☐ CoQ industry benchmark in shipyards: **5%–15% of revenue** - ☐ **DPMO** = (Defects × $10^{6}$) / (Units × Opportunities) - ☐ **Six Sigma** target = **3.4 DPMO** - ☐ $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ = minimum acceptable for shipbuilding; $\geq 1.67$ = world-class - ☐ **Zero Defects ≠ perfection**; it is a *standard of performance and mindset* - ☐ Always use units (Rs., mm, %, DPMO) and add interpretive sentences in numerical answers - ☐ In KTU 2024 Scheme, diagrams (flowcharts, comparison tables) carry implicit weightage in 14-mark answers > **End of Module 1 Notes — Quality Philosophy and Management Strategies**

topics.title: Major Quality Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Quality Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Major Quality Problems in Shipbuilding

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Quality Problems (MQPs)** in shipbuilding are defined as critical deviations, defects, or non-conformities occurring during the design, fabrication, assembly, or outfitting phases of a vessel that compromise structural integrity, seaworthiness, operational safety, classification society compliance, or contractual specifications. These problems are categorized by their severity (critical, major, minor), origin (welding, material, dimensional, surface, corrosion), and detectability (visual, NDT-based, operational).

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST862)**, MQPs are studied under the umbrella of **Quality Assurance (QA)** and **Quality Control (QC)** frameworks, with reference to standards issued by **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)**, **ISO 9001:2015**, **AWS D1.1**, **ASME Sec IX**, and classification rules from **IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Building a Ship is Like Stitching a Giant Steel Garment:**
> Imagine you are tailoring an enormous jacket (the ship hull) out of thick steel plates instead of fabric. Each "stitch" is a weld, each "panel" is a rolled plate, and each "button" is a riveted or bolted connection. Quality problems are like loose threads, misaligned panels, weak stitches, or fabric tears. Just as a tailor checks seams under a magnifying glass, shipbuilders employ **Quality Assurance** inspectors and **NDT technicians** to catch flaws before the "garment" (ship) is worn by its "user" (crew and cargo). A single missed defect — like a torn seam at the shoulder — can cause the entire structure to fail under stress (rough seas, cyclic loading).

### Categories of Major Quality Problems (High-Level Taxonomy)

| S.No | Category | Description |
|------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Welding Defects** | Cracks, porosity, slag inclusions, undercut, lack of fusion, lack of penetration |
| 2 | **Material Defects** | Lamellar tearing, laminations, inclusions, rolling defects |
| 3 | **Dimensional / Geometric Deviations** | Plate distortion, misalignment, tolerance excursions, angularity errors |
| 4 | **Surface & Coating Issues** | Poor surface preparation, paint failures, blistering, rust creep |
| 5 | **Corrosion-Related Defects** | Galvanic corrosion, pitting, wastage, erosion-corrosion |
| 6 | **Structural / Assembly Defects** | Misaligned stiffeners, improper block jointing, sequence errors |
| 7 | **Outfitting / Piping Defects** | Leaks, misalignment, wrong material, system contamination |
| 8 | **NDT-Revealed Defects** | Sub-surface flaws detected by UT, RT, MT, PT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** According to industry statistics compiled by classification societies, **welding-related defects account for approximately 60–70%** of all major quality problems in ship construction, making them the single most critical category in the QA framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Welding Defects — The Dominant MQP Category

### (a) Cracks

Cracks are **linear discontinuities** caused by localized stress exceeding the material's fracture strength during or after welding. They are classified as the most severe defect type.

**Sub-classification of Cracks:**

- **Hot Cracks (Solidification Cracks):** Form at elevated temperatures during weld solidification. Caused by low-melting constituents (S, P) segregating to grain boundaries.
- **Cold Cracks (Hydrogen-Induced Cracking, HIC):** Form at ambient temperatures due to diffused hydrogen, restrained stresses, and susceptible microstructure (hard martensite).
- **Reheat Cracks:** Form during post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) in creep-resistant steels.
- **Lamellar Tears:** Step-like cracks in the through-thickness direction of plates, caused by high Z-direction restraint and inclusions (MnS).

**Governing Relationship for Cracking Susceptibility:**

$$ \text{CSI} = \frac{\text{Mn} + \text{S}}{\text{C} + \text{Mn} + \text{Si} + \text{Ni} + \text{Cr} + \text{Mo}} \times 10^3 $$

Where CSI is the **Carbon Steel Cracking Susceptibility Index**. Values **> 6** indicate high cracking tendency.

### (b) Porosity

Porosity consists of **cavities or voids** in the weld metal caused by gas entrapment during solidification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Causes:** Contaminated base metal (oil, rust, moisture), improper shielding gas flow, excessive welding speed, inadequate gas coverage, damp electrodes.

**Acceptance Criteria (per AWS D1.1):**

$$ d_{\text{porosity}} \leq \frac{T}{4} \text{ or } 4\,\text{mm (whichever is smaller)} $$

Where $d_{\text{porosity}}$ is the maximum individual pore diameter and $T$ is the plate thickness.

### (c) Slag Inclusion

Non-metallic solid material entrapped in the weld metal or between weld and base metal. Originates from **flux coatings**, **welding slag**, or **oxide films**.

### (d) Lack of Fusion & Lack of Penetration

- **Lack of Fusion (LOF):** Failure of weld metal to fuse with base metal or adjacent weld bead.
- **Lack of Penetration (LOP):** Weld metal does not extend through the full joint thickness.

**Acceptance per IACS UR W17:**

$$ \text{LOP}_{\text{max}} \leq 0.2\,T \quad \text{(for butt welds)} $$

### (e) Undercut

A groove melted into the base metal adjacent to the weld toe and left unfilled by weld metal.

$$ U_{\text{depth}} \leq 0.5\,\text{mm} \quad \text{(for primary structural welds)} $$

### (f) Overlap

Weld metal that has flowed over the surface of the base metal without fusion.

## 2.2 Material Defects

### Lamellar Tearing

A **step-like crack** occurring in the through-thickness (Z) direction of rolled plates, parallel to the rolled surface. Driven by **high Z-direction strain** from restrained weld shrinkage.

**Z-direction Performance Classification (per ASTM A770):**

| Class | Avg. % Reduction in Area (Z) | Application |
|-------|------------------------------|-------------|
| Z15 | ≥ 15% | Moderate restraint |
| Z25 | ≥ 25% | High restraint |
| Z35 | ≥ 35% | Severe restraint (offshore, naval) |

## 2.3 Dimensional & Geometric Deviations

| Parameter | Typical Tolerance (Hull) | Source |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------|
| Plate Flatness | $\pm 2\,\text{mm/m}$ | Workshop standard |
| Block Flatness | $\pm 4\,\text{mm over 2 m span}$ | Class-approved ITP |
| Stiffener Alignment | $\pm 3\,\text{mm from theoretical line}$ | IACS S21 |
| Block Erection Gap | $\pm 2\,\text{mm}$ | Yard procedure |
| Angularity (Transverse) | $\leq 1^\circ$ | Hull GA |

## 2.4 Surface Preparation & Coating Defects

The **Sa 2½ (Near-White Metal Blast)** standard per ISO 8501-1 is the most common reference:

$$ R_a \geq 50\,\mu m \quad \text{and visual cleanliness} \geq 95\% $$

Common coating failures: **blistering, peeling, rust creep, under-film corrosion, mud-cracking, sagging**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Matrix

| Defect Type | Governing Equation / Rule | Unit | Severity |
|-------------|---------------------------|------|----------|
| Porosity | $d_{\text{pore}} \leq \min\left(\frac{T}{4}, 4\right)$ | mm | Major |
| Undercut | $U_d \leq 0.5$ | mm | Major |
| LOP | $\text{LOP} \leq 0.2\,T$ | mm | Critical |
| Crack Susceptibility | $\text{CSI} = \frac{\text{Mn} + \text{S}}{\text{C} + \text{Mn} + \text{Si} + \text{Ni} + \text{Cr} + \text{Mo}} \times 10^3$ | index | Critical |
| Z-direction Ductility | $\text{RA}_Z \geq 15/25/35$ | % | Critical |
| Surface Profile | $R_a \geq 50$ | μm | Major |
| Plate Distortion | $\delta \leq \frac{L}{1000}$ | mm | Major |
| Class Survey Gap | $\pm 2$ | mm | Major |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These tolerances are directly enforced by **classification surveyors** during new-building surveys. A single critical defect (e.g., transverse crack in a hatch coaming) can **stop production**, trigger **rewelding**, and delay vessel delivery by weeks — costing **millions of rupees per day**.

## 2.6 Severity Classification (IACS UR Z17 / Z23)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-tier classification used by classification societies:**
> - **Critical:** Immediate stop-work; root-cause analysis mandatory; rectification before continuation.
> - **Major:** Disposition required (repair, accept-as-is with engineering justification, or reject).
> - **Minor:** Cosmetic or below acceptance threshold; record and monitor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Cracking Susceptibility Index (CSI)

**Problem Setup:** A butt weld in a higher-strength shipbuilding plate (EH36) is being evaluated. The chemical composition (wt%) is:

$$ \text{C} = 0.16,\ \text{Mn} = 1.45,\ \text{S} = 0.025,\ \text{Si} = 0.30,\ \text{Ni} = 0.10,\ \text{Cr} = 0.05,\ \text{Mo} = 0.02 $$

**Step 1 — Recall the governing formula:**

$$ \text{CSI} = \frac{\text{Mn} + \text{S}}{\text{C} + \text{Mn} + \text{Si} + \text{Ni} + \text{Cr} + \text{Mo}} \times 10^3 $$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$ \text{Numerator} = \text{Mn} + \text{S} = 1.45 + 0.025 = 1.475 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator (sum of alloying elements):**

$$ \text{Denominator} = \text{C} + \text{Mn} + \text{Si} + \text{Ni} + \text{Cr} + \text{Mo} $$

$$ = 0.16 + 1.45 + 0.30 + 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.02 = 2.08 $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the ratio and scale by $10^3$:**

$$ \text{CSI} = \frac{1.475}{2.08} \times 10^3 = 0.7091 \times 10^3 = 709.1 $$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**

$$ \text{CSI} = 709.1 \gg 6 \implies \text{Very High Cracking Susceptibility} $$

This indicates the steel is **highly susceptible to hot cracking**, demanding:
- Preheating to $\geq 100\,^\circ\text{C}$
- Controlled heat input $\leq 2.5\,\text{kJ/mm}$
- Post-weld hydrogen bake-out

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Acceptance of Porosity in a 25 mm EH36 Plate

**Step 1 — State governing relation:**

$$ d_{\text{porosity}} \leq \min\left(\frac{T}{4},\, 4\,\text{mm}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Compute $T/4$:**

$$ \frac{T}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25\,\text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the minimum rule:**

$$ d_{\text{porosity,max}} = \min(6.25,\, 4) = 4\,\text{mm} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** No individual pore may exceed **4 mm** in diameter, regardless of the 6.25 mm theoretical limit. This is the **stricter code clause wins** principle in AWS D1.1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Lack-of-Penetration Acceptance (T = 20 mm)

**Step 1 — Governing rule:**

$$ \text{LOP} \leq 0.2\,T $$

**Step 2 — Compute limit:**

$$ \text{LOP}_{\text{max}} = 0.2 \times 20 = 4\,\text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Any measured LOP > 4 mm in a 20 mm butt weld constitutes a **Critical Defect** — mandatory repair.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Lamellar Tearing Risk in a Stiffened Plate

**Scenario:** A 30 mm thick EH36 plate with stiffener fillet welds in a 70 mm T-joint.

**Step 1 — Check Z-direction ductility class:**

If supplied as **Z15**, then:

$$ \text{RA}_Z \geq 15\% $$

**Step 2 — Restraint check:** With a 30 mm plate and 70 mm stiffener, the **Z-direction strain** at the fillet toe can reach:

$$ \varepsilon_Z \approx \frac{\delta_{\text{shrink}}}{t_{\text{plate}}} \approx \frac{2\,\text{mm}}{30\,\text{mm}} = 6.67\% $$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$ \text{If } \varepsilon_Z > \text{allowable ductility} \implies \text{Upgrade to Z25 or Z35} $$

In this case, $\varepsilon_Z = 6.67\%$ may exceed Z15 capacity → recommend **Z25 plate procurement** with controlled welding sequence.

---

## 3.5 Summary Decision Matrix

| Defect | Detected By | Repair Method | Class Survey |
|--------|-------------|---------------|--------------|
| Crack | MT, PT, UT | Grind + re-weld | Hold point |
| Porosity | RT, UT | Grind + re-weld | Witness |
| Slag | UT, RT | Grind + re-weld | Witness |
| LOF / LOP | UT, RT | Re-weld from root | Hold point |
| Undercut | Visual | Grind smooth + repair weld | Witness |
| Lamellar Tear | UT (TOFD), phased-array | Engineering disposition | Hold point |
| Distortion | Survey | Flame straighten / mechanical | Witness |
| Coating Failure | Visual, adhesion test | Re-blast + re-coat | Witness |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
