<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PENST862, Dec 2024 Style]**

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: List any SIX major quality problems encountered in shipbuilding and briefly explain why welding defects dominate this category.**

**Model Answer (Structured for 3 Marks):**

**Six major quality problems:**
1. Cracks (welding)
2. Porosity (welding)
3. Slag inclusion (welding)
4. Undercut (welding)
5. Lamellar tearing (material)
6. Plate distortion (dimensional)

**Why welding defects dominate:**
- Welding is the **primary joining method** in ship hulls (>80% of joints are welded).
- The process involves **localized melting, solidification, and shrinkage**, creating complex thermal and mechanical stresses.
- Human skill dependency, parameter sensitivity, and environmental factors all contribute to defect formation.
- *(1 mark for listing 6, 1 mark for welding reasoning, 1 mark for explaining dominance)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between Lack of Fusion (LOF) and Lack of Penetration (LOP). State their acceptance criteria per IACS UR W17.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LOF | LOP |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Definition | Weld metal not fused with base metal or adjacent bead | Weld root not extended through full joint thickness |
| Location | Sidewall / inter-pass | Root region |
| Cause | Low heat input, fast travel, contamination | Narrow gap, root face too high, low current |
| Acceptance | Zero tolerance (critical) | $\text{LOP} \leq 0.2\,T$ |

*(2 marks for differentiation + 1 mark for acceptance criteria)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, PENST862, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of welding cracks** in shipbuilding, with reference to hot cracks, cold cracks, reheat cracks, and lamellar tearing. For each type, mention the **causative mechanism** and at least **one preventive measure**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A butt weld in a 28 mm thick DH36 plate is subjected to ultrasonic testing. The CSI of the steel is computed as 480. Evaluate the **cracking susceptibility** and recommend a **welding procedure** to mitigate the risk. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a):

**1. Hot Cracks (Solidification Cracks) — 2 Marks:**
- Form during solidification at temperatures near the solidus.
- Mechanism: Low-melting constituents (S, P) segregate to grain boundaries; shrinkage strain exceeds ductility.
- Prevention: Control S ≤ 0.008%, P ≤ 0.025%; use low-heat-input procedures; avoid deep V-groove with high restraint.

**2. Cold Cracks (Hydrogen-Induced) — 2 Marks:**
- Form at ambient temperatures, typically delayed (HIC, delayed cracking).
- Mechanism: Diffusible hydrogen + restrained stress + hard martensite microstructure.
- Prevention: Pre-heat to 100–150°C; use low-hydrogen electrodes (AWS E7018); PWHT; control hydrogen to ≤ 5 ml/100 g.

**3. Reheat Cracks — 1.5 Marks:**
- Form during PWHT in Cr-Mo, Cr-Mo-V steels.
- Mechanism: Precipitation strengthening during reheat reduces creep ductility at grain boundaries.
- Prevention: Avoid sharp notches; use step-cooling simulation; select PWHT temperature carefully.

**4. Lamellar Tearing — 1.5 Marks:**
- Step-like cracks in through-thickness direction.
- Mechanism: High Z-direction strain from weld shrinkage acting on MnS inclusions.
- Prevention: Use Z-direction ductility tested plate (Z25/Z35); balanced welding sequence; buttering layer.

#### Model Answer to (b):

**Step 1 — Calculate CSI:**

$$ \text{CSI} = \frac{\text{Mn} + \text{S}}{\text{C} + \text{Mn} + \text{Si} + \text{Ni} + \text{Cr} + \text{Mo}} \times 10^3 $$

Given CSI = 480.

**Step 2 — Interpret the value:**

$$ 480 \gg 6 \implies \text{High cracking susceptibility} $$

**Step 3 — Required Preventive Measures:**
- **Preheating:** $\geq 125\,^\circ\text{C}$ for 28 mm DH36.
- **Interpass temperature:** $\leq 230\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Heat input:** $1.5 \leq Q \leq 2.5\,\text{kJ/mm}$.
- **Electrode:** Low-hydrogen type (e.g., AWS E8018-C1 or equivalent).
- **Hydrogen control:** Bake electrodes at 300–350°C for 2 hours; store in ovens at 150°C.
- **Joint design:** Use double-V or double-U groove to balance shrinkage.
- **PWHT:** Stress-relief at 595 ± 15°C if specified.
- **NDT:** Mandatory UT + MT after 48 hours (delayed crack detection).

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Even though CSI < 500 suggests marginally lower risk, the plate thickness (28 mm) amplifies restraint, so the above WPS is mandatory.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **major non-welding quality problems** in shipbuilding under the categories of: (i) material defects, (ii) dimensional deviations, (iii) surface and coating failures. For each category, give at least **two examples with causes**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A double-bottom plate (30 mm) in a tanker is being grit-blasted to Sa 2½ before epoxy coating. The surface profile measured is $R_a = 38\,\mu m$. Evaluate whether the **surface preparation meets ISO 8501-1 and ISO 8503** requirements, and suggest corrective action if needed. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a):

**(i) Material Defects — 2.5 Marks:**
- **Lamellar tearing:** Step-like cracks in plate through-thickness; caused by MnS inclusions + Z-direction restraint.
- **Lamination:** Planar discontinuity from rolling; caused by gas porosity in ingot.
- **Inclusion / segregation:** Non-metallic stringers; from deoxidation.
- **Surface imperfections:** Scabs, scabs, gouges; from improper handling.

**(ii) Dimensional Deviations — 2.5 Marks:**
- **Plate distortion (waviness):** Caused by uneven welding heat input; out-of-flatness > 2 mm/m.
- **Stiffener misalignment:** Caused by poor tack welding or template error.
- **Block misalignment at erection:** Caused by thermal expansion, sag measurement error.
- **Angularity error in transverse sections:** Caused by cumulative fabrication tolerance.

**(iii) Surface and Coating Failures — 2 Marks:**
- **Blistering:** Caused by moisture/osmosis under coating.
- **Rust creep:** Caused by inadequate surface profile.
- **Peeling / delamination:** Caused by incompatibility between coats or contamination.
- **Mud cracking:** Caused by excessive DFT or solvent entrapment.

#### Model Answer to (b):

**Step 1 — Reference requirement:**

$$ R_{a,\text{min}} = 50\,\mu m \quad \text{(per ISO 8503-2, Grade Fine)} $$

**Step 2 — Compare with measured value:**

$$ R_{a,\text{measured}} = 38\,\mu m < 50\,\mu m $$

**Step 3 — Visual cleanliness check:**
Assume visual inspection confirms Sa 2½ appearance (≥ 95% cleanliness). Even so, profile is **insufficient**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$ \text{Profile NON-COMPLIANT} \implies \text{Re-blast required} $$

**Step 5 — Corrective Action:**
- Use **finer abrasive** (e.g., G25 steel grit or garnet).
- Increase blasting pressure to **70–80 psi**.
- Verify with **Keane-Tator comparator** or **stylus profilometer** at multiple locations.
- Document via **surface inspection report** before applying primer.
- Apply primer within **4 hours** of blasting (avoid flash rust).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing LOF and LOP** — examiners specifically look for clear distinction between sidewall fusion failure and root penetration failure. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the units** in formula derivations — always state the unit of each variable. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Not mentioning the standard** — when stating acceptance limits, always cite **AWS D1.1, IACS UR W17, or ISO 8501-1** for full credit.
> 4. **Skipping preventive measures** — in questions on defects, examiners expect **cause + detection + repair + prevention**. A one-sided answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Numerical errors in CSI** — forgetting to multiply by $10^3$ is the most frequent error.
> 6. **Writing generic statements** — avoid "welding defects are bad"; instead, quantify: "60–70% of MQPs in hulls are weld-related."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PENST862 Module 1:**

- **Welding defects dominate** — ~60–70% of all MQPs in shipbuilding.
- **CSI formula** must be memorized:

$$ \text{CSI} = \frac{\text{Mn} + \text{S}}{\text{C} + \text{Mn} + \text{Si} + \text{Ni} + \text{Cr} + \text{Mo}} \times 10^3 > 6 \Rightarrow \text{High Risk} $$

- **Acceptance limits to memorize:**
  - Undercut $\leq 0.5\,mm$
  - Porosity $\leq \min(T/4,\ 4\,mm)$
  - LOP $\leq 0.2\,T$
  - Surface profile $R_a \geq 50\,\mu m$ (Sa 2½)
- **Cracks are always CRITICAL** — zero acceptance tolerance.
- **Lamellar tearing** → solved by **Z-direction tested plate** (Z15/Z25/Z35).
- **Three severity tiers:** Critical / Major / Minor (per IACS UR Z17).
- **NDT methods and their targets:**
  - **UT** → sub-surface (cracks, LOF, LOP, lamellar)
  - **RT** → volumetric (porosity, slag, wormholes)
  - **MT** → surface & sub-surface ferromagnetic cracks
  - **PT** → surface open defects (non-magnetic materials)
- **Surface preparation:** Sa 2½ is the most common shipbuilding standard.
- **Classification societies** — IACS, IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV — all enforce similar QA standards.
- **Always cite the standard** (AWS, ISO, IACS, ASME) in exam answers for full marks.
- **Distortion control:** Sequence welding, backstep technique, and mechanical restraint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Between QA and QC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Difference Between QA and QC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quality Assurance (QA)** is a *proactive, process-oriented, systematic management philosophy* that encompasses all the planned and systematic activities implemented within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. In the shipbuilding context (governed by IACS, ISO 9001:2015, and classification societies like IRS, LR, DNV, ABS), QA governs the entire lifecycle — from steel plate procurement, hull design approval, welding procedure specification (WPS), to sea trials and delivery.

**Quality Control (QC)** is a *reactive, product-oriented, inspection-based discipline* that involves the operational techniques and activities used to fulfill requirements for quality. It focuses on identifying defects in finished products through measurement, testing, and inspection. In a shipyard, QC is the actual hands-on execution: dimensional checks of a hull block, ultrasonic testing (UT) of a welded joint, dye penetrant test (DPT) on a propeller blade, and hydrostatic testing of a fuel tank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> - **QA = "Build quality INTO the process"** (Prevention-oriented)
> - **QC = "Inspect quality OUT of the product"** (Detection-oriented)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Bakery Analogy** 🍞
Imagine a master baker making 1,000 loaves of bread daily:
- **QA** is the baker deciding the recipe, fixing the oven temperature at **180°C**, training the staff, sourcing verified flour, and establishing a checklist for every shift. The baker is *preventing* bad bread from ever being made.
- **QC** is the quality inspector who takes a knife, cuts each loaf, weighs it, and rejects the burnt ones at the end of the conveyor belt. The inspector is *detecting* bad bread.

In shipbuilding: **QA** writes the Welding Procedure Specification (WPS); **QC** actually performs the visual inspection and NDT on the welded joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory hook:** QA is like an umbrella covering the whole factory. QC is the raincoat worn only by the inspector.

## 1.3 Key Industry Standards Referenced

- **ISO 9001:2015** – QMS requirements
- **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**
- **Class Rules** – IRS, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas
- **AWS D1.1 / ASME Section IX** – Welding QC

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How QA and QC Function in a Shipyard

### QA Functionality (System-Level)
1. **Documentation Control** – Managing ITPs (Inspection and Test Plans), QAPs (Quality Assurance Plans), WPS, PQR.
2. **Process Audit** – Internal audits to verify WPS is being followed.
3. **Training & Qualification** – Certifying welders (6GR positions), NDT Level II/III inspectors per **ASNT/ISO 9712**.
4. **Vendor/Subcontractor Evaluation** – Approving steel mills, paint suppliers.
5. **Preventive Action** – CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) closure.

### QC Functionality (Product-Level)
1. **Receiving Inspection** – Verifying steel plate Mill Test Certificates (MTC).
2. **In-Process Inspection** – Fit-up checks before welding, interpass temperature measurement.
3. **Final Inspection** – Dimensional survey, NDT, leak testing.
4. **Measuring Equipment Calibration** – Using calibrated UT probes, vernier calipers.
5. **Defect Disposition** – Rework, repair, or scrap decision.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Quality Control (QC)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Orientation** | Process | Product |
| **Focus** | Prevention | Detection |
| **Approach** | Proactive | Reactive |
| **Scope** | Entire QMS / project lifecycle | Specific product / batch / joint |
| **Timing** | Before & during production | After production / in-process |
| **Responsible By** | Management + QA dept + everyone | Dedicated QC inspectors |
| **Tools** | Audits, procedures, training, FMEA | Gauges, NDT, calipers, testing |
| **Documentation** | Quality Manual, QAP, ITP, WPS | Inspection Reports, NCRs, Test Certificates |
| **Cost Nature** | Prevention cost (low long-term) | Appraisal + Failure cost (high) |
| **Output** | Confidence in process | Acceptance / Rejection decision |
| **KTU Mnemonic** | "Plan-Do-Check-Act driver" | "Inspect-Test-Measure executor" |
| **Shipbuilding Example** | Writing WPS for hull welding | UT of the welded butt joint |
| **Standard** | ISO 9001 Clause 7 / 8 | ISO 9001 Clause 8.3 / 9.1 |
| **Statistical Tool** | SPC, Control Charts setup | Actual control chart data plotting |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental engineering rationale is **Cost of Quality (CoQ)**. Industry data shows that detection after the fact can cost **10× to 100× more** than prevention. In shipbuilding, a weld defect found at the block stage (QC) costs ₹X; if that same defect is found during sea trials or in service, it can cost **₹100X** plus reputation loss.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defense Shipyards (MDL, GRSE)** require QA certification (QSCS) before they can bid for naval contracts.
- **Class Societies** will not issue a **Class Certificate** (and thus the ship cannot trade) without both QA documentation AND QC records.
- **Offshore platforms** require QA at design stage (DNV-OS-C101) and QC at fabrication (DNV-OS-C401).
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Relationship Matrix

## 3.1 The Sequential Relationship Between QA and QC

In a KTU answer, students often confuse whether QA comes before QC. The logical flow is shown below as an exhaustive step-by-step analytical derivation:

### Step 1: Customer / Class Requirement is Raised
$$\text{QMS Requirement} \rightarrow \text{QA Planning triggered}$$

### Step 2: QA Department Develops Documentation
$$\text{ITP} + \text{WPS} + \text{QAP} = \text{QA Package}$$

### Step 3: Production Begins Using Approved Procedures
$$\text{Process Execution} = f(\text{QA-defined Procedures})$$

### Step 4: QC Activates Hold and Witness Points per ITP
$$\text{QC Inspection} = \text{Verify conformance at Hold Points (H) and Witness Points (W)}$$

### Step 5: QC Issues Result
$$\text{Output} = \begin{cases} \text{Acceptance Certificate (if pass)} \\ \text{Non-Conformance Report (if fail)} \end{cases}$$

### Step 6: QA Triggers CAPA on NCR
$$\text{NCR} \rightarrow \text{Root Cause Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Corrective Action} \rightarrow \text{Update WPS/ITP}$$

### Step 7: Continuous Improvement (PDCA Cycle)
$$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Repeat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This sequence proves QA is the **enabler** and QC is the **verifier** — they are complementary, not interchangeable.

## 3.2 Mathematical Representation of the Cost of Quality (CoQ)

A common KTU numerical / conceptual question is the relationship between prevention, appraisal, and failure costs.

$$\text{Total CoQ} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}$$

Where:

$$C_{\text{Prevention}} \propto \text{QA Investment}$$

$$C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} \propto \text{QC Investment}$$

$$C_{\text{External Failure}} \rightarrow \text{Tends to 0 as } C_{\text{Prevention}} \rightarrow \infty$$

**Interpretation:** As QA (prevention) spend increases, the failure cost decreases exponentially — this is the economic justification for preferring QA over QC in modern shipyards.

## 3.3 Venn Relationship Diagram (Conceptual Derivation)

The relationship between QA, QC, and the broader QMS can be expressed:

$$\text{QMS} \supset \text{QA} \supset \text{QC}$$

Meaning:
- Every **QC** activity is part of **QA**.
- Every **QA** activity is part of the broader **QMS**.
- But **QA ≠ QC** (they are not equal in scope).
- Their **intersection** is the *Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)*.

$$\text{QA} \cap \text{QC} = \{\text{ITP, NDT Procedures, Test Records}\}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 QA vs QC — Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer and Class Requirement] --> B[QA Department]
    B --> C[Quality Manual and QAP]
    B --> D[Welding Procedure Specification WPS]
    B --> E[Inspection and Test Plan ITP]
    C --> F[Production Floor]
    D --> F
    E --> G[QC Department]
    F --> G
    G --> H{QC Inspection Result}
    H -->|Pass| I[Acceptance Certificate]
    H -->|Fail| J[Non Conformance Report NCR]
    J --> K[Root Cause Analysis]
    K --> L[Corrective and Preventive Action CAPA]
    L --> B
    I --> M[Final Delivery to Shipowner]
    M --> N[Continuous Improvement PDCA Loop]
    N --> B

    classDef qaBox fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef qcBox fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef procBox fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decBox fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px

    class B,C,D,E qaBox
    class G,H,I,J,K,L qcBox
    class F,M,N procBox
    class A decBox
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Mapping — Where QA and QC Operate in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DESIGNSTAGE[Design Stage]
        D1[Design QA Review]:::qa
        D2[Class Drawing Approval]:::qa
    end

    subgraph PROCURESTAGE[Procurement Stage]
        P1[Vendor QA Audit]:::qa
        P2[Incoming Material QC]:::qc
    end

    subgraph FABSTAGE[Fabrication Stage]
        F1[WPS Qualification QA]:::qa
        F2[Welder Qualification QA]:::qa

    end

    subgraph ERECTSTAGE[Erection Stage]

    subgraph TRIALSTAGE[Trials and Delivery]

        T2[Trial Result QC Measurement]:::qc
    end

    DESIGNSTAGE --> PROCURESTAGE --> FABSTAGE --> ERECTSTAGE --> TRIALSTAGE

```

## 4.3 PDCA Cycle — The Backbone of QA (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan\nEstablish QAP WPS ITP] --> D[Do\nExecute Production]
    D --> C[Check\nQC Inspection and NDT]
    C --> A[Act\nCAPA and Improvement]
    A --> P
    classDef pdca fill:#37474f,stroke:#263238,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    class P,D,C,A pdca
```
---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Quality Assurance is a management tool. Quality Control is a production tool." Justify this statement with reference to shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**
- QA is a *management tool* because it deals with planning, documentation, training, audits, and process design. The QA Manager in a shipyard approves the WPS, qualifies welders, and audits the production line — these are managerial functions.
- QC is a *production tool* because it is executed on the shop floor using measuring instruments, NDT equipment, gauges, and inspection checklists. QC inspectors physically measure plate thickness, perform UT on welds, and verify dimensional accuracy of hull blocks.
- **Conclusion:** The statement is justified because QA governs *how* work is done, while QC governs *whether* the work meets the standards set by QA.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List any six points of difference between Quality Assurance and Quality Control.**

**Model Answer (6 bullets):**
1. QA is **process-oriented**; QC is **product-oriented**.
2. QA is **proactive/preventive**; QC is **reactive/detective**.
3. QA covers the **entire QMS lifecycle**; QC covers **specific batches/joints**.
4. QA is the responsibility of **all employees**; QC is the responsibility of **dedicated inspectors**.
5. QA tools include **audits, training, procedures**; QC tools include **gauges, NDT, testing**.
6. QA output is **confidence in process**; QC output is **accept/reject decision**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Quality Assurance in shipbuilding. Discuss the role of classification societies in QA.

**Model Answer:**

Quality Assurance in shipbuilding is a **systematic, planned set of activities** embedded within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** that ensures the design, material selection, fabrication, and delivery of a vessel conform to specified requirements and applicable rules. It is governed by **ISO 9001:2015** principles and the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**.

**Role of Classification Societies in QA:**

1. **Rule Setting** – Societies like **IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV** publish detailed rules for hull scantlings, welding, NDT, and materials.
2. **Approval of QA Manuals** – Shipyards must submit their Quality Assurance Plan (QAP), WPS, and ITPs for society approval before construction begins.
3. **Surveillance Audits** – Class surveyors conduct periodic audits to verify that QA procedures are being implemented.
4. **Certification** – Issue of **Class Certificate** (Hull ⊕ Machinery) is only after QA+QC compliance is demonstrated.
5. **Continuous Monitoring** – Annual surveys ensure the QA system remains effective throughout the ship's life.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between Quality Assurance and Quality Control with a suitable shipbuilding example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **QA** | **QC** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Planned activities to provide confidence | Operational techniques to verify quality |
| Focus | Process | Product |
| Approach | Preventive | Detective |
| Ship Example | Preparing & approving a WPS for shell plating butt joint | Performing 100% UT on the executed shell butt weld |
| Document | QAP, WPS, ITP | Inspection Report, Test Certificate |
| Timing | Before & during | After & in-process |
| Responsibility | QA dept + management | QC inspector |

**Shipbuilding Example:**
For the **propeller shaft forging**:
- **QA:** Approving the forging procedure, qualifying the forging vendor, reviewing the heat treatment procedure, and approving the QAP with the classification society.
- **QC:** Conducting dimensional inspection of the shaft, performing **ultrasonic testing (UT)** as per class rules, verifying hardness, and issuing the **Mill Test Certificate** and **Surveyor's Certificate**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** "Without QC, QA is a paper exercise. Without QA, QC is a random act." Discuss this statement with reference to ISO 9001:2015.

**Model Answer:**

The statement captures the **interdependence** of QA and QC within the ISO 9001:2015 framework.

**Without QC, QA is a paper exercise:**
If QA only produces documents (WPS, QAP, ITP) but no one verifies actual conformance, defects will pass through. Example: A shipyard may have an excellent WPS for the double bottom welding, but if no QC inspector actually performs the visual or NDT inspection, defective welds may enter service, causing catastrophic failure.

**Without QA, QC is a random act:**
If there is no planned ITP, no calibrated procedure, no qualified welder, and no documented acceptance criteria, then whatever QC measures are taken will be inconsistent and legally/technically indefensible during a class survey or warranty claim.

**ISO 9001:2015 Link:**
- **Clause 7 (Support) and Clause 8 (Operation)** correspond to QA planning.
- **Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation)** and **Clause 10 (Improvement)** require QC monitoring, measurement, and CAPA.
- ISO 9001 explicitly mandates **both** — proving they are inseparable components of the QMS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Cost of Quality concept and justify why QA is preferred over QC from an economic standpoint in modern shipyards.

**Model Answer:**

**Cost of Quality (CoQ)** has four components:

$$C_{Total} = C_{Prevention} + C_{Appraisal} + C_{Internal Failure} + C_{External Failure}$$

| **Component** | **Linked to** | **Example in Shipyard** |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention Cost | QA | WPS qualification, welder training, audits |
| Appraisal Cost | QC | NDT, dimensional checks, surveys |
| Internal Failure | QC outcome | Rework of defective weld at block stage |
| External Failure | QC outcome | Hull crack in service, warranty repair |

**Economic Justification for QA:**
- **Internal Failure** cost = **Rework cost** ≈ 3× the prevention cost.
- **External Failure** cost (e.g., total loss at sea, salvage, litigation, reputation loss) can be **100×–1000×** the prevention cost.
- Therefore, investment in **QA (prevention)** yields the highest ROI — *"A stitch in time saves nine."*
- Modern shipyards like **Hyundai Heavy Industries, Cochin Shipyard, MDL** invest heavily in QA digitization (BIM, digital twins) to drive down CoQ.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing QA = QC** — *Never* write they are the same. They are **interrelated but distinct**.
> 2. **Confusing QA with Quality Audit** — QA is the system; audit is one tool within QA.
> 3. **Forgetting class society reference** — KTU examiners expect mention of **IACS/IRS/DNV/LR** in any shipbuilding QA/QC answer.
> 4. **Writing only textbook definitions** — Always give a **shipbuilding example** (hull welding, propeller shaft, NDT, sea trial). Marks are reserved for application.
> 5. **Ignoring ISO 9001:2015 clause numbers** — Referencing **Clauses 7, 8, 9, 10** adds examiner-visible depth.
> 6. **In Part B, not using tables or diagrams** — A well-drawn **comparison table** can earn 2–3 easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **QA = Process + Prevention + System-wide + Managerial** → "Build quality in."
- 🔹 **QC = Product + Detection + Job-specific + Operational** → "Inspect quality out."
- 🔹 **QMS ⊃ QA ⊃ QC** — nested hierarchy, not synonyms.
- 🔹 **IACS QSCS + ISO 9001:2015 + Class Rules** = legal backbone of shipbuilding quality.
- 🔹 **ITP (Inspection & Test Plan)** is the bridge document where QA plans meet QC execution.
- 🔹 **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle drives continuous improvement in QA.
- 🔹 **Cost of Quality** mathematically justifies why modern shipyards invest more in QA than QC.
- 🔹 **Welder Qualification + WPS Approval = QA**; **Actual Weld Inspection + NDT = QC**.
- 🔹 Always write **at least one shipbuilding-specific example** in any KTU answer on this topic.
- 🔹 Remember the **formula**: $\text{QMS} \supset \text{QA} \supset \text{QC}$ — useful for 1-mark MCQs and 3-mark direct questions.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Management Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Management Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Management Principles (QMPs)** are a set of **fundamental beliefs, norms, rules, and values** that are accepted and practiced universally as the foundation for an organization's quality management system. They form the conceptual backbone of the **ISO 9000 family of standards** (specifically **ISO 9000:2015**), guiding the establishment, implementation, maintenance, and continual improvement of a Quality Management System (QMS).

In the context of shipbuilding, Quality Management Principles serve as the strategic compass that directs shipyards, classification societies, and original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) toward consistent delivery of vessels that satisfy **customer requirements**, **regulatory codes** (e.g., **SOLAS**, **MARPOL**, **IACS UR**), and **statutory maritime law**.

The **seven Quality Management Principles** recognized by ISO are:

1. **Customer Focus**
2. **Leadership**
3. **Engagement of People**
4. **Process Approach**
5. **Improvement**
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making**
7. **Relationship Management**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shipyard as a Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine a high-end restaurant kitchen preparing a five-course meal. Each dish (the product) must satisfy the customer's palate (customer focus). The Head Chef sets the tone and direction (leadership). Sous chefs, line cooks, and dishwashers must all be competent and committed (engagement of people). Recipes, cooking sequences, and plating workflows form a process approach. The Chef continuously experiments to improve flavors (improvement). Tasting notes and customer reviews guide changes (evidence-based decision making). Finally, the kitchen maintains strong ties with local farms, suppliers, and delivery services (relationship management). The seven QMPs work exactly the same way in a shipyard — a ship is the "meal," the shipowner is the "customer," and the design office, fabrication shop, and outfitting jetty are the "kitchen stations."

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

In Quality Management, the standardized metrics used to gauge adherence to these principles include:

- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** — dimensionless score, typically **0 to 100%**
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — dimensionless defect rate
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — expressed as a percentage (**%**)
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — measured in monetary units (e.g., **USD**, **INR**, **EUR**)
- **On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** delivery metric — dimensionless percentage (**%**)
- **Six Sigma Level** — measured in **σ (sigma)** units, industry benchmark is **4.5σ → 3.4 DPMO**
- **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** — dimensionless, minimum acceptable value is **1.33**

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are **not physical constants** like the speed of light; they are **standardized key performance indicators (KPIs)** universally adopted by quality professionals. The **4.5 sigma** benchmark is specifically derived from Motorola's Six Sigma methodology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Quality Management Principles — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Principle 1: Customer Focus

- **Core Premise:** The primary goal of any QMS is to meet customer needs and exceed customer expectations.
- **Strategic Rationale ("Why"):** Sustained success is achieved when an organization attracts and retains the confidence of customers.
- **Operational Steps ("How"):**
  1. Identify customer needs and expectations.
  2. Link customer objectives to organizational objectives.
  3. Communicate customer requirements throughout the shipyard.
  4. Measure customer satisfaction systematically.
  5. Manage customer relationships proactively.
- **Shipbuilding Application:** Class society survey feedback, shipowner warranty claims, post-delivery vessel performance reviews.

#### 2.1.2 Principle 2: Leadership

- **Core Premise:** Leaders at all levels establish unity of purpose, direction, and engagement.
- **Strategic Rationale:** Leadership creates the internal environment in which people can become fully involved.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. Communicate the organization's vision and quality policy.
  2. Establish trust and eliminate fear.
  3. Provide people with the required resources, training, and authority.
  4. Inspire and recognize contributions.
- **Shipbuilding Application:** Shipyard Managing Director setting the tone for zero-defect hull erection; Production Manager empowering foremen with NCR (Non-Conformance Report) closure authority.

#### 2.1.3 Principle 3: Engagement of People

- **Core Premise:** Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels enhance the organization's capability to create value.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. Encourage open discussion and sharing of knowledge.
  2. Promote individual and team achievements.
  3. Conduct periodic training needs analyses.
- **Shipbuilding Application:** Welder qualification certification programs (e.g., per **AWS D1.1**, **ISO 9606**); pipefitter competency matrices.

#### 2.1.4 Principle 4: Process Approach

- **Core Premise:** Consistent and predictable results are achieved more effectively when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. Define process objectives and their interaction.
  2. Assign ownership and accountability.
  3. Measure process performance (KPIs).
  4. Manage risks and opportunities.
- **Shipbuilding Application:** Block erection sequence — **steel cutting → blasting → priming → sub-assembly → assembly → erection → outfitting → launch → commissioning**.

#### 2.1.5 Principle 5: Improvement

- **Core Premise:** Successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement as a permanent objective.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. Promote improvement at all organizational levels.
  2. Use the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle.
  3. Recognize and reward improvements.
- **Shipbuilding Application:** Kaizen events on panel line productivity; reducing plate nesting waste.

#### 2.1.6 Principle 6: Evidence-Based Decision Making

- **Core Premise:** Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data are more likely to produce desired results.
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. Determine measurements needed.
  2. Ensure data is accurate, reliable, and secure.
  3. Analyze data using appropriate statistical methods.
- **Shipbuilding Application:** Using **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts to monitor welding parameters (voltage, current, travel speed).

#### 2.1.7 Principle 7: Relationship Management

- **Core Premise:** For sustained success, an organization manages its relationships with interested parties (suppliers, regulators, society).
- **Operational Steps:**
  1. Identify and select suppliers capable of delivering value.
  2. Share expertise, resources, and information.
  3. Establish joint development activities.
- **Shipbuilding Application:** Long-term agreements with steel mills for **Grade A, AH36, DH36** plates; partnerships with classification societies (**DNV**, **LR**, **ABS**, **BV**, **IRS**).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the key equations used in quality management measurement. These are **dimensionless** or **monetary** metrics, not physics-based formulas.

| Metric Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects (count); Units (count); Opportunities (count) | Welds per ship hull, pipes per system |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units Passing First Inspection}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%$ | Both terms in count | Measures fabrication quality |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | Each $\text{FPY}_{i}$ as a decimal | Multi-stage ship assembly |
| **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min \left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$ | $\mu$ = process mean; $\sigma$ = std deviation; USL, LSL = spec limits | Plate thickness tolerance control |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | All terms in monetary units (INR/USD/EUR) | Rework, scrap, warranty claims |
| **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** | $\text{CSAT} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Satisfaction Ratings}}{\text{Max Possible Score}} \times 100\%$ | Both terms in score units | Shipowner feedback |
| **Sigma Level ($\sigma_{\text{level}}$)** | $\sigma_{\text{level}} = \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}\cdot 2}\right) + 1.5$ | $\Phi^{-1}$ = inverse normal CDF; +1.5 = long-term shift | Quality benchmarking |
| **On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** | $\text{OTIF} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered OTIF}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100\%$ | Both terms in count | Vessel delivery KPI |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of QMPs in Engineering

The seven principles are not abstract philosophical statements; they directly translate into **audit-able clauses** of ISO 9001:2015. For a shipbuilding company seeking **ISO 9001 certification**, the QMS documentation must explicitly demonstrate how each principle is operationalized. Moreover, **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** audits cross-reference these principles when certifying shipyards for hull and machinery construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)

The process capability index measures how well a process conforms to its specification limits. It is derived from the assumption that process output follows a **normal distribution** $N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$.

**Step 1 — Standardize the Specification Limits**

We define the standardized distance from the process mean to each specification limit.

$$
Z_{\text{USL}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

$$
Z_{\text{LSL}} = \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 2 — Explain the Logic**

This conversion expresses the specification limits in terms of standard deviations from the mean. A larger value means the specification limit is farther from the mean, indicating a more capable process.

**Step 3 — Define $C_p$ (Process Capability, Two-Sided)**

The bilateral capability index assumes the process is centered between LSL and USL.

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for Centering — Introduce $C_{pk}$**

Since real processes are rarely perfectly centered, we take the worst-case (minimum) of the two one-sided capabilities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min \left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right) \\[6pt]
       &= \min \left( \dfrac{Z_{\text{USL}}}{3}, \dfrac{Z_{\text{LSL}}}{3} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation Thresholds**

- $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → **Excellent** (Six Sigma territory)
- $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ → **Acceptable** (industry standard)
- $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → **Marginal**
- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → **Incapable** (process exceeds specification)

### 3.2 Worked Example — Plate Thickness Control

A shipyard cuts steel plates with target thickness **$\mu = 12.0$ mm** and standard deviation **$\sigma = 0.4$ mm**. Specification limits are **LSL = 10.8 mm** and **USL = 13.2 mm**.

**Step 1 — Compute upper-side capability**

$$
C_{\text{upper}} = \dfrac{13.2 - 12.0}{3 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{1.2}{1.2} = 1.00
$$

**Step 2 — Compute lower-side capability**

$$
C_{\text{lower}} = \dfrac{12.0 - 10.8}{3 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{1.2}{1.2} = 1.00
$$

**Step 3 — Take the minimum**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.00, 1.00) = 1.00
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

A $C_{pk}$ of **1.00** indicates the process is **marginally capable**. The shipyard should investigate sources of variation (e.g., plate mill tolerance, cutting tool wear) to raise $C_{pk}$ to at least **1.33** before signing the classification society's **Material Test Certificate (MTC)** protocol.

### 3.3 Derivation of Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

For an $n$-stage process, where each stage $i$ has first-pass yield $\text{FPY}_i$, the overall throughput yield is the product of the individual yields.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= \text{FPY}_{1} \times \text{FPY}_{2} \times \cdots \times \text{FPY}_{n} \\[6pt]
          &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Explain Each Stage's Probability**

Each $\text{FPY}_i$ represents the probability that a unit passes stage $i$ without rework. Probabilities of independent stages multiply.

**Step 2 — Worked Numerical Example**

Consider a 5-stage hull block assembly:

- Stage 1 (Cutting): FPY = 0.98
- Stage 2 (Welding): FPY = 0.95
- Stage 3 (Blasting): FPY = 0.97
- Stage 4 (Painting): FPY = 0.96
- Stage 5 (Erection): FPY = 0.94

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.97 \times 0.96 \times 0.94 \\[6pt]
          &= 0.8137 \approx 81.37\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

This means roughly **18.63%** of blocks will require some rework before final acceptance. Such hidden quality costs erode shipyard profit margins, reinforcing Principle 5 — **Improvement**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Principle-to-Tool Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix maps each QMP to commonly deployed industrial tools. This is a **high-yield KTU table** that examiners frequently test.

| QMP | Industrial Tools & Frameworks | Shipbuilding Application |
|---|---|---|
| Customer Focus | VOC (Voice of Customer), Kano Model, QFD | Owner-specification workshops |
| Leadership | Hoshin Kanri, Policy Deployment | Yard-wide quality policy |
| Engagement of People | Suggestion Schemes, Skill Matrix | Welder certification ladders |
| Process Approach | SIPOC, Turtle Diagrams, BPMN | Block erection process maps |
| Improvement | PDCA, Kaizen, Six Sigma, 5S | Panel line productivity gains |
| Evidence-Based Decision Making | SPC, MSA, Hypothesis Testing | Welding parameter monitoring |
| Relationship Management | SRM (Supplier Relationship Mgmt), SLAs | Steel mill long-term agreements |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    nodeQMS --> nodeP3
    nodeQMS --> nodeP4
    nodeQMS --> nodeP5
    nodeQMS --> nodeP6
    nodeQMS --> nodeP7

    nodeP1 --> nodeP4

> [!NOTE]

In a shipyard, **Customer Focus (P1)** drives the **Process Approach (P4)**, which generates data for **Evidence-Based Decision Making (P6)**, which fuels **Improvement (P5)**, and so on.

### 4.2 The PDCA Cycle Linked to Improvement (Principle 5)
