<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three Quality Management Principles as per ISO 9000:2015 and briefly explain the "Process Approach" principle.**

**Model Answer:**

The three Quality Management Principles are:

1. **Customer Focus** — Meeting customer needs and exceeding expectations.
2. **Leadership** — Establishing unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Process Approach** — Managing activities as interrelated processes.

**Process Approach (detailed):** The process approach treats every activity as a process with inputs, controls, outputs, and feedback. In shipbuilding, hull erection can be modeled as a process where steel plates (input) are cut, welded, and assembled (transformation) to produce a hull block (output). Consistency and predictability are achieved by defining, measuring, and controlling each subprocess.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for listing, 2 marks for explanation)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define DPMO. A shipyard produces 500 hull blocks per quarter, with each block having 80 welding inspection points. If 240 weld defects are recorded, calculate the DPMO.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities) is the number of defects per million opportunities, used as a normalized measure of process quality.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
           &= \dfrac{240}{500 \times 80} \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
           &= \dfrac{240}{40{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
           &= 0.006 \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
           &= 6{,}000 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks calculation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B requires **internal choice**. Two fully-worked alternatives are provided below.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven Quality Management Principles** as per ISO 9000:2015. For each principle, give **one shipbuilding-specific example**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Principle | Shipbuilding Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Shipyard conducts quarterly shipowner feedback surveys to refine design and after-sales service. |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Yard Director issues a "Zero Rework by 2026" quality policy, allocating budget for training. |
| 3 | **Engagement of People** | Welders are certified per **ISO 9606-1** and awarded bonus incentives for defect-free welds. |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Pipe spool fabrication workflow is documented as a SIPOC (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers) diagram. |
| 5 | **Improvement** | Kaizen teams reduce panel line cycle time from 90 to 75 minutes per block. |
| 6 | **Evidence-Based Decision Making** | SPC charts on **welding current and arc voltage** trigger tool maintenance when Cp drops below 1.33. |
| 7 | **Relationship Management** | Long-term agreement with a Korean steel mill for guaranteed **AH36 plate** supply. |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per principle with example)*

**(b)** A shipbuilding process has **5 stages** with the following First Pass Yields: 0.98, 0.95, 0.97, 0.96, and 0.94. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **overall defect rate**. If management demands an RTY of **90%**, how many additional stages must achieve 100% FPY, OR by what factor must the average FPY improve? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute RTY**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.97 \times 0.96 \times 0.94 \\[6pt]
          &= 0.8137 \text{ or } 81.37\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Overall Defect Rate**

$$
\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - 0.8137 = 0.1863 \text{ or } 18.63\%
$$

**Step 3 — Set Target RTY = 0.90**

Let the average FPY be $x$ across 5 stages. Then:

$$
x^{5} = 0.90
$$

$$
x = 0.90^{1/5}
$$

$$
x = \exp\left( \dfrac{\ln(0.90)}{5} \right) = \exp\left( \dfrac{-0.10536}{5} \right) = \exp(-0.02107)
$$

$$
x \approx 0.9792 \text{ or } 97.92\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Improvement Factor**

Current average FPY:

$$
\text{Avg}_{\text{current}} = \dfrac{0.98 + 0.95 + 0.97 + 0.96 + 0.94}{5} = \dfrac{4.80}{5} = 0.960
$$

$$
\text{Improvement Factor} = \dfrac{0.9792}{0.960} \approx 1.0200
$$

Thus, the **average FPY must improve by approximately 2%** (from 96.0% to 97.92%).

*(7 marks — 2 marks RTY, 2 marks defect rate, 3 marks improvement calculation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Process Approach** with the **Inspection-Based Approach** in quality management. Why is the process approach preferred in modern shipbuilding? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Inspection-Based Approach | Process Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Detecting defects in finished product | Preventing defects at source |
| **Timing** | After production | During production |
| **Cost Impact** | High (scrap, rework, warranty) | Low (prevention cheaper than cure) |
| **Responsibility** | Quality department only | Everyone in the value chain |
| **Tools** | Sampling inspection, gauges | SPC, FMEA, control plans |
| **Philosophy** | "Find and fix" | "Build it right first time" |

**Why Preferred in Shipbuilding:**

1. **Complex Supply Chain:** Modern ships contain millions of components sourced globally; end-of-line inspection cannot catch all latent defects.
2. **Regulatory Pressure:** IACS and class societies require process-based quality records (e.g., **welding procedure qualification records — WPQR**).
3. **Cost of Failure:** A defect discovered after launch (e.g., hull plate crack) costs **100× to 1000× more** to rectify than during fabrication.
4. **Customer Expectation:** Shipowners demand traceability from raw material certificate to delivery.
5. **Continuous Improvement:** Only process-level data can drive Kaizen and Six Sigma projects.

*(7 marks — 4 marks comparison, 3 marks justification)*

**(b)** For a **plate cutting process** with target thickness **$\mu = 14$ mm**, $\sigma = 0.5$ mm, **USL = 15.5 mm**, and **LSL = 12.5 mm**, compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$**

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{15.5 - 12.5}{6 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{3.0}{3.0} = 1.00
$$

**Step 2 — Compute upper-side capability**

$$
C_{\text{upper}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{15.5 - 14.0}{3 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1.5}{1.5} = 1.00
$$

**Step 3 — Compute lower-side capability**

$$
C_{\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{14.0 - 12.5}{3 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1.5}{1.5} = 1.00
$$

**Step 4 — Take the minimum**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.00, 1.00) = 1.00
$$

**Step 5 — Comment**

- $C_p = 1.00$ indicates the **process spread exactly equals the specification spread**.
- $C_{pk} = 1.00$ indicates the process is **perfectly centered but only marginally capable**.
- The process is **at risk** of producing out-of-specification plates. **Recommendation:** Reduce $\sigma$ from 0.5 mm to ≤0.375 mm (to achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$) through better blade maintenance and incoming material inspection.

*(7 marks — 1 mark each step, 2 marks for comment and recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Misquoting $C_p$ as $C_{pk}$** (or vice versa). $C_p$ assumes centered process; $C_{pk}$ accounts for off-centering. Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\times 10^{6}$ factor** in DPMO calculations — a frequent source of error worth losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Vague answers on QMPs** — students often list principle names without shipbuilding-specific examples, losing up to 3 marks in 7-mark sub-parts. Always contextualize.
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in RTY** — forgetting that percentages must be converted to decimals before multiplying.
> 5. **Ignoring units** in metric definitions — even though dimensionless, state the metric explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Seven QMPs (ISO 9000:2015):** Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement of People, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decision Making, Relationship Management.
> - **PDCA cycle** is the operational engine of Principle 5 (Improvement).
> - **DPMO** normalizes defects across opportunities; always multiply by $10^{6}$.
> - **FPY** is a percentage; **RTY** is the product of FPYs across stages (use decimals).
> - **$C_p$** assumes centered process; **$C_{pk}$** is the minimum of upper and lower one-sided capabilities.
> - **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the industry-accepted threshold for capable processes.
> - **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
> - **Six Sigma benchmark** = **4.5σ** with **3.4 DPMO** (after 1.5σ long-term shift).
> - **IACS QSCS** audits cross-reference the seven QMPs when certifying shipyards.
> - **Always contextualize QMP answers** with shipbuilding examples — this is what distinguishes KTU high-scoring answers from generic ones.
> - **Tools by principle:** VOC/QFD/Kano (P1), Hoshin Kanri (P2), Skill Matrix (P3), SIPOC/BPMN (P4), Kaizen/PDCA/5S/Six Sigma (P5), SPC/MSA (P6), SRM/SLAs (P7).
> - **Avoid common errors:** confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$, forgetting $10^{6}$ in DPMO, using percentages in RTY multiplication, omitting shipbuilding context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Focus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Customer Focus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Customer Focus** is the **Quality Management Principle (QMP #2)** defined in **ISO 9000:2015** and **ISO 9001:2015** as: *"The primary focus of quality management is to meet customer needs and to strive to exceed customer expectations."* In the context of shipbuilding, Customer Focus is the disciplined organizational practice of identifying the **Owner's Requirements**, **Class Society Rules**, **Flag State Regulations**, **International Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC)**, and **End-User Operational Needs**, then systematically translating them into **design specifications, material selections, workmanship standards, and lifecycle support services**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Customer Focus is the foundational pillar upon which all subsequent QMS processes (Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement) are built. Without robust customer focus, the *Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)* cycle collapses at its first stage.

### 1.2 The Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015 Context)

Customer Focus is one of the **7 QMPs** mandated under ISO 9001:2015. The complete set is:

1. Customer Focus
2. Leadership
3. Engagement of People
4. Process Approach
5. Improvement
6. Evidence-Based Decision Making
7. Relationship Management

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship-as-a-Hotel" Intuition

Imagine a five-star floating hotel that you have chartered for a voyage across the Atlantic. The hotel manager (the shipyard) does not simply hand you a generic brochure. Instead, the manager sits with you (the owner) and asks:

- *How many guests will sleep aboard?* → Defines **accommodation layout**.
- *Will you serve alcohol? Will the route cross protected marine parks?* → Defines **MARPOL Annex V compliance, holding tanks**.
- *Do you need helicopter landing capability?* → Drives **helideck structural reinforcement**.
- *How many years will the ship operate before its first special survey?* → Drives **corrosion protection class** and **steel grade selection**.

This *active dialogue* before, during, and after construction is **Customer Focus**. It is **not** passive acceptance of an order — it is the proactive co-creation of value.

### 1.4 Core Metrics & Standard References

| Metric / Standard | Definition | Application in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | Quantified measure of customer perception | Post-delivery owner surveys |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | Likelihood of recommendation (0–10 scale) | Naval fleet owner feedback |
| **Conformance to Specification (CTS)** | % of build items meeting approved drawings | Hull, piping, electrical |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | % of milestones hit as per contract | Keel-laying → Launching → Delivery |
| **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.1.2** | "Focus on consistently providing products that meet customer requirements" | Top management responsibility |
| **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.2** | "Requirements for products and services" | Tender → Contract review process |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In shipbuilding, the "customer" is often a **dual-layered entity**: the **Direct Customer** (the Shipowner/Operator who signs the contract) and the **Indirect Customer** (the Passengers, Crew, Flag State Inspectors, Classification Surveyors, and ultimately, the Environment). A robust Quality Management System must address both layers simultaneously.

### 1.5 Why Customer Focus is Critical in Shipbuilding

The shipbuilding industry has unique characteristics that amplify the importance of Customer Focus:

- **Long Build Cycles:** A vessel may take 18–36 months from contract to delivery. Requirements can drift during this period.
- **High Capital Intensity:** A single ship costs anywhere from **$20 million to $500+ million**. Mistakes are extremely expensive.
- **Safety-Critical Outputs:** Lives depend on the quality of welding, watertight integrity, and fire safety systems.
- **Regulatory Complexity:** Compliance with **IACS Unified Requirements**, **SOLAS**, **MARPOL**, and **MLC** is non-negotiable.
- **Globalized Supply Chain:** Components come from dozens of countries; traceability of customer requirements must be preserved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Customer Focus in QMS

#### Pillar 1 — Understanding Customer Needs
The organization must identify **current and future** needs. In shipbuilding, this translates to:
- Reviewing the **Owner's Specification** (the "Yellow Book" or technical specification).
- Consulting **Class Society Rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, KR, IRS).
- Engaging **End-User Departments**: Naval Architecture, Marine Engineering, Electrical, Interior Outfitting.

#### Pillar 2 — Communicating Customer Requirements Internally
Customer requirements must be cascaded from the **Top Management** down to the **Welder on the shop floor** through:
- **Documented information** (drawings, work instructions, ITPs).
- **Training and competency matrices**.
- **Kick-off meetings** for each work package.

#### Pillar 3 — Measuring Customer Satisfaction
This is the quantitative backbone. Methods include:
- **Surveys** (Likert scale 1–5 or 1–7).
- **Complaint tracking** (NCRs — Non-Conformance Reports).
- **Warranty claims** analysis.
- **Repeat business ratio**.

#### Pillar 4 — Managing Customer Relationships
- Designated **Key Account Managers** (Shipbuilding has dedicated *Project Managers* per vessel).
- Customer portals providing real-time progress (e.g., **CIMS — Computer Integrated Manufacturing Systems**).
- **Service after sales**: Post-delivery support, dry-docking assistance, spare parts.

#### Pillar 5 — Acting on Customer Feedback
The **PDCA cycle** must close the loop:
- **Plan:** Establish objectives based on customer feedback.
- **Do:** Implement changes (e.g., revised welding procedure).
- **Check:** Measure effectiveness via repeat surveys.
- **Act:** Standardize improvements into the QMS.

### 2.2 The Voice of Customer (VoC) Capture Process

The **Voice of Customer (VoC)** is the structured methodology to capture customer needs. The hierarchical decomposition is:

**VoC → CTQ (Critical to Quality) → KPOV (Key Process Output Variables)**

**Example Walkthrough:**
1. **VoC:** "The vessel must operate in arctic conditions."
2. **CTQ:** Hull steel must withstand -40°C without brittle fracture.
3. **KPOV:** Steel grade **EH36** with Charpy V-notch impact tested at **-40°C ≥ 34 J**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $\text{CSI} = \left( \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}{n \cdot R_{max}} \right) \times 100$ | $R_i$ = individual response, $R_{max}$ = max scale, $n$ = sample size | **%** | Range: 0–100 |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | $P$ = Promoters (9–10), $D$ = Detractors (0–6) | **Score (-100 to +100)** | Industry benchmark: >50 excellent |
| **Conformance Rate** | $\text{CR} = \left( \dfrac{N_{conforming}}{N_{total}} \right) \times 100$ | $N_{conforming}$ = items passing, $N_{total}$ = items inspected | **%** | Applied to weld joints, pipe spools |
| **Customer Complaint Rate (CCR)** | $\text{CCR} = \dfrac{C_{complaints}}{N_{delivered}} \times 1000$ | $C_{complaints}$ = number of complaints, $N_{delivered}$ = vessels delivered | **‰ (per mille)** | Lower is better |
| **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{started}} \times 100$ | $N_{passed}$ = passed first time, $N_{started}$ = started | **%** | Used in block fabrication |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{N_{on\_time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{on\_time}$ = on-time milestones, $N_{total}$ = total milestones | **%** | Keel lay, launch, delivery dates |
| **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | $\text{CES} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i$ | $E_i$ = individual effort rating (1–7) | **Score (1–7)** | Lower = less effort = better |
| **Retention Rate** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{C_{end} - C_{new}}{C_{start}} \times 100$ | $C$ = customers | **%** | Repeat-order indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to compute CSI or NPS in an exam, always state the **scale used**, **sample size**, and **interpretation range**. Marks are awarded for these contextual notes, not just the arithmetic.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

Customer Focus metrics are used as:
- **Predictive indicators of contract renewal** (shipyards track NPS to forecast next-order book).
- **Audit evidence** for ISO 9001:2015 surveillance audits.
- **Risk registers** — low CSI scores trigger **management review** corrective actions.
- **Benchmarking tools** comparing shipyards across Korea, Japan, China, and Europe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A shipyard conducted a post-delivery survey of **20 shipowners**. Each owner rated overall satisfaction on a **5-point Likert scale** (1 = Highly Dissatisfied, 5 = Highly Satisfied). The responses were:

**4, 5, 3, 5, 4, 2, 5, 4, 4, 3, 5, 4, 5, 4, 3, 4, 5, 4, 3, 5**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

From the problem statement:
- Sample size: $n = 20$
- Maximum possible rating on scale: $R_{max} = 5$
- Individual responses: $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_{20}$ as listed above.

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of all responses.**

We tally the frequencies first:

| Rating | Frequency |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 4 |
| 4 | 8 |
| 5 | 7 |
| **Total** | **20** |

Now compute the sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{20} R_i &= (2 \times 1) + (3 \times 4) + (4 \times 8) + (5 \times 7) \\
&= 2 + 12 + 32 + 35 \\
&= 81
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum possible sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \cdot R_{max} &= 20 \times 5 \\
&= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the CSI formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSI} &= \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}{n \cdot R_{max}} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \frac{81}{100} \right) \times 100 \\
&= 81\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

A CSI of **81%** is generally classified as **"Good"** in the shipbuilding industry benchmark (typically: <60% Poor, 60–75% Satisfactory, 75–85% Good, >85% Excellent).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Net Promoter Score (NPS) Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A shipyard surveyed **150 vessel operators** about whether they would recommend the yard to other operators. The breakdown:
- **Promoters** (rated 9 or 10): **78 operators**
- **Passives** (rated 7 or 8): **52 operators**
- **Detractors** (rated 0 to 6): **20 operators**

**Step 1 — Validate the count.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total respondents} &= 78 + 52 + 20 \\
&= 150 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert counts to percentages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%P &= \left( \frac{78}{150} \right) \times 100 = 52\% \\
\%D &= \left( \frac{20}{150} \right) \times 100 = 13.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the NPS formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= \%P - \%D \\
&= 52 - 13.33 \\
&= 38.67 \\
&\approx 39
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Industry benchmarks for shipbuilding NPS:

| NPS Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| < 0 | Critical — Reputational damage |
| 0 to 30 | Acceptable |
| 30 to 50 | Good |
| 50 to 70 | Excellent |
| > 70 | World-Class |

An NPS of **39** places the shipyard in the **"Good"** category.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Conformance Rate in Pipe Spool Fabrication

**Problem Statement:** During a class survey, **240 pipe spools** were inspected. **218** passed dimensional and NDT checks on the first inspection.

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{conforming} &= 218 \\
N_{total} &= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Conformance Rate formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= \left( \frac{218}{240} \right) \times 100 \\
&= 90.83\% \\
&\approx 90.83\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering decision.**

A conformance rate of **90.83%** means **9.17%** of pipe spools required rework. The yard should investigate via **Pareto analysis** whether these non-conformances cluster around specific welders, materials, or joint types — directly feeding back into Customer Focus improvement actions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Linking Customer Voices to Shipbuilding Artifacts

| Customer Voice (VoC) | CTQ (Critical to Quality) | Measurable Metric (KPOV) | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Vessel must operate in polar waters" | Hull steel resists brittle fracture | Charpy energy $\geq 34\text{ J}$ at $-40^\circ\text{C}$ | Charpy V-notch test per **ASTM A370** |
| "Cargo hold must remain dry for 25 years" | Watertight integrity | Zero leakage under hose test | Hose test per **IACS S21** |
| "Engine must meet IMO Tier III" | NOx emissions within limits | NOx $\leq 3.4 \text{ g/kWh}$ | EIAPP certificate & onboard testing |
| "Crew comfort in heavy seas" | Stabilizer effectiveness | Roll reduction $\geq 60\%$ | Sea trial measurement |
| "Fast turnaround at port" | Crane cycle time | $\leq 45$ seconds per cycle | Harbor acceptance test |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **KTU high-yield concept**. Examiners frequently test a student's ability to trace a customer requirement from a *subjective statement* down to a *measurable engineering parameter*. This is the **House of Quality (HoQ)** concept from **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Customer Focus Process Flow in a Shipyard (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipowner Requirement Origin] --> B[Contract Review & Risk Assessment]
    B --> C[VoC Capture & Documentation]
    C --> D[CrossFunctional Translation Team]
    D --> E[Design Specification & Class Rules]
    E --> F[Material Procurement & Vendor Qualification]
    F --> G[Production Planning & Work Packaging]
    G --> H[Fabrication & Assembly with ITP]
    H --> I[Class Survey & Internal Inspection]
    I --> J{Quality Gate Passed?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Sea Trial & HAT]
    J -->|No| L[NCR Issued Corrective Action]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Vessel Delivery & Owner Handover]
    M --> N[PostDelivery Survey CSI Calculation]
    N --> O{CSI meets Target?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Update QMS Best Practices]
    O -->|No| Q[Management Review & CAPA]
    Q --> R[Process Improvement]
    R --> A
    P --> A
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The diagram is a **closed-loop PDCA cycle** anchored at nodes A and R.
- The decision diamonds **J** and **O** are quality gates.
- Node **L** (NCR) is the **rework loop**, which is expected and managed.
- The output of node **N** drives **continuous improvement** back into the next contract cycle.

---

### 4.2 Customer Stakeholder Hierarchy in Shipbuilding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary Customer Direct Owner] --> A1[Shipowner Operator]
    A --> A2[Charterer Lessee]
    B[Secondary Customer End User] --> B1[Crew Onboard Personnel]
    B --> B2[Passengers Cruise Vessels]
    C[Tertiary Customer Regulator] --> C1[Flag State Administration]
    C --> C2[Classification Society]
    C --> C3[Port State Control]
    D[Quaternary Customer Society] --> D1[Environmental Stakeholders]
    D --> D2[Insurance Underwriters]
    D --> D3[Financing Banks]

    A1 --> Z[Shipyard QMS Must Satisfy ALL]
    A2 --> Z
    B1 --> Z
    B2 --> Z
    C1 --> Z
    C2 --> Z
    C3 --> Z
    D1 --> Z
    D2 --> Z
    D3 --> Z
```

**Interpretation:** The shipyard does not serve a *single* customer. The QMS must demonstrate compliance with a **multi-tier stakeholder map**, each with distinct (and sometimes conflicting) requirements. Effective Customer Focus means prioritizing and balancing these tiers without breaching regulatory floors.

---

### 4.3 Customer Feedback → QMS Improvement (Cause-and-Effect Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Survey Response] --> B[Data Aggregation]
    B --> C[Statistical Analysis Pareto]
    C --> D[Root Cause Investigation 5 Whys Fishbone]
    D --> E[Corrective Action Plan CAPA]
    E --> F[Process Modification]
    F --> G[QMS Document Update]
    G --> H[Training of Workforce]
    H --> I[Implementation in Next Contract]
    I --> J[Re survey & Verification]
    J --> K{Meets Target?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Standardize Best Practice]
    K -->|No| D
    L --> A
```

**Note:** This is a **feedback control loop**. If verification fails, the system **re-enters root cause analysis** rather than terminating — embodying the principle of **continual improvement (ISO 9001:2015 Clause 10)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: Define Customer Focus as a Quality Management Principle. Why is it particularly critical in the shipbuilding industry?**

**Model Answer:**

Customer Focus is one of the seven Quality Management Principles defined in **ISO 9001:2015**, requiring the organization to identify current and future customer needs, meet customer requirements, and strive to exceed customer expectations.

In shipbuilding, it is critical because:
1. **Long build cycles (18–36 months)** allow requirements to drift; continuous customer engagement prevents misalignment.
2. **High capital intensity** ($20M–$500M+ per vessel) means rework costs are enormous.
3. **Safety-critical nature** — a defect can cost lives at sea.
4. **Multi-stakeholder complexity** — Direct owners, end-users, regulators, and classification societies all impose requirements.
5. **Regulatory non-compliance** leads to vessel detention, fines, and reputational damage.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: List and briefly explain any three methods used to measure customer satisfaction in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Customer Satisfaction Surveys:** Structured questionnaires (e.g., Likert scale) administered post-delivery. They yield quantitative metrics like the **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)**.

2. **Net Promoter Score (NPS):** Measures the percentage of customers who would recommend the shipyard to peers. Calculated as $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$.

3. **Non-Conformance Reports (NCRs) and Warranty Claims:** Tracking post-delivery defects and warranty interventions provides an objective indicator of in-service customer dissatisfaction.

4. **Repeat Order Ratio:** The percentage of contracts awarded by returning customers. This is a strong leading indicator of satisfaction.

5. **Customer Effort Score (CES):** Measures how easy it is for customers to interact with the shipyard during build (e.g., responding to RFIs).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write vague answers like *"Customer focus is important because customers matter."* Examiners deduct up to **4 marks** for generic statements. Always tie answers to **shipbuilding-specific evidence** (long build cycles, classification society rules, weld quality, etc.).
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse Customer Focus with Customer Service. **Customer Focus is a strategic QMS principle; Customer Service is a tactical post-sale activity.**
> 3. **Always show the formula, substitution, and final numerical answer** in calculation questions. Marks are split: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer.
> 4. **Cite ISO 9001:2015 clauses** wherever applicable — examiners reward this with bonus marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Choice A]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Voice of Customer (VoC)** methodology as applied to shipbuilding. Using an example of an arctic-class vessel, demonstrate how a customer's subjective requirement is decomposed into a measurable engineering specification. Include in your answer the concepts of **CTQ (Critical to Quality)** and **KPOV (Key Process Output Variable)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard surveyed **120 shipowners** post-delivery on a **7-point satisfaction scale**. The responses were:

- **Rating 7:** 30 owners
- **Rating 6:** 45 owners
- **Rating 5:** 25 owners
- **Rating 4:** 12 owners
- **Rating 3:** 5 owners
- **Rating 2:** 2 owners
- **Rating 1:** 1 owner

Calculate the **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** and interpret the result using the standard benchmark.

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(a):**

The **Voice of Customer (VoC)** is a structured process for capturing the customer's stated and unstated needs, translating them into actionable specifications. In shipbuilding, VoC is captured through:

1. **Contract Review** — reviewing the Owner's Technical Specification.
2. **Kick-off Meetings** — joint sessions with the owner's technical team.
3. **Design Review Iterations** — basic design, contract design, and detailed design reviews.
4. **Class and Flag State Consultations** — verifying regulatory expectations.
5. **Operational Feedback Loops** — feedback from crew and charterers.

**Decomposition for an Arctic-Class Vessel:**

**Step 1 — Capture VoC:**
The shipowner states: *"We need this vessel to operate year-round in the Northern Sea Route, even during winter."*

**Step 2 — Identify CTQ (Critical to Quality):**
The CTQ is: *"The hull structure must withstand extreme low temperatures without brittle fracture."*

**Step 3 — Translate to Engineering Specification:**
- Material: **EH36 grade steel** (per **IACS UR S6**).
- Charpy V-notch impact test at **−40°C** with minimum energy absorption of **34 J**.

**Step 4 — Define KPOV (Key Process Output Variable):**
The measurable KPOV is: *"Charpy impact energy value ≥ 34 J at −40°C for every heat of EH36 steel supplied."*

**Step 5 — Verification Method:**
- **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** review per **EN 10204 3.2**.
- Independent lab testing for each heat.
- Welding procedure qualification record (**WPQR**) tested at the same low temperature.

This VoC → CTQ → KPOV chain is the **House of Quality** principle from **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**.

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

From the problem:
- Sample size: $n = 120$
- Maximum rating: $R_{max} = 7$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of all responses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{120} R_i &= (7 \times 30) + (6 \times 45) + (5 \times 25) + (4 \times 12) + (3 \times 5) + (2 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) \\
&= 210 + 270 + 125 + 48 + 15 + 4 + 1 \\
&= 673
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum possible sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \cdot R_{max} &= 120 \times 7 \\
&= 840
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the CSI formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSI} &= \left( \frac{\sum R_i}{n \cdot R_{max}} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \frac{673}{840} \right) \times 100 \\
&= 80.12\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

| CSI Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| < 60% | Poor |
| 60–75% | Satisfactory |
| 75–85% | Good |
| > 85% | Excellent |

A CSI of **80.12%** falls in the **"Good"** category, indicating that the shipyard meets customer expectations but has scope to improve toward the "Excellent" tier.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Choice B]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **seven Quality Management Principles (QMPs)** of ISO 9001:2015, with special emphasis on how **Customer Focus (QMP #1)** interacts with **Leadership (QMP #2)**, **Process Approach (QMP #4)**, and **Relationship Management (QMP #7)** in the context of a shipbuilding QMS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard delivered **48 vessels** over the year. During the warranty period, **36 complaint tickets** were raised by owners. Compute the **Customer Complaint Rate (CCR) per 1000 vessels** and recommend a **Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA)** strategy.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(a):**

The **seven QMPs of ISO 9001:2015** are:

1. **Customer Focus**
2. **Leadership**
3. **Engagement of People**
4. **Process Approach**
5. **Improvement**
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making**
7. **Relationship Management**

**Interaction Analysis in a Shipbuilding QMS:**

- **Customer Focus ↔ Leadership (QMP #1 ↔ QMP #2):** Top management must establish the **Quality Policy** aligned with customer expectations. The **Managing Director** of a shipyard sets the tone by publicly committing to customer satisfaction during the **annual management review**. Without leadership commitment, customer focus becomes a hollow slogan.

- **Customer Focus ↔ Process Approach (QMP #1 ↔ QMP #4):** Customer requirements are converted into **interconnected processes** — Design → Procurement → Fabrication → Assembly → Commissioning → Delivery → After-Sales. Each process has measurable **inputs, outputs, KPIs, and owners**. The **Process Approach** ensures customer focus is operationalized into daily work, not just stated in policy.

- **Customer Focus ↔ Relationship Management (QMP #1 ↔ QMP #7):** Relationship management extends customer focus beyond the shipowner to **suppliers, classification societies, and flag states**. A shipyard cannot deliver customer satisfaction if its steel supplier delivers defective plates. Thus, customer focus is sustained by a **network of trusted partnerships**.

The synergy of these principles forms the foundation of a **robust, customer-centric shipbuilding QMS**.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{complaints} &= 36 \\
N_{delivered} &= 48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CCR formula (per 1000 vessels).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCR} &= \frac{C_{complaints}}{N_{delivered}} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{36}{48} \times 1000 \\
&= 750 \text{ complaints per 1000 vessels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

A CCR of **750 per 1000 vessels** is **significantly elevated**. Industry benchmark for shipbuilding is typically **< 300 per 1000**. This indicates systemic issues requiring structured CAPA.

**Step 4 — Recommended CAPA Strategy.**

| CAPA Phase | Action |
|---|---|
| **Containment (Corrective)** | Immediate recall/repair of affected systems; halt similar work in progress. |
| **Root Cause Analysis** | Apply **5-Whys** and **Ishikawa Fishbone** across Man, Machine, Material, Method, Environment, Measurement. |
| **Corrective Action** | Modify the offending process (e.g., revise welding procedure, retrain welders, requalify supplier). |
| **Preventive Action** | Update the QMS documents, inspection test plans (ITPs), and competency matrices. |
| **Verification** | Re-measure CCR over the next 12 months; target reduction to **< 300 per 1000**. |
| **Management Review** | Present findings at the next **management review meeting** for top management endorsement. |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:**

- Customer Focus is **QMP #1 of ISO 9001:2015** (some references list it as #2 depending on ordering convention — check the exact QMP sequence in your textbook).
- Customer Focus is **not the same** as Customer Service — one is strategic, the other is tactical.
- Always cite **ISO 9001:2015 Clauses 5.1.2 and 8.2** in exam answers for bonus marks.
- Key formulas (memorize verbatim):
  - $\text{CSI} = \left( \dfrac{\sum R_i}{n \cdot R_{max}} \right) \times 100$
  - $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$
  - $\text{CR} = \left( \dfrac{N_{conforming}}{N_{total}} \right) \times 100$
  - $\text{CCR} = \dfrac{C_{complaints}}{N_{delivered}} \times 1000$
- The **VoC → CTQ → KPOV** chain is tested frequently — practice with at least **three shipbuilding examples**.
- Shipbuilding has a **multi-tier customer hierarchy**: Direct Owner → End User → Regulator → Society. Always mention this in essays.
- **PDCA** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) underpins customer focus implementation.
- The **House of Quality (HoQ)** from **QFD (Quality Function Deployment)** is the canonical tool for translating customer needs into specifications.
- Common **NCRs** in shipbuilding traced back to weak customer focus: missed weld specs, wrong material grade, dimensional non-conformance, late regulatory submissions.
- Always state **assumptions, scale, sample size, and benchmark** when solving numerical problems.
- Remember that the **customer is the ultimate judge of quality** — not the shipyard, not the classification society, not the flag state. The yard's quality is only "proven" when the customer signs the delivery protocol without dispute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leadership
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Leadership

# Leadership in Quality Assurance — A Foundational Study

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Leadership):**
> *Leadership* is the ability of an individual, group, or organization to **influence, guide, direct, and motivate** others toward the achievement of clearly defined goals. In the specific context of **Quality Assurance (QA) in shipbuilding**, leadership is the practice of establishing a unifying quality vision, building commitment across multi-disciplinary shipyard teams (designers, welders, fitters, inspectors, project managers), and ensuring that every plate, pipe, and process conforms to classification society rules (e.g., **IRS, DNV, LR, ABS**) and ISO 9001:2015 requirements.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Shipyard Captain":**
> Imagine a Panamax bulk carrier being constructed at a shipyard. The vessel has a **Classification Surveyor** who certifies the steel. Imagine the ship itself is the "quality outcome" and the shipyard is the "organization." The **Captain of the ship** does not personally weld every plate or paint every hull section, but he:
> 1. Defines the **voyage route** (sets the quality vision/policy).
> 2. Steers the **wheel** to keep the vessel on course (monitors KPIs, audits, NCRs).
> 3. Inspires the **crew** to give their best, even during a storm (manages a culture of continuous improvement during design changes or rework pressure).
> 
> In the same way, a **Quality Leader in shipbuilding** does not personally tighten every bolt — he aligns the *people*, *processes*, and *standards* so that quality is **built-in**, not inspected-in.

### Core Components of Leadership in a QA Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Pillars of QA Leadership (KTU Board Exam Favorite):**
> 1. **Vision** — Articulating the quality policy (e.g., zero-defect hull delivery).
> 2. **Influence** — Persuading cross-functional teams to follow QMS procedures.
> 3. **Accountability** — Owning the outcomes of audits, CAPA, and customer satisfaction.
> 4. **Transformation** — Driving a cultural shift from reactive inspection to proactive prevention.

| Term | Standard Reference | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **QMS** | ISO 9001:2015, Clause 5 | Leadership & Commitment requirement |
| **Quality Policy** | ISO 9001:2015, Clause 5.2 | Established by top management |
| **PDCA Cycle** | Deming Cycle | Used by leaders to drive improvement |
| **CAPA** | Corrective & Preventive Action | Leadership-owned escalation tool |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Leadership vs. Management — The Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners *frequently* set 7-mark questions contrasting **Leadership** and **Management**. Memorize this contrast.

- **Management** is about *controlling complexity* — scheduling the block erection, allocating welders, maintaining drawings register.
- **Leadership** is about *coping with change* — adopting new IACS standards, introducing robotic welding, transitioning to digital twin inspections.

### 2.2 Major Leadership Theories (KTU High-Yield)

The following are the **5 most-tested leadership theories** in KTU Quality Management papers:

#### A. Trait Theory (1930s–1940s)
- **Premise:** Leaders are *born*, not made. They possess innate traits.
- **Key Traits:** Self-confidence, integrity, intelligence, decisiveness, emotional stability.
- **Criticism in QA context:** Ignores situational demands (e.g., a leader in a dry-dock QA crisis needs different traits than one in a design office).

#### B. Behavioral Theory (Ohio State & Michigan Studies, 1940s–1950s)
- **Premise:** Leadership can be *learned* through observable behaviors.
- **Two Dimensions Identified:**
  - **Initiating Structure** (task-oriented): e.g., enforcing WPS, monitoring weld inspection logs.
  - **Consideration** (people-oriented): e.g., mentoring QC inspectors, recognizing good workmanship.

#### C. Contingency / Situational Theory (Fiedler, 1967; Hersey-Blanchard, 1969)
- **Premise:** There is **no single best style**. Effectiveness depends on:
  - Leader–member relations.
  - Task structure (highly structured in shipbuilding — class rules).
  - Position power.
- **Hersey-Blanchard Four Styles:**
  1. **Telling** (S1) — High task, low relationship. (Used with new QC trainees).
  2. **Selling** (S2) — High task, high relationship. (Used during new ISO rollout).
  3. **Participating** (S3) — Low task, high relationship. (Used with experienced inspectors).
  4. **Delegating** (S4) — Low task, low relationship. (Used with autonomous quality teams).

#### D. Transformational Leadership (Bass, 1985)
- **Premise:** Leaders inspire followers to *exceed* expectations by raising awareness of higher-order goals.
- **4 I's Framework:**
  1. **Idealized Influence** — Acts as role model (e.g., Chief QA walking the shipyard floor).
  2. **Inspirational Motivation** — Articulates a compelling quality vision.
  3. **Intellectual Stimulation** — Encourages innovation in NDT methods.
  4. **Individualized Consideration** — Coaches each team member.

#### E. Servant Leadership (Greenleaf, 1970)
- **Premise:** The leader serves *first* and leads *second*. Quality is achieved by empowering workers.
- **Highly relevant** in modern shipbuilding TQM culture (cf. Toyota Production System adapted in Hyundai Heavy Industries).

### 2.3 Leadership Styles in Quality Assurance

| Style | Decision-Making | Best Use Case in Shipbuilding | Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Autocratic** | Centralized | Emergency weld defect rectification at sea-trial | Low morale, suppresses RCA reporting |
| **Democratic** | Consultative | Setting annual quality objectives | Slow in crisis |
| **Laissez-faire** | Decentralized | R&D on new composite hull materials | Lack of direction, missed class deadlines |
| **Bureaucratic** | Rule-based | Statutory compliance with SOLAS, MARPOL | Inflexible to innovation |
| **Charismatic** | Visionary | Turnaround of a failing QA department | Over-dependence on one person |
| **Transactional** | Reward–punishment | Achieving monthly weld inspection KPIs | No long-term culture building |
| **Transformational** | Inspirational | Industry 4.0 / digital QA transformation | May neglect short-term deadlines |

### 2.4 KTU Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Matrix for Board Exams**

| Concept | Originator | Core Idea | One-Line Recall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trait Theory | Stogdill | Leaders are born | "Born with charisma." |
| Behavioral | Ohio State | Leaders are made | "Task vs People." |
| Contingency | Fiedler | Style depends on situation | "It depends." |
| Situational | Hersey–Blanchard | Adapt to follower maturity | "Match follower readiness." |
| Transformational | Bass | Inspire beyond expectations | "Raise the bar." |
| Servant | Greenleaf | Serve first, lead second | "Lead by serving." |
| Path-Goal | House | Clear path to goals | "Remove roadblocks." |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipbuilding, leadership directly determines:
- **Defect Density** (number of NCRs per ton of steel).
- **Rework Cost** (typically 4–6% of contract value in merchant ships).
- **Delivery Timeliness** (leadership on critical path activities like block erection and outfitting).
- **Customer Satisfaction Index** (post-delivery feedback from ship-owner).

A weak QA leader produces a ship full of punch-list items; a strong QA leader produces a *right-first-time* vessel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Derivation of Leadership Effectiveness in QA

### 3.1 The Leadership Effectiveness Equation (Conceptual Derivation)

Although leadership is qualitative, KTU examiners appreciate a **structured model**. We can derive a Leadership Effectiveness Index (LEI) for QA:

$$\text{LEI} = f(V, C, S, A, M)$$

where each variable is defined below.

Step 1 — **Define the variables.**
- $V$ = Quality Vision clarity (0–10 scale).
- $C$ = Communication effectiveness (0–10).
- $S$ = Situational adaptability (0–10).
- $A$ = Accountability & authority (0–10).
- $M$ = Motivation & morale index (0–10).

Step 2 — **Combine using a multiplicative model** (because weakness in any one variable collapses overall effectiveness):

$$
\text{LEI} = \frac{V \cdot C \cdot S \cdot A \cdot M}{10^5} \times 100\%
$$

Step 3 — **Interpret the result.**
- $\text{LEI} \geq 80\%$ → **High-Performance QA Leadership** (right-first-time ships, ISO 9001 certified, low NCR backlog).
- $50\% \leq \text{LEI} < 80\%$ → **Average** (acceptable but reactive quality).
- $\text{LEI} < 50\%$ → **Ineffective** (high rework, missed class surveys, customer complaints).

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Illustration:**
> A shipyard Quality Manager has $V=9$, $C=7$, $S=8$, $A=9$, $M=6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LEI} &= \frac{9 \times 7 \times 8 \times 9 \times 6}{10^5} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{27216}{100000} \times 100\% \\
&= 27.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Despite a strong vision and authority, weak motivation ($M=6$) and communication ($C=7$) drag down overall effectiveness to *only 27.22%* — the yard should invest in training and recognition programs.

### 3.2 The Path-Goal Model — Step-by-Step Application to a Shipyard QA Scenario

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Path-Goal Theory (House, 1971)** states that a leader's job is to clarify the path to goal achievement. The leader adopts **4 behaviors** based on subordinate and environmental contingency.

**Step-by-Step Application:**

**Step 1 — Identify subordinate characteristics.**  
Workers in the QA weld inspection team: experienced but fatigued from 6-day weeks, low morale.

**Step 2 — Identify environmental contingencies.**  
Tight delivery deadline, complex LNG carrier piping class rules.

**Step 3 — Choose leader behavior.**
- Since workers are **demotivated** → adopt **Supportive Behavior**: show concern, reduce stress, acknowledge good weld passes.
- Since the **task is ambiguous and technically complex** → adopt **Achievement-Oriented Behavior**: set challenging but attainable quality targets (e.g., 95% first-pass weld acceptance).

**Step 4 — Implement and monitor.**
- Hold weekly "Quality Kaizen" huddles.
- Display weld defect Pareto charts on shop-floor boards.

**Step 5 — Re-evaluate.**  
Use LEI formula to re-measure after 3 months.

### 3.3 Deming's 14-Point Leadership Principles (Adapted for Shipbuilding)

W. Edwards Deming's philosophy, while a TQM framework, is fundamentally about **leadership**:

| Deming Point | Shipyard QA Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. Create constancy of purpose | Long-term commitment to defect-free hull delivery |
| 2. Adopt the new philosophy | Reject the "acceptable defect rate" mindset |
| 3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality | Build quality into welding procedures, not just inspect afterward |
| 4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone | Choose suppliers and subcontractors on quality track record |
| 5. Improve constantly every process | Kaizen in pipe-bending, blasting, and painting |
| 6. Institute training on the job | Continuous WPS/NDT training |
| 7. Institute leadership | Replace supervision with true leadership |
| 8. Drive out fear | Encourage reporting of near-misses and NCRs |
| 9. Break down barriers between departments | Integrate design–production–QA silos |
| 10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations | Replace "Zero Defects" posters with systemic fixes |
| 11. Eliminate numerical quotas | Don't push welders to meet speed at the cost of quality |
| 12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship | Respect the welder as a craftsman |
| 13. Institute a vigorous programme of education | Cross-skill QC staff |
| 14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation | Top-down + bottom-up QA culture |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Leadership Styles — Decision Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipyard QA Manager Faces a Decision] --> B{Is it a Crisis?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Autocratic Style]
    B -- No --> D{Is Team Experienced?}
    D -- No --> E[Tell and Sell S1 S2]
    D -- Yes --> F{Is the Task Creative?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Participating S3]
    F -- No --> H{Is the Team Autonomous?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Delegating S4]
    H -- No --> J[Selling S2]
    C --> K[Execute and Document]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Review via LEI Formula]
    L --> M{LEI greater than 80 percent}
    M -- Yes --> N[Maintain Style]
    M -- No --> O[Recalibrate Leadership Approach]
```

```mermaid

        A1[Integrity and Ethics] --> A2[Quality Policy Alignment]

    T -- Yes --> U[Directive Behavior]
    T -- No --> V[Supportive Behavior]

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

#### **Q1. Define leadership. How is it different from management?**
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Leadership is the ability to influence a group of individuals toward the achievement of goals. In a shipbuilding QA context, it means driving the entire shipyard team toward defect-free ship delivery.

Difference:
- **Leadership** focuses on *setting direction, aligning people, and motivating change*.
- **Management** focuses on *planning, organizing, and controlling* complex processes like block erection and outfitting schedules.

A Quality Manager must perform **both** — manage processes (planning inspections, scheduling audits) **and** lead people (inspire a culture of right-first-time quality).

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for distinction.)*

---

#### **Q2. State any three leadership styles. Mention one quality-assurance context where each is suitable.**
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Autocratic** — Suitable during emergency rectification of a major weld defect just before sea trials.
2. **Democratic** — Suitable when establishing the annual quality objectives with the cross-functional QA team.
3. **Transformational** — Suitable when driving the shipyard's digital transformation to Industry 4.0 smart quality inspection.

*(Award: 1 mark per correct style + context.)*

---

### **PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Model Paper Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the major leadership theories with reference to their suitability in a shipbuilding quality-assurance environment.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major leadership theories and their QA suitability are:

1. **Trait Theory** — Posits leaders possess innate qualities. *Application:* A Chief QA officer naturally needs integrity, technical knowledge, and decisiveness to certify class compliance. *Limitation:* Ignores situation; a great office leader may fail in a noisy fabrication shop.

2. **Behavioral Theory** — Focuses on what leaders do (task vs. people). *Application:* A QA supervisor showing **Initiating Structure** ensures welders follow WPS; showing **Consideration** boosts morale and NCR reporting.

3. **Contingency / Fiedler Theory** — Style must match situation. *Application:* When the task (class rules) is highly structured and leader has high power, a task-oriented style works best for routine inspection.

4. **Situational Theory (Hersey-Blanchard)** — Leader adapts to follower maturity. *Application:* New QC trainees need **Telling** (S1); experienced inspectors need **Delegating** (S4).

5. **Transformational Theory** — Inspires beyond expectations. *Application:* A visionary QA leader can drive the shipyard from 6% rework cost to under 1% in three years through the 4 I's.

6. **Servant Leadership** — Puts workers first. *Application:* Modern shipyards adopting TPS/Lean benefit from servant-style leaders who empower welders to stop the line on defect.

**Conclusion:** No single theory fully explains shipyard QA leadership. A *blended* approach — transformational vision with situational flexibility — works best in modern shipbuilding.

*(Award: 1 mark per theory with application, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** *Discuss Deming's 14 Points as a leadership framework for achieving quality in shipbuilding.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Dr. W. Edwards Deming's 14 Points are essentially a **leadership manifesto** for quality. Their application to shipbuilding is given below.

- **Point 1 — Constancy of Purpose:** The yard commits to long-term defect-free delivery, not short-term profit from rushed ships.
- **Point 2 — Adopt the New Philosophy:** Top management rejects the "industry-acceptable defect rate" mindset.
- **Point 3 — Cease dependence on inspection:** Quality is *built-in* to the welding procedure specification (WPS), not bolted on by 100% MPI.
- **Point 4 — End lowest-price purchasing:** Steel plates and pipes are sourced from mills with proven quality history.
- **Point 5 — Improve constantly:** Kaizen events on pipe-bending, blasting, painting, outfitting.
- **Point 6 — Institute training:** Continuous WPS, NDT, and class-rule training for welders and inspectors.
- **Point 7 — Institute leadership:** Supervisors become *coaches*, not policemen.
- **Point 8 — Drive out fear:** Workers freely report near-misses without blame.
- **Point 9 — Break down silos:** Design, production, and QA integrate via concurrent engineering.
- **Point 10 — Eliminate slogans:** No "Zero Defects" posters; instead, fix the welding fixture that causes distortion.
- **Point 11 — Eliminate quotas:** Welder is not pushed to finish 30 joints a day at the cost of porosity.
- **Point 12 — Remove barriers to pride:** A welder's craftsmanship is respected and recognized.
- **Point 13 — Education:** Self-development for all employees.
- **Point 14 — Transformation:** Top management leads the change.

**Conclusion:** Deming's framework transforms shipyard leadership from a *control* orientation to a *systemic improvement* orientation, which is the foundation of TQM in modern shipbuilding.

*(Award: ~0.5 mark per point elaborated, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**(a)** *Compare and contrast Transformational, Transactional, and Servant leadership styles. Which is most suitable for leading a shipyard's TQM implementation? Justify.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Transformational | Transactional | Servant |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Core Idea** | Inspire beyond expectations | Reward–punishment exchanges | Serve first, lead second |
| **Focus** | Vision, change, people development | Goals, compliance, contracts | People's growth and well-being |
| **Leader Role** | Visionary | Manager | Steward |
| **Motivator** | Higher-order purpose | Extrinsic rewards | Empowerment |
| **Strength in QA** | Drives culture change | Hits inspection KPIs | Builds long-term morale |
| **Weakness in QA** | May neglect deadlines | No innovation | Slow in crisis |

**Most suitable for TQM in shipbuilding:** A **blend of Transformational + Servant** leadership.

*Justification:* TQM is fundamentally a *cultural* transformation requiring both an inspiring vision (Transformational) and deep worker empowerment (Servant). Transactional alone would merely maintain status quo and miss the cultural shift Deming envisioned. Therefore, the modern shipyard QA leader must be **transformational in vision** and **servant in execution**.

*(Award: 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for identification of best blend, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

**(b)** *With a suitable example, apply the Path-Goal Theory of leadership to a quality-assurance scenario in a shipyard.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Path-Goal Theory (House, 1971)** proposes that a leader's behavior must adapt to subordinate characteristics and environmental factors to clarify the path toward goal achievement.

**Four Leader Behaviors:**
1. **Directive** — Tell subordinates what is expected.
2. **Supportive** — Show concern for well-being.
3. **Participative** — Consult before deciding.
4. **Achievement-Oriented** — Set challenging goals.

**Shipyard QA Scenario:**
The Quality Manager oversees a 40-member NDT team tasked with completing ultrasonic testing (UT) of 8,000 butt welds in the LNG cargo tank within 12 weeks. The team is technically skilled but demoralized by extended hours and unclear priorities.

**Application of Path-Goal:**

- **Subordinate factor — low morale + high ability** → Adopt **Supportive Behavior**: introduce flexible shifts, recognize top UT inspectors monthly, install a rest area near the test station.
- **Environmental factor — ambiguous priorities + tight schedule** → Adopt **Directive Behavior**: publish a clear UT sequence (lower tank → upper tank → dome) and daily targets.
- **Subordinate factor — high ability** → Adopt **Achievement-Oriented Behavior**: challenge the team to a 5% reduction in false-positive readings via better calibration.
- **Need for buy-in on a new UT software rollout** → Adopt **Participative Behavior**: invite senior UT inspectors to a feedback session before final roll-out.

**Result:** The Quality Manager, by *mixing all four* behaviors, effectively clears the path to the 12-week goal while keeping the team engaged.

**Conclusion:** Path-Goal Theory is a practical, contingency-based model perfectly suited for the dynamic, multi-stakeholder environment of shipyard QA.

*(Award: 2 marks for theory outline, 3 marks for scenario application, 2 marks for conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully!**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often *list* leadership theories without **applying** them to shipbuilding — losing 2–3 marks per sub-question.
> - **Pitfall 2:** In Deming's 14 Points, students *memorize* the points but fail to *contextualize* them to shipbuilding (welds, NDT, class rules) — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark per point.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Confusing **Quality Control (QC)** with **Quality Assurance (QA)** in definitions — QC is *inspection*; QA is the *entire system*. Examiners are strict here.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Writing leadership styles without naming *when* and *where* each is used in a shipyard. Always provide a real shipyard context.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Skipping the **conclusion** line. KTU board examiners allocate 1–2 marks for a synthesized conclusion in 14-mark answers.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Definition:** Leadership = influencing others to achieve quality goals.
- **Leadership vs. Management:** Vision/people/change vs. Plan/organize/control.
- **5 Must-Know Theories:** Trait, Behavioral, Contingency, Transformational, Servant.
- **Hersey-Blanchard 4 Styles:** S1 Telling, S2 Selling, S3 Participating, S4 Delegating.
- **Bass's 4 I's:** Idealized Influence, Inspirational Motivation, Intellectual Stimulation, Individualized Consideration.
- **Deming's 14 Points:** Especially Points 3, 7, 8, 11 in QA context.
- **Best Style for TQM:** Transformational + Servant blend.
- **Path-Goal Theory:** Mix of Directive, Supportive, Participative, Achievement-Oriented.
- **Shipyard Application:** ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5 mandates **top management leadership**.
- **Quality Leader's Role:** Set policy, ensure resources, communicate, review, empower.
- **Avoid:** Inspection-only mindset; fear culture; silos between design/production/QA.
- **One-Liners to Remember:**
  - "Leaders do the right things; managers do things right." — Drucker
  - "Quality is not inspected-in, it is built-in." — Deming
  - "The customer is the next process." — Shipbuilding TQM slogan
- **Numerical Awareness:** Rework cost in merchant shipbuilding ≈ 4–6% of contract value; strong leadership can halve this.
- **Key Standards:** ISO 9001:2015, IACS QSCS, Class rules (IRS/DNV/LR/ABS).
- **Mnemonic for Theories:** **TBCS-S** = Trait, Behavioral, Contingency, Situational, Servant.
- **Final Tip:** Always end your answer with a *shipbuilding-specific conclusion* — examiners reward contextualized answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Involvement of People
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Involvement of People

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Involvement of People** is one of the **seven quality management principles** defined by **ISO 9000:2015** (and previously recognized in ISO 9000:2000 / 9004). It states that an organization thrives when it actively involves and empowers *all* its people — from top management to frontline workers — at *all* levels and across *all* functions, recognizing them as the essential resource that drives quality, innovation, and continuous improvement.

In the **shipbuilding context**, this principle translates into the structured engagement of welders, fitters, plumbers, electricians, shipwrights, painters, supervisors, design engineers, quality inspectors, and top management in a unified effort to deliver defect-free, on-spec, and on-time vessels.

**Key Standard Reference:** **ISO 9001:2015, Clause 7 — Support / Clause 7.2 — Competence**, and the quality principle framework of **ISO 9000:2015, Clause 2.2**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Crew of a Ship at Sea"** 🚢
>
> Imagine a 200-metre cargo ship crossing the Indian Ocean. The Captain (Top Management) may chart the course, but it is the *able seaman on watch*, the *engine room technician*, the *cook*, and the *helmsman* who actually keep the ship moving and safe. If the Captain never communicates, never listens, and never trusts the crew to make decisions at their level, the ship will inevitably fail — a *millimeter* of rudder misalignment, a *degree* of boiler overheating, or a *misread* radar return can sink the vessel.
>
> **Involvement of People in shipbuilding is exactly this:** Quality is not the responsibility of the Quality Department alone — it is the daily commitment of every hand that cuts steel, tacks a weld, fits a pipe, or signs a drawing. When each person *owns* quality, the ship floats. When only a few own it, the ship sinks.

### 1.3 Core Terms (Highlighted for KTU)

- **Competence:** The demonstrated ability to apply knowledge, skills, and behaviours to achieve intended results.
- **Empowerment:** The authority and accountability given to people to make decisions within their area of responsibility.
- **Engagement:** The emotional and intellectual commitment an employee has to the organization and its goals.
- **Ownership of Quality:** The mindset where every employee treats quality as *their personal* responsibility, not the inspector's.
- **Continual Improvement:** A permanent objective achieved through the active participation of all levels of staff.

### 1.4 Why This Principle Exists

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The principle of Involvement of People exists because **80% of shipbuilding defects originate at the worker-operator interface** (per multiple classification society root-cause studies). Quality cannot be *inspected into* a ship — it must be *built-in* by the people who actually do the work.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Principle

The principle rests on three pillars of reasoning:

- **Human-Centric Quality:** Quality is a *human achievement*, not a procedural one. Procedures can be written, but only people can *interpret, adapt, and execute* them intelligently.
- **Deming's Chain Reaction:** Better quality → lower costs → improved productivity → market capture → stay-in-business → create jobs. This chain only fires when people at every level are active participants.
- **Systems Thinking:** A shipyard is a complex socio-technical system. Engagement of people ensures *systemic* quality rather than *patchwork* quality.

### 2.2 The "How" — Typical Activities to Achieve Involvement

A shipyard demonstrates this principle through the following structured activities:

- **Establish a Quality Policy** that is *communicated, understood, applied, and reviewed* at every level.
- **Define Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities** clearly in the Quality Management System (QMS) documentation.
- **Provide Competence-Based Training** — welder qualification tests, NDT certification, ISM Code familiarization, Lean Six Sigma green belt.
- **Set SMART Objectives** at *individual, team, departmental,* and *organizational* levels.
- **Empower Frontline Decision-Making** — stop-work authority, kaizen suggestion systems, defect tagging authority.
- **Facilitate Two-Way Communication** — toolbox talks, suggestion schemes, open-door policies, daily morning briefings.
- **Reward and Recognize** — quality circles, "Welder of the Month," zero-defect team awards.
- **Conduct Management Reviews** that *include* worker representative feedback.
- **Promote Quality Culture** — through leadership walkabouts, visual management, and "gemba" presence of managers on the shop floor.

### 2.3 Benefits of Involving People in Shipbuilding

| # | Benefit Category | Concrete Outcome in a Shipyard |
|---|------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Improved Quality | Reduction in welding rework rates (typically **40–60% drop** in mature QMS yards) |
| 2 | Enhanced Motivation | Lower absenteeism, higher employee retention (saves **3× monthly salary** per retained skilled worker) |
| 3 | Increased Innovation | Frontline welders and fitters generate most shop-floor kaizen ideas |
| 4 | Better Safety Culture | Reduced LTI (Lost Time Injuries) — proven correlation with engagement levels |
| 5 | Higher Productivity | Engaged yards achieve **15–25%** higher man-hour productivity |
| 6 | Customer Trust | Fewer class-society non-conformities → faster delivery and stronger owner confidence |
| 7 | Cost Reduction | Cost of poor quality (COPQ) drops measurably year-on-year |
| 8 | Knowledge Retention | Tacit shipbuilding knowledge is preserved through mentoring and documentation |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Key Terms & Metrics)

| Concept | Definition / Metric | KTU Significance |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Quality Management Principles** | The **7 principles** of ISO 9000:2015 that guide QMS implementation | High — direct syllabus topic |
| **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.2** | Competence — organization shall determine and ensure competence | Frequently asked |
| **Empowerment** | Authority + accountability at the lowest practical level | Central to TQM |
| **QMS — Quality Management System** | The formalized set of policies, processes, and records | Module-wide context |
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan–Do–Check–Act — requires people involvement at every phase | Cross-module link |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous incremental improvement driven by *all* employees | TQM concept |
| **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality — prevention $\lt$ appraisal $\lt$ internal failure $\lt$ external failure | Strategic principle |
| **Stop-Work Authority** | Right of any worker to halt a job if quality/safety is at risk | Practical demonstration |
| **Quality Circle** | Small group of workers solving quality problems in their work area | Engagement tool |
| **Toolbox Talk** | Short, focused safety/quality briefing on the shop floor | Daily practice |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Shipbuilding & Offshore:** Critical for handling complex one-off designs where worker judgement fills procedural gaps.
- **Aerospace:** Adopted as "**Human Factors Engineering**" in AS9100.
- **Automotive:** Foundation of the Toyota Production System and Industry 4.0 worker-led cells.
- **Construction:** Drives "Quality by Everyone" culture in high-rise and infrastructure projects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework (Adapted Derivation)

> Since this is a **management-principles topic** rather than a computational one, the "derivation" here is a *logical progression framework* — building the principle from its conceptual foundation to its measurable outcomes.

### 3.1 The Logical Build-Up of the Principle

We construct the principle through six sequential logical steps:

**Step 1 — Acknowledge the Human Foundation**
$$\text{Organization} = f(\text{People, Process, Product})$$

An organization is fundamentally a *human system*. Without active people, even the best process and product design fails. This is the **axiom** on which the principle rests.

**Step 2 — Recognize Tacit Knowledge**
$$\text{Explicit Knowledge} + \text{Tacit Knowledge} = \text{Full Competence}$$

Workers on the shop floor possess *tacit knowledge* (experience, intuition, situational awareness) that cannot be captured in any procedure manual. Engaging them *releases* this knowledge into the QMS.

**Step 3 — Apply Deming's 94/6 Rule**
$$\text{Problems}_{\text{system}} = 94\%, \quad \text{Problems}_{\text{worker}} = 6\%$$

When quality issues arise, the system (designed by management) is the dominant cause. Involving people *in system design* eliminates the root cause of 94% of issues. This is why leadership participation is non-negotiable.

**Step 4 — Link Engagement to Outcomes**
$$\text{Engagement} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Defect Rate} \downarrow, \quad \text{Productivity} \uparrow, \quad \text{Safety} \uparrow$$

Engagement is the *independent variable*; quality, productivity, and safety are *dependent variables*. In mature shipyards, statistical evidence shows a strong negative correlation between engagement index and NCR (Non-Conformance Report) count.

**Step 5 — Apply the PDCA Loop at Every Level**
$$\text{Plan}_{\text{individual}} \to \text{Do}_{\text{individual}} \to \text{Check}_{\text{individual}} \to \text{Act}_{\text{individual}}$$

The PDCA cycle, originally a *management* tool, is replicated at the *individual worker* level. This is the operational form of the principle.

**Step 6 — Achieve Continual Improvement**
$$\text{Improvement}_{n+1} = \text{Improvement}_n \times (1 + r)$$

where $r$ is the *rate of engagement-driven improvement*. Sustainable, compounding gains in quality are only possible when *all* people contribute, not just a few experts.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Principle to ISO 9001:2015 Clauses

| ISO 9001:2015 Clause | What It Demands | How "Involvement of People" Satisfies It |
|----------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------------------|
| 5.1 Leadership | Top management commitment | Leaders visibly engage with frontline workers |
| 5.3 Organizational Roles | Defined responsibilities | Each person knows their QMS role |
| 7.1 Resources | Provision of resources | People are recognized as the primary resource |
| 7.2 Competence | Demonstrated competence | Training, qualification, evaluation |
| 7.3 Awareness | Awareness of QMS policy | Communication at all levels |
| 7.4 Communication | Internal/external communication | Two-way, multi-channel systems |
| 10.2 Nonconformity | Handling of NCRs | Empowered stop-work and corrective action |
| 10.3 Continual Improvement | Ongoing improvement | Kaizen, suggestions, lessons learned |

### 3.3 The Three-Line Defence Model (Shipyard Application)

A classic shipbuilding quality application of the principle is the **Three-Line Defence Model**, where every person plays a defined quality role:

- **First Line — Operators (Welders, Fitters, Electricians, Painters):** Build quality *in*; perform self-checks; raise NCRs proactively.
- **Second Line — Supervisors and Engineers:** Verify first-line work; coach; solve systemic issues.
- **Third Line — Independent QA / Classification Surveyors:** Audit the system; provide assurance to the customer and flag state.

This model mathematically distributes quality responsibility:

$$\text{QA Effectiveness} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} (\text{Line}_i \text{Competence} \times \text{Line}_i \text{Engagement})$$

If *any* one of the three lines has zero engagement, the system fails. This is the strongest empirical argument for the principle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Involvement-of-People Framework in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management - Strategic Commitment] --> B[Quality Policy and Objectives]
    B --> C[Communication and Awareness Programme]
    C --> D[Competence and Training System]
    D --> E[Empowered Frontline Workers]
    E --> F[Self-Check and Stop-Work Authority]
    F --> G[Quality Circles and Kaizen Teams]
    G --> H[Feedback Loop to Management]
    H --> A
    D --> I[Supervisors - Second Line Defence]
    I --> J[Independent QA - Third Line Defence]
    J --> H
    E --> K[Customer and Class Society Confidence]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> H
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three-Line Defence in Shipbuilding Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1A[Welder / Fitter] --> L1B[Self-Inspection]
    L1B --> L1C{Defect Detected?}
    L1C -- Yes --> L1D[Stop-Work and Raise NCR]
    L1C -- No --> L1E[Forward to Second Line]
    L1D --> L1E
    L1E --> L2A[Supervisor Verification]
    L2A --> L2B[Engineer Review]
    L2B --> L2C[Corrective Action]
    L2C --> L3A[Independent QA Audit]
    L3A --> L3B[Classification Society Survey]
    L3B --> L3C[Customer Acceptance]
    L2C --> L2D[Lessons Learned Feedback]
    L2D --> L1A
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — People-Involvement Operating Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER - Demands and Expectations"]
        I1[Customer Requirements]
        I2[Class Society Rules]
        I3[Statutory Regulations]
    end

    subgraph PeopleLayer["PEOPLE LAYER - The Core of QMS"]
        P1[Top Management]
        P2[Design Engineers]
        P3[Production Engineers]
        P4[Skilled Trades - Welders, Fitters, Electricians]
        P5[Quality Inspectors]
        P6[Support Functions - HR, Stores, Finance]
    end

    subgraph ProcessLayer["PROCESS LAYER - Value-Adding Activities"]
        PR1[Cutting and Shaping]
        PR2[Welding and Fabrication]
        PR3[Outfitting and Erection]
        PR4[Surface Treatment]
        PR5[Inspection and Testing]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER - Quality Outcomes"]
        O1[Defect-Free Hull and Outfit]
        O2[On-Time Delivery]
        O3[Customer and Class Satisfaction]
        O4[Reduced COPQ]
    end

    I1 --> PeopleLayer
    I2 --> PeopleLayer
    I3 --> PeopleLayer
    P1 --> ProcessLayer
    P2 --> ProcessLayer
    P3 --> ProcessLayer
    P4 --> ProcessLayer
    P5 --> ProcessLayer
    P6 --> ProcessLayer
    ProcessLayer --> O1
    ProcessLayer --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
    O4 --> P1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Continual Improvement Cycle (PDCA at the Worker Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Plan - Set Personal Quality Goal] --> Q2[Do - Execute Work to Standard]
    Q2 --> Q3[Check - Self-Verify Output]
    Q3 --> Q4{Conformance Achieved?}
    Q4 -- No --> Q5[Act - Correct and Learn]
    Q5 --> Q1
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q6[Act - Standardize and Improve]
    Q6 --> Q1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the quality management principle of "Involvement of People" as per ISO 9000.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Involvement of People is one of the seven quality management principles of ISO 9000:2015. It emphasizes that an organization achieves its quality objectives most effectively when it actively engages, empowers, and values every person at all levels and functions. In shipbuilding, this means that quality is built by the welders, fitters, supervisors, engineers, and managers working together as a unified system, not by the Quality Department alone. Competent, empowered, and motivated people form the foundation of a successful Quality Management System (QMS). *(3 marks — definition, expansion, shipbuilding context.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
