**List any three typical activities through which "Involvement of People" can be achieved in a shipyard.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The three typical activities are:
1. **Competence-based training and qualification** — e.g., welder performance qualification tests, NDT certification, ISM code training. *(1 mark)*
2. **Empowerment through stop-work authority and kaizen suggestion systems** — giving every worker the right and responsibility to halt unsafe or non-conforming work. *(1 mark)*
3. **Two-way communication and recognition** — toolbox talks, open-door policies, and quality-circle awards. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students often *describe* the principle but fail to **apply** it to a **shipbuilding** context — and lose 3–4 marks. Always anchor the answer in a *shipyard example* (e.g., welder, block erection, outfitting).
> 2. Many students write only the **"benefits"** and skip the **"how to achieve it"** — for a 14-mark question, both are mandatory.
> 3. Avoid generic textbook reproduction. KTU valuators reward **structured headings, diagrams, and KTU syllabus-specific terminology** (QMS, ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.2, PDCA, Three-Line Defence).
> 4. Never write one-paragraph essays. Use **bullet points, sub-headings, and at least one block diagram** for full marks.

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**Q3 (a).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain in detail the principle of "Involvement of People" as one of the ISO 9000 quality management principles. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
The principle of Involvement of People, as per ISO 9000:2015 (Clause 2.2), states that an organization depends on the active involvement of *all* its people at *all* levels to deliver value and achieve continual improvement. It recognizes that people — when competent, empowered, and engaged — are the *essential resource* of any organization.

**2. Key Rationale / "Why" (2 marks):**
- Quality is built by people, not just by procedures and inspections.
- Engaged workers generate ideas, raise concerns early, and own outcomes.
- Deming's philosophy stresses that 94% of problems are system-related; systems are designed and improved by *people* working together.

**3. How It Works (3 marks):**
- **Competence:** The organization determines the necessary competence and ensures people are qualified.
- **Empowerment:** People are given the authority and accountability to act on quality issues (e.g., stop-work authority).
- **Engagement:** A culture of ownership is built through training, communication, recognition, and involvement in problem-solving (kaizen, quality circles).

**Conclusion:** In shipbuilding, the principle is operationalized through structured training, clearly defined roles (per ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.3), empowered frontline decision-making, and management-walk-through (gemba) leadership presence.

---

**Q3 (b).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Discuss the major benefits of involving people in shipbuilding quality assurance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Improved Product Quality (2 marks):**
When welders, fitters, and electricians are engaged and competent, defects are *prevented* rather than *detected*. Empirical evidence from mature QMS shipyards shows a **40–60% reduction** in welding rework rates within 2–3 years of structured people-involvement programs.

**2. Higher Productivity (1 mark):**
Engaged workers are more efficient; shipyards report **15–25%** higher man-hour productivity compared to yards that treat quality as an inspector-only function.

**3. Innovation and Kaizen (1 mark):**
Frontline workers possess *tacit knowledge* — they propose the most effective shop-floor improvements. Quality circles and suggestion systems yield hundreds of cost-saving ideas annually in large shipyards.

**4. Enhanced Safety Culture (1 mark):**
Engagement correlates strongly with reduced Lost Time Injuries (LTI). Workers who feel valued are more likely to follow safety procedures and stop unsafe work.

**5. Cost Reduction and Customer Satisfaction (1 mark):**
COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality) reduces; class society holds and rework costs drop. Customers receive a higher-quality vessel on time, strengthening long-term relationships.

**6. Knowledge Retention and Morale (1 mark):**
Involvement creates a culture of mentoring and documentation, retaining critical shipbuilding knowledge in an industry plagued by skilled-labour shortages.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative***

**Q4 (a).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Describe the key methods and activities through which an organization can achieve "Involvement of People" in its Quality Management System. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Establishing a Shared Quality Policy and Objectives (1 mark):**
The QMS policy must be *communicated, understood, and applied* at every level. In a shipyard, this means translating the corporate quality policy into department-level and individual-level objectives (e.g., a welder's weekly defect target).

**2. Defining Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities (1 mark):**
Per ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.3, every person must know their QMS role. Shipyards use RACI matrices, job descriptions, and procedure ownership charts.

**3. Competence, Training, and Awareness (2 marks):**
- **Initial competence verification** — qualification tests for welders, NDT operators, painters.
- **Ongoing training** — refresher courses, new-technology familiarization, cross-skilling.
- **Awareness programmes** — induction training on QMS policy, customer requirements, and the consequences of non-conformance.

**4. Empowerment and Two-Way Communication (2 marks):**
- **Stop-work authority** for any worker on quality or safety grounds.
- **Toolbox talks, suggestion schemes, open-door policies.**
- **Quality circles and kaizen teams** for problem-solving.

**5. Recognition, Reward, and Continual Improvement (1 mark):**
- "Zero Defect" awards, "Welder of the Month," team recognition.
- Management review of people-involvement metrics (training hours, suggestion count, engagement index).

---

**Q4 (b).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain how the principle of "Involvement of People" is applied specifically in the shipbuilding industry. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. From Design to Delivery — A Chain of Engagement (2 marks):**
Shipbuilding is a *concurrent* and *complex* activity involving design, steel fabrication, block assembly, outfitting, painting, commissioning, and sea trials. *Every* stage requires competent, engaged people:
- **Design stage:** Naval architects engage with production engineers for buildability.
- **Steel stage:** Welders apply qualified procedures, perform self-checks, and tag defects.
- **Block stage:** Fitters verify dimensional tolerances against the *lines plan*.
- **Outfitting stage:** Plumbers, electricians, and HVAC technicians coordinate with supervisors.
- **Erection stage:** Crane operators, riggers, and alignment technicians work as one team.

**2. Three-Line Defence Model (2 marks):**
- **First line (operators):** Build quality *in*; raise NCRs proactively.
- **Second line (supervisors/engineers):** Verify, coach, solve systemic issues.
- **Third line (independent QA/classification surveyors):** Audit, assure, and certify.
*Every person plays a defined role; the system fails if any line disengages.*

**3. Competence and Certification Framework (1 mark):**
- Welders certified to **AWS D1.1 / ASME IX / ISO 9606**.
- NDT operators certified to **ISO 9712 / SNT-TC-1A**.
- All personnel familiarized with the **ISM Code, SOLAS, and class rules** as applicable.

**4. Communication and Culture (1 mark):**
- **Daily toolbox talks** on safety and quality topics.
- **Visual management** boards on the shop floor — defect trends, kaizen updates.
- **Gemba walks** by shipyard leadership to engage directly with workers.

**5. Recognition Systems (1 mark):**
- Zero-defect block awards, team-of-the-month recognition, monetary and non-monetary incentives tied to quality KPIs.

**Conclusion:** The successful delivery of a complex vessel — on time, on budget, and to class — is possible only when *every* person, from the steel-yard helper to the project manager, is an engaged owner of quality.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Involvement of People** is the **3rd of 7 quality management principles** of **ISO 9000:2015**.
- ✅ Quality is **built by people, not inspected in** — the principle recognises the *human* essence of an organisation.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Competence + Empowerment + Engagement.
- ✅ Anchored in **ISO 9001:2015, Clauses 5.1, 5.3, 7.2, 7.3, 7.4, and 10.2/10.3**.
- ✅ **Shipbuilding application:** The **Three-Line Defence Model** — Operators (1st), Supervisors/Engineers (2nd), Independent QA/Class (3rd).
- ✅ Key tools: **Toolbox talks, Kaizen, Quality Circles, Suggestion Schemes, Gemba Walks, Stop-Work Authority, Visual Management, RACI Matrix.**
- ✅ Engagement drives: **↓ Defects (40–60%)**, **↓ Rework**, **↑ Productivity (15–25%)**, **↓ LTI**, **↓ COPQ**.
- ✅ **Deming's 94/6 rule:** 94% of problems are system-related — engaging people *in system design* prevents them.
- ✅ **PDCA at the individual level** turns the principle into a daily operational habit.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Writing generic definitions without a **shipbuilding-specific example** — always anchor your answer in a welder, fitter, block, or shipyard context.
- ✅ **Valuator's tip:** A clear **block diagram** of the Three-Line Defence or the Involvement Framework earns at least **1–2 extra marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Process Approach # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (ISO 9001:2015 Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Process Approach (KTU / ISO 9001:2015 Definition)** > The *Process Approach* is a Quality Management System (QMS) principle that mandates the systematic identification, management, and interaction of **interrelated processes** within an organization to achieve its intended results more effectively. It replaces the older "procedure-based" silo thinking with a holistic, **risk-based, customer-focused, lifecycle-driven** framework. In the context of **PENST862 – Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding**, the Process Approach treats every shipbuilding activity — from *contract review* to *steel cutting*, *hull erection*, *outfitting*, *launching*, and *sea trials* — as a **controlled, measurable, continuously improving process**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a Ship is Like Cooking a Biryani" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box for First-Time Learners** > Imagine you are preparing a traditional Kerala **Biryani** for 500 guests: > 1. You don't randomly start cooking — you **Plan** the menu (PDCA → *Plan*). > 2. You gather rice, spices, chicken — these are **Inputs**. > 3. You marinate, parboil rice, fry onions — these are **Process Steps**. > 4. The cooked biryani in the handi is the **Output**. > 5. The guests (and your reputation) are the **Customers**. > 6. You taste-test (Quality Control / *Check*) and adjust salt next time (*Act*). > > A ship is exactly the same — except the rice is **steel plates**, the handi is the **dry dock**, the guests are the **shipowner and class society**, and the recipe is the **production specification**. The **Process Approach** is the formal "cookbook" that documents every step, measures every variable (temperature = weld preheat), and ensures consistency across the entire shipyard. ## 1.3 Why Shipbuilding *Demands* the Process Approach | Shipbuilding Reality | Process Approach Solution | |---|---| | A single vessel involves **millions** of discrete activities | Process mapping brings structure | | Multiple trades (welders, fitters, electricians, painters) work in parallel | Defines **interfaces & hand-offs** | | Strict regulatory oversight (IRS, DNV, ABS, LR, BV) | Provides **documented evidence & traceability** | | High cost of rework (₹₹₹ per mm of cut steel) | **Defect prevention** via preventive controls | | Long build cycles (12–36 months) | **Continual improvement (Kaizen)** | > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** Always remember — *Process Approach is NOT about drawing a flowchart. It is a management philosophy that converts an organization from a "functional silo" into a "network of value-adding processes."* ## 1.4 Core Elements of a "Process" (KTU Terminology) Every process, whether it is *Hull Steel Cutting* or *Pipe Spool Fabrication*, must explicitly define six elements: 1. **Inputs** — Raw materials, drawings, work orders, skills (e.g., grade-A steel plates, NC cutting data). 2. **Outputs** — The deliverable product or sub-process result (e.g., cut profiles ready for assembly). 3. **Controls** — Standards, specifications, inspection criteria (e.g., ISO 9013, class rules). 4. **Resources** — Machines, manpower, environment (e.g., CNC plasma cutter, certified welders). 5. **Owner / Responsibility** — A single accountable person (Process Owner). 6. **Metrics / KPIs** — How success is measured (e.g., *first-pass yield %, rework hours*). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PDCA Cycle — The Engine of the Process Approach The **Plan-Do-Check-Act** (Deming / Shewhart Cycle) is the operational heartbeat of the Process Approach. It is integrated into **every clause of ISO 9001:2015**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **PDCA Mapping for Shipbuilding Quality Assurance:** | PDCA Phase | Shipbuilding Activity Example | KTU Memory Hook | |---|---|---| | **PLAN** | Establish the Welding Procedure Specification (WPS), identify hazards, define KPIs | "Design the recipe" | | **DO** | Execute welding in the panel line, train welders, log production data | "Cook the dish" | | **CHECK** | NDT (UT/RT/MPI), dimensional surveys, KPI monitoring | "Taste and measure" | | **ACT** | Issue Corrective Action Request (CAR), update WPS, CAPA | "Improve the recipe for next batch" | ## 2.2 Risk-Based Thinking (Clause 6.1 of ISO 9001:2015) Modern Process Approach **mandates** that every process owner must proactively address: - **Risks** — Threats to process output (e.g., welder shortage, monsoon delays). - **Opportunities** — Areas for innovation (e.g., automation, robotic welding). > [!NOTE] > **Risk Priority Number (RPN) Formula — Essential for KTU Numerical Questions:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \end{aligned} $$ Where: - **S** = Severity (1 to 10) - **O** = Occurrence / Likelihood (1 to 10) - **D** = Detectability — *note: higher D means harder to detect* (1 to 10) ## 2.3 Process Performance Metrics — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Use `\vert` inside tables to avoid markdown breaking — never use `|`.** | # | Metric / Formula | Engineering Meaning in Shipbuilding | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Welds / blocks accepted without rework | **%** | | 2 | **Rework Cost Ratio** $\text{RCR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{rework}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Fraction of man-hours spent on defects | **%** | | 3 | **Process Capability Index** $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Tolerance compliance for plate cutting, pipe bending | **Dimensionless** | | 4 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ where $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality | Efficiency of CNC cutter, panel line crane | **%** | | 5 | **Defects Per Unit (DPU)** $\text{DPU} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{Units produced}}$ | NDT defects per block | **defects/block** | | 6 | **Sigma Level (DPMO → σ)** $\sigma = f(\text{DPMO})$ | Six-Sigma quality benchmark | **σ** | | 7 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Deliveries on time}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \times 100$ | Ship handover vs contract date | **%** | | 8 | **RPN (Risk)** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA prioritization for welding, blasting | **Score** | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** > - **USL** = Upper Specification Limit (e.g., max plate thickness tolerance = +2 mm) > - **LSL** = Lower Specification Limit > - $\sigma$ = standard deviation of the process > - **DPMO** = Defects Per Million Opportunities ## 2.4 SIPOC — The Master Process Identification Tool > [!IMPORTANT] > **SIPOC** is the standard KTU / ISO 9001 method to *document any high-level process*. It is a high-yield exam topic. **S — Suppliers** (who provides the input?) **I — Inputs** (what is required?) **P — Process** (the 4–6 high-level steps) **O — Outputs** (the deliverable) **C — Customers** (who receives it?) ### SIPOC Example — "Hull Block Fabrication" | S | I | P | O | C | |---|---|---|---|---| | Steel mill, Drawing office | Steel plates, profiles, NC data, drawings | Cut → Assemble → Weld → Inspect → Blast → Paint | Painted, dimensionally approved block | Outfitting berth, Erection team | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Hyundai Heavy Industries, Fincantieri), the Process Approach is implemented through: - **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** — digital process control. - **Digital twin** of each block — process simulation. - **Class society Type Approval** of processes — not just products. - **Lean + Six Sigma** integration — operationalizing PDCA. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical Derivations ## 3.1 How to Apply the Process Approach — A Step-by-Step Methodology (KTU Board Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **This 8-step framework is the standard answer structure for any "Explain Process Approach in Shipbuilding" 14-mark question.** **Step 1: Identify the QMS Scope & Interested Parties** - Customers: shipowners, class societies (IRS, LR, DNV). - Statutory bodies: flag state, MARPOL, SOLAS. **Step 2: Map Core, Support & Management Processes** - *Core*: steel cutting → block assembly → erection → outfitting → launching → trials. - *Support*: procurement, HR, calibration, IT. - *Management*: management review, internal audit, risk review. **Step 3: Determine Sequence & Interaction** - Use a **process flowchart** with hand-off arrows. **Step 4: Define Process Owner, KPIs & Risks** - Each process gets ONE owner. **Step 5: Establish Criteria & Methods for Monitoring** - KPIs, audit checklists, KPI dashboards. **Step 6: Implement the Controls (Documented Procedures, ITPs)** - Inspection and Test Plans (ITP) are the operational document. **Step 7: Measure, Analyze & Evaluate Performance** - Monthly KPI review meeting. **Step 8: Take Actions for Continual Improvement** - Corrective Action, Preventive Action (CAPA), Kaizen events. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — FMEA & RPN Calculation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Style Numerical — Practice this exact pattern.** **Question:** During a shipbuilding project, three failure modes were identified in the *submerged arc welding* process of a hull panel: | Failure Mode | S | O | D | |---|---|---|---| | F1: Porosity | 6 | 5 | 4 | | F2: Lack of fusion | 9 | 3 | 5 | | F3: Undercut | 4 | 7 | 6 | Identify the highest-priority failure mode and the corresponding action threshold. ### Solution (Step-by-Step): **Step 1 — Recall the RPN formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RPN} = S \times O \times D \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute RPN for F1 (Porosity).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RPN}_{F1} &= 6 \times 5 \times 4 \\ &= 120 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute RPN for F2 (Lack of fusion).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RPN}_{F2} &= 9 \times 3 \times 5 \\ &= 135 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute RPN for F3 (Undercut).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RPN}_{F3} &= 4 \times 7 \times 6 \\ &= 168 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Rank and decide.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RPN}_{F3} \; (168) \; > \; \text{RPN}_{F2} \; (135) \; > \; \text{RPN}_{F1} \; (120) \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion:** *Undercut (F3)* has the highest RPN, hence the **top priority** for corrective action — typically addressed via reduced travel speed, finer voltage tuning, and post-weld dressing inspections. **Industry threshold:** RPN > 100 → mandatory action. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Process Capability Index ($C_p$) **Question:** A CNC plate-cutting machine cuts 20 mm thick steel. Specification limits are: - USL = 20.4 mm thickness remaining. - LSL = 19.6 mm. - Sample mean $\bar{x}$ = 20.0 mm. - Standard deviation $\sigma$ = 0.10 mm. Compute $C_p$ and interpret the process capability. ### Solution (Step-by-Step): **Step 1 — Recall the formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \frac{20.4 - 19.6}{6 \times 0.10} \\ &= \frac{0.8}{0.6} \\ &= 1.333 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation Rule:** $C_p \geq 1.33$ → **Process is capable** (industry threshold for shipbuilding). Since $C_p = 1.33$, the cutting process **meets** the minimum capability requirement. If $C_p < 1.00$, the process is incapable and requires re-engineering. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — First Pass Yield (FPY) **Question:** In a shipyard's outfitting division, 240 pipe spools were produced in one week. 198 passed all hydrostatic and dimensional tests on the first attempt. Compute the FPY. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Recall formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FPY} = \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FPY} &= \frac{198}{240} \times 100 \\ &= 0.825 \times 100 \\ &= 82.5\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** An FPY of **82.5%** indicates a *17.5% rework rate*, which is moderate. World-class shipyards target FPY > 95% on pipe spool fabrication. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The PDCA Cycle — Visualised ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PLAN
Define process, KPIs, risks, WPS"] B["DO
Execute cutting, welding, assembly"] C["CHECK
NDT, dimensional survey, KPI review"] D["ACT
CAPA, Kaizen, update WPS"] A ==> B B ==> C C ==> D D ==> A ``` ## 4.2 Generic Process Model (Inputs → Outputs) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SUPPLIERS S1["Steel Mill"] S2["Drawing Office"] S3["Class Society"] end subgraph INPUTS I1["Steel Plates"] I2["Approved Drawings"] I3["Class Rules"] end subgraph PROCESS_BLOCK["PROCESS - Hull Block Fabrication"] P1["Cut"] P2["Assemble"] P3["Weld"] P4["Inspect"] P5["Blast and Paint"] end subgraph OUTPUTS O1["Painted Block"] end subgraph CUSTOMERS C1["Erection Team"] C2["Outfitting Team"] end S1 --> I1 S2 --> I2 S3 --> I3 I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P4 P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> O1 O1 --> C1 O1 --> C2 ``` ## 4.3 Shipbuilding Process Interaction Map (High-Yield Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MANAGEMENT_PROCESSES M1["Management Review"] M2["Internal Audit"] M3["Risk and Opportunity Register"] end subgraph CORE_PROCESSES CP1["Contract Review"] CP2["Basic and Functional Design"] CP3["Steel Cutting"] CP4["Block Assembly"] CP5["Hull Erection"] CP6["Outfitting"] CP7["Launching"] CP8["Sea Trials and Delivery"] end subgraph SUPPORT_PROCESSES SP1["Procurement"] SP2["Welding Coordination"] SP3["NDT and Inspection"] SP4["Calibration"] SP5["HR and Training"] end CP1 --> CP2 --> CP3 --> CP4 --> CP5 --> CP6 --> CP7 --> CP8 SP1 --> CP3 SP2 --> CP4 SP3 --> CP4 SP3 --> CP5 SP4 --> CP3 SP5 --> CP2 M1 -.-> CP1 M2 -.-> CP4 M3 -.-> CP3 ``` ## 4.4 PDCA Application in Welding — Block-Level Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["PLAN - Develop WPS and PQR"] P2["PLAN - Define ITP and Acceptance Criteria"] D1["DO - Weld test coupon"] D2["DO - Train welders and qualify"] D3["DO - Execute production welds"] C1["CHECK - Visual inspection"] C2["CHECK - NDT UT and RT"] C3["CHECK - Compare KPI to target"] A1["ACT - Issue CAR if defects found"] A2["ACT - Update WPS and retrain"] A3["ACT - Standardize improvement"] P1 --> P2 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 A3 -.-> P1 ``` > [!TIP] > **For KTU Board Exams:** Always label arrows between processes (e.g., "drawings," "inspection reports," "feedback"). Directional arrows are mandatory marks per the valuation key. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the Process Approach as per ISO 9001:2015. Mention any two benefits.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The *Process Approach* is a Quality Management principle (Clause 4.4 of ISO 9001:2015) that mandates the **systematic identification, management, and interaction of interrelated processes** to achieve intended results. **Two benefits:** 1. **Continual improvement** through PDCA cycles (1.5 marks). 2. **Enhanced customer satisfaction** by aligning processes with customer requirements and class rules (1.5 marks). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List the six core elements that must be defined for every process in a shipyard QMS.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Inputs (raw materials, drawings, work orders). 2. Outputs (deliverables such as painted blocks, welded panels). 3. Controls (ITP, WPS, class rules). 4. Resources (manpower, machinery, consumables). 5. Process Owner (single accountability). 6. KPIs / Performance Metrics (FPY, OTD, $C_p$). *(0.5 marks × 6 elements)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both options carry 7 + 7 sub-parts. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the **PDCA cycle** in detail and apply each phase to the **submerged arc welding process** used for fabricating hull panels in a shipyard. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **SIPOC model** for the *Hull Block Fabrication* process. List the process owner and two KPIs for this process. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks - **PLAN (2 marks):** Develop and qualify the WPS as per ASME IX / ISO 15614. Define weld parameters (current, voltage, speed), preheat, interpass temperature. Identify KPIs (e.g., FPY, defect rate) and risks (e.g., hydrogen cracking). - **DO (2 marks):** Issue work instructions to certified welders, ensure base metal traceability, log every weld with a unique weld map number, control environmental conditions. - **CHECK (1.5 marks):** Perform visual inspection (ISO 5817 level B), NDT (UT/RT), measure KPI deviations, conduct FMEA reviews. - **ACT (1.5 marks):** Issue Corrective Action Requests (CAR), update WPS based on lessons learned, conduct Kaizen events, retrain welders if needed, and feed improvements back to PLAN. #### Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks - **SIPOC Table (4 marks):** | S | I | P | O | C | |---|---|---|---|---| | Steel mill, drawing office, class society | Steel plates, profiles, NC data, WPS, ITP | Cut → Assemble → Weld → Inspect → Blast → Paint | Approved painted block | Erection team, outfitting team, class surveyor | - **Diagram (2 marks):** Use the Mermaid block style shown in **Section 4.2** with directional arrows and labeled hand-offs. - **Process Owner (0.5 mark):** *Block Fabrication Superintendent*. - **Two KPIs (0.5 mark):** First Pass Yield (FPY) ≥ 95% and Rework Cost Ratio (RCR) ≤ 3%. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the concept of **risk-based thinking** integrated within the Process Approach. Compute the **RPN** for the following three failure modes of a robotic welding cell and identify the highest-priority failure. **(7 marks)** | Failure Mode | S | O | D | |---|---|---|---| | F1: Wire feed jam | 5 | 6 | 3 | | F2: Torch collision | 8 | 2 | 4 | | F3: Arc blow | 6 | 5 | 5 | **(b)** Compute the **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** for a pipe-bending machine where the target outer diameter is 200 mm, USL = 200.5 mm, LSL = 199.5 mm, sample mean $\bar{x}$ = 200.1 mm, and $\sigma$ = 0.12 mm. Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks - **Risk-Based Thinking explanation (3 marks):** RBT (ISO 9001:2015 Clause 6.1) requires the organization to **proactively identify risks and opportunities** that could affect process output conformity. It replaces preventive action with **forward-looking planning** integrated into every process. In welding, risks include welder skill gaps, equipment failure, and consumable defects. - **RPN Calculations (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RPN}_{F1} &= 5 \times 6 \times 3 = 90 \\ \text{RPN}_{F2} &= 8 \times 2 \times 4 = 64 \\ \text{RPN}_{F3} &= 6 \times 5 \times 5 = 150 \end{aligned} $$ **Highest Priority:** **F3 — Arc Blow (RPN = 150)** → requires immediate corrective action such as adjusting ground clamp position, reducing current, and inspecting workpiece grounding. #### Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Recall formula (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \frac{200.5 - 199.5}{6 \times 0.12} \\ &= \frac{1.0}{0.72} \\ &= 1.389 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Check centering (2 marks):** Compute $C_{pk}$: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk} &= \min\left\{\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right\} \\ &= \min\left\{\frac{200.5 - 200.1}{0.36},\; \frac{200.1 - 199.5}{0.36}\right\} \\ &= \min\left\{1.111,\; 1.667\right\} \\ &= 1.111 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):** Both $C_p = 1.39$ and $C_{pk} = 1.11$ exceed the threshold of 1.00 → the bending process is **capable but slightly off-centre**. Recommend recalibration of mandrel alignment to bring the mean closer to 200.0 mm. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Process Approach Questions (KTU 2024):** > 1. **Generic Answers** — Writing textbook definitions without linking to *shipbuilding*. Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for non-contextualized answers. > 2. **Forgetting Process Owner** — Every process *must* have a named owner; otherwise, **0.5 mark deduction** in 7-mark questions. > 3. **Confusing PDCA with ISO Clauses** — PDCA is a *cycle*; ISO 9001 *uses* PDCA. Do not equate them. > 4. **Skipping KPIs** — A 14-mark answer without measurable KPIs scores at most **8/14**. > 5. **Missing Arrows in Diagrams** — Mermaid / flowchart without directional arrows = **1 mark deduction**. > 6. **RPN Miscalculation** — Writing $S + O + D$ instead of multiplication = **2 marks lost** instantly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Process Approach (Module 2):** - ☐ **Definition:** Systematic management of interrelated processes (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 4.4). - ☐ **Six Process Elements:** Inputs, Outputs, Controls, Resources, Owner, KPIs. - ☐ **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (Deming Wheel) — applied to **every** process. - ☐ **Risk-Based Thinking:** Proactive identification of risks & opportunities. - ☐ **SIPOC Model:** Suppliers – Inputs – Process – Outputs – Customers (high-yield exam tool). - ☐ **Key Formulas:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$; $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$; $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$; $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$. - ☐ **Shipbuilding Application:** Hull cutting → Assembly → Welding → Inspection → Blasting → Painting → Erection → Outfitting → Launching → Trials → Delivery. - ☐ **Documented Evidence:** ITP, WPS, PQR, NDT reports, KPI dashboards, CAR / CAPA records. - ☐ **Continual Improvement Tools:** Kaizen, Lean, Six Sigma, 5-Why analysis. - ☐ **Avoid Pitfalls:** Always contextualize to shipbuilding; always include KPIs; always label diagrams. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's One-Line Mnemonic:** *"Process Approach = PDCA + SIPOC + RPN + KPIs — applied to shipbuilding."*

topics.title: System Approach to Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Approach to Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Systems Approach to Management** is a holistic, interdisciplinary methodology that views an organization (such as a shipyard) as an **integrated, purposeful system** composed of mutually interacting subsystems, which transforms inputs into outputs through structured processes while continuously adapting to its environment via feedback mechanisms.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST862)**, the systems approach is defined as a managerial philosophy that:

$$\text{System} = \{ \text{Inputs} \rightarrow \text{Process (Transformation)} \rightarrow \text{Outputs} \rightarrow \text{Feedback} \}$$

where the **organization** is treated not as a collection of independent departments, but as a unified whole where the performance of each element (design, procurement, fabrication, inspection, delivery) directly affects the integrity, cost, schedule, and quality of the final product (the vessel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> The systems approach treats management as a system of interrelated and interdependent sub-systems working in coordination to achieve organizational objectives through optimal resource utilization, with quality assurance as a cross-cutting sub-system spanning all functional areas.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board Examiner's Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **System** | A set of interrelated and interdependent components working together toward a common objective. |
| **Subsystem** | A smaller, self-contained system that is part of a larger system. |
| **Boundary** | The demarcation that separates a system from its external environment. |
| **Environment** | Everything outside the system boundary that influences it. |
| **Input** | Resources (material, information, human effort) entering the system. |
| **Throughput / Process** | The transformation mechanism applied to inputs. |
| **Output** | The finished product or service delivered to the environment. |
| **Feedback** | Information about output performance used to correct or improve the system. |
| **Synergy** | The principle that $1 + 1 = 3$; the whole is greater than the sum of its parts. |
| **Entropy** | The natural tendency of systems to move toward disorder if unmanaged. |
| **Equifinality** | Different starting conditions and paths can lead to the same end state. |
| **Homeostasis** | The self-regulating tendency of a system to maintain internal stability. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Shipyard is a Living Organism"** — An Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **modern shipyard** is the human body:
- The **heart** pumps oxygen (capital and energy) throughout the organization.
- The **lungs** absorb fresh ideas and contracts (inputs from the environment).
- The **skeleton** provides structure (organizational hierarchy and procedures).
- The **nervous system** carries signals (information flow and communication).
- The **immune system** fights disease (Quality Assurance and Safety Department).

If you treat a fever with only a band-aid (a localized fix), the underlying infection (root cause) spreads. Similarly, in shipbuilding, a **welding defect** is not just a weld problem — it may trace back to a **design error**, a **procurement issue** (wrong electrode), a **training gap** (welder skill), or a **supervision failure**. The **Systems Approach** forces the Quality Manager to look at the **entire chain**, not just the symptom.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Recall — "BEE-IPOF":**
> **B**oundary → **E**nvironment → **E**ntities → **I**nputs → **P**rocess → **O**utputs → **F**eedback.
> Memorize this sequence for any descriptive KTU question on systems theory.

## 1.4 Historical Context for KTU Examinations

| Era | Contributor | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **1940s** | Ludwig von Bertalanffy | General Systems Theory (biological foundations) |
| **1950s** | Norbert Wiener | Cybernetics (feedback control) |
| **1960s** | Stafford Beer | Management Cybernetics; Viable System Model |
| **1960s** | Jay Forrester | System Dynamics (industrial modeling) |
| **1970s** | ISO | Adopted systems thinking into quality standards (ISO 9000 family) |
| **Modern** | ISO 9001:2015 | Process approach + Risk-based thinking (systems view) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Characteristics of the Systems Approach to Management

The systems approach is defined by the following non-negotiable principles. Each is a **high-yield KTU 2024 board point** and students should be able to explain each in **2–3 lines** in the examination hall.

1. **Holism (Wholeness)** — The organization is studied as a whole, not as isolated parts. Quality cannot be "inspected in" at the end; it is the emergent property of all subsystems.
2. **Interdependence** — Every subsystem depends on others. A delay in steel procurement (procurement subsystem) cascades into fabrication delays and ultimately affects the delivery date.
3. **Hierarchy** — Systems are nested within larger systems. A *welding bay* is a subsystem of a *fabrication shop*, which is a subsystem of a *shipyard*, which is a subsystem of the *maritime industry*, which is a subsystem of the *global economy*.
4. **Openness** — Shipyards are *open systems* — they exchange materials, money, contracts, regulations, and waste with the environment.
5. **Goal-Directedness (Purposefulness)** — Every system exists to achieve an objective (e.g., deliver a vessel on time, on budget, to class-approved quality).
6. **Transformational Process** — Inputs (raw steel, drawings, labor) are converted into outputs (the finished ship).
7. **Feedback Loops** — Performance data (NCRs, weld defect rates, dimensional tolerances) is fed back to correct processes.
8. **Synergy** — Coordination between subsystems produces results unattainable by individual effort.
9. **Dynamic Equilibrium** — The system constantly adjusts to internal and external disturbances.
10. **Equifinality** — There are multiple valid paths to the same quality outcome.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Principles Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this topic is conceptual (Management discipline), the "formula sheet" is replaced by a **Principles × Application Matrix** tuned for KTU board valuation.

| # | Principle | Shipbuilding QA Interpretation | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Holism** | Total quality is a system property, not a department's property. | High (8–10 marks) |
| 2 | **Suboptimization Warning** | Optimizing one department (e.g., paint) may harm the whole (e.g., launch schedule). | High (8–10 marks) |
| 3 | **Open System Boundary** | Shipyard interacts with classification societies, customers, regulators, suppliers. | Medium (5–7 marks) |
| 4 | **Negative Entropy** | QA, training, calibration act as anti-entropy mechanisms. | Medium (5–7 marks) |
| 5 | **Feedback Control** | Inspection reports → corrective action → process improvement → re-inspection. | High (8–10 marks) |
| 6 | **Hierarchical Levels** | Welder → Foreman → Superintendent → QA Manager → Yard Director. | Medium (5–7 marks) |
| 7 | **Equifinality** | Same vessel quality achievable through different welding processes (SMAW, FCAW, GMAW). | Low (2–4 marks) |
| 8 | **Synergy (1+1=3)** | Coordinated design + procurement + fabrication outperforms siloed excellence. | High (8–10 marks) |

## 2.3 The Open System Model — Applied to a Shipyard

A shipbuilding enterprise, viewed as a system, can be modeled as:

$$
\text{System} = f(\text{Inputs}, \text{Transformation}, \text{Outputs}, \text{Feedback}, \text{Environment})
$$

### 2.3.1 Input Subsystem
- **Materials:** Steel plates, pipes, electrical cables, outfitting items.
- **Information:** Design drawings, class rules (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS), regulatory codes.
- **Human Resources:** Welders, fitters, engineers, supervisors.
- **Capital:** Working capital, loans, credit lines.
- **Equipment:** Cranes, CNC plasma cutters, welding machines, blasting equipment.

### 2.3.2 Transformation Subsystem
- **Design** (CAD/CAE) → **Material procurement** → **Cutting & forming** → **Sub-assembly** → **Block assembly** → **Erection on slipway** → **Launching** → **Outfitting** → **Sea trials** → **Delivery**.

### 2.3.3 Output Subsystem
- The **finished vessel** (cargo ship, tanker, naval frigate, offshore platform).
- **Documentation**: As-built drawings, Class certificates, stability booklets.
- **Customer satisfaction** + **reputation** in the maritime market.

### 2.3.4 Feedback Subsystem
- **Non-Conformance Reports (NCRs)**
- **Customer complaints and warranty claims**
- **Class surveyor's findings**
- **Internal audit observations**
- **Sea trial performance data**

### 2.3.5 Environment (External)
- **Suppliers** (steel mills, equipment vendors)
- **Customers** (ship owners, governments)
- **Classification Societies** (rule-makers and certifiers)
- **Regulators** (maritime authorities, pollution control boards)
- **Competitors** (other shipyards, globally)
- **Macro-environment** (trade, oil prices, geopolitical situation)

## 2.4 Why Systems Approach is Indispensable in Shipbuilding QA

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trigger Phrase** — Examiners often begin a 14-mark question with: *"Explain the systems approach to management. How is it applied in quality assurance during ship construction?"*

The systems approach is essential because shipbuilding is characterized by:

- **Extreme Interdependence:** Hull → piping → electrical → outfitting cannot be planned in silos; a piping change affects structural drawings.
- **High Consequence of Failure:** A single undetected weld defect can cause catastrophic loss of the vessel and human life.
- **Long Project Cycles:** 12–36 months from keel-laying to delivery demands continuous feedback.
- **Multiple Stakeholders:** Owner, designer, yard, class society, flag state, vendors — all are part of the *extended system*.
- **Regulatory Complexity:** SOLAS, MARPOL, IACS UR standards — every subsystem must comply.

## 2.5 Difference Between Traditional (Closed) and Systems (Open) Approach

| Dimension | Traditional Approach | Systems Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **View of Organization** | Collection of independent departments. | Network of interdependent subsystems. |
| **Decision-making** | Top-down, siloed. | Holistic, cross-functional. |
| **Problem Solving** | Symptom-based, reactive. | Root-cause, preventive. |
| **Quality View** | Inspection at the end. | Built into every process. |
| **Environment** | Ignored or treated as fixed. | Treated as dynamic and influential. |
| **Communication** | Vertical, hierarchical. | Multi-directional, networked. |
| **Suitability for Shipbuilding** | Poor — causes suboptimization. | Excellent — matches real complexity. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Application Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advisory for KTU 2024 Examiners:** This module is conceptual; therefore, the "derivation" is replaced by a **structured analytical walkthrough** showing how a Quality Manager applies the systems approach to a real shipbuilding scenario.

## 3.1 The Five-Step Application Framework

The systems approach to management is executed through a disciplined **five-step analytical framework**. Each step is explained below with explicit rationale and shipbuilding application.

### STEP 1 — Define the System Boundary

**Action:** Clearly delineate what is *inside* the system and what is *outside*.

**Rationale:** Without a boundary, the analysis becomes unbounded and unfocused. The boundary determines which variables are controllable and which are environmental.

**Shipbuilding Application:** For a QA subsystem, the boundary includes the welding procedure, welder qualification, electrode storage, weld inspection, and NCR closure — *inside*. The boundary excludes the world steel market — *outside* (treated as environment).

---

### STEP 2 — Identify the Subsystems and Their Interdependencies

**Action:** Decompose the system into subsystems and map the interconnections.

**Rationale:** A system is only as strong as its weakest interaction. Identifying links allows targeted intervention.

**Shipbuilding Application:** A steel-hull ship construction system decomposes into:

$$
\text{Hull System} = \{ \text{Design}, \text{Procurement}, \text{Cutting}, \text{Assembly}, \text{Welding}, \text{Inspection}, \text{Blasting}, \text{Painting} \}
$$

Each subsystem is linked: a drawing revision in *Design* forces a *Procurement* re-order, which delays *Cutting*, which delays *Assembly* — a clear cascade.

---

### STEP 3 — Establish Inputs, Processes, Outputs, and Feedback (IPOF Model)

**Action:** For each subsystem, document the four IPOF elements.

**Rationale:** This makes the system auditable, measurable, and improvable.

**Detailed IPOF for the Welding Subsystem (Worked Example):**

| Element | Welding Subsystem Description |
|---|---|
| **Inputs** | Steel plates (grade AH36), welding procedure specification (WPS), qualified welders, electrodes (E7018), welding machine, shielding gas. |
| **Process** | Pre-heating → tack welding → root run → fill passes → cap pass → post-weld cleaning. |
| **Outputs** | Welded joint meeting radiographic / ultrasonic acceptance criteria. |
| **Feedback** | NDT reports, welder efficiency data, electrode consumption logs, distortion measurements. |

---

### STEP 4 — Analyze Feedback and Implement Corrective Loops

**Action:** Compare actual outputs against planned outputs; identify deviation; correct the process.

**Rationale:** Without feedback, the system cannot self-correct, leading to *positive entropy* (disorder).

**Shipbuilding Application (Closed-Loop Feedback Flow):**

$$
\text{NDT Defect Found} \rightarrow \text{NCR Raised} \rightarrow \text{Root Cause Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Corrective Action} \rightarrow \text{Process Update} \rightarrow \text{Re-inspection}
$$

This is a classic *negative feedback loop* in cybernetic terms — it *reduces* the deviation from standard.

---

### STEP 5 — Optimize for Synergy, Not Suboptimization

**Action:** Evaluate decisions from the perspective of the *whole system*, not just the affected subsystem.

**Rationale:** The systems approach explicitly warns against *suboptimization* — a local optimum can be a global disaster.

**Shipbuilding Application:**
- **Wrong Decision (Suboptimization):** The painting department optimizes by using the cheapest paint to reduce their department cost. Result: paint fails in 6 months → warranty claim → global reputation damage → loss of future contracts.
- **Right Decision (Systemic):** The QA department selects a marine-grade paint with marginally higher cost, ensuring 10-year durability and protecting the yard's reputation and warranty liability.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical-Style Example: Defect Rate Reduction Using Feedback

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a management topic, KTU examiners occasionally frame a "calculation-style" question to test whether students can quantify the impact of systems feedback. Below is a representative worked example.

**Problem Statement (from a typical internal KTU assignment):**
A shipyard's hull fabrication line had a defect rate of **8%** in Q1. After applying the systems approach (welding procedure revision, welder retraining, electrode standardization), the rate dropped to **2%** in Q2. Calculate the percentage reduction and interpret the result from a systems perspective.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Given Data**
- Initial defect rate: $D_1 = 8\%$
- Improved defect rate: $D_2 = 2\%$

**Step 2 — Apply the Percentage Reduction Formula**

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{D_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{8 - 2}{8} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{6}{8} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = 0.75 \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**Step 4 — Systems Interpretation (the KTU-specific twist)**

The 75% reduction is **not** due to any single intervention. It is the **synergistic effect** of:
- Revised WPS (Process subsystem change),
- Retrained welders (Human subsystem change),
- Standardized electrodes (Input subsystem change),
- Improved inspection feedback (Feedback subsystem change).

Had the yard applied *only* welder retraining (a single-subsystem fix), the reduction would likely have been 20–30%, not 75%. This demonstrates the **synergy principle** ($1 + 1 = 3$).

**Final Answer:** A 75% reduction in weld defects was achieved, validating the systems approach.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Suboptimization vs. System Optimization in Shipbuilding

| Decision Scenario | Local View (Suboptimization) | Systemic View (Correct) | Net Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Choose cheapest steel supplier | Procurement cost saved. | Inferior steel → more weld repairs → delivery delay. | **Net Loss** |
| Skip pre-weld cleaning to save time | Faster throughput. | Higher porosity → NCR → rework cost. | **Net Loss** |
| Use one universal WPS | Simpler documentation. | Mismatch with material → joint failure. | **Net Loss** |
| Calibrate NDT equipment quarterly | Saves calibration cost. | Drift in readings → missed defects. | **Net Loss** |
| Invest in welder training | Higher HR cost. | Fewer defects, higher productivity, fewer NCRs. | **Net Gain (Synergy)** |
| Implement digital weld tracking | High IT investment. | Real-time data → instant feedback → systemic improvement. | **Net Gain (Synergy)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Open System Model of a Shipyard

<!--
Mermaid Safeguard Reminder:
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (yardInput, transformProc, outputShip, feedbackLoop, envClass).
- No reserved words used as IDs.
- All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown bold inside quotes).
-->

```mermaid
graph LR
    envMarket[ENV MARKET DEMAND REGULATIONS CLASS RULES]
    envSuppliers[ENV SUPPLIERS STEEL EQUIPMENT]
    envClass[ENV CLASS SOCIETY FLAG STATE]

    yardInput[INPUT SUBSYSTEM MATERIAL INFO LABOR CAPITAL EQUIPMENT]
    transformProc[TRANSFORMATION SUBSYSTEM DESIGN CUTTING ASSEMBLY WELDING OUTFITTING TRIALS]
    outputShip[OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM FINISHED VESSEL DOCUMENTATION REPUTATION]

    feedbackLoop[FEEDBACK SUBSYSTEM NCR AUDITS CUSTOMER COMPLAINTS SEA TRIAL DATA]

    envMarket --> yardInput
    envSuppliers --> yardInput
    yardInput --> transformProc
    transformProc --> outputShip
    outputShip --> feedbackLoop
    feedbackLoop --> transformProc
    outputShip --> envClass
    envClass --> feedbackLoop
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Hierarchical Decomposition of a Shipbuilding Quality System

```mermaid
graph TD
    sysGlobal[SHIPBUILDING GLOBAL SYSTEM]
    sysYard[YARD MANAGEMENT SYSTEM]
    sysDesign[DESIGN SUBSYSTEM]
    sysProc[PROCUREMENT SUBSYSTEM]
    sysFab[FABRICATION SUBSYSTEM]
    sysQA[QA SUBSYSTEM]

    subWeld[WELDING SUBSYSTEM]
    subNDE[NDE SUBSYSTEM]
    subPaint[PAINTING SUBSYSTEM]
    subMat[INCOMING MATERIAL CONTROL]
    subDoc[DOCUMENT CONTROL]

    sysGlobal --> sysYard
    sysYard --> sysDesign
    sysYard --> sysProc
    sysYard --> sysFab
    sysYard --> sysQA

    sysQA --> subWeld
    sysQA --> subNDE
    sysQA --> subPaint
    sysQA --> subMat
    sysQA --> subDoc
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Closed-Loop Feedback Cycle (Cybernetic View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[STANDARD WPS WELDER QUALIFIED MATERIAL APPROVED]
    B[EXECUTE WELDING PROCESS]
    C[INSPECT NDT VISUAL DIMENSIONAL]
    D{CONFORMANCE CHECK}
    E[ISSUE NCR ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS]
    F[CORRECTIVE ACTION WPS REVISION RETRAINING REWORK]
    G[UPDATE STANDARD CLOSE LOOP]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -->|PASS| G
    D -->|FAIL| E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> A
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Synergy vs. Suboptimization (Decision Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[QUALITY RELATED DECISION]
    q1{IS THE OPTION CHEAPEST LOCALLY?}
    path1[LOCAL COST MINIMIZATION]
    path2[WHOLE SYSTEM EVALUATION]
    res1[POSSIBLE SUBOPTIMIZATION HIDDEN COST IN OTHER SUBSYSTEMS]
    res2[SYNERGY MAXIMIZED GLOBAL QUALITY DELIVERY REPUTATION]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|YES WITHOUT ANALYSIS| path1
    q1 -->|NO| path2
    path1 --> res1
    path2 --> res2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]
**Define the systems approach to management. List any four of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** The systems approach to management is a holistic methodology that views an organization as a unified, purposeful system of interrelated and interdependent subsystems which transform inputs into outputs through coordinated processes, while interacting with and adapting to its environment through feedback mechanisms.

**Any Four Characteristics (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Holism** — the whole is greater than the sum of its parts.
2. **Interdependence** — subsystems depend on each other.
3. **Openness** — the system exchanges matter, energy, and information with the environment.
4. **Feedback** — outputs are monitored and used to correct processes.
5. **Goal-directedness** — every subsystem works toward a common objective.
6. **Synergy** — coordinated action produces results greater than individual effort.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]
**Explain the terms *entropy* and *equifinality* as used in systems theory. How do they apply to a shipbuilding organization?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Entropy (1.5 Marks):** Entropy is the natural tendency of a system to move toward disorder, decay, and loss of differentiation if no corrective energy is applied. In a shipyard, entropy manifests as increasing weld defects, deteriorating equipment accuracy, undocumented process changes, and skill erosion among workers. Quality assurance, training, calibration, and process audits act as *negative entropy* mechanisms that import order into the system.

**Equifinality (1.5 Marks):** Equifinality is the principle that a system can reach the same end state from different starting conditions and through different paths. In shipbuilding, the same class-approved hull quality can be achieved through multiple welding processes (SMAW, FCAW, GMAW), different steel suppliers, and various erection sequences — provided each path is properly controlled.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write only a textbook definition. Examiners award 4–5 marks for **application to shipbuilding**.
> - **Do NOT** list 10 characteristics without explanation. Pick 5–6 and *explain* them with one shipbuilding example each.
> - **Always** close your answer with the **consequence of NOT adopting the systems approach** — this is the favorite 2-mark tail of KTU evaluators.
> - **Avoid** vague phrases like *"it is important in management."* Use shipyard-specific terms: *weld procedure, NDT, class survey, block erection, slipway, dry dock, outfitting, NCR*.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, June 2024]
**(a)** Explain the systems approach to management in detail. Discuss its salient features and explain how it differs from the traditional (closed) approach to management. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the input–process–output–feedback (IPOF) model as applied to the *Quality Assurance subsystem* of a shipyard constructing a 50,000 DWT bulk carrier. Identify at least three feedback mechanisms used in this context. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B-A(a):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The systems approach, rooted in *General Systems Theory* (Ludwig von Bertalanffy, 1940s), treats an organization as a purposeful, open, integrated whole. It emerged as a response to the limitations of the *classical (closed)* approach, which viewed departments as independent units.

**Salient Features (4 Marks — 1 each):**
1. **Holism and Synergy:** The yard is studied as a unified whole. A defect in welding, when viewed systemically, may trace back to design tolerance, electrode storage humidity, or welder fatigue. Synergy implies that coordinated design-procurement-fabrication outperforms siloed excellence.
2. **Interdependence and Hierarchy:** A delay in the *steel cutting* subsystem (lower level) cascades into the *block assembly* subsystem, affecting the *launching* subsystem. Each level is nested in the next higher level.
3. **Open System Boundary:** The shipyard exchanges contracts, capital, raw materials, scrap, and waste with the environment (owners, class societies, regulators, suppliers).
4. **Feedback and Self-Regulation:** Inspection data, NCRs, sea-trial findings, and warranty claims form the feedback loop, enabling continuous correction.

**Comparison with Traditional Approach (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional (Closed) | Systems (Open) |
|---|---|---|
| View | Departmental silos | Integrated whole |
| Problem-solving | Symptom-based | Root-cause, systemic |
| QA location | End-of-line inspection | Embedded in every process |
| Communication | Vertical | Multi-directional |

**Conclusion:** The systems approach is essential in modern shipbuilding, where complexity, regulatory burden, and interdependence make siloed management impossible. **(Included in the 7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B-A(b):**

**Block Diagram of the QA Subsystem (3 Marks):**
A block diagram is required showing: *Inputs (steel, WPS, qualified welders, equipment, drawings)* → *Process (cutting, fit-up, welding, NDT, blasting, painting)* → *Outputs (class-approved welded joint, documentation)* → *Feedback (NCRs, audit reports, customer complaints, sea-trial data)* → back to *Process*.

**Explanation of the IPOF Model in Bulk Carrier Construction (2 Marks):**
- **Inputs** are controlled through *incoming material inspection* and *welder qualification records*.
- **Process** is controlled through *WPS qualification*, *welding parameter monitoring*, and *in-process inspection*.
- **Outputs** are verified by *class surveyors* (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's Register) and *dimensional surveys*.
- **Feedback** is captured through *NCR systems*, *internal audits*, and *management review meetings*.

**Three Feedback Mechanisms (2 Marks):**
1. **Non-Conformance Reports (NCRs)** — raised when output fails to meet specified quality; trigger root-cause analysis.
2. **Internal Quality Audits** — scheduled audits verify adherence to the Quality Management System (ISO 9001:2015).
3. **Sea-Trial Performance Data** — operational feedback from the vessel's first voyage; any defects trigger warranty action.
4. *(Optional fourth)*: Customer complaint handling and class surveyor observations.

**Closing Statement:** Through these feedback loops, the QA subsystem continuously self-corrects, embodying the systems approach. **(Included in 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, December 2023]
**(a)** What is meant by *synergy* and *suboptimization* in systems theory? Illustrate each with one example from a shipbuilding organization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of *negative entropy* in a shipyard. Explain how the Quality Assurance Department acts as an anti-entropy mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B-B(a):**

**Synergy (3.5 Marks):**
**Definition:** Synergy is the principle that coordinated action of subsystems produces a result greater than the algebraic sum of their individual efforts, expressed as $1 + 1 = 3$.

**Shipbuilding Example:** A shipyard that integrates its *design office*, *steel stockyard*, and *fabrication shop* through a digital Production Information Management System (PIMS) achieves faster block delivery, fewer rework cycles, and better material traceability than the sum of each department working independently. The integrated system delivers a 20% reduction in overall project time — a synergistic gain unattainable by any single department's effort.

**Suboptimization (3.5 Marks):**
**Definition:** Suboptimization is the pitfall in which a subsystem is optimized locally, but the optimization degrades the performance of the whole system.

**Shipbuilding Example:** The *painting department* chooses a low-cost marine paint to minimize its departmental cost. The paint fails prematurely in tropical waters, leading to extensive warranty claims, a damaged yard reputation, and loss of future contracts. The yard's *global* cost rises dramatically. Thus, the local optimum became the global disaster.

**Conclusion:** The systems approach insists on whole-system optimization, achieved only when synergy is maximized and suboptimization is consciously avoided.

#### **Model Solution for B-B(b):**

**Negative Entropy — Concept (2 Marks):**
In systems theory, *entropy* is the natural tendency of any system to move toward disorder, randomness, and loss of differentiation. *Negative entropy (negentropy)* is the import of order, information, and energy to counter this drift. Open systems must continuously import negentropy to survive and grow.

**Role in a Shipyard (2 Marks):**
A shipyard is a high-entropy-risk system: large workforce, complex processes, harsh environment, multiple contractors, long project cycles. Without intervention, defect rates rise, equipment drifts out of calibration, skills erode, and documentation decays — the system slides into disorder.

**QA Department as Anti-Entropy Mechanism (3 Marks):**
The Quality Assurance Department counters entropy through:
1. **Calibration of NDT equipment** — restores measurement order.
2. **Welder qualification and re-certification** — maintains skill order.
3. **WPS qualification and periodic revalidation** — restores process order.
4. **Internal audits and document control** — restores informational order.
5. **Trend analysis of NCRs** — converts disorderly defects into actionable knowledge.

**Conclusion:** QA is the principal negentropy-importing subsystem in a shipyard; without it, the system decays.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Pin This to Your Wall:**

- [ ] **System Definition:** A set of interrelated parts working toward a common goal.
- [ ] **Open System:** Exchanges material, energy, information with the environment. (Shipyard is an open system.)
- [ ] **Closed System:** No exchange (theoretical; no real organization is fully closed).
- [ ] **IPOF Model:** Inputs → Process → Outputs → Feedback. (Master this diagram.)
- [ ] **Synergy:** $1 + 1 = 3$. Whole > sum of parts.
- [ ] **Suboptimization:** Local optimum = global disaster. (Always a KTU favorite.)
- [ ] **Entropy:** Disorder tendency; countered by QA, training, calibration.
- [ ] **Equifinality:** Many paths → same goal.
- [ ] **Homeostasis:** Self-regulation toward internal stability.
- [ ] **Feedback Types:** *Negative feedback* (corrects deviation — used in QA); *Positive feedback* (amplifies — used for continuous improvement).
- [ ] **Hierarchy:** Welder → Foreman → Superintendent → QA Manager → Yard Director → Maritime Industry → Global Economy.
- [ ] **Application Keywords for Shipbuilding:** WPS, NDT, NCR, class society, block erection, outfitting, sea trial, slipway, dry dock.
- [ ] **Always end your answer** with the consequence of *not* adopting the systems approach (cost overrun, rework, class detention, warranty claim, ship loss).
- [ ] **Favourite Examiner Phrases** to recognize in the question paper:
  - *"Discuss the systems approach in the context of shipbuilding quality."*
  - *"Explain the IPOF model with reference to a shipyard."*
  - *"How does QA act as a feedback system?"*
  - *"Illustrate suboptimization with a shipbuilding example."*
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** **BEE-IPOF** — Boundary, Environment, Entities, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Feedback.
- [ ] **Diagrams to practice drawing in exam:** (i) Open system block diagram, (ii) Hierarchical decomposition of a shipyard, (iii) Closed-loop feedback cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** A 14-mark question on this topic almost always carries **2 marks for a labelled diagram**. Practice drawing the **Open System Block Diagram** and the **Closed-Loop Feedback Cycle** — they are universally applicable and examiner-friendly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continual Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continual Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continual Improvement** (as per **ISO 9000:2015**, Clause 3.3.13) is defined as:
> *"Recurring activity to enhance performance."*

In the context of **Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding (PENST862)**, Continual Improvement (CI) is a structured, organization-wide, and perpetual commitment to upgrading the shipyard's quality management system (QMS), production processes, product reliability (hull integrity, machinery performance, coating durability), and customer satisfaction (owner/class society compliance). It is the **10th Clause of ISO 9001:2015** and is considered the *engine room* of every certified shipyard QMS.

### 1.2 Continual vs. Continuous — A Critical Distinction

| Term | Meaning | KTU Exam Tip |
|------|---------|--------------|
| **Continual Improvement** | Recurring improvements in *increments* (step-wise) over time | **Always** use this term for ISO 9001 |
| **Continuous Improvement** | Non-stop, uninterrupted, flow-style improvement (Japanese *Kaizen* philosophy) | Often confused — examiners deduct marks if used interchangeably |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship as a Living System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Consider a ship sailing across the ocean. Even a newly built vessel requires **regular dry-docking**, hull cleaning, propeller polishing, engine tuning, and repainting. None of these are "repairs after a failure" — they are **planned, recurring enhancements** to keep the ship performing at its peak. *Continual Improvement in a shipyard works exactly the same way*: it is not about fixing what is broken (that's corrective action), but about *gradually raising the bar* so that fewer things break in the first place.

### 1.4 Why Continual Improvement Matters in Shipbuilding

- Reduces **rework** in block fabrication, pipe fitting, and electrical cabling.
- Cuts **dry-dock rectification costs** (often $\vert 10{-}15\% \vert$ of new-build contract value).
- Improves **on-time delivery (OTD)** — a key KPI under IACS UR S21/S22.
- Strengthens **class society audits** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) by demonstrating proactive QMS maturity.
- Lowers **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — internal failures, external failures, appraisal, and prevention costs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PDCA Cycle — Backbone of Continual Improvement

The **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle, also called the **Deming Cycle** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is the universal model used to drive CI in shipyards.

**Step 1 — PLAN:**
- Identify the problem (e.g., excessive weld rework in Panel Line A).
- Analyse root causes using the **5 Whys** or **Ishikawa Fishbone** tool.
- Set a measurable objective (e.g., reduce weld rework from $8\%$ to $\leq 3\%$ in 6 months).

**Step 2 — DO:**
- Pilot the improvement on a small scale (e.g., introduce *K-TIG* welding on one panel).
- Train welders, update WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) per **ASME IX / IACS UR W**.

**Step 3 — CHECK:**
- Measure results against KPIs.
- Use **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts (X-bar, R-chart) to verify stability.

**Step 4 — ACT:**
- If successful → standardize via **Management of Change (MOC)** and update QMS documents.
- If not → revise the plan and loop back (this is the *recurring* nature of CI).

> [!NOTE]
> The PDCA cycle must be **re-entered continuously** at the end of each Act phase — this is what makes improvement *continual*, not a one-off project.

### 2.2 Core CI Tools (The "Shipyard CI Toolkit")

| Tool | Purpose | Typical Shipbuilding Use |
|------|---------|--------------------------|
| **5 Whys** | Root cause identification | Tracing a pipe-support weld failure to welder qualification lapse |
| **Ishikawa Fishbone** | Categorical cause-effect analysis | Coating blistering causes grouped under *Material, Method, Manpower, Machine, Measurement, Environment* |
| **Pareto Analysis (80/20)** | Prioritisation of vital few problems | 80% of outfitting delays caused by 20% of missing-owner-supplied parts |
| **FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis)** | Risk ranking (RPN) | Evaluating risk of main engine turbocharger failure modes |
| **SPC / Control Charts** | Process stability monitoring | Monitoring plate-cutting tolerance in CNC plasma cutter |
| **Benchmarking** | Comparing against best-in-class | Comparing block erection cycle time against Korean / Japanese yards |
| **Kaizen Events** | Short-burst, focused CI workshops | 3-day event to reduce crane idle time in Module Assembly Bay |

### 2.3 Corrective Action vs. Preventive Action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Corrective Action (CA):** Action to **eliminate the cause** of a *detected* non-conformity (e.g., re-weld a defective joint and stop using the faulty electrode batch).
> - **Preventive Action (PA):** Action to **eliminate the cause** of a *potential* non-conformity (e.g., revise the electrode storage SOP after noticing humidity data trending upward).

Both CAs and PAs feed into Continual Improvement, but CAs are *reactive* while PAs are *proactive*.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Continual Improvement Quantitative Metrics (use `\vert` for absolute values to preserve Markdown table syntax)**

| Metric | Formula | Units | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ \;=\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF} \;+\; C_{AP} \;+\; C_{PV}$ | $\text{INR / USD per ship}$ | $C_{IF}$ = internal failure, $C_{EF}$ = external failure, $C_{AP}$ = appraisal, $C_{PV}$ = prevention |
| **Rework Cost Ratio (RCR)** | $RCR \;=\; \dfrac{C_{rework}}{C_{total\;production}} \times 100$ | $\vert \% \vert$ | KTU benchmark for shipyards: $RCR \leq 5\%$ |
| **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY \;=\; \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{total\;units}} \times 100$ | $\vert \% \vert$ | Higher = better CI maturity |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $OTD \;=\; \dfrac{N_{ships\;delivered\;on\;time}}{N_{ships\;contracted}} \times 100$ | $\vert \% \vert$ | Major shipowner KPI |
| **Process Capability Index $C_p$** | $C_p \;=\; \dfrac{USL \;-\; LSL}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | $C_p \geq 1.33$ = capable process |
| **Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$** | $C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Accounts for process centring |
| **Pareto Cumulative %** | $P_{cum,i} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j} \times 100$ | $\vert \% \vert$ | Used to identify "vital few" |
| **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** | $RPN \;=\; S \times O \times D$ | integer | $S$ = severity, $O$ = occurrence, $D$ = detection |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern shipyards (e.g., **Hyundai Heavy Industries, Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock**), CI is operationalised through:
- **Daily Kaizen Boards** at every panel line and outfitting shop.
- **Monthly Management Review Meetings** (ISO 9001 Clause 9.3) where CI targets are reviewed.
- **Digital CI tracking** via QMS software (e.g., *Intelex, ETQ Reliance, SAP QM*).
- **Lessons-Learned Databases** shared across projects to prevent repetition of past defects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ from First Principles

Let a shipbuilding process (e.g., **automatic plasma plate cutting thickness**) have:
- Specification limits: $LSL$ (Lower) and $USL$ (Upper) in mm.
- Process mean: $\mu$ (mm).
- Process standard deviation: $\sigma$ (mm).

**Step 1 — Define the natural process tolerance.**

For a normally distributed process, the natural spread covers $\pm 3\sigma$ about the mean, i.e., $6\sigma$ total.

$$
\text{Natural tolerance} \;=\; 6\sigma
$$

**Step 2 — Define the specification tolerance.**

$$
\text{Specification tolerance} \;=\; USL \;-\; LSL
$$

**Step 3 — Form the basic capability ratio $C_p$.**

$$
C_p \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Specification tolerance}}{\text{Natural tolerance}} \;=\; \dfrac{USL \;-\; LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for process centring.**

When the process mean $\mu$ drifts away from the centre of the specification, one side becomes critical. The two one-sided indices are:

$$
C_{pk,\;upper} \;=\; \dfrac{USL \;-\; \mu}{3\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk,\;lower} \;=\; \dfrac{\mu \;-\; LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the minimum (the binding constraint).**

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Interpretation (KTU Board standard):**
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → Six-Sigma level (world-class shipyard process).
- $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ → Capable (acceptable for class society).
- $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → Marginally capable — improvement needed.
- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → Incapable — must trigger a **PDCA improvement project**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: COPQ Reduction in a Panamax Bulker Build

A shipyard's data for a single vessel build (all values in $\text{₹ Lakhs}$):

- Internal failure (rework, scrap): $C_{IF} = 240$
- External failure (warranty, dry-dock claims): $C_{EF} = 180$
- Appraisal (inspection, NDT, class survey): $C_{AP} = 90$
- Prevention (training, WPS revision, FMEA): $C_{PV} = 60$

**Step 1 — Sum the components.**

$$
COPQ \;=\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF} \;+\; C_{AP} \;+\; C_{PV}
$$

$$
COPQ \;=\; 240 \;+\; 180 \;+\; 90 \;+\; 60
$$

$$
COPQ \;=\; 570 \;\;\text{₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 2 — After a CI project (Kaizen + FMEA), internal failure drops to $C_{IF}^{new} = 120$.**

$$
COPQ^{new} \;=\; 120 \;+\; 180 \;+\; 90 \;+\; 60
$$

$$
COPQ^{new} \;=\; 450 \;\;\text{₹ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the reduction.**

$$
\Delta COPQ \;=\; COPQ \;-\; COPQ^{new} \;=\; 570 \;-\; 450
$$

$$
\Delta COPQ \;=\; 120 \;\;\text{₹ Lakhs}
$$

$$
\text{Reduction \%} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta COPQ}{COPQ} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{120}{570} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reduction \%} \;\approx\; 21.05\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Often a modest $\sim 21\%$ COPQ cut is achieved by **doubling** the prevention budget — a classic *Pareto leverage point* that KTU examiners love to test.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right CI Tool

| Problem Type | Best CI Tool | Why |
|--------------|--------------|-----|
| Single isolated defect, unknown cause | **5 Whys** | Quick, low-cost, frontline-friendly |
| Multiple possible causes across departments | **Ishikawa Fishbone** | Categorises causes into 6 M's |
| Many defects, need to prioritise | **Pareto Chart** | Ranks by frequency / cost |
| High-consequence failure modes (engine, propeller, steering) | **FMEA** | Quantifies risk via RPN |
| Ongoing variability, need statistical proof | **SPC / Control Charts** | Detects special vs. common causes |
| Need to compare yard performance vs. competitors | **Benchmarking** | Provides external reference |
| Sudden, team-based burst of improvement | **Kaizen Event** | 3–5 day focused workshop |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The PDCA Cycle in a Shipyard Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN: Identify problem and analyse root cause using 5 Whys or Fishbone. Define KPI target."] --> B["DO: Pilot improvement on one block or one panel line. Train welders, update WPS and SOPs."]
    B --> C["CHECK: Collect data, plot SPC control charts, compare KPI with target. Validate Cpk and FPY."]
    C --> D{"Did KPI meet target?"}
    D -- YES --> E["ACT: Standardise the change via Management of Change. Update QMS documents. Roll out yard wide."]
    D -- NO --> F["ACT: Revise plan, identify new root causes, re loop back to PLAN with lessons learned."]
    E --> A
    F --> A
```

### 4.2 Continual Improvement — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT SIDE: Data Sources"]
        I1["Class society findings"]
        I2["Owner complaints and warranty claims"]
        I3["Internal NCRs and rework logs"]
        I4["Audit reports internal and external"]
        I5["Workforce suggestions and Kaizen cards"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["CI ENGINE: PDCA LOOP"]
        P["PLAN: Root cause analysis and target setting"]
        D["DO: Pilot implementation"]
        C["CHECK: SPC, KPI dashboard, Cpk computation"]
        AC["ACT: Standardise or revise"]
        P --> D --> C --> AC --> P
    end

    subgraph TOOLS["CI TOOLBOX"]
        T1["5 Whys"]
        T2["Ishikawa Fishbone"]
        T3["Pareto"]
        T4["FMEA"]
        T5["SPC Charts"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT SIDE: Improvements"]
        O1["Reduced rework and scrap"]
        O2["Lower COPQ"]
        O3["Higher OTD and FPY"]
        O4["Improved Cpk and process capability"]
        O5["Updated SOPs, WPS, and training records"]
    end

    subgraph GOVERNANCE["GOVERNANCE LAYER"]
        G1["Management Review ISO 9001 Clause 9.3"]
        G2["QMS Documentation Control"]
        G3["Corrective and Preventive Action Log"]
    end

    I1 --> P
    I2 --> P
    I3 --> P
    I4 --> P
    I5 --> P
    T1 --> P
    T2 --> P
    T3 --> P
    T4 --> P
    T5 --> C
    AC --> O1
    AC --> O2
    AC --> O3
    AC --> O4
    AC --> O5
    G1 --> AC
    G2 --> AC
    G3 --> P
```

### 4.3 Ishikawa Fishbone Template (Shipbuilding Coating Defect Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["COATING BLISTERING ON HULL BOTTOM"] --> M1["MATERIAL: Wrong paint, expired primer, contaminated solvent"]
    P --> M2["METHOD: Incorrect DFT, rushed overcoat interval, wrong applicator"]
    P --> M3["MANPOWER: Untrained painters, no supervision, fatigue"]
    P --> M4["MACHINE: Faulty airless spray, clogged nozzle, wrong pressure"]
    P --> M5["MEASUREMENT: Uncalibrated DFT gauge, missing surface profile check"]
    P --> M6["ENVIRONMENT: High humidity, low steel temperature, salt deposits"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between "Continual Improvement" and "Continuous Improvement". Why is the former the correct term in ISO 9001:2015?*
