> 3. For documentation hierarchy questions, students frequently **omit Level 4 (Records)** and lose 2 marks. Always mention all four levels.
> 4. Confusing "Quality Control" with "Quality Assurance" with "Quality Management" — these are **distinct terms** in ISO vocabulary and must not be used interchangeably.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Anchor:** QMS = *“Set of interrelated or interacting elements used to direct and control activities with regard to quality.”* (ISO 9001:2015)
- **Three Components:** Structure + Documentation + Resources.
- **Seven Principles (ISO 9001:2015):** Customer focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process approach, Improvement, Evidence-based decisions, Relationship management.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (the engine of continual improvement).
- **Documentation Pyramid:** 4 levels — Manual → SOPs → Work Instructions → Records.
- **Shipbuilding Relevance:** QMS is mandated by IACS UR Z17/Z23 and is a prerequisite for ISO 9001:2015 certification.
- **Key Metrics to Memorize:** Defect Density, FPY, COPQ%, OTD, $C_p \geq 1.33$.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always give **shipyard-specific examples** (welding, NDT, block erection, sea trials) — generic answers fetch only partial marks.
- **Forgotten but Critical:** The 2015 revision introduced **risk-based thinking** and **organizational context (Clause 4)** — both are high-yield topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Description and Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Description and Use

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Description and Use" Mean in Shipbuilding QA?

In the context of **Quality Assurance (QA) in Shipbuilding** (PENST862), Module 3 broadly focuses on the **inspection instruments, measuring tools, and Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) equipment** that shipyard quality controllers rely on. The phrase "**Description and Use**" refers to the systematic study of:

- What the tool/instrument **is** (its construction, working principle, technical specifications)
- What the tool/instrument is **used for** (its practical application in ship construction, hull inspection, weld quality verification, dimensional checks, and compliance with classification society standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Quality Assurance in shipbuilding relies on a chain of **measurement, inspection, and testing activities**. "Description and Use" covers the identification, constructional features, operating principle, and field application of the principal inspection instruments and NDT methods used at various stages of hull fabrication, outfitting, and sea trials.

## 1.2 Categories of Tools Covered Under "Description and Use"

| Category | Examples | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimensional Measuring Tools** | Vernier caliper, micrometer, dial gauge, feeler gauge | Linear dimensions, thickness, clearances |
| **Welding Inspection Tools** | Fillet weld gauge, weld gauge, macro-etch specimen kit | Weld profile, leg length, throat thickness |
| **Surface Inspection Tools** | Magnifying glass, boroscope/endoscope, dye penetrant kit | Crack, porosity, surface defect detection |
| **NDT Equipment** | Ultrasonic flaw detector, X-ray/gamma radiography unit, MPI yoke | Internal and sub-surface flaw detection |
| **Pressure & Leak Testing** | Hydrostatic test pump, manometer, soap solution kit | Tank, pipe, and boundary integrity |
| **Alignment & Level Tools** | Dumpy level, theodolite, plumb bob, laser aligner | Hull block alignment, shaft alignment |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Shipyard as a Hospital

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of a shipyard like a hospital. Just as doctors use stethoscopes, X-ray machines, and blood pressure monitors to "diagnose" a patient, shipyard QA engineers use calipers, ultrasonic flaw detectors, and hydrostatic pumps to "diagnose" the health of a ship.
> - The **vernier caliper** is the QA engineer's "ruler-with-superpowers" — capable of measuring to **0.02 mm** precision.
> - The **ultrasonic flaw detector** is the "X-ray machine" of the shipyard — it sends sound waves into steel to reveal hidden cracks.
> - The **hydrostatic test pump** is the "blood pressure cuff" — it pressurises a tank to check if it can safely hold its cargo.

**In short:** Every instrument described in this topic has a specific **diagnostic role** in ensuring the ship is built right the first time.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is KTU-High-Yield

- It directly links to the KTU 2024 Course Outcomes **CO3** (Apply appropriate QA/QC tools in ship construction) and **CO4** (Interpret inspection results against classification society standards).
- It is a **routine Part A (3-mark)** question: *"Describe any two measuring instruments used in shipbuilding QA."*
- It forms the **descriptive preamble** (3 to 5 marks) of a Part B (14-mark) question on NDT or weld inspection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Description and Use — Dimensional Measuring Instruments

### 2.1.1 Vernier Caliper

**Description:**
A vernier caliper is a precision instrument used to measure **linear external, internal, and depth dimensions** of a component. It consists of a **main scale** (graduated in mm) and a **sliding vernier scale** (typically 20 divisions over 19 mm of the main scale, giving a least count of **0.05 mm**).

**Use in Shipbuilding:**
- Measuring plate thickness (with extended-jaw models up to 600 mm).
- Checking pipe outer diameter and wall clearance.
- Verifying machined component dimensions during engine and stern gear outfitting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Least Count Formula:**
> $$\text{LC} = \frac{\text{Value of 1 Main Scale Division (MSD)} - \text{Value of 1 Vernier Scale Division (VSD)}}{\text{Number of Vernier Divisions}}$$

### 2.1.2 Outside Micrometer

**Description:**
A micrometer is a precision gauge that uses a calibrated **screw thread** (typically 0.5 mm pitch) to convert small linear movements into large, readable rotations on a thimble scale. The standard least count is **0.01 mm**.

**Use in Shipbuilding:**
- Final acceptance of shaft diameter and bearing journal dimensions.
- Plate and pipe thickness measurement above 25 mm.
- Verification of wire rope diameter during mooring system inspection.

### 2.1.3 Dial Indicator (Dial Gauge / DTI)

**Description:**
A Dial Test Indicator (DTI) converts small linear plunger displacement into amplified **rotational needle movement** through a rack-and-pinion or lever mechanism. Available in graduations of **0.01 mm** and **0.001 mm**.

**Use in Shipbuilding:**
- Monitoring **shaft alignment** and **deflection** during engine installation.
- Checking **flatness** and **run-out** of flanges, propeller shaft, and stern tube.
- Verifying **bearing clearance** after white-metal lining.

### 2.1.4 Fillet Weld Gauge (Weld Gauge)

**Description:**
A multi-purpose welding inspection gauge with movable blades to measure **leg length (z)**, **throat thickness (t)**, **convexity/concavity**, and **weld profile angle (α)**.

**Use in Shipbuilding:**
- Compliance check of fillet welds in hull, superstructure, and bracket connections against **IACS UR W17** and classification rules (e.g., **ABS**, **DNV**, **IRS**).
- WPS/PQR qualification tests.

### 2.1.5 Feeler Gauge

**Description:**
A set of accurately ground steel strips of known thickness (0.05 mm to 1.0 mm), used to measure small **gaps and clearances** between two mating surfaces.

**Use in Shipbuilding:**
- Measuring **gasket compression** in pipe joints.
- Checking **bearing clearance** and **stem clearance** in valves.

## 2.2 Description and Use — Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods

### 2.2.1 Visual Testing (VT) — the Foundation of All NDT

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Principle** | Direct or remote-visual observation of the component surface. |
| **Equipment** | Magnifying glass (10×), borescope/endoscope, mirrors, bright LED lamps, digital camera. |
| **Use** | First-line inspection of welds, hull plates, coating surfaces, and inside tanks. Mandated by **ASME V Article 9** and **IACS UR Z17**. |
| **Limitation** | Detects only surface-breaking defects. |

### 2.2.2 Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Principle** | A magnetic field is induced in a ferromagnetic part. Flux leakage at surface/near-surface discontinuities attracts ferrous particles, forming a visible indication. |
| **Equipment** | MPI yoke (AC or DC), dry powder or wet fluorescent suspension, UV lamp (for fluorescent MPI). |
| **Use** | Detection of **surface and sub-surface cracks** in welds, crankshafts, propeller shafts, and marine castings. |
| **Standard** | **ASTM E709**, **ISO 9934-1**, **IACS UR W27**. |

### 2.2.3 Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Principle** | A low-surface-tension penetrant liquid seeps into surface-breaking defects by capillary action. After cleaning, a developer draws the penetrant back out as a visible indication. |
| **Steps** | (1) Pre-clean → (2) Apply penetrant (10–30 min dwell) → (3) Remove excess → (4) Apply developer → (5) Inspect. |
| **Use** | Detection of **surface cracks, porosity, laps, and cold shuts** in non-magnetic materials (austenitic stainless steel, aluminium, bronze propellers). |
| **Standard** | **ASTM E165**, **ISO 3452-1**, **IACS UR W28**. |

### 2.2.4 Ultrasonic Testing (UT)

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Principle** | High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) are transmitted into the material via a **piezoelectric transducer**. Reflections from flaws or back-wall are analysed on a CRT/LCD display. |
| **Modes** | Pulse-Echo, Through-Transmission, TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction), Phased Array. |
| **Use** | Detection of **laminations, lack of fusion, porosity, and cracks** in thick hull plates (> 12 mm), propeller shaft, and welded joints. **Thickness gauging** of corroded plates. |
| **Standard** | **ASTM A435/A435M**, **ASTM E114**, **IACS UR Z23**, **ASME V Articles 4 & 5**. |

### 2.2.5 Radiographic Testing (RT)

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Principle** | Penetrating **X-rays or gamma rays** (Ir-192, Co-60) pass through the part and expose a film (or digital detector). Internal flaws appear as dark regions on the developed radiograph due to differential absorption. |
| **Use** | Volumetric inspection of **butt welds** in hull, high-pressure piping, and pressure vessel nozzle welds. Reveals **porosity, slag, lack of fusion, root concavity, and cracks**. |
| **Standard** | **ASTM E1032**, **ASME V Article 2**, **IACS UR W26**. |
| **Safety** | Strict radiation zoning, dosimeter use, RPO appointment, and **AERB** (India) / **IRRG** certification of operators. |

## 2.3 Description and Use — Hydrostatic & Leak Testing Equipment

### 2.3.1 Hydrostatic Test Pump

**Description:** A hand- or motor-operated pump capable of generating test pressures typically up to **1.5 × design pressure** (or **2 × design** for class-required tests). Connected to a calibrated **pressure gauge** (0.5 % accuracy, dead-weight tester calibrated) and a water reservoir.

**Use:**
- Testing of **fuel oil tanks, ballast tanks, cargo holds, and boiler shells** for leak-tightness.
- Hydrostatic test of **sea chest valves, ballast pipes, and fire-fighting lines** during commissioning.

### 2.3.2 Pneumatic / Air Leak Test Rig

**Description:** A low-pressure (typically ≤ 0.2 bar) air supply with manometer and **soap solution** (or proprietary leak detection fluid) for joint testing.

**Use:** Leak testing of compressed air systems, CO₂ fixed fire-fighting piping, and refrigerant lines where water testing is impractical.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — "Description and Use"

| Tool / Method | What It Does | Where It is Used in Shipbuilding | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vernier Caliper | Linear dimension ± 0.05 mm | Plate, pipe, machined parts | ISO 3599 |
| Outside Micrometer | Linear dimension ± 0.01 mm | Shaft, journal, wire rope | ISO 3611 |
| Dial Indicator | Run-out, alignment, flatness | Shaft alignment, flange check | ISO 2172 |
| Fillet Weld Gauge | Weld profile, leg length | Hull and bracket welds | ISO 2553 / AWS A2.4 |
| Feeler Gauge | Gap, clearance | Gasket, bearing | ISO 2251 |
| Visual Testing (VT) | Surface defects | All welds, coating | ASME V Art. 9 / IACS UR Z17 |
| MPT | Surface/near-surface cracks | Ferritic welds, shafts | ASTM E709 / ISO 9934-1 |
| DPT | Surface cracks | Austenitic SS, bronze | ASTM E165 / ISO 3452-1 |
| UT | Internal flaws, thickness | Thick plate, shaft, butt welds | ASTM E114 / IACS UR Z23 |
| RT | Volumetric internal flaws | Critical butt welds | ASTM E1032 / ASME V Art. 2 |
| Hydrostatic Pump | Pressure integrity | Tanks, piping, boilers | IACS UR P2 / ASME BPVC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (Examiner's Favourite):**
> - **Surface defect in austenitic SS** → DPT (NOT MPT, since SS is non-magnetic).
> - **Surface defect in mild steel** → MPT or DPT (MPT is faster, DPT is more sensitive).
> - **Sub-surface or internal defect in thick plate** → UT or RT.
> - **Volumetric record-keeping required for class** → RT.
> - **Thickness loss due to corrosion** → UT thickness gauging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Methods & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Least Count of a Vernier Caliper (KTU-type Calculation)

> **Question:** A vernier caliper has its main scale graduated in **mm** and **20 vernier scale divisions** coincide with **19 mm** on the main scale. Calculate its least count.

**Step 1 — Identify the value of 1 Main Scale Division (MSD).**
Since the main scale is graduated in mm:
$$\text{1 MSD} = 1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the value of 1 Vernier Scale Division (VSD).**
Given that 20 VSD = 19 MSD:
$$\text{1 VSD} = \frac{19 \text{ mm}}{20} = 0.95 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Least Count formula.**
$$\text{LC} = \text{1 MSD} - \text{1 VSD}$$
$$\text{LC} = 1.00 \text{ mm} - 0.95 \text{ mm}$$
$$\text{LC} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** The vernier caliper can resolve a measurement to **0.05 mm**. This is the standard resolution of metric vernier calipers used in shipyard QA.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Least Count of a Micrometer (Concept Reinforcement)

> **Question:** A metric micrometer has a screw pitch of **0.5 mm** and the thimble scale is divided into **50 equal divisions**. Calculate the least count.

**Step 1 — Recall the micrometer formula.**
$$\text{LC} = \frac{\text{Screw Pitch}}{\text{Number of Thimble Divisions}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**
$$\text{LC} = \frac{0.5 \text{ mm}}{50}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**
$$\text{LC} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** Standard least count of a metric micrometer = **0.01 mm**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Selection of NDT Method (Analytical Reasoning)

> **Question:** During the inspection of an austenitic stainless-steel pipe weld in a ship's LNG cargo system, a surface crack is suspected. As a shipyard QA engineer, which NDT method would you recommend and why?

**Step 1 — Identify the material property.**
Austenitic stainless steel is **non-magnetic** (permeability ≈ 1.0).

**Step 2 — Eliminate incompatible methods.**
- **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** requires the part to be **ferromagnetic** → ❌ Not applicable.
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** can detect the crack but is **over-specified and hazardous** for a simple surface check → ❌ Not first choice.

**Step 3 — Select the appropriate method.**
**Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** is the correct method because:
- Works on **any non-porous material** — magnetic or non-magnetic.
- Specifically detects **surface-breaking defects** like cracks, laps, and porosity.
- Safe, portable, and inexpensive.

**Step 4 — State the governing standard.**
- **ASTM E165** (or **ASTM E1417** for fluorescent DPT).
- **ISO 3452-1**.
- **IACS UR W28**.

> **Result:** Recommend **DPT** as per ASTM E165. Document the result in the **NDT Report (Form 4)** and sign off as a **Level II NDT operator**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — NDT Method Selection Logic

| Defect Type | Material | Stage | Recommended Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface crack | Mild steel | In-process | MPT (fast) |
| Surface crack | Austenitic SS | In-process | DPT |
| Surface crack | Aluminium bronze propeller | Final | DPT or FPI |
| Sub-surface slag | Butt weld, mild steel | Post-weld | UT or RT |
| Through-thickness lamination | New plate | Receipt | UT (shear wave) |
| Corrosion thickness | In-service hull plate | Survey | UT thickness gauge |
| Root concavity | Pipe butt weld | Post-weld | RT (preferred) |
| Weld profile, leg length | Any | In-process | Fillet weld gauge |
| Tank leak | Tank boundary | Pre-delivery | Hydrostatic test |
| Shaft concentricity | Stern shaft | Pre-launch | Dial indicator + laser |

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Hydrostatic Test Pressure Calculation

> **Question:** A ballast tank is designed for a working pressure of **0.4 MPa**. As per IACS UR P2, what is the minimum hydrostatic test pressure that must be applied at the yard?

**Step 1 — Recall the IACS UR P2 rule.**
For pressure vessels and tanks, the hydrostatic test pressure is **1.5 × design pressure** (or as agreed with the classification society, but not less than 1.25 ×).

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**
$$P_{\text{test}} = 1.5 \times P_{\text{design}}$$
$$P_{\text{test}} = 1.5 \times 0.4 \text{ MPa}$$
$$P_{\text{test}} = 0.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Add the gauge accuracy margin.**
The test pressure gauge must have an accuracy of **± 0.5 % of full scale** and must have been calibrated within the last **6 months** against a **dead-weight tester**.

> **Result:** Minimum test pressure = **0.6 MPa** (≈ 6 bar), held for **30 minutes** with no pressure drop and no visible leak. Recorded in the **Hydrostatic Test Certificate**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Description and Use of an Instrument (Generic Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow of any inspection instrument** described in this topic — from the physical stimulus to the QA engineer's decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Under Test] --> B[Physical Stimulus Input]
    B --> C[Transducer / Sensor]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning Unit]
    D --> E[Display / Indicator]
    E --> F[QA Engineer Interpretation]
    F --> G{Acceptance Criteria}
    G -- Pass --> H[Record in NDT Report]
    G -- Fail --> H2[Mark Defect and Issue NCR]
    H2 --> I[Rework / Repair]
    I --> J[Re-Inspection]
    J --> F
```

**Reading the diagram (top-to-bottom):**
1. **Component Under Test** — e.g., a hull plate, weld, or shaft.
2. **Physical Stimulus** — the input energy: mechanical contact, sound wave, magnetic field, X-ray beam, or pressurised water.
3. **Transducer / Sensor** — converts the stimulus into a measurable signal (e.g., piezoelectric crystal in UT, photographic film in RT, lever in a dial gauge).
4. **Signal Conditioning** — amplification, filtering, digitisation.
5. **Display** — analogue dial, CRT, digital LCD, or radiograph film.
6. **Interpretation** — the QA engineer reads the display.
7. **Decision** — compared against **classification society acceptance criteria** (IACS UR W, ASTM, ASME).
8. **Action** — pass → record; fail → **Non-Conformance Report (NCR)** → rework → re-inspection.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical NDT Method Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Defect to be Detected]) --> Q1{Material is Magnetic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Defect is Surface Only?}
    Q1 -- No --> DPT[Dye Penetrant Testing]
    Q2 -- Yes --> MPT[Magnetic Particle Testing]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Volumetric Record Needed?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> RT[Radiographic Testing]
    Q3 -- No --> UT[Ultrasonic Testing]
    MPT --> End([Document in NDT Report])
    DPT --> End
    UT --> End
    RT --> End
```

**Reading the diagram:**
This is the **decision tree** the QA engineer mentally walks through before picking an NDT method. The same tree appears as a frequent **3-mark short-answer** question in KTU exams.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Test Rig Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Tank[Test Water Reservoir] --> Pump[Hand / Motor Pump]
    Pump --> Pipe[Test Pipe Line]
    Pipe --> Gauge[Calibrated Pressure Gauge]
    Pipe --> Vessel[Component Under Test e.g. Tank]
    Vessel --> Vent[Air Vent Valve at Highest Point]
    Pipe --> Drain[Drain Valve at Lowest Point]
    Gauge --> Operator[QA Engineer and Class Surveyor]
    Vent --> Operator
    Operator --> Decision{Pressure Held for 30 min with No Drop}
    Decision -- Yes --> Pass[Issue Hydrostatic Test Certificate]
    Decision -- No --> Fail[Mark Defect and Issue NCR]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
Shows the **physical layout** of a typical hydrostatic test rig as used during the construction of ballast tanks, fuel tanks, and pipe sections. The QA engineer and the **classification society surveyor** jointly witness the test.

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **block-level functional schematics**. They are exam-ready illustrations that students can redraw within 4–5 minutes to earn full descriptive marks in KTU Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly describe any three dimensional measuring instruments used in shipbuilding quality assurance.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Vernier Caliper** — A precision slide-calliper with a main scale (mm) and a sliding vernier scale (typically 20 VSD = 19 MSD, **LC = 0.05 mm**). Used for measuring external/internal diameters, depths, and plate thicknesses up to 600 mm.

2. **Outside Micrometer** — A screw-based gauge with a **0.5 mm pitch spindle** and a 50-division thimble, giving a least count of **0.01 mm**. Used for precise measurement of shaft diameters, wire ropes, and bearing journals.

3. **Dial Indicator (DTI)** — A plunger-type gauge converting small linear movements (0.01 mm graduations) into amplified rotational needle deflection. Used for measuring **shaft alignment**, **run-out**, and **flatness** of flanges and bearings.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Magnetic Particle Testing and Dye Penetrant Testing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MPT | DPT |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Magnetic flux leakage attracts particles | Capillary action of penetrant into cracks |
| Material | Ferromagnetic only | Any non-porous material |
| Detects | Surface & near-surface defects | Surface-breaking defects only |
| Equipment | Yoke, powder, UV lamp | Penetrant, cleaner, developer |
| Standard | ASTM E709 | ASTM E165 |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the principle, equipment, procedure, and shipbuilding applications of **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**. State the governing standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A classification society surveyor requires **volumetric inspection** of a 28 mm thick butt weld in the side shell of a bulk carrier. Compare the suitability of **UT** and **RT** for this application and recommend one with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Principle:** Ultrasonic Testing uses high-frequency sound waves (**1 MHz to 25 MHz**) generated by a **piezoelectric transducer**. The waves are coupled into the material via a gel couplant. The waves travel through the material and reflect from any internal discontinuity (flaw) or the back wall. The reflected pulses are displayed on a **CRT/LCD screen** as peaks.

**Equipment:**
- Pulser-receiver unit (flaw detector)
- Piezoelectric probe (straight beam or angle beam, e.g., 45°/60°/70°)
- Couplant (gel, oil, or water)
- Calibration block (e.g., **IIW V1 block** or **AWS RC block**)
- C-scan / TOFD add-ons for advanced analysis

**Procedure:**
1. **Surface preparation** — remove paint, rust, and scale; smooth the surface to ≤ 6.3 µm Ra.
2. **Calibration** — set the **time-base, sensitivity, and range** on a calibration block.
3. **Scanning** — move the probe in a raster pattern with a defined overlap (typically ≥ 10 %).
4. **Evaluation** — measure **flaw depth, size, and location** from the A-scan display.
5. **Reporting** — record in the **NDT Report (Form 4)** with a sketch.

**Shipbuilding Applications:**
- Inspection of thick **butt welds** in hull plating, decks, and bulkheads.
- Detection of **laminations** in rolled plates during material receipt.
- **Thickness gauging** of corroded hull plates during in-service surveys.
- **Shaft and propeller** inspection for internal forging defects.

**Governing Standards:**
- **ASTM E114** (straight beam UT)
- **ASTM A435 / A435M** (plate lamination)
- **IACS UR Z23.1** (UT for hull)
- **ASME Section V, Articles 4 and 5**

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Comparison of UT and RT for the 28 mm Butt Weld:**

| Parameter | UT | RT |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity to planar defects (cracks) | **High** (cracks reflect strongly) | **Moderate** (depends on orientation) |
| Volumetric defects (porosity, slag) | Detected, but characterisation is qualitative | **Excellent volumetric record** on film |
| Permanent record for the class | Optional (A-scan printout) | **Mandatory radiographic film** |
| Radiation hazard | None | **Yes — requires licensed facility, RPO, and zoning** |
| Field use in yard | **Highly portable** | Source change and radiation safety overhead |
| Speed on thick weld | Fast | Slower due to setup and exposure |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (film + processing + safety) |

**Recommendation:** Although **RT** is the **classical volumetric method** that produces a permanent, easily archived film record and is widely accepted by all classification societies, for a 28 mm butt weld in a **yard environment** with a high inspection volume, **UT (specifically TOFD or Phased Array)** is the modern, faster, and safer alternative.

**Final choice:** Either **RT** (if the class surveyor mandates a film) or **UT with TOFD** (if accepted by the class under **IACS UR Z23.2 / Z23.3 alternative method approval**). The justification must be backed by the project Inspection and Test Plan (ITP).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the construction, working principle, and shipbuilding use of a **Hydrostatic Test Pump and Pressure Gauge system**. Mention the relevant IACS rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional flow of any NDT method** from the component under test to the final acceptance decision. Use a specific example (say, MPT of a propeller shaft). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Construction:**
A typical yard hydrostatic test rig consists of:
- **Test water reservoir** (clean, fresh, non-corrosive water).
- **Hand-operated or motor-driven pump** with a pressure-multiplier plunger.
- **High-pressure hose and fittings** rated for ≥ 1.5 × test pressure.
- **Calibrated pressure gauge** (Bourdon type, **0.5 % FS accuracy**, calibration valid for 6 months, dial marked in red at the test pressure).
- **Safety relief valve** set at 110 % of test pressure.
- **Vent valves** at the highest point and **drain valves** at the lowest point of the component.

**Working Principle:**
Water is incompressible, so when the pump pressurises the sealed component, any leak manifests as a **visible drip, pressure drop on the gauge, or weeping joint**. The test pressure is held for a stipulated period (**typically 30 minutes for tanks, 10 minutes for piping**) with no pressure drop.

**Shipbuilding Use:**
- **Tank testing** — fuel oil, ballast, fresh water, sludge, and cargo tanks.
- **Pipe spool testing** — fire-fighting main, ballast main, bilge line.
- **Valve body testing** — sea chest, overboard discharge, and boiler mountings.
- **Boiler pressure test** during commissioning.

**IACS Rule:** **IACS UR P2** (Pressure Vessels and Boilers — Materials, Design, and Testing) and **IACS UR Z17.1** for procedural compliance.

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

Refer to the **Block Diagram — Description and Use of an Instrument (Generic Functional Architecture)** in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Applied Example — MPT of a Propeller Shaft:**

| Step | MPT Application |
|---|---|
| 1. Component | Marine propeller shaft (forged steel, ferromagnetic) |
| 2. Stimulus | Magnetic field (AC or DC yoke) |
| 3. Transducer | Flux leakage pattern on the surface |
| 4. Signal Conditioning | Magnetic particles accumulate at leakage points |
| 5. Display | Visible indication under white or UV light |
| 6. Interpretation | QA Level II reads indication size, location, orientation |
| 7. Acceptance | Compare against **ASTM E709** and **IACS UR W27** acceptance limits |
| 8. Action | Pass → record in MPT report; Fail → NCR + grinding + re-test |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the standard** — Writing "ASTM E709" for DPT or "ASTM E165" for MPT. *Always match the method to the correct standard.*
> 2. **Forgetting the least count** — A vernier caliper question without the **LC = 0.05 mm** statement is considered incomplete. Always state the LC.
> 3. **Using MPT on stainless steel** — This is the **#1 trap answer**. Stainless steel is non-magnetic; only DPT or eddy current works.
> 4. **Skipping the safety aspect of RT** — Examiner expects a mention of **radiation zoning, dosimeter, and licensed operator**. Missing it = 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Ignoring the governing standard** — Every method description **must** end with a standard reference (ASTM / ISO / IACS UR). This is a 1-mark "freebie" that students often forget.
> 6. **No mention of calibration** — Pressure gauge, micrometer, and UT flaw detector all need **periodic calibration**. Examiners reward this.
> 7. **No mention of acceptance criteria** — A measurement is meaningless unless compared to a specification (e.g., ISO 2768, IACS UR W, class rules).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **"Description"** = *what it is* → construction, working principle, technical specification.
- **"Use"** = *where it is applied* → shipyard stage, component, classification rule.
- **Vernier caliper LC = 0.05 mm**; **Micrometer LC = 0.01 mm**; **Dial gauge = 0.01 mm**.
- **VT** is the **first and mandatory** step in any NDT sequence.
- **MPT** → ferromagnetic materials only (mild steel, carbon steel).
- **DPT** → any non-porous material (austenitic SS, aluminium, bronze).
- **UT** → internal defects in thick plate, thickness gauging, shaft inspection.
- **RT** → volumetric record, critical welds, piping — but **radiation hazard**.
- **Hydrostatic test pressure = 1.5 × design pressure** (IACS UR P2 default).
- **Every instrument must be calibrated** — micrometer with slip gauges, gauge with dead-weight tester, UT with IIW block.
- **Always cite a standard** — ASTM, ISO, ASME, IACS UR, or classification society rule.
- **Document everything** — NDT Report, Hydrostatic Certificate, ITP sign-off, NCR if failed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrated Quality Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integrated Quality Management System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Quality Management System (IQMS)** is a unified management framework that combines multiple discipline-specific management systems — such as **Quality Management System (QMS)**, **Environmental Management System (EMS)**, **Occupational Health and Safety Management System (OHSMS)**, and **Energy Management System (EnMS)** — into a single, coherent, and harmonized structure governed by common policies, processes, documentation, audits, and management review.

In the context of **shipbuilding and marine engineering**, an IQMS aligns **ISO 9001:2015** (Quality), **ISO 14001:2015** (Environment), **ISO 45001:2018** (OH&S), and the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** requirements with classification society rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, KR) into one operational system. This single system replaces parallel, redundant, and often conflicting subsystems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand that modern shipyards are required by **IACS QSCS** to maintain a single quality system that also satisfies customer, statutory, and regulatory requirements. The integrated approach eliminates duplication, reduces cost, and ensures uniform compliance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a shipyard as a **large kitchen in a five-star hotel**. Instead of having **separate managers** for hygiene, fire safety, customer satisfaction, ingredient sourcing, and energy saving — each running isolated checklists, separate audits, and producing their own reports — the hotel appoints **one head chef-coordinator** who merges all five concerns into a **single, unified kitchen operations manual**.

**Mapping the analogy:**

| Kitchen Concept | Shipyard IQMS Concept |
|---|---|
| Five different inspectors | Multiple standards (ISO 9001, 14001, 45001, etc.) |
| One unified SOP manual | One integrated management system manual |
| Single daily checklist | Single combined audit program |
| One head chef responsible | Top Management (Managing Director / CEO) |
| One performance review | Single Management Review Meeting |

The benefit is **simplicity, less paperwork, faster decision-making, and a holistic view of "how the shipyard is performing overall."**

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Examiner Standard Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Management System (MS):** A set of interrelated elements an organization uses to establish policies, objectives, and processes to achieve those objectives.
> - **Integration:** The process of combining multiple management systems into a single, common framework, sharing one policy, one manual, one set of procedures, and one audit schedule.
> - **Common Elements:** Clauses shared by different ISO standards (e.g., Context of Organization, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement — Annex SL High-Level Structure).
> - **Management Review:** A formal, periodic evaluation by top management of the suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness of the integrated system.
> - **Continual Improvement:** Recurring activity to enhance performance, using audit results, analysis of events, corrective actions, and management review outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Integration is Needed in Shipbuilding

A modern shipyard must simultaneously comply with:

1. **Customer contractual requirements** (hull construction, machinery installation, outfitting)
2. **Statutory regulations** (SOLAS, MARPOL, flag state rules)
3. **Classification society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, KR, IRS, RINA, ClassNK)
4. **International standards** (ISO 9001, 14001, 45001, 50001)
5. **Industry-specific schemes** (IACS QSCS, IMO, BIMCO, shipowner PMS requirements)

Without integration, the shipyard would maintain **5 to 8 separate manuals, separate audit teams, separate corrective action systems, and separate management reviews** — leading to inefficiency, conflicts, and excessive documentation cost.

### 2.2 Pillars of an IQMS

1. **Single Policy Statement** — One top-level policy addressing Quality, Environment, OH&S, and Energy.
2. **Single Manual** — One integrated Quality, Environmental, Health, Safety, and Energy (QEHSEn) manual.
3. **Unified Documentation** — Common procedures, forms, work instructions, and records.
4. **Combined Audit Program** — One internal audit team covering all standards in a single audit cycle.
5. **Joint Management Review** — One meeting where all system performances are reviewed together.
6. **Integrated Objectives and KPIs** — One set of measurable objectives aligned with business strategy.
7. **Common Risk-Based Thinking** — Unified risk and opportunity register (as per **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 6.1** and **ISO 45001:2018 Clause 6.1.2**).

### 2.3 ISO Annex SL — The High-Level Structure (HLS)

All modern ISO management system standards share the same **10-clause structure** (Annex SL). This is the **structural backbone** of any IQMS.

| Clause | Title | Purpose in IQMS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scope | Defines boundaries of the integrated system |
| 2 | Normative References | Lists standards used |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | Common glossary across all disciplines |
| 4 | Context of Organization | Internal/external issues, interested parties, scope |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment, policy, roles |
| 6 | Planning | Risks, opportunities, objectives, compliance obligations |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning and control, emergency preparedness |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review |
| 10 | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to **list the 10 clauses of Annex SL in order**. Memorize this sequence as a single block — it is the most common 3-mark question.

### 2.4 The PDCA Cycle in IQMS

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act** (Deming / Shewhart) cycle is the engine of an IQMS.

- **Plan:** Establish integrated objectives, processes, and risk treatment plans.
- **Do:** Execute the processes (welding, painting, pipe fitting, electrical installation, etc.) as per documented procedures.
- **Check:** Monitor, measure, audit, and review performance against policy and objectives.
- **Act:** Take corrective actions, eliminate root causes, and pursue continual improvement.

### 2.5 Steps to Build an IQMS in a Shipyard

1. **Top Management Commitment** — Issue a charter, allocate resources, and appoint an MR (Management Representative).
2. **Gap Analysis** — Compare current separate systems against integrated requirements.
3. **Define Scope** — Identify shipyard departments, processes, and physical boundaries covered.
4. **Develop Integrated Policy** — Single policy covering all disciplines.
5. **Map Processes and Interactions** — Create a process map showing inputs, outputs, and interfaces.
6. **Prepare Unified Documentation** — One manual, common procedures, integrated forms.
7. **Training and Awareness** — Train all employees on the integrated system.
8. **Implementation** — Roll out the integrated system across shipbuilding activities.
9. **Combined Internal Audits** — Conduct integrated audits on a planned schedule.
10. **Joint Management Review** — Hold one annual management review.
11. **Certification** — Apply for integrated certification (a single audit by a certification body for multiple standards — "combined audit").

### 2.6 KTU Formula / Metric Sheet (IQMS Specific)

> [!NOTE]
> Quality management relies on a small set of measurable indicators. KTU board questions may include 2-mark numerical sub-parts requiring the use of these formulas.

$$
\text{NCR Closure Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of NCRs closed within target date}}{\text{Total number of NCRs raised}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Audit Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of clauses audited}}{\text{Total number of applicable clauses}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}
$$

$$
\text{First Pass Yield (FPY)} = \frac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)} = \frac{\text{Number of defects}}{\text{Number of opportunities per unit} \times \text{Number of units}} \times 10^6
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that **DPMO** is converted to **Sigma Level** via the standard Six Sigma conversion table — KTU may ask a 2-mark conceptual question: *"What DPMO corresponds to 6-Sigma?"* The answer is **3.4 DPMO**.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding

In practical shipyard terms, an IQMS enables:
- **Single audit window** before a vessel's class survey — saving 30–40% audit man-hours.
- **Unified corrective action for welding defects, paint failures, and pipe leaks** — root cause analysis (5-Why, Fishbone) applied uniformly.
- **Joint management review** of **weld rejection rates, near-miss reports, environmental spills, and energy consumption** in one meeting.
- **Simpler compliance proof** when a shipowner, class surveyor, port state control, and certification body audit the same yard in a year.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating Audit Coverage (Step-by-Step)

A shipyard's integrated manual contains **120 clauses** drawn from ISO 9001, 14001, 45001, and IACS QSCS. During an internal audit, the audit team covered **96 clauses**. Compute the **Audit Coverage %**.

**Step 1 — Identify total applicable clauses.**

$$
\text{Total Clauses} = 120
$$

**Step 2 — Identify clauses covered in audit.**

$$
\text{Clauses Covered} = 96
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Audit Coverage formula.**

$$
\text{Audit Coverage} = \frac{\text{Clauses Covered}}{\text{Total Clauses}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{Audit Coverage} = \frac{96}{120} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{96}{120} = 0.80
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
\text{Audit Coverage} = 0.80 \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Final Answer:** The integrated internal audit covered **80% of clauses** — which falls short of the typical KTU / IACS benchmark of **100% coverage over a 3-year cycle** (i.e., all clauses audited at least once every three years).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Analysis

A shipyard reports the following annual figures in INR (₹):

- Prevention Cost: ₹ 12,00,000
- Appraisal Cost: ₹ 8,00,000
- Internal Failure Cost (rework, scrap, weld repairs): ₹ 25,00,000
- External Failure Cost (warranty, customer complaints, port state detentions): ₹ 15,00,000

Compute the **Total COPQ** and the **Failure Cost Ratio**.

**Step 1 — State the COPQ formula.**

$$
\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\text{COPQ} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Sum in pairs.**

$$
12{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
25{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Final sum.**

$$
\text{COPQ} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Failure Cost Ratio.**

$$
\text{Failure Cost Ratio} = \frac{\text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}}{\text{COPQ}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Failure Cost Ratio} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

**Interpretation:** **66.67% of total quality cost is failure cost** — meaning the shipyard is **"firefighting" rather than preventing**. A well-run IQMS typically drives this ratio below **50%** by investing more in prevention.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — First Pass Yield in a Pipe-Fabrication Shop

A pipe fabrication workshop produces **500 pipe spools** in a month. After first inspection, **45 spools** are rejected for dimensional and weld defects. Calculate **First Pass Yield (FPY)** and **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — assume each spool has **6 critical quality characteristics**.

**Step 1 — Compute FPY.**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Units passing = 500 − 45.**

$$
\text{Units Passing} = 500 - 45 = 455
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{455}{500} \times 100\% = 91\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute DPMO.**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 500 \times 6 = 3000
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{45}{3000} \times 10^6
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\frac{45}{3000} = 0.015
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.015 \times 10^6 = 15{,}000 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Sigma Level (using standard table).**

$$
15{,}000 \text{ DPMO} \approx 3.55 \text{ Sigma}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **3.55-Sigma** pipe fabrication line has roughly **15,000 defects per million opportunities** — meaning a shipyard operating at this level would experience one defective pipe spool in every 67 spools produced, which is unacceptable for marine class requirements. A Six-Sigma target of **3.4 DPMO** is the KTU benchmark.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Separate vs. Integrated Systems

| Parameter | Separate Systems | Integrated System (IQMS) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of manuals | 4 to 6 | **1** |
| Number of internal audits per year | 8 to 12 | **3 to 4** |
| Audit man-hours per year (approx.) | 1500 | **700** |
| Number of management reviews | 4 to 6 | **1** |
| Documentation effort | High (duplicated) | **Low (unified)** |
| Cost of compliance | High | **Reduced by 30–40%** |
| Communication across departments | Fragmented | **Unified** |
| Top management visibility | Partial | **Holistic** |
| Risk treatment | Discipline-specific | **Holistic** |
| Continual improvement | Independent | **Joint and prioritized** |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping of IQMS Requirements to Shipyard Functions

| Shipyard Function | ISO 9001 | ISO 14001 | ISO 45001 | IACS QSCS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steel cutting and forming | Process control | Waste segregation | PPE, machine guarding | Welding procedure qualification |
| Block assembly | Dimensional control | Emissions from welding | Hot work permits | WPS, PQR, WPQ |
| Pipe fabrication | FPY, leak testing | Solvent handling | Confined space entry | Hydrostatic test records |
| Outfitting | Workmanship standards | Dust, noise control | Working at height | Class survey attendance |
| Painting | Surface preparation | VOC emissions, waste paint | Respiratory protection | Coating system records |
| Sea trials | Functional testing | Discharge to sea | Crew safety | Class and flag trials |
| Dry-dock | Repair procedures | Hazardous waste | Confined space, lifting | Class renewal survey |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an IQMS in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management Leadership Commitment] --> B[Integrated QEHSEn Policy Statement]
    B --> C[Integrated Manual and Documentation Set]
    C --> D1[Quality Processes QMS]
    C --> D2[Environmental Processes EMS]
    C --> D3[OH and S Processes OHSMS]
    C --> D4[Energy Processes EnMS]
    C --> D5[IACS QSCS and Class Requirements]
    D1 --> E[Operational Implementation in Shipyard Departments]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F1[Monitoring and Measurement]
    E --> F2[Combined Internal Audit Program]
    E --> F3[Customer and Class Survey Feedback]
    F1 --> G[Single Management Review Meeting]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Continual Improvement and Corrective Action Loop]
    H --> A
```

---

### 4.2 PDCA Cycle Mapped to IQMS Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan: Integrated Objectives, Risk Register] --> P2[Do: Execute Production Welding, Pipe, Paint]
    P2 --> C1[Check: Internal Audits, KPIs, Customer Feedback]
    C1 --> A1[Act: Corrective Action, Root Cause Analysis]
    A1 --> P1
```

---

### 4.3 Process Map — Integration of Multiple Standards into One Audit Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[ISO 9001 QMS] --> M[Common Documentation and Procedures]
    S2[ISO 14001 EMS] --> M
    S3[ISO 45001 OHSMS] --> M
    S4[ISO 50001 EnMS] --> M
    S5[IACS QSCS] --> M
    M --> AUD[Single Integrated Internal Audit]
    AUD --> MR[Joint Management Review]
    MR --> CI[Continual Improvement Project]
    CI --> M
```

---

### 4.4 Risk-Based Thinking Flow in IQMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Identify Interested Parties] --> X2[Determine Issues and Requirements]
    X2 --> X3[Identify Risks and Opportunities]
    X3 --> X4[Plan Actions to Address Risks]
    X4 --> X5[Integrate Actions into QMS Processes]
    X5 --> X6[Evaluate Effectiveness of Actions]
    X6 --> X3
```

---

### 4.5 Hierarchy of Integrated Documentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1: Integrated Manual] --> L2[Level 2: Integrated Procedures]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Work Instructions and SOPs]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Forms Templates and Records]
    L1 --> EXT1[External Standard ISO 9001]
    L1 --> EXT2[External Standard ISO 14001]
    L1 --> EXT3[External Standard ISO 45001]
    L1 --> EXT4[External Standard IACS QSCS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define **Integrated Quality Management System (IQMS)**. List any **four benefits** of integrating QMS with EMS and OHSMS in a shipyard.

**Model Answer:**
An **IQMS** is a single, unified management system that combines multiple discipline-specific systems (Quality, Environment, OH&S, Energy) under a common policy, manual, audit, and management review framework.

**Four benefits in a shipyard:**
1. Reduction in duplicated documentation and audit effort.
2. Single management review covers all aspects — saving time and cost.
3. Holistic risk-based thinking across quality, safety, and environment.
4. Improved compliance with class society (IACS QSCS) and statutory requirements.
5. (Any four valid points — full credit.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** What is the **Annex SL High-Level Structure**? Why is it important for integration?

**Model Answer:**
**Annex SL** is the standardized framework published by ISO that defines the **10 common clauses** (Scope, Normative References, Terms, Context, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement) used by all modern ISO management system standards.

**Importance for integration:**
1. Provides a **common structural backbone** — making it possible to combine multiple standards into one manual.
2. Uses **identical terminology** across standards — reducing confusion.
3. Aligns **PDCA, risk-based thinking, and leadership requirements** uniformly.
4. Allows **combined audits and certifications** by a single certification body.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT list standards without explaining** their relevance to shipyard functions — examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for "irrelevant memorization."
> 2. When asked for "benefits," always **link to shipyard-specific examples** (e.g., welding, painting, sea trials). Generic answers score poorly.
> 3. For numerical sub-parts, **show formula → substitution → calculation → answer** in that exact order. Skipping substitution causes 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Annex SL clauses must be in correct order** — wrong sequence = 0 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) | [KTU University Exam — Model Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **structure of an Integrated Quality Management System** in a shipbuilding organization. Discuss the role of **Annex SL** in enabling integration of ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and ISO 45001. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard's integrated internal audit program covers **180 clauses** in a 12-month cycle. The audit team actually audited **150 clauses** and raised **40 non-conformities (NCRs)**, of which **32 were closed within target date**.
Calculate:
   (i) **Audit Coverage %**
   (ii) **NCR Closure Rate %**
   (iii) **Failure Cost Ratio** if Prevention = ₹6,00,000; Appraisal = ₹4,00,000; Internal Failure = ₹18,00,000; External Failure = ₹12,00,000. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

**Part (a) — IQMS Structure and Annex SL (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An IQMS in a shipbuilding organization is a unified system that combines the **Quality Management System (QMS)** as per ISO 9001, the **Environmental Management System (EMS)** as per ISO 14001, the **Occupational Health and Safety Management System (OHSMS)** as per ISO 45001, and the **IACS QSCS** requirements into a single, integrated framework.

**Structural Components of IQMS (4 marks):**
1. **Single Integrated Policy** — One policy covering quality, environment, OH&S, and energy commitments.
2. **Single Integrated Manual** — One manual referencing all applicable standards.
3. **Unified Procedures** — Common procedures for document control, internal audit, corrective action, management review, training, and records management.
4. **Combined Internal Audit Program** — One audit schedule covering all standards.
5. **Joint Management Review** — One review meeting with multi-disciplinary input.
6. **Integrated Documentation Hierarchy** — Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
7. **Process Approach with Risk-Based Thinking** — Each shipyard process (welding, painting, pipe fabrication) is mapped with its risks and controls.

**Role of Annex SL in Integration (2 marks):**
- Annex SL provides the **10-clause common structure** (Scope, Normative References, Terms, Context, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement).
- All modern ISO standards (9001, 14001, 45001, 50001) follow this structure, making integration **mechanically simple**.
- It enables **common terminology, common documentation, and combined audits** by a single certification body.
- It supports **PDCA cycle** and **risk-based thinking** uniformly across all systems.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions (7 marks)**

**(i) Audit Coverage % (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Audit Coverage} = \frac{\text{Clauses Covered}}{\text{Total Clauses}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Audit Coverage} = \frac{150}{180} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Audit Coverage} = 0.8333 \times 100\% = 83.33\%
$$

**(ii) NCR Closure Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{NCR Closure Rate} = \frac{\text{NCRs Closed on Time}}{\text{Total NCRs Raised}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{NCR Closure Rate} = \frac{32}{40} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**(iii) Failure Cost Ratio (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Total COPQ} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Total COPQ} = 40{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Failure Cost} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Failure Cost Ratio} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Audit Coverage = **83.33%**
- NCR Closure Rate = **80%**
- Failure Cost Ratio = **75%**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative | [KTU University Exam — Model Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the **steps involved in establishing an Integrated Quality Management System** in a shipyard. Highlight the **role of top management** and the importance of **combined internal audits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipe fabrication unit produced **1200 spools** in a quarter. **90 spools** failed first inspection. Each spool has **4 critical quality characteristics**. Calculate:
   (i) **First Pass Yield (FPY) %**
   (ii) **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**
   (iii) The approximate **Sigma Level** (use the standard DPMO-to-Sigma table). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

**Part (a) — Steps to Establish IQMS (7 marks)**

1. **Top Management Commitment (1 mark)** — Issue a written charter, allocate budget, appoint the Management Representative (MR), and demonstrate leadership by participating in policy formation and management review.
2. **Gap Analysis (1 mark)** — Compare existing separate systems against the integrated requirements of ISO 9001, 14001, 45001, and IACS QSCS. Identify missing or conflicting elements.
3. **Scope Definition (0.5 mark)** — Define shipyard boundaries, departments, processes, and applicability.
4. **Integrated Policy and Objectives (1 mark)** — Issue one policy and one set of measurable objectives covering quality, environment, OH&S, and energy.
5. **Unified Documentation (1 mark)** — Develop one integrated manual, common procedures, and shared records.
6. **Training and Awareness (0.5 mark)** — Train all employees, including outsourced contractors, on the integrated system.
7. **Implementation and Operation (1 mark)** — Apply the integrated system across welding, pipe fabrication, painting, outfitting, and sea trials.
8. **Combined Internal Audits and Joint Management Review (1 mark)** — Audit all standards in one program; review all performances in one management review meeting; feed outputs into continual improvement.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions (7 marks)**

**(i) First Pass Yield (2 marks):**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{1200 - 90}{1200} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{1110}{1200} \times 100\% = 92.5\%
$$

**(ii) DPMO (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 1200 \times 4 = 4800
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{90}{4800} \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.01875 \times 10^6 = 18{,}750 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**(iii) Sigma Level (2 marks):**

Using the standard DPMO-to-Sigma conversion table:

$$
18{,}750 \text{ DPMO} \approx 3.6 \text{ Sigma}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- FPY = **92.5%**
- DPMO = **18,750**
- Sigma Level ≈ **3.6 σ**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — IQMS**
>
> - **IQMS** = unified system combining QMS + EMS + OHSMS + EnMS + IACS QSCS.
> - **Annex SL** = 10-clause common structure for all modern ISO standards.
> - **PDCA cycle** is the operating engine of every integrated system.
> - **Single Policy, Single Manual, Single Audit, Single Management Review** = the "4 Slogans" of IQMS.
> - **Top Management** has the highest responsibility — leadership and commitment (Clause 5 of Annex SL).
> - **Risk-based thinking** (Clause 6) is mandatory under ISO 9001:2015 and ISO 45001:2018.
> - **IACS QSCS** specifically requires shipyards to maintain an integrated quality system certified by an IACS-accredited body.
> - **Common metrics:** Audit Coverage %, NCR Closure Rate, COPQ, FPY, DPMO, Sigma Level.
> - **3-Sigma ≈ 66,807 DPMO; 4-Sigma ≈ 6,210 DPMO; 5-Sigma ≈ 233 DPMO; 6-Sigma ≈ 3.4 DPMO** — memorize this conversion band.
> - **Benefits to memorize:** reduced documentation, reduced audit cost, unified risk treatment, single management review, better top-management visibility.
> - **Document hierarchy (4 levels):** Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records/Forms.
> - **Combined audit** is when a single certification body audits multiple standards in one visit; **integrated audit** is when the shipyard's own internal audit covers multiple standards in one program.
> - **Continual improvement** is achieved through the cycle: Audit → Nonconformity → Root Cause Analysis (5-Why / Fishbone) → Corrective Action → Verification → Closure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced QMS Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advanced QMS Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

**Advanced Quality Management System (QMS) Software** in the context of shipbuilding refers to the integrated, multi-layered digital ecosystem of enterprise applications that automate, monitor, and continuously improve quality processes across the entire shipbuilding lifecycle — from initial steel cutting and block fabrication to outfitting, commissioning, sea trials, and post-delivery Class surveys. It extends beyond the traditional document-centric ISO 9001 framework by incorporating real-time data acquisition, predictive analytics, and closed-loop corrective action management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):** "An Advanced QMS Software is a digitally integrated platform combining ERP, MES, PLM, and IoT-driven analytics to enforce, document, and statistically validate quality conformance at every stage of ship construction, ensuring traceability compliant with IACS, ISO 9001:2015, and Class Society requirements."

The principal sub-domains that constitute an Advanced QMS Software stack in a modern shipyard are:

- **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)** — Master data, procurement, finance, and project scheduling layers.
- **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** — Shop-floor execution, work-instruction dispatch, and real-time production tracking.
- **PLM (Product Lifecycle Management)** — Engineering data backbone linking 3D CAD models to manufacturing BOMs.
- **CAQ (Computer-Aided Quality)** — Statistical Process Control (SPC), CMM data management, and NDT records.
- **CRM / SRM** — Customer and supplier interface modules.
- **BI / Analytics Layer** — Dashboards powered by Big Data and AI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to know *what* each layer does, *how* they integrate, and *why* the shipbuilding industry (with its long lead times, complex supply chains, and Class Society oversight) uniquely requires such a digital QMS stack.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Shipyard Cockpit"

Imagine a modern shipyard as the **cockpit of a giant aircraft carrier**. The captain (Project Manager) cannot see the entire vessel from the bridge. He relies on:

- **Radar (IoT Sensors)** — Weld temperature probes, paint thickness gauges, GPS trackers on blocks.
- **Flight Instruments (MES)** — Real-time readouts of which block is at which outfitting stage.
- **Aircraft Maintenance Log (QMS Documents)** — Every rivet, weld, and valve catalogued.
- **Black Box (Database Layer)** — Records every action, retrievable 20 years later.
- **Autopilot & AI Co-Pilot (Predictive Analytics)** — Forecasts schedule slippage, weld defects, and material shortages *before* they happen.

The **Advanced QMS Software** is the *integrated glass cockpit* that fuses all these into one seamless interface. Without it, a shipyard is flying "blindfolded" — relying on paper travellers, manual sign-offs, and tribal knowledge — which is unsustainable for a vessel containing **millions of parts** and **thousands of kilometres of welding**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

The following quantitative metrics define a modern QMS deployment:

- **First-Time-Right (FTR) Rate** ≥ **85%** for outfitting blocks.
- **Weld Defect Rate (WDR)** < **2%** measured as NDT rejection per 100 m of weld.
- **NCR (Non-Conformance Report) Closure Cycle Time** ≤ **14 working days**.
- **Right-First-Time Rework Cost** targeted at < **3% of contract value**.
- **Document Retrieval Time** from QMS ≤ **30 seconds** vs. days in legacy paper systems.
- **Traceability Time** (Steel Plate → Installed Position) < **2 hours** end-to-end.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU exams:** Always pair a QMS module with the *specific KPI* it improves. ERP improves schedule adherence, MES improves FTR, CAQ improves WDR, and BI improves management visibility.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of an Advanced QMS Software

The architecture is conventionally decomposed into **five functional layers**, each with discrete data ownership and exchange protocols. KTU frequently tests this hierarchy.

### Layer 1 — Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) Layer
- **Function:** Master data management, procurement of steel and machinery, finance, HR, and project portfolio management.
- **Software Examples:** **SAP S/4HANA**, Oracle ERP Cloud, Infor LN (specifically designed for shipbuilding).
- **Quality Role:** Vendor qualification, purchase order specifications, and **mill certificate** linkage to inbound steel.

### Layer 2 — Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) Layer
- **Function:** Single source of truth for the 3D CAD model (hull forms, piping isometric, outfitting), engineering change management (ECN/ECR), and **BOM (Bill of Materials)** explosion.
- **Software Examples:** **AVEVA Marine (E3D/Outfitting)**, Siemens Teamcenter, Dassault ENOVIA, Hexagon SVID.
- **Quality Role:** As-built deviation tracking against the design 3D model, enabling *digital twin* comparison.

### Layer 3 — Manufacturing Execution System (MES) Layer
- **Function:** Converts the engineering BOM into routings and work instructions, dispatches them to the shop floor, and captures actuals (man-hours, machine time, defect tags).
- **Software Examples:** **AVEVA MES**, Siemens Opcenter Execution, Dassault DELMIA.
- **Quality Role:** Issue of **e-permits-to-work** for hot work in confined spaces (a critical shipyard safety/quality issue).

### Layer 4 — Computer-Aided Quality (CAQ) Layer
- **Function:** Captures **CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)** data, **SPC charts**, NDT results (UT/RT/MT/PT), and weld logs.
- **Software Examples:** **Hexagon PC-DMIS**, Mitutoyo COSMOS, Babbitt B&G Gauge, **IQM Software**, CAQ=AG (by AQON).
- **Quality Role:** Automatic generation of **Inspection Test Plans (ITP)** and Class surveyor-ready reports.

### Layer 5 — Business Intelligence & Analytics Layer
- **Function:** Aggregates data from all upper layers, applies statistical and ML models, and visualises KPIs.
- **Software Examples:** Microsoft Power BI, Tableau, Qlik, **AVEVA PI System** for time-series historian.
- **Quality Role:** Root cause analysis (Pareto, Fishbone), predictive defect modelling, and management review dashboards.

## 2.2 Data Flow & Integration Protocols

Advanced QMS software is **only as good as its integration backbone**. Three integration paradigms dominate:

- **Point-to-Point EDI (Legacy):** Direct file exchange between two systems (e.g., ERP → MES daily CSV). Brittle and unscalable.
- **ESB (Enterprise Service Bus):** Middleware layer (e.g., MuleSoft, IBM WebSphere) acting as a *universal translator* between heterogeneous systems.
- **API-First / Microservices (Modern):** RESTful APIs, **OPC UA** for shop-floor IoT, and event-driven messaging via **Apache Kafka**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Explain the role of OPC UA in modern shipyard QMS."* — **Answer:** OPC UA (Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture) is the **ISO/IEC 62541** standard for secure, platform-independent industrial communication. It allows welding robots, CMMs, and PLCs to publish structured quality data (telemetry, alarms, certificates) directly into the QMS database in real time, eliminating manual data entry.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application in Advanced QMS | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-Time-Right Rate | $\text{FTR} = \frac{N_{\text{passed first inspection}}}{N_{\text{total inspected}}} \times 100\%$ | Measures MES + CAQ effectiveness | % (target ≥ 85%) |
| Process Capability Index | $C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Used in SPC charts of weld bead geometry | Dimensionless (target ≥ 1.33) |
| Process Capability Index (centred) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Welding parameter stability monitoring | Dimensionless (target ≥ 1.33) |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | $\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^6$ | Six-Sigma quality benchmarking | ppm |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Multi-stage yield (cutting → welding → blasting) | Fraction (0–1) |
| OCR (Outfitting Completion Rate) | $\text{OCR} = \frac{\text{Committed Outfit Items}}{\text{Planned Outfit Items}} \times 100\%$ | QMS schedule adherence KPI | % |
| NCR Aging Index | $\text{NAI} = \frac{\sum (t_{\text{closed}} - t_{\text{raised}})}{N_{\text{NCR}}}$ | Average NCR closure time | Days (target ≤ 14) |
| IoT Data Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Welding arc current/voltage sampling | Hz (e.g., ≥ 10 kHz for arc stability) |
| Weld Heat Input | $H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | CAQ automatic validation against WPS | kJ/mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to compute $C_{pk}$, explicitly state the assumption of **normality** and **process stability** (no special-cause variation in the control chart). This is a high-frequency KTU trap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Advanced QMS software enables what is now termed the **"Smart Shipyard"** or **"Shipyard 4.0"**. Its practical utilities include:

1. **Single-Piece Flow Traceability:** From **mill certificate of a steel plate** → **cutting nest** → **panel identification** → **block assembly** → **erection unit** → **final hull section** → **sea trial dossier**. The Class surveyor can press a button and trace any weld in 2 hours.
2. **Predictive Defect Prevention:** AI models trained on historical NDT data flag high-risk welds *before* they are made, prompting intervention.
3. **Regulatory Compliance Automation:** IACS UR Z17 (Procedural Requirements for Service Suppliers) and ISO 9001:2015 clause 7.5 (Documented Information) become *self-auditing*.
4. **Digital Twin Alignment:** Real-time comparison of *as-built* point clouds (from laser scanning) vs. *as-designed* 3D model detects out-of-tolerance hull forms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) for a Weld Bead

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):** During automated fillet welding of a stiffener, the target leg length is **8.0 mm** with a specification tolerance of **± 0.6 mm**. A sample of 25 weld legs gives a mean $\mu = 8.12$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.18$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and comment on process capability.

**Given:**
- $\text{USL} = 8.0 + 0.6 = 8.6$ mm
- $\text{LSL} = 8.0 - 0.6 = 7.4$ mm
- $\mu = 8.12$ mm
- $\sigma = 0.18$ mm
- $n = 25$

**Step 1: Write the standard $C_p$ formula.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$C_p = \frac{8.6 - 7.4}{6 \times 0.18}$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator and denominator separately.**

$$\text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 1.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$6\sigma = 6 \times 0.18 = 1.08 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Divide to obtain $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \frac{1.2}{1.08} = 1.111$$

**Step 5: Compute the upper-side capability $C_{pu}$.**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{8.6 - 8.12}{3 \times 0.18}$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the numerator and denominator.**

$$8.6 - 8.12 = 0.48 \text{ mm}$$

$$3\sigma = 3 \times 0.18 = 0.54 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Divide to obtain $C_{pu}$.**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{0.48}{0.54} = 0.889$$

**Step 8: Compute the lower-side capability $C_{pl}$.**

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{8.12 - 7.4}{3 \times 0.18}$$

**Step 9: Evaluate the numerator and denominator.**

$$8.12 - 7.4 = 0.72 \text{ mm}$$

$$3\sigma = 0.54 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 10: Divide to obtain $C_{pl}$.**

$$C_{pl} = \frac{0.72}{0.54} = 1.333$$

**Step 11: $C_{pk}$ is the minimum of the two-sided capabilities.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\ C_{pl}) = \min(0.889,\ 1.333) = 0.889$$

**Step 12: Interpret the result.**

| Index | Value | Standard Industry Threshold | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_p$ | 1.111 | $\geq 1.33$ preferred | Marginal |
| $C_{pk}$ | 0.889 | $\geq 1.33$ for Class acceptance | **Not Capable** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often compute $C_p$ and *assume* it equals $C_{pk}$. The whole point of $C_{pk}$ is to penalise **off-centre processes**. Here, although the *spread* is acceptable ($C_p > 1$), the *centring* is poor (mean drifted upward to 8.12), and the lower $C_{pk}$ correctly flags the off-centring.

**Step 13: Engineering recommendation.** Adjust the robot travel speed or wire feed rate to shift the mean downward by 0.12 mm. Re-sample after 30 welds and recompute $C_{pk}$ — the target is $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of QMS Module vs. Shipyard Problem

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam table** that examiners frequently ask students to *complete* or *justify*.

| Shipyard Quality Problem | Primary QMS Module | Secondary Support Module | Expected KPI Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weld defects reappearing after WPS revision | CAQ (SPC + Weld Log DB) | PLM (revision control of WPS) | WDR ↓ from 3% to 1.5% |
| Material traceability gap during Class audit | ERP (Mill Cert linkage) | MES (Material Movement) | Traceability time ↓ to < 2 h |
| Schedule slippage due to NCR pile-up | MES (e-PTW & routing) | BI (Predictive bottleneck) | NCR closure time ↓ 30% |
| Hull-form deviation exceeding tolerance | PLM (3D model) + CMM/LASER | CAQ (as-built vs. as-designed) | Rework cost ↓ 50% |
| Supplier delivering out-of-spec steel | ERP (Vendor scorecard) | BI (Supplier analytics) | Incoming rejection ↓ 40% |
| Outfitting clashes discovered at erection | PLM (Clash detection) | MES (Work instruction) | Rework hours ↓ 25% |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — Computing Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

**Problem:** A block passes through 4 sequential stages: Cutting ($Y_1 = 0.96$), Welding ($Y_2 = 0.92$), Blasting ($Y_3 = 0.98$), Painting ($Y_4 = 0.94$). Compute RTY.

**Step 1:** State the RTY formula.

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$\text{RTY} = 0.96 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \times 0.94$$

**Step 3:** Multiply sequentially with intermediate rounding to 4 decimals.

$$0.96 \times 0.92 = 0.8832$$

$$0.8832 \times 0.98 = 0.8655$$

$$0.8655 \times 0.94 = 0.8136$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage.

$$\text{RTY} = 81.36\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpret. Roughly **18.64% of blocks** are incurring *some* rework or scrap across these four stages. The biggest contributors are welding (8% loss) and painting (6% loss) — these are the priority targets for Six-Sigma DMAIC projects.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Advanced QMS Software Stack

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the five-layer architecture, the data flow direction, and the key integration protocols between layers. This is the most examination-relevant visualisation in the module.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["LAYER 1 - ERP - Master Data and Finance"]:::layer1Style
    layer2["LAYER 2 - PLM - 3D CAD and BOM Engine"]:::layer2Style
    layer3["LAYER 3 - MES - Shop Floor Execution"]:::layer3Style
    layer4["LAYER 4 - CAQ - Quality and NDT Records"]:::layer4Style
    layer5["LAYER 5 - BI and Analytics - Dashboards and AI"]:::layer5Style
    iot1["IoT Sensors - Welding Arc CMM and Laser Scanner"]:::iotStyle
    class1["Class Society Surveyor Portal"]:::extStyle
    vendor1["Steel Supplier Mill Certificate API"]:::extStyle
    buyer1["Shipowner Client Portal"]:::extStyle

    vendor1 -- EDI or API --> layer1
    layer1 -- Material BOM and Routing --> layer3
    layer2 -- 3D Model and Engineering BOM --> layer3
    layer3 -- Production Actuals and AsBuilt Data --> layer4
    iot1 -- OPC UA Streaming --> layer4
    layer4 -- SPC and NDT Reports --> layer5
    layer1 -- Procurement and Schedule --> layer5
    layer5 -- Dashboards --> class1
    layer5 -- Quality Dossier --> buyer1

    classDef layer1Style fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer2Style fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b3d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer3Style fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#8a3a00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer4Style fill:#c62828,stroke:#6a1010,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer5Style fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#3a0a55,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef iotStyle fill:#f9a825,stroke:#a36800,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef extStyle fill:#eceff1,stroke:#455a64,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Workflow Diagram — Closed-Loop Corrective Action (CAPA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ncrRaise["NCR Raised in CAQ System"]:::qaStyle
    ncrClassify["Root Cause Analysis\n5-Why and Fishbone"]:::qaStyle
    ncrAction["Corrective Action Plan in MES"]:::qaStyle
    ncrImplement["Implementation on Shop Floor"]:::qaStyle
    ncrVerify["Effectiveness Verification by CAQ"]:::qaStyle
    ncrClose["NCR Closure and Lessons Learned in PLM"]:::qaStyle
    ncrFeed["BI Dashboard Update for Management Review"]:::qaStyle

    ncrRaise --> ncrClassify --> ncrAction --> ncrImplement --> ncrVerify --> ncrClose --> ncrFeed
    ncrFeed -.feedback.-> ncrRaise

    classDef qaStyle fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Data Integration Topology — API-First vs. ESB

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    legacy["Legacy ERP"]:::legacyStyle
    mesApp["MES Application"]:::modernStyle
    caqApp["CAQ Application"]:::modernStyle
    plmApp["PLM Application"]:::modernStyle
    esb["Enterprise Service Bus\nMiddleware Translator"]:::hubStyle
    apiGateway["API Gateway\nREST and OPC UA"]:::hubStyle
    lake["Data Lake for AI Training"]:::hubStyle

    legacy -- Batch Files --> esb
    mesApp -- REST API --> apiGateway
    caqApp -- OPC UA --> apiGateway
    plmApp -- GraphQL --> apiGateway
    esb --> apiGateway
    apiGateway --> lake

    classDef legacyStyle fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef modernStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef hubStyle fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to study these diagrams for KTU:** Memorise the *layer names in order from top-management to shop-floor* (ERP → PLM → MES → CAQ → BI). A frequent exam phrasing is *"From top to bottom, describe the flow of data in a shipyard QMS architecture."* — answer in that order.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between Computer-Aided Quality (CAQ) and Manufacturing Execution System (MES) software in a shipyard context. State one shipbuilding-specific function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **MES (1 Mark):** MES is a shop-floor execution system that converts the engineering BOM into routings and work instructions, dispatches them to the welders/fitters, and captures actuals like man-hours, machine time, and defect tags. *Shipbuilding-specific function:* issuing **electronic Permits-to-Work (e-PTW)** for hot work in confined compartments and tanks, which is critical for both safety and quality.
