- **CAQ (1 Mark):** CAQ is a quality-centric software layer that captures inspection data, manages CMM/SPC/NDT records, and auto-generates Inspection Test Plans. *Shipbuilding-specific function:* automated generation of **Class Society surveyor-ready weld inspection dossiers** and linkage of NDT results to specific weld seams via the WPS number.
- **Key Distinction (1 Mark):** MES focuses on *production execution and routing*; CAQ focuses on *inspection, conformance, and statistical validation*. They are tightly coupled, but MES optimises *throughput* while CAQ ensures *conformance*.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is OPC UA? Why is it significant for the Advanced QMS Software stack in a shipyard?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** OPC UA (Open Platform Communications – Unified Architecture) is an **ISO/IEC 62541** standard industrial communication protocol that enables secure, platform-independent exchange of structured data between shop-floor devices and higher-level software systems.
- **Significance for QMS (1 Mark):** It allows welding robots, CMMs, PLCs, and paint-thickness gauges to stream real-time telemetry (arc current, voltage, travel speed, dimensional readings) **directly** into the CAQ/BI layer, eliminating manual transcription errors and enabling real-time SPC.
- **Shipyard-specific advantage (1 Mark):** Unlike legacy OPC Classic (Windows-only), OPC UA runs on embedded Linux controllers and rugged tablets used in dusty, hot, salt-sprayed shipyard environments, making it the de-facto standard for **Smart Shipyard 4.0** deployments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Module 3 Internal Choice Set]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five-layer architecture** of an Advanced QMS Software stack in a modern shipyard. Explain the role of each layer with at least one shipbuilding-specific example. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A welding robot produces fillet welds with target leg length **10.0 mm ± 0.8 mm**. A sample of 30 welds gives mean $\mu = 10.18$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.22$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and comment on the process capability for shipbuilding Class acceptance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Diagram (3 Marks):** Draw a block diagram with five stacked rectangles labelled (top to bottom): **ERP → PLM → MES → CAQ → BI/Analytics**, with arrows indicating bi-directional data flow and an external IoT sensor cluster feeding the CAQ layer.

**Layer-by-Layer Explanation (4 Marks — 1 Mark each concise but shipbuilding-flavoured):**

1. **ERP Layer:** Master data, finance, procurement. *Shipbuilding example:* Manages **steel mill certificate linkage** for each inbound plate; vendor scorecard for suppliers.
2. **PLM Layer:** 3D CAD model of hull/piping, BOM explosion, engineering revisions. *Shipbuilding example:* Auto-routes an ECN (Engineering Change Notice) to all impacted drawings, work instructions, and Class-approved dossiers.
3. **MES Layer:** Shop-floor execution, routing, e-PTW, barcode/RFID scanning of parts. *Shipbuilding example:* Tracks a hull block from cutting shop → sub-assembly → block assembly → dock erection with **digital traveller**.
4. **CAQ Layer:** SPC charts, CMM data, NDT records, weld log, ITP auto-generation. *Shipbuilding example:* Stores every **UT/RT/MT/PT report** linked to a specific weld seam, ready for Class surveyor download.
5. **BI / Analytics Layer:** Dashboards, predictive models, management review. *Shipbuilding example:* AI model flags welds with high predicted defect probability based on historical NDT and parameter data.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $\text{USL} = 10.0 + 0.8 = 10.8$ mm
- $\text{LSL} = 10.0 - 0.8 = 9.2$ mm
- $\mu = 10.18$ mm, $\sigma = 0.22$ mm

**Step 1 (1 Mark): $C_p$ formula.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark): Substitute and compute.**

$$C_p = \frac{10.8 - 9.2}{6 \times 0.22} = \frac{1.6}{1.32} = 1.212$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark): Compute $C_{pu}$.**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{10.8 - 10.18}{3 \times 0.22} = \frac{0.62}{0.66} = 0.939$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark): Compute $C_{pl}$.**

$$C_{pl} = \frac{10.18 - 9.2}{3 \times 0.22} = \frac{0.98}{0.66} = 1.485$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark): $C_{pk}$.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.939,\ 1.485) = 0.939$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark): Interpretation.** $C_p = 1.21$ (marginal — just below preferred 1.33) and $C_{pk} = 0.939$ (**not capable** for Class acceptance, which requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$). The mean is drifted **upward** by 0.18 mm.

**Step 7 (1 Mark): Recommendation.** Reduce wire feed speed or adjust torch angle to shift the mean downward; re-validate after 30-sample re-check.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Module 3 Internal Choice Set]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of a **digital twin** in shipbuilding. How does it integrate with the Advanced QMS Software stack to enable closed-loop quality control? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A block fabrication yard has 4 sequential stages with the following first-pass yields: Cutting = 97%, Welding = 91%, Blasting = 96%, Painting = 93%. Compute the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and identify the priority stage for a Six-Sigma DMAIC project. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 Marks):** A **digital twin** in shipbuilding is a dynamic, real-time digital replica of a physical ship (or ship block) that mirrors its geometry, material state, and operating conditions throughout the lifecycle — from design, construction, and commissioning to in-service operation.

**Integration with QMS Layers (3 Marks — 1 Mark each link):**
- **PLM link:** The 3D CAD model forms the *as-designed* baseline of the digital twin.
- **CAQ link:** CMM and laser-scanner data form the *as-built* state, continuously updating the twin with actual dimensional deviations.
- **MES link:** Production status (paint applied, equipment installed, weld completed) is overlaid onto the twin, giving a *4D construction twin* (3D + time).
- **BI link:** Anomalies between *as-designed* and *as-built* are flagged in real time, triggering NCRs in CAQ before they propagate.

**Closed-Loop Quality Control Example (2 Marks):** When a laser scan detects hull plate flatness deviation of 3 mm (exceeding the 2 mm Class tolerance), the digital twin automatically: (i) generates an NCR in CAQ, (ii) notifies the MES of the affected routing, (iii) reassigns the rework to the next free fitter, and (iv) updates the BI dashboard for the Project Manager — all within minutes. This is the **closed-loop** that paper-based QMS cannot achieve.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark): State RTY formula.**

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{4} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark): Substitute values.**

$$\text{RTY} = 0.97 \times 0.91 \times 0.96 \times 0.93$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks): Multiply sequentially with intermediate steps shown.**

$$0.97 \times 0.91 = 0.8827$$

$$0.8827 \times 0.96 = 0.8474$$

$$0.8474 \times 0.93 = 0.7881$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark): Convert to percentage.**

$$\text{RTY} = 78.81\%$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark): Identify the bottleneck.**

| Stage | Yield | First-Pass Loss | % of Total Loss |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting | 97% | 3% | 14.2% |
| **Welding** | **91%** | **9%** | **42.5%** |
| Blasting | 96% | 4% | 18.9% |
| Painting | 93% | 7% | 33.0% |

**Step 6 (1 Mark): Conclusion.** Welding accounts for **42.5% of the total first-pass loss** despite being only one of four stages. This is the **priority stage for a Six-Sigma DMAIC project**, focusing on WPS optimisation, welder certification refresh, and real-time SPC monitoring via the CAQ layer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating ERP with PLM:** ERP handles *commercial/transactional* data; PLM handles *engineering/lifecycle* data. Writing "ERP stores 3D models" loses 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting Class Society context:** Shipbuilding QMS is unique because every major weld is witnessed by a Class Surveyor (LR, DNV, ABS, BV). Always tie the answer to Class compliance.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** $C_{pk}$ is dimensionless, but $\sigma$ and tolerances must carry the **mm** unit. Penalty: 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Omitting the centring in $C_{pk}$:** Many students compute only $C_p$ and skip $C_{pu}/C_{pl}$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Forgetting ISO 9001:2015 clause numbers:** When writing about documented information, cite **Clause 7.5**; for risk-based thinking, cite **Clause 6.1**. Examiners reward ISO awareness.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Five-Layer QMS Architecture (memorise in order):** ERP → PLM → MES → CAQ → BI/Analytics.
- **Key software brands (KTU favourite):** SAP/Oracle (ERP), AVEVA Marine (PLM/MES), Hexagon PC-DMIS (CAQ), AVEVA PI (BI Historian).
- **OPC UA** is the modern shipyard IoT standard (ISO/IEC 62541) — replaces OPC Classic and supports Linux-based controllers.
- **Digital Twin = 3D CAD (PLM) + As-Built Scan (CAQ) + 4D Schedule (MES)** — the foundation of closed-loop QMS.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the de-facto Class acceptance threshold; always compute both $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.
- **RTY** is a multiplicative yield metric — even 95% per stage can drop below 80% across 4 stages.
- **FTR ≥ 85%** is the industry benchmark for outfitting blocks; **NCR closure ≤ 14 days** is the cycle-time KPI.
- **Class Society** (LR, DNV, ABS, BV, IRS) is the *external customer* of the shipyard QMS — every QMS deliverable must be surveyor-readable.
- **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.5** = Documented Information; **Clause 9.2** = Internal Audit; **Clause 10.2** = Nonconformity and Corrective Action — these are the three most-cited clauses in KTU answers.
- **Six-Sigma DMAIC** is the analytical backbone of the BI layer — Define, Measure, Analyse, Improve, Control.
- **Integration architectures to know:** Point-to-Point EDI (legacy) → ESB middleware → API-First microservices (modern).
- **Traceability chain to remember:** Mill Certificate → Plate → Cutting Nest → Panel → Block → Hull Section → Sea Trial Dossier.
- **Predictive Quality** uses ML models on historical NDT data; the output is a *defect probability score* per weld.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Total Quality Management in Ship Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Total Quality Management in Ship Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Total Quality Management (TQM)?

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that systematically integrates all functions and processes within an enterprise — from product conception through design, production, delivery, and after-sales service — to continuously improve quality, satisfy customer requirements, and reduce total cost. In the shipbuilding context, TQM coordinates hull, machinery, electrical, outfitting, and shipyard fabrication processes under a unified quality culture, governed by ISO 9001 and Classification Society rules (IACS members such as **IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV, KR, CCS, RINA, RS, ClassNK**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** TQM is **NOT** a department — it is a *company-wide operating system*. Every welder, fitter, planner, designer, and manager in the shipyard is part of the quality chain.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Shipyard is a Hospital"
Imagine a shipyard as a **multi-specialty hospital**:

| Hospital Element | Shipbuilding TQM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient (the customer) | The Shipowner / Vessel |
| Doctors & Surgeons | Welders, Fitters, Outfitters |
| Pathology Lab | NDT Department (UT, RT, MT, PT) |
| Hospital Quality Cell | Shipyard QA/QC Department |
| Treatment Protocol | IACS Rules + Approved Drawings |
| Recovery Rate | First-Time-Right (FTR) Yield |

Just as a single hygiene failure in the OT can infect the patient, a single bad weld can fail the entire hull in service. TQM ensures **every "cell" of the shipyard protects the final product**.

---

## 1.2 Historical Evolution — The TQM Gurus

The foundation of TQM rests on the work of five pivotal quality pioneers. KTU examiners frequently ask comparative questions on these contributors.

| Guru | Core Contribution | Famous Saying |
|---|---|---|
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | Statistical Quality Control; PDCA Cycle origin | *"Data is the voice of the process."* |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management; System thinking | *"Quality is made in the boardroom."* |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Q-P-C) | *" Fitness for use is the definition of quality."* |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Concept of Total Quality Control | *"Quality is everyone's job, not just the QC dept."* |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Zero Defects; Quality is Free; 4 Absolutes | *"The system causes the problem, not the worker."* |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles; Fishbone diagram | *"Quality begins and ends with education."* |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss Function; Robust Design | *"The loss to society is the true cost of quality."* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Deming vs. Crosby in Shipbuilding Context:** Deming pushes for **statistical process improvement** (e.g., monitoring weld deposition rates), while Crosby enforces **zero-defect discipline** (e.g., no rework culture on the slipway). Modern Korean and Japanese shipyards blend both.

---

## 1.3 Why TQM is Critical in Shipbuilding

Shipbuilding is **unique among manufacturing industries** because the product:
1. Is **one-of-a-kind or low-volume** (each vessel differs by tonnage, flag, owner requirements).
2. Has an **operational life of 25–30 years** in harsh marine environments (corrosion, cyclic loading).
3. Must comply with **multiple regulatory bodies simultaneously** (Flag State, Class Society, MARPOL, SOLAS, IACS UR, IMO).
4. Involves **massive parallel workflows** (hull, machinery, electrical, outfitting) across many subcontractors.
5. Faces **late-stage defect detection** (a missed weld defect may surface during sea trials or even in service).

TQM addresses these by embedding quality at the **design and planning stage**, not just at the final inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (2024 Scheme):** When a 14-mark question is asked on TQM, examiners expect: *Definition → Gurus → Principles → Shipbuilding-specific application → Tools → Conclusion*. Memorize this 6-step answer skeleton.

---

## 1.4 Relationship Between QA, QC, QM, and TQM

This is a **high-frequency 3-mark question** in KTU examinations.

$$
\text{QM (Quality Management)} \;\supset\; \text{TQM (Total Quality Management)}
$$

$$
\text{TQM} \;=\; \text{QA} \;+\; \text{QC} \;+\; \text{Continuous Improvement} \;+\; \text{People Involvement}
$$

| Term | Focus | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Operational; detect defects | Reactive / Corrective |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | Systemic; prevent defects | Proactive / Process-based |
| **Quality Management (QM)** | Strategic; policy framework | Top-down |
| **TQM** | Cultural; holistic philosophy | Organization-wide, continuous |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Principles of TQM (ISO 9000 / KTU Favourite)

1. **Customer-Focused Organization** — The shipowner is the primary customer; the crew, cargo, and environment are secondary stakeholders.
2. **Leadership** — Senior yard management must visibly drive quality (e.g., weekly "Quality Walks" on the slipway).
3. **Involvement of People** — Every yard worker, from a welder to a procurement officer, is empowered and accountable.
4. **Process Approach** — Manage activities as interconnected processes, not isolated tasks.
5. **System Approach to Management** — Hull construction, outfitting, and machinery installation are subsystems of one shipbuilding system.
6. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Small, daily, incremental improvements (e.g., reducing pipe-bending rework by 2% every quarter).
7. **Factual Approach to Decision Making** — Decisions backed by SPC data, not intuition.
8. **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** — Long-term contracts with steel mills, propeller manufacturers, and engine makers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** **"C.L.I.P.S.M.F.M."** — *Customer, Leadership, Involvement, Process, System, Factual, Mutual, (Improvement added)*. Use this to recall the 8 principles in order.

---

## 2.2 The Deming Cycle — PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)

The **PDCA cycle** is the operational heartbeat of TQM. In a shipyard:

- **Plan:** Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) is drafted and qualified.
- **Do:** Welder executes the joint on the panel line.
- **Check:** NDT (UT/RT) verifies the joint; visual + dimensional inspection records data.
- **Act:** If defects appear, root-cause analysis updates the WPS → next panel benefits.

The cycle repeats block by block, panel by panel, block-construction after block-construction, until the hull is complete. This is **continuous improvement made visible**.

---

## 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Financial Backbone of TQM

The most important **quantitative tool** for TQM. COQ converts quality philosophy into rupees/dollars, making it palatable to shipyard CFOs.

$$
\text{Total COQ} \;=\; C_{P} \;+\; C_{A} \;+\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF}
$$

| Symbol | Category | Shipbuilding Example |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{P}$ | **Prevention Cost** | WPS qualification, welder training, design review, FMEA workshops |
| $C_{A}$ | **Appraisal Cost** | NDT (RT/UT/MT/PT), dimensional surveys, class surveyor fees, hydrostatic tests |
| $C_{IF}$ | **Internal Failure Cost** | Rework, scrap steel, repair welding, schedule slippage |
| $C_{EF}$ | **External Failure Cost** | Warranty claims, dry-dock repairs, customer compensation, class detentions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight (Examiner's Favourite):** A well-run shipyard invests heavily in $C_{P}$ to **drive down** $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$. The optimal strategy is to **minimize the SUM** $C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$, not just one component. A shipyard that skips WPS qualification to "save money" will pay 10× more in hull-rework.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In all formulas below, the absolute value / magnitude symbol is written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table layout.

| # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{COQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Total Cost of Quality | Cost justification of QA dept |
| 2 | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | First-Time Yield (single process) | Welder block-level yield |
| 3 | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_{i}$ | Rolled Throughput Yield (n processes) | Whole-ship FTR |
| 4 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | Defects per Million Opportunities | Six Sigma in shipyard |
| 5 | $\text{DPML} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times L}$ | Defects per Million Lines (weld) | Welding quality KPI |
| 6 | $\sigma\text{-level} = 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times \ln(\text{DPMO})}$ | Six Sigma conversion | Strategic benchmarking |
| 7 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability (centered) | Plate-cutting tolerance |
| 8 | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Process Capability (off-center) | Pipe-bending accuracy |
| 9 | $P(p) = \dfrac{n_{\text{defective}}}{n_{\text{inspected}}} \times 100$ | Percent Defective (p-chart input) | Lot-wise weld acceptance |
| 10 | $L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2}$ | Taguchi Loss Function | Customer-dissatisfaction cost |
| 11 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | Slipway / Goliath crane utilization |
| 12 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | OEE sub-factor | Yard equipment reliability |

where:
- $N_{\text{good}}$ = number of good units at first pass
- $N_{\text{total}}$ = total units processed
- $D$ = number of defects
- $U$ = number of units
- $O$ = opportunities for error per unit
- $L$ = lines of code / weld length inspected
- $n$ = sample size
- $k$ = Taguchi quality loss coefficient
- $x$ = actual value, $T$ = target value
- $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality
- $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, USL/LSL = upper/lower spec limits

---

## 2.5 The Seven Tools of Quality Control (Ishikawa's Old QC Tools)

| # | Tool | Primary Use in Shipyard |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone)** | Root-cause analysis of welding defects |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Daily defect tally at panel line |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | Monitor weld-bead height, plate-thickness, paint DFT |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Distribution of dimensional tolerances |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Identify the "vital few" defect types (e.g., 80% of rework from 20% of weld types) |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Correlation between welder skill and defect rate |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Document NDT process, WPS flow, material traceability |

> [!NOTE]
> **New 7 Tools (Management-Oriented):** Affinity diagram, Interrelationship Digraph, Tree Diagram, Matrix Diagram, Matrix Data Analysis, Arrow Diagram (PERT/CPM), PDPC. These are common in **14-mark KTU questions on TQM tools**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of TQM in Marine Engineering

1. **Classification Society Compliance** — IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS) endorses shipyards with mature QMS.
2. **Major OEM Requirements** — Navy, US Coast Guard, and cruise-line operators audit yard TQM maturity before placing orders.
3. **Warranty & Insurance** — A yard with documented TQM pays lower hull-insurance premiums.
4. **Lean Shipbuilding** — TQM + Lean = reduced block-construction cycle time.
5. **Defence & Naval Applications** — Warship programs demand AS9100 / naval TQM standards with full traceability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Quality Optimization Model

A KTU 2024 favourite. The yard manager wants the **optimal** $C_{P}$ that minimizes total COQ.

### Step 1 — State Assumptions
- Internal failure cost $C_{IF}$ is **inversely** related to prevention cost $C_{P}$.
- Appraisal cost $C_{A}$ is **relatively constant** for a stable product type.
- External failure cost $C_{EF}$ decreases as $C_{P}$ increases, but with a lag.

### Step 2 — Empirical Relationship
A widely cited industry model assumes:

$$
C_{IF}(C_{P}) \;=\; \dfrac{k_{1}}{C_{P}}
$$

$$
C_{EF}(C_{P}) \;=\; \dfrac{k_{2}}{C_{P} + c}
$$

where $k_{1}, k_{2}, c$ are positive constants determined from past yard data.

### Step 3 — Total COQ Function

$$
\text{COQ}(C_{P}) \;=\; C_{P} \;+\; C_{A} \;+\; \dfrac{k_{1}}{C_{P}} \;+\; \dfrac{k_{2}}{C_{P} + c}
$$

### Step 4 — Minimize by Calculus

$$
\dfrac{d(\text{COQ})}{dC_{P}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{k_{1}}{C_{P}^{2}} \;-\; \dfrac{k_{2}}{(C_{P} + c)^{2}} \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 5 — Optimal Prevention Cost

$$
1 \;=\; \dfrac{k_{1}}{C_{P}^{2}} \;+\; \dfrac{k_{2}}{(C_{P} + c)^{2}}
$$

This transcendental equation is solved iteratively (e.g., Newton-Raphson) on actual yard financial data.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given:** A shipyard records the following annual quality costs (in ₹ lakhs):
- Prevention cost $C_{P} = 50$
- Appraisal cost $C_{A} = 80$
- Internal failure cost $C_{IF} = 320$
- External failure cost $C_{EF} = 150$

**Required:** (a) Total COQ, (b) Percentage of revenue if annual turnover is ₹ 5,000 lakhs, (c) Comment on quality strategy.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Substitute into the master equation.

$$
\text{COQ} \;=\; 50 \;+\; 80 \;+\; 320 \;+\; 150
$$

$$
\text{COQ} \;=\; 600 \;\; \text{lakhs}
$$

**Step (b):** COQ as a percentage of revenue.

$$
\% \text{COQ} \;=\; \dfrac{600}{5000} \times 100 \;=\; 12\%
$$

**Step (c) — Examiner's expected commentary:** "A COQ of 12% of revenue is **high** for a modern shipyard (best-in-class is 4–6%). Internal failure cost $C_{IF} = 320$ dominates. The yard should **increase prevention spending** (welder training, design reviews) to reduce $C_{IF}$."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Indices $C_{p}$ and $C_{pk}$

### Step 1 — Define Specification Limits
For a shipyard plate-cutting operation:
- USL = +2.0 mm (max allowable over-thickness)
- LSL = −1.0 mm (min allowable)
- Target nominal thickness = 12.0 mm

### Step 2 — Compute Process Mean and Standard Deviation
After sampling 25 cut plates:
- Mean $\mu = 12.1$ mm
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 0.35$ mm

### Step 3 — Compute $C_{p}$ (Process Capability, Centered Case)

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 - (-1.0)}{6 \times 0.35}
$$

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{3.0}{2.10} \;=\; 1.43
$$

A $C_{p} \geq 1.33$ indicates the process is **capable** (industry benchmark for shipbuilding plating).

### Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (Capability Adjusted for Centering)

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; \min\left[\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]
$$

**Upper-side component:**

$$
\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 - 12.1}{3 \times 0.35} \;=\; \dfrac{-10.1}{1.05} \;=\; -9.62
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This negative value is mathematically valid but indicates the mean lies outside USL** — the process is wildly off-center. In a real KTU problem, ensure $\mu$ lies between LSL and USL. Let us revise: Suppose the correct mean is $\mu = 11.5$ mm.

**Corrected (with $\mu = 11.5$ mm):**

Upper-side:

$$
\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 - 11.5}{1.05} \;=\; \dfrac{-9.5}{1.05} \;=\; -9.05
$$

This remains problematic. Let us choose realistic shipyard data: a reheat flange thickness with USL = 14 mm, LSL = 10 mm, $\mu = 11.8$ mm, $\sigma = 0.45$ mm.

**Realistic Upper-side:**

$$
\dfrac{14 - 11.8}{3 \times 0.45} \;=\; \dfrac{2.2}{1.35} \;=\; 1.63
$$

**Realistic Lower-side:**

$$
\dfrac{11.8 - 10}{3 \times 0.45} \;=\; \dfrac{1.8}{1.35} \;=\; 1.33
$$

**Final $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; \min[1.63,\; 1.33] \;=\; 1.33
$$

Interpretation: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → process is **capable and well-centered**.

### Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion
Since $C_{pk} = C_{p} = 1.33$, the cutting process is **centered and capable**. No re-centering required. The yard may now attempt a **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** to push $C_{pk} > 1.67$ (a "world-class" target for naval applications).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Six Sigma DPMO to σ-Level Conversion

### Step 1 — Define DPMO
For a block-construction stage with:
- Units processed: $U = 500$ hull blocks
- Defects observed: $D = 85$
- Opportunities per block: $O = 20$ (weld starts, plate fits, paint DFT, dimension checks, etc.)

### Step 2 — Compute DPMO

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \;=\; \dfrac{85 \times 10^{6}}{500 \times 20} \;=\; \dfrac{85 \times 10^{6}}{10{,}000} \;=\; 8{,}500
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to σ-Level

$$
\sigma \;=\; 0.8406 \;+\; \sqrt{29.37 \;-\; 2.221 \times \ln(\text{DPMO})}
$$

$$
\ln(8500) \;=\; 9.0484
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; 0.8406 \;+\; \sqrt{29.37 \;-\; 2.221 \times 9.0484}
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; 0.8406 \;+\; \sqrt{29.37 \;-\; 20.0965}
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; 0.8406 \;+\; \sqrt{9.2735}
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; 0.8406 \;+\; 3.0453
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; 3.886
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation
A σ-level of **3.89** corresponds to a defect rate of **8,500 DPMO**. World-class shipyards target **4.5 σ (≈ 1,350 DPMO)** and naval programs target **5 σ (≈ 233 DPMO)**. The current yard is at the **industry average** — improvement is needed, primarily through prevention investment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which TQM Tool

| Problem Type in Shipyard | Best TQM Tool | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Excess weld porosity | Fishbone + Pareto + SPC | Find root cause, prioritize, monitor |
| Pipe-bending out of tolerance | Histogram + $C_{pk}$ analysis | Visualize distribution, check capability |
| Schedule slippage | Arrow Diagram + Flowchart | Critical path + workflow clarity |
| Customer complaint pattern | Pareto + Scatter | Identify dominant issues, correlate causes |
| Supplier quality variation | Control Chart + Supplier Audit | Monitor incoming material batch quality |
| Cost reduction | COQ model + Taguchi Loss | Quantify financial benefit of quality |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The PDCA Cycle in a Shipyard Welding Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN : Draft WPS Welding Procedure Specification and qualify with test plate"] --> B["DO : Welder executes the joint on the hull panel using qualified WPS"]
    B --> C["CHECK : NDT inspection UT RT MT PT and visual dimensional survey"]
    C --> D{"ACT : Are defects detected?"}
    D -- "Yes - Defects found" --> E["Root cause analysis 5 Why analysis update WPS retrain welder"]
    E --> A
    D -- "No - Joint accepted" --> F["Document lessons learned feed into next panel WPS"]
    F --> A
```

## 4.2 TQM Pillars — Integrated Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["PILLAR 1 - CUSTOMER FOCUS"]
        C1["Shipowner requirements"] --> C2["Class society rules"]
        C2 --> C3["Flag state regulations"]
    end
    subgraph P2["PILLAR 2 - CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT KAIZEN"]
        K1["PDCA cycle"] --> K2["Six Sigma projects"]
        K2 --> K3["Kaizen events on shop floor"]
    end
    subgraph P3["PILLAR 3 - PEOPLE INVOLVEMENT"]
        H1["Welder training"] --> H2["Quality circles"]
        H2 --> H3["Empowered workforce"]
    end
    subgraph P4["PILLAR 4 - PROCESS MANAGEMENT"]
        M1["WPS qualification"] --> M2["SPC control charts"]
        M2 --> M3["Poka yoke error proofing"]
    end
    subgraph P5["PILLAR 5 - FACT BASED DECISIONS"]
        F1["Defect data logging"] --> F2["Capability indices Cp Cpk"]
        F2 --> F3["Six Sigma metrics DPMO"]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Cost of Quality — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COQ["TOTAL COST OF QUALITY - COQ"]
        CP["CP - PREVENTION COST"] --> TOTAL["COQ SUM"]
        CA["CA - APPRAISAL COST"] --> TOTAL
        CIF["CIF - INTERNAL FAILURE COST"] --> TOTAL
        CEF["CEF - EXTERNAL FAILURE COST"] --> TOTAL
    end
    CP -. "WPS qualification, training, FMEA" .-> YARD["SHIPYARD FINANCIAL IMPACT"]
    CA -. "NDT, surveys, class fees" .-> YARD
    CIF -. "Rework, scrap, slippage" .-> YARD
    CEF -. "Warranty, compensation, drydock" .-> YARD
    YARD --> DEC["STRATEGIC DECISION: Increase CP to reduce CIF and CEF"]
```

## 4.4 The Seven QC Tools — Mapping to Shipyard Activities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["CAUSE AND EFFECT FISHBONE"]
        TB1["Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment"]
    end
    subgraph T2["CHECK SHEET"]
        TB2["Daily weld defect tally at panel line"]
    end
    subgraph T3["CONTROL CHART"]
        TB3["X-bar R chart for plate thickness"]
    end

        TB4["Distribution of pipe OD measurements"]
    end

        TB5["80 percent of rework from 20 percent of weld joints"]
    end
    subgraph T6["SCATTER"]
        TB6["Welder experience years vs defect rate"]
    end
    subgraph T7["FLOWCHART"]
        TB7["NDT process flow from VT to UT to RT"]
    end
    T1 --> SHIP["SHIP QUALITY DECISION"]
    T2 --> SHIP
    T3 --> SHIP
    T4 --> SHIP
    T5 --> SHIP
    T6 --> SHIP
    T7 --> SHIP
```

flowchart TD

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four principles of TQM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 + 2 split):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** TQM is a comprehensive management philosophy that involves every employee in continuously improving all processes — design, procurement, fabrication, outfitting, and after-service — to meet customer and regulatory requirements at the lowest total cost.
- **Four Principles (2 marks, 0.5 each):**
  1. **Customer Focus** — The shipowner, class society, and end users are the focus of every process.
  2. **Process Approach** — Hull, machinery, and outfitting activities are managed as a system of interconnected processes.
  3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Daily incremental improvement in weld quality, dimensional accuracy, and cycle time.
  4. **Factual Decision Making** — Decisions grounded in SPC data, DPMO, $C_{pk}$, and COQ analysis rather than intuition.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) in a shipbuilding context.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Preventive, system-oriented | Detective, product-oriented |
| **Focus** | The *process* that builds the product | The *product* itself |
| **Approach** | Plan → Do → Check → Act cycle | Inspect → Reject → Rework → Accept |
| **Example in Shipyard** | Approving WPS, qualifying welders, training | Visual inspection of welds, dimensional check, NDT |
| **Timing** | Before and during production | After production of an item or stage |
| **Tool** | Procedures, standards, audits | Gauges, NDT, statistical sampling |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** concept as applied to shipbuilding. Discuss each of the four cost categories with suitable shipyard examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard has the following annual quality cost data (in ₹ lakhs):

| Category | Value |
|---|---|
| Prevention Cost $C_{P}$ | 60 |
| Appraisal Cost $C_{A}$ | 90 |
| Internal Failure Cost $C_{IF}$ | 280 |
| External Failure Cost $C_{EF}$ | 120 |

If the annual turnover is ₹ 4,000 lakhs, **calculate** the total COQ, the COQ as a percentage of revenue, and **comment** on the quality strategy of the yard. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Cost of Quality in Shipbuilding — Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial impact of all activities related to achieving and ensuring product quality, expressed as the sum of prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs. In shipbuilding, COQ is significant because each vessel involves large tonnages of steel, complex outfitting, and long service life.

**The Four Categories (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Prevention Cost ($C_{P}$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects before they occur.
   - Examples: WPS qualification, welder certification, design review, FMEA workshops, supplier audits, preventive equipment maintenance.
2. **Appraisal Cost ($C_{A}$):** Costs of *detecting* defects through inspection and testing.
   - Examples: NDT (UT/RT/MT/PT) of welds, dimensional surveys, class surveyor fees, hydrostatic tests of tanks, sea trial instrumentation.
3. **Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$):** Costs of defects *found before* delivery to the shipowner.
   - Examples: Rework welding, scrap steel from miscut plates, schedule slippage from blocked slipway, repair of machinery, repainting of damaged coatings.
4. **External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$):** Costs of defects *found after* delivery — most damaging.
   - Examples: Hull warranty claims, emergency dry-dock repairs, shipowner compensation, class detentions, loss of reputation, insurance premium hikes.

**Strategic Insight (1.5 marks):** As $C_{P}$ rises, $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ fall disproportionately. The optimal TQM strategy is to **invest in prevention** so that the *total* COQ is minimized. Best-in-class shipyards have COQ in the range 4–6% of revenue, with the largest share being $C_{P} + C_{A}$.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Apply the COQ master equation.**

$$
\text{COQ} \;=\; C_{P} \;+\; C_{A} \;+\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
\text{COQ} \;=\; 60 \;+\; 90 \;+\; 280 \;+\; 120
$$

$$
\text{COQ} \;=\; 550 \;\;\text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute COQ as a percentage of revenue.**

$$
\% \text{COQ} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{COQ}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{COQ} \;=\; \dfrac{550}{4000} \times 100 \;=\; 13.75\%
$$

**Step 4 — Strategic Commentary (3 marks):**

- A COQ of **13.75% of revenue** is **alarmingly high**. World-class shipyards operate at 4–6%.
- **Internal failure cost $C_{IF} = 280$** is the dominant cost (≈ 51% of COQ), indicating **excessive rework and scrap**.
- **External failure cost $C_{EF} = 120$** is non-trivial, suggesting the yard is delivering vessels with warranty issues — a major reputational risk.
- **Prevention spending $C_{P} = 60$** is too low (only ≈ 11% of COQ). The yard should **double $C_{P}$** through:
  1. Welder re-certification programmes.
  2. Earlier-stage design review using DFMEA.
  3. Investment in mechanised welding to reduce human-error rate.
- Expected outcome: $C_{IF}$ may drop by 40% within 12 months, lowering total COQ to ≈ 8% of revenue — a target consistent with industry leaders.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)**. How is it applied to welding quality control in a modern shipyard? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Seven Tools of Quality Control (Old QC Tools)** of Ishikawa. For each tool, give **one shipyard-specific application**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) The Deming Cycle — PDCA Applied to Shipbuilding Welding [7 Marks]

**Conceptual Foundation (2 marks):** The PDCA cycle, also called the Deming Wheel or Shewhart Cycle, is a four-stage iterative methodology for continuous improvement: **Plan → Do → Check → Act.** It transforms abstract quality goals into operational, repeatable actions.

**Stage-by-Stage Application in Welding (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **PLAN:** The welding engineer drafts a **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** based on parent-metal grade (e.g., AH36), joint geometry, and Class Society rules. The WPS is qualified on a test coupon per **ASME Section IX / IACS UR W17**.
2. **DO:** The welder executes the joint on the hull panel using the qualified WPS. All parameters (current, voltage, travel speed, preheat) are logged on a **weld-log sheet**.
3. **CHECK:** **NDT inspectors** carry out Visual Testing (VT), Ultrasonic Testing (UT), Radiographic Testing (RT), and Magnetic Particle Testing (MT) per acceptance criteria. Dimensional surveys verify distortion control.
4. **ACT:** If defects appear, a **root-cause analysis (5-Why + Fishbone)** is performed. The WPS is updated, the welder is retrained, or the equipment is recalibrated. Lessons learned are documented and fed back into the next panel's plan.

**Why PDCA Works in Shipyards (1 mark):** Shipbuilding is **process-dominant**, and welding is a repetitive operation. PDCA enables incremental tightening of process capability, reducing $C_{IF}$ and improving DPMO metrics over successive construction cycles.

---

#### (b) Ishikawa's Seven Tools of Quality Control [7 Marks — 1 per tool]

| # | Tool | Description | Shipyard Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone)** | Maps potential causes of an effect across 6M categories: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Milieu | Root-cause analysis of **excessive weld porosity** in a panel |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | A simple, structured form for tallying defect occurrences | Daily tally of weld defects (porosity, undercut, lack of fusion) at the panel line |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | A time-series plot with UCL, LCL, and centerline for process monitoring | **X̄–R chart** of plate-cutting thickness variation across a shift |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Bar-chart representation of data distribution | Distribution of pipe outer-diameter measurements after bending |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Bar + line chart showing cumulative impact, separating "vital few" from "trivial many" | 80% of hull rework arises from 20% of joint types — prioritise corrective action |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Plot of paired data to identify correlation | Correlating welder years of experience with defect rate per 100 welds |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Symbol-based diagram of process sequence | NDT process flow: VT → MT → UT → RT → Acceptance Sign-off |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** These seven tools — when used together — provide a **data-driven, visible, and auditable** quality system. They are the **practical DNA of TQM** in any shipyard, regardless of yard size or vessel type.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Pitfalls (Read carefully before writing the exam):**
> 1. **Do not write only definitions.** Examiners award 60% of marks for **shipyard-specific application examples**. A generic answer on PDCA without a welding example will lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Numerical errors in COQ:** Many students add $C_{A}$ twice or forget to convert units (lakhs vs crores). Always cross-check totals.
> 3. **Misnaming the gurus:** Writing "Juran proposed PDCA" or "Deming invented the Pareto chart" guarantees zero marks for that line.
> 4. **Confusing QA with QC:** This is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in Part A. Memorize the difference in *one line* each.
> 5. **Skipping the Pareto principle in the Seven Tools:** Pareto is mandatory in KTU answers; examiners look for it explicitly.
> 6. **No diagram:** A 14-mark TQM answer without a flowchart / fishbone / control-chart sketch is graded at most 11/14.
> 7. **Writing Six Sigma formula without units:** Always carry the unit (DPMO is unitless, but σ-level is dimensionless). Note "≈ 3.89σ" not just "3.89".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION — Memorize This Section First**

- [ ] **TQM Definition:** Holistic, organization-wide, customer-focused, continuous-improvement philosophy covering all functions.
- [ ] **Gurus & One-Liners:** Shewhart (SQC), Deming (14 Points, system thinking), Juran (Q-P-C trilogy), Crosby (Zero Defects, 4 Absolutes), Feigenbaum (Total Quality Control), Ishikawa (QC Circles, Fishbone), Taguchi (Loss Function).
- [ ] **8 Principles (Mnemonic "C.L.I.P.S.M.F.M."):** Customer focus, Leadership, Involvement of people, Process approach, System approach, Continuous improvement, Factual decisions, Mutually beneficial suppliers.
- [ ] **PDCA:** Plan → Do → Check → Act — applied to WPS qualification, welding, NDT, and rework loops.
- [ ] **COQ Equation:** $C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$. Best yard target: 4–6% of revenue.
- [ ] **Yield Metrics:** $\text{FTY} = N_{\text{good}}/N_{\text{total}}$; $\text{RTY} = \prod \text{FTY}_i$.
- [ ] **Six Sigma:** $\text{DPMO} = D \times 10^{6} / (U \times O)$; $\sigma \text{-level} = 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(\text{DPMO})}$.
- [ ] **Process Capability:** $C_{p} = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL})/(6\sigma)$; $C_{pk} = \min[(\text{USL} - \mu)/(3\sigma),\; (\mu - \text{LSL})/(3\sigma)]$.
- [ ] **Interpretation Benchmarks:** $C_{p}, C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ = capable; $\geq 1.67$ = world-class; σ-level $\geq 4.5$ = Six Sigma quality.
- [ ] **Seven Old QC Tools:** Fishbone, Check sheet, Control chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart.
- [ ] **Seven New QC Tools (Management):** Affinity, Interrelationship Digraph, Tree, Matrix, Matrix Data Analysis, Arrow (PERT), PDPC.
- [ ] **5S in shipyard:** Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- [ ] **Shipbuilding-specific quality bodies:** IACS, ClassNK, DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS, KR, RS, RINA, CCS; ISO 9001; IACS QSCS.
- [ ] **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^2$ — every deviation from target causes financial loss to society.
- [ ] **OEE:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ — used for shipyard cranes, slipways, and panel-line equipment.
- [ ] **Difference Flash Card:** QA = *system* (prevent); QC = *product* (detect); QM = *policy*; TQM = *culture*.
- [ ] **Order of TQM Implementation:** Top-down commitment → Training → Process mapping → Pilot → Tool deployment → Supplier integration → Continuous improvement.
- [ ] **For Numerical Answer Framing:** Always show (i) formula, (ii) substitution, (iii) result with unit, (iv) interpretation. Missing any of these costs 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategic Quality Management and Human Factors in Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strategic Quality Management and Human Factors in Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strategic Quality Management (SQM)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Quality Management (SQM)** is the systematic integration of quality principles into the long-term strategic planning of an organization. It aligns quality objectives with the corporate vision, mission, and competitive strategy of a shipyard to achieve sustained customer satisfaction, regulatory compliance (IACS, IMO, SOLAS), and operational excellence across the entire ship lifecycle — from design and steel-cutting to commissioning, sea-trials, and in-service support.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, SQM is treated as a **management-level discipline** that transcends the inspection-based "find-and-fix" paradigm and embraces a **prevention-based, design-driven, and people-centric philosophy**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Shipyard-as-Aeroplane" Analogy

Imagine the shipyard is a **commercial airliner in flight**. The flight plan (corporate strategy) decides *where* the plane is going. The **autopilot** represents the Quality Management System (QMS), constantly making micro-corrections to keep the aircraft on course. The **pilots (top management)** set the strategic destination and monitor high-level instruments. The **cabin crew and ground engineers (workforce)** are the *human factors* who execute the procedures.

- If the **flight plan is flawed**, no amount of cabin crew excellence can land the plane safely → this is the role of **Strategic Quality**.
- If the **pilots ignore altitude warnings** or the **crew skips checklists**, even a perfect flight plan crashes → this is the role of **Human Factors in Quality**.

Thus, SQM ensures the shipyard is *aiming at the right harbour*, while Human Factors ensure *the crew can actually steer there without error*.

### Standard Metrics & Industry Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key SQM metrics (often appearing in KTU 14-mark questions):
> - **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** = Internal Failure + External Failure + Appraisal + Prevention
> - **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — Six Sigma benchmark: **3.4 DPMO** = 6σ
> - **Right-First-Time (RFT) rate** in shipbuilding target ≥ **95%**
> - **PPM (Parts Per Million)** defect rate at sea-trial acceptance
> - **NCR (Non-Conformance Report) closure cycle time** — KPI for human-factor efficiency

---

## 1.2 Human Factors in Quality (HFQ)

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Human Factors in Quality** is the multidisciplinary domain that examines how human capabilities, limitations, behaviour, training, motivation, communication, and organizational culture influence quality outcomes in ship construction. It draws from ergonomics, cognitive psychology, human reliability analysis (HRA), and organizational behaviour to minimize human error and maximize workforce contribution to quality.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Welding Robot vs. Welder" Story

A shipyard installs a fully automated welding robot on the block-assembly line. The robot produces welds of consistent quality 24/7. However, the **fit-up tolerances** depend on human plate-markers, the **welding procedure approval** depends on human inspectors, and the **robot's path programming** depends on human engineers. A single misread drawing by a human can cause **thousands of robot welds to be scrapped**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** In shipbuilding, *humans are still the dominant source of variation* because the product is **one-of-a-kind (custom-built)** — unlike automotive or electronics — so human factors cannot be eliminated, only *engineered, trained, and managed*.

### The Two Pillars of this Module

| Pillar | Strategic Focus | Tactical Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **SQM** | Vision, Mission, Policy, QMS deployment | Quality planning, QFD, Six Sigma |
| **HFQ** | Culture, leadership, competence framework | Training, ergonomics, human error reduction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Strategic Quality Management

### 2.1.1 Deming's 14 Points — Adapted for Shipbuilding

Deming's philosophy provides the **strategic backbone** for SQM. Mapping it to shipbuilding:

1. **Create constancy of purpose** — Long-term commitment to build defect-free ships (not just profit).
2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — Move from "acceptance sampling" to "zero-defect design".
3. **Cease dependence on inspection** — Build quality *into* the hull, outfit, and systems.
4. **End lowest-tender contracting** — Source steel, pipes, equipment from quality-rated vendors (ISO 9001 + IACS QSCS).
5. **Improve constantly** — Kaizen in every department (steel stockyard, panel line, outfitting dock).
6. **Institute training on the job** — Welders, fitters, NDT inspectors.
7. **Institute leadership** — Supervisors must *coach*, not police.
8. **Drive out fear** — So workers report near-misses (e.g., misalignment of a propeller shaft) without blame.
9. **Break down barriers between departments** — Design ↔ Production ↔ Quality ↔ Procurement integration.
10. **Eliminate slogans** — "Zero defect tomorrow" is meaningless without process change.
11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** — They incentivize rework-hiding.
12. **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship** — Clean welding bays, calibrated tools.
13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education** — Continuous learning in NDT, coatings, marine electrical.
14. **Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation** — Top-down + bottom-up.

### 2.1.2 Juran's Quality Trilogy (Strategic Levels)

Juran's framework maps directly to shipyard strategy:

| Trilogy Stage | Shipyard Activity | Strategic Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Planning** | Identify customers (Owner, Class, Navy), establish SPC goals | Project Quality Plan (PQP) |
| **Quality Control** | Evaluate actual welding, coating, alignment performance vs. plan | ITP, NCR, WPS/PQR |
| **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement (kaizen) | Quality Circles, Six Sigma projects |

### 2.1.3 Crosby's Zero-Defect Philosophy

- Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness").
- System of prevention, not inspection.
- Performance standard: **Zero Defects (ZD)**.
- Price of non-conformance (**PONC**) drives measurement.

### 2.1.4 Ishikawa's Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) at Strategic Level

The **6M** categories adapted to shipyard strategic failure:

- **Man (Workforce)** — Skill gap, low morale
- **Machine (Equipment)** — Crane downtime, CNC plasma table wear
- **Material (Steel/Outfit)** — Plate out-of-tolerance, paint shelf-life expired
- **Method (Process)** — Outdated WPS, missing ITP step
- **Measurement (Inspection)** — Uncalibrated UT probe
- **Mother Nature (Environment)** — Humidity affecting coating, sea-state delaying trials

### 2.1.5 Balanced Scorecard (BSC) for Shipyard SQM

Four perspectives of strategic quality:

$$
\text{BSC} = f(\text{Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning \& Growth})
$$

**Shipyard BSC Example (KTU-style):**

| Perspective | Strategic Quality Objective | Measure (KPI) |
|---|---|---|
| Financial | Reduce COPQ | % of revenue lost to rework |
| Customer (Owner) | On-time, defect-free delivery | RFT at delivery, owner satisfaction index |
| Internal Process | First-pass yield in block fabrication | % blocks passing dimensional check |
| Learning & Growth | Workforce competence index | Hours of training/employee/year |

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Pillars of Human Factors in Quality

### 2.2.1 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model of Human Error

This is the **most exam-relevant human-factors model** for KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** states that accidents (quality failures) occur when *latent failures* in the organization align with *active failures* by frontline workers, allowing a hazard to pass through the holes (defences) of the system.

**Layers of defence (cheese slices) in a shipyard:**
1. **Organizational influences** — Quality policy, management decisions, budgeting.
2. **Unsafe supervision** — Inadequate WPS approval, poor shift handover.
3. **Preconditions for unsafe acts** — Worker fatigue, poor lighting, missing PPE.
4. **Unsafe acts** — Skipping visual inspection, misreading drawing revision.

A weld defect (e.g., lack of penetration in a hatch coaming) emerges only when holes in **all four layers** align momentarily.

### 2.2.2 SHELL Model (Edwards, 1972)

Used extensively in maritime and aviation safety:

- **S** — Software (drawings, WPS, ITP, ERP)
- **H** — Hardware (cranes, welding sets, NDT machines)
- **E** — Environment (weather, noise, lighting, yard layout)
- **L** — Liveware (the worker — physical, mental state)
- **L** — Liveware (co-workers, supervisors)

Mismatch between any two components produces a **human-factor failure**.

### 2.2.3 Heinrich's Domino Theory (Industrial Accident Causation)

Five dominoes:

$$
\text{Ancestry} \rightarrow \text{Fault} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Incident} \rightarrow \text{Loss}
$$

**Shipbuilding application:** A welder's inadequate training (ancestry) leads to skipping preheat (fault), causing an underbead crack (act), which is detected at NDT (incident), leading to rework cost (loss).

### 2.2.4 Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) — Quantitative Side

**Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction (THERP)** uses:

$$
\text{HEP} = \text{Probability of failure to perform a task within required time, given conditions}
$$

**Nominal HEP values** (from THERP tables) commonly used in KTU problems:

| Task Type | Nominal HEP |
|---|---|
| Routine, highly practiced | **0.0002** |
| Skill-based, familiar | **0.001** |
| Rule-based, moderate complexity | **0.01** |
| Knowledge-based, novel problem | **0.1 to 0.5** |

### 2.2.5 Quality Circles (Japanese Kaizen Teian)

Small groups (6–12 workers) from the same shop-floor area (e.g., panel line, outfitting) who meet weekly to:
- Identify quality problems
- Analyse root causes
- Propose solutions to management

**Strategic value in shipbuilding:** Frontline welders know more about plate distortion than any engineer — empowering them captures tacit knowledge.

### 2.2.6 Ergonomics in Shipyard Quality

The "**MATCH**" principle:
- **M**ovement within reach zones
- **A**ttention to posture (welding in confined spaces)
- **T**ool grip and weight (cable management)
- **C**omfort and climate (ventilation in paint shop)
- **H**eight/visibility of work (overhead outfitting)

Bad ergonomics → fatigue → human error → quality defects → rework.

### 2.2.7 Cultural Theory — Hofstede's Dimensions Affecting Quality Culture

| Dimension | Shipyard Implication for Quality |
|---|---|
| High Power Distance | Workers hide errors from supervisors → suppressed NCRs |
| High Uncertainty Avoidance | Over-documentation but slow decisions |
| Collectivism | Strengthens Quality Circles and team-based kaizen |
| Long-term Orientation | Sustains SQM investments (5–10 year horizons) |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with standard quality engineering texts. Use them directly in KTU numerical/descriptive answers.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **COPQ** = Internal Failure + External Failure + Appraisal + Prevention | COPQ | Currency | Total quality cost |
| 2 | **Yield** $Y = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Y | dimensionless | First-pass yield in block erection |
| 3 | **Rolled-Throughput Yield** $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | RTY | dimensionless | Multi-stage (cutting → welding → blasting → painting) |
| 4 | **DPMO** $= \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times N}$ | DPMO | defects/million op. | Six Sigma capability |
| 5 | **Sigma Level** $\sigma = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1.5 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5$ | σ | dimensionless | Process capability target |
| 6 | **Cpk** $= \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Cpk | dimensionless | Plate thickness tolerance |
| 7 | **Cp** $= \dfrac{\text{USL}-\text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Cp | dimensionless | Process potential |
| 8 | **Pareto 80/20** | — | — | 80% of defects from 20% of causes |
| 9 | **HEP (Nominal)** — THERP table | HEP | probability | Human error quantification |
| 10 | **Error Rate Reduction** $= 1 - \dfrac{\text{HEP}_{\text{after}}}{\text{HEP}_{\text{before}}}$ | Δ | dimensionless | Effectiveness of training |
| 11 | **Training Effectiveness Ratio** $\text{TER} = \dfrac{\text{Performance gain}}{\text{Hours of training}}$ | TER | per-hour | HRD ROI |
| 12 | **Absenteeism Rate** $= \dfrac{\text{Absent days}}{\text{Total working days}} \times 100$ | AR | % | Workforce stability KPI |
| 13 | **Suggestion Implementation Rate** $= \dfrac{\text{Accepted ideas}}{\text{Total ideas submitted}}$ | SIR | % | Quality Circle vitality |
| 14 | **Customer Satisfaction Index** $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\Sigma w_i s_i}{\Sigma w_i}$ | CSI | dimensionless | Strategic voice-of-customer |
| 15 | **BSC Weighted Score** $S = \sum w_p \cdot m_p$ | S | score | Strategic balanced scorecard |

**Important note on table syntax:** All absolute-value or modulus notations have been written using `\vert` or `\mid` rendering. For example, $\vert x \vert$ instead of the markdown-breaking $\vert x \vert$ pipe syntax.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **COPQ** is reported in the **Annual Quality Report** submitted to the shipyard board and influences vendor selection.
- **DPMO** drives the decision to invest in automation (e.g., robotic welding vs. manual).
- **Cpk** is monitored for **plate cutting tolerance** (Class requirement: ±2 mm on plate edges).
- **HEP tables** are used in **HAZOP** and **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** assessments for critical ship systems like LNG fuel-gas systems.
- **CSI** informs the **next-generation ship design** strategy (e.g., dual-fuel, ammonia-ready).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Strategic Quality Deployment in Shipbuilding

This section walks through the **logical and mathematical flow** of deploying strategic quality into shipyard operations. Each sub-section is broken down step by step.

### 3.1.1 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — The "House of Quality"

QFD is the **strategic tool** that translates *what the customer wants* (the Owner/Class society/Navy requirements) into *how the shipyard will deliver it* (engineering, production, quality specs).

**Step-by-step construction:**

**Step 1 — Identify "Whats" (Customer Requirements).** From the owner's specification, class rules, and SOLAS/MARPOL regulations:

$$
\text{WHATS} = \{R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n\}
$$

Example for a Bulk Carrier:
- $R_1$: Cargo hold capacity ≥ 78,000 DWT
- $R_2$: Hull-girder strength for safe navigation in heavy seas
- $R_3$: Coating durability ≥ 15 years
- $R_4$: Fuel consumption ≤ design target
- $R_5$: Delivery within 18 months

**Step 2 — Identify "Hows" (Technical Requirements).** From shipyard engineering:

$$
\text{HOWS} = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_m\}
$$

Example:
- $T_1$: Plate thickness (mm)
- $T_2$: Steel grade (e.g., AH36)
- $T_3$: Welding process (FCAW/SAW)
- $T_4$: Coating system (epoxy + anti-fouling)
- $T_5$: Hull form optimization

**Step 3 — Build the Relationship Matrix** $r_{ij}$ with symbols 9 (strong), 3 (medium), 1 (weak):

$$
R = [r_{ij}]_{n \times m}, \quad r_{ij} \in \{1, 3, 9\}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Importance Weight** of each "How" using:

$$
w_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}
$$

where $w_i$ is the customer-importance weight of requirement $R_i$, and $r_{ij}$ is the relationship score between $R_i$ and $T_j$.

**Step 5 — Compute Target Values (Benchmarks)** for each $T_j$ by averaging competitor ships and the yard's capability ceiling.

**Step 6 — Identify Trade-offs (Roof of the House)** by computing correlation matrix $C_{jk}$ (positive = synergy, negative = trade-off).

> Example trade-off: Increasing $T_1$ (plate thickness) raises $T_2$ (steel grade demand) but reduces $T_4$ (coating flexibility). The strategic decision weights DWT vs. CAPEX.

**Step 7 — Deployment Downstream.** The $w_j$ values become the **priority weights** for:
- Process FMEA severity scoring
- Inspection Test Plan (ITP) hold-point selection
- Resource allocation in the shipyard

### 3.1.2 Six Sigma DMAIC — Strategic Deployment in Shipyard Processes

Define → Measure → Analyse → Improve → Control. Each step maps to a specific shipyard action:

| DMAIC Step | Shipyard Action | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **D**efine | Identify project (e.g., reduce propeller-shaft alignment NCRs) | Project Charter, SIPOC |
| **M**easure | Collect NCR data over 6 months | Pareto, Control charts |
| **A**nalyse | Fishbone + 5-Why root cause | Ishikawa, Regression |
| **I**mprove | Pilot laser-alignment system | DOE, Pilot run |
| **C**ontrol | Update ITP, train crew, monitor Cpk | SPC, SOPs |

### 3.1.3 Rolled-Throughput Yield (RTY) Derivation

Consider a 5-stage block fabrication: **cutting → welding → blasting → painting → outfitting**.

Stage yields (hypothetical):

$$
Y_1 = 0.98,\quad Y_2 = 0.95,\quad Y_3 = 0.97,\quad Y_4 = 0.94,\quad Y_5 = 0.96
$$

**Step-by-step RTY calculation:**

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{5} Y_i
$$

Compute each product step explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= Y_1 \cdot Y_2 \cdot Y_3 \cdot Y_4 \cdot Y_5 \\
&= (0.98)(0.95)(0.97)(0.94)(0.96)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-step A:** Multiply $Y_1 \cdot Y_2$:

$$
0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.9310
$$

**Sub-step B:** Multiply result by $Y_3$:

$$
0.9310 \times 0.97 = 0.90307
$$

**Sub-step C:** Multiply by $Y_4$:

$$
0.90307 \times 0.94 = 0.848886
$$

**Sub-step D:** Multiply by $Y_5$:

$$
0.848886 \times 0.96 = 0.814931
$$

$$
\boxed{RTY \approx 0.8149 = 81.49\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although each stage has >94% yield, the cumulative first-pass yield is only **~81%**, meaning ~18.5% of blocks require some rework — a key SQM KPI.

### 3.1.4 DPMO and Sigma-Level Derivation (Six Sigma)

Suppose in a 6-month period, a shipyard processes:
- Total units $N = 120$ blocks
- Opportunities per unit $U = 50$ (welds, dimensional checks, coating inspections)
- Defects found $D = 240$

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times N}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{50 \times 120} \\
&= \frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{6000} \\
&= 40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert DPMO to Yield:**

$$
Y = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} = 1 - \frac{40{,}000}{10^{6}} = 0.96 = 96\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sigma Level** using the standard long-term Z-table with 1.5σ shift:

$$
Z_{LT} = \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5
$$

For $Y = 0.96$, $\Phi^{-1}(0.96) \approx 1.7507$.

$$
\sigma = 1.7507 + 1.5 = 3.2507 \approx 3.25\sigma
$$

**Interpretation:** The shipyard is operating at approximately **3.25 sigma**, well below the world-class 6σ benchmark (3.4 DPMO). Strategic SQM would mandate a Six Sigma project to close the gap.

### 3.1.5 Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Breakdown — Worked Example

A mid-size shipyard's quality cost data for one fiscal year:

| Category | Sub-category | Cost (₹ Crore) |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Failure** | Rework labour | 18 |
| | Rework material scrap | 7 |
| | Rework machine hours | 5 |
| **External Failure** | Warranty claims after delivery | 12 |
| | Owner-claim settlements | 8 |
| | Reputation loss (estimated) | 10 |
| **Appraisal** | Inspection labour | 9 |
| | NDT equipment depreciation | 4 |
| | Calibration | 2 |
| **Prevention** | Training | 6 |
| | Quality planning | 4 |
| | Vendor qualification | 3 |

**Step 1 — Compute Internal Failure Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{IF}} = 18 + 7 + 5 = 30 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute External Failure Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{EF}} = 12 + 8 + 10 = 30 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Appraisal Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{A}} = 9 + 4 + 2 = 15 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Prevention Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{P}} = 6 + 4 + 3 = 13 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 5 — Total COPQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} &= C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}} + C_{\text{A}} + C_{\text{P}} \\
&= 30 + 30 + 15 + 13 \\
&= 88 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Failure cost share (KTU-favourite analytical point):**

$$
\text{Failure Share} = \frac{C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}}}{\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}}} = \frac{60}{88} \approx 68.2\%
$$

**Strategic Insight:** Failure costs are **68% of COPQ** — far above the Juran benchmark of ≤ 40%. This signals a **reactive quality culture** dominated by rework and warranty claims, and justifies increased **prevention investment**.

### 3.1.6 Human Error Rate Reduction Through Training — Numerical

A shipyard identifies that welders in the panel line have a **nominal HEP of 0.01** (rule-based) for missing pre-heat. After a targeted 40-hour training programme:

- **Post-training HEP** drops to **0.002**.

**Step 1 — Error Rate Reduction:**

$$
\Delta_{\text{HEP}} = 1 - \frac{\text{HEP}_{\text{after}}}{\text{HEP}_{\text{before}}}
$$

$$
\Delta_{\text{HEP}} = 1 - \frac{0.002}{0.01} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8 = 80\%
$$

**Step 2 — Training Effectiveness Ratio (TER):**

$$
\text{TER} = \frac{\text{Performance gain}}{\text{Hours of training}} = \frac{0.80}{40} = 0.02 \text{ per hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimated Cost Avoidance (Strategic Justification):**

If 100 such errors/year occur at average rework cost of ₹ 50,000 each:

$$
\text{Errors avoided} = 100 \times 0.80 = 80 \text{ errors}
$$

$$
\text{Cost avoidance} = 80 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹ 40 lakh}
$$

**Step 4 — ROI of training:**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000 - \text{Training cost}}{40{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

This becomes the **strategic case** the SQM team presents to the board to expand human-factors investment.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix Summary (For Quick Revision)

| Concept | Strategic Question | Mathematical Output |
|---|---|---|
| RTY | How efficient is the multi-stage process? | Product of yields |
| DPMO / σ | How capable is the process in statistical terms? | Defects-per-million and sigma |
| COPQ | Where is quality money being spent? | Sum of failure + appraisal + prevention |
| HEP / ΔHEP | How much does training reduce errors? | Ratio of post/pre HEP |
| QFD weights | What should engineering prioritize? | Weighted sum of relationships |
| BSC | Are we balanced strategically? | Weighted score across 4 perspectives |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Strategic Quality Management Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Corporate Vision<br>and Mission"] --> nodeB["Quality Policy<br>SQM Charter"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Quality Objectives<br>SMART Goals"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Quality Planning<br>QFD and PQP"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Quality Control<br>ITP and SPC"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Quality Improvement<br>Kaizen and Six Sigma"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Feedback Loop<br>Management Review"]
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeH["Customer Satisfaction<br>Owner and Class"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
```

**Reading the diagram:** Strategic quality is a **closed-loop system**. The Corporate Vision cascades downward through policy, objectives, planning, control, and improvement, while continuous feedback from the customer (Owner/Class) and management review loops back to refine the vision itself. This is the **Deming PDCA cycle at the strategic level**.

---

## 4.2 House of Quality (QFD) — Schematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ["Customer<br>Requirements<br>WHATS"] --> nodeR["Relationship<br>Matrix"]
    nodeT["Engineering<br>Characteristics<br>HOWS"] --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeW["Importance<br>Weights"]
    nodeW --> nodeU["Target<br>Values"]
    nodeT --> nodeV["Technical<br>Correlations<br>Roof"]
    nodeU --> nodeD["Downstream<br>Deployment<br>FMEA and ITP"]
    nodeV --> nodeD
```

**Interpretation:** The left wall lists *whats* (customer requirements), the ceiling lists *hows* (engineering characteristics), the central matrix shows relationships, the right wall shows importance weights, and the roof shows technical trade-offs. Outputs feed FMEA and ITP.

---

## 4.3 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model — Shipyard Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeL1["Layer 1<br>Organizational<br>Influences"] --> nodeL2["Layer 2<br>Unsafe<br>Supervision"]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3["Layer 3<br>Preconditions<br>for Unsafe Acts"]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4["Layer 4<br>Unsafe Acts<br>at Workplace"]
    nodeL4 --> nodeAC["Accident<br>or Quality<br>Failure"]
    nodeL1 -.-> nodeAC
    nodeL2 -.-> nodeAC
    nodeL3 -.-> nodeAC
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each layer is a *cheese slice with holes* representing latent weaknesses. The four arrows on the right show that a *single misalignment of holes* across all four layers allows the hazard trajectory to pass through and produce a quality failure (e.g., a defective weld discovered after block erection).

---

## 4.4 SHELL Model — Human Factors Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSW["Software<br>Drawings WPS ITP"] --- nodeHW["Hardware<br>Cranes Welding Sets"]
    nodeHW --- nodeEN["Environment<br>Weather Noise Lighting"]
    nodeEN --- nodeL1["Liveware 1<br>The Worker"]
    nodeL1 --- nodeL2["Liveware 2<br>Supervisors and Peers"]
    nodeL2 --- nodeSW
    nodeL1 --- nodeSW
    nodeL1 --- nodeHW
    nodeL1 --- nodeEN
```

**Interpretation:** The four peripheral components (S, H, E, L2) interface with the central **Liveware-1 (the worker)**. A mismatch anywhere causes a quality or safety incident. Shipyards that redesign jobs to reduce mismatch (e.g., ergonomic jigs, clearer drawings, better lighting) see measurable drops in human-error NCRs.

---

## 4.5 Six Sigma DMAIC Flow for Shipyard Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDM["DEFINE<br>Project Charter<br>Problem Statement"] --> nodeMS["MEASURE<br>Data Collection<br>Pareto Chart"]
    nodeMS --> nodeAN["ANALYSE<br>Root Cause<br>Fishbone 5-Why"]
    nodeAN --> nodeIM["IMPROVE<br>Pilot Solution<br>DOE"]
    nodeIM --> nodeCT["CONTROL<br>SOP Update<br>SPC Monitoring"]
    nodeCT --> nodeDM
```

**Interpretation:** DMAIC is a continuous-improvement loop. After completing "Control", the project findings may surface a *new* quality problem, restarting the loop — this is the **Kaizen engine**.

---

## 4.6 COPQ Optimization — Strategic Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeCOPQ["COPQ Greater than<br>Threshold"] --> nodeCHK{"Failure Cost<br>Share"}
    nodeCHK -- "Above 60 percent" --> nodeACT1["Invest in Prevention<br>Training Vendor Audit"]
    nodeCHK -- "Below 40 percent" --> nodeACT2["Maintain Appraisal<br>and Tweak Prevention"]
    nodeCHK -- "Between 40 and 60" --> nodeACT3["Balanced Increase<br>in all Categories"]
    nodeACT1 --> nodeMON["Monitor RTY<br>DPMO Cpk"]
    nodeACT2 --> nodeMON
    nodeACT3 --> nodeMON
```

**Reading the diagram:** The SQM team uses this decision tree annually to allocate the quality budget. A high failure-cost share (>60%) is the *strategic red flag* — it triggers prevention investment as the highest-ROI lever.
