# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**Q: Define Strategic Quality Management. How does it differ from routine Quality Control in a shipbuilding context?**

**Model Answer (3-mark structure: 1 mark definition + 1 mark differentiation + 1 mark shipbuilding example):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Strategic Quality Management (SQM) is the long-term, top-down integration of quality principles into corporate strategy, aligning the shipyard's mission, quality policy, and resource allocation to achieve sustained customer satisfaction, regulatory compliance, and competitive advantage.
>
> **Differentiation (1 mark):**
> - **Quality Control (QC)** is *tactical and reactive* — detecting defects at inspection hold-points using ITP, NDT, and dimensional checks.
> - **SQM** is *strategic and proactive* — setting the vision, deploying QFD, choosing Six Sigma projects, and building a prevention-based culture across the shipyard.
>
> **Shipbuilding Example (1 mark):** Routine QC inspects every hatch cover weld with MPI. SQM, by contrast, decides that the shipyard will invest in **robotic welding with in-process monitoring** to *prevent* weld defects from occurring in the first place — reducing QC dependency.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Q: Briefly explain Reason's Swiss Cheese Model of human error with reference to a shipyard welding defect.**

**Model Answer (3-mark structure: 1 mark concept + 1 mark four layers + 1 mark application):**

> **Concept (1 mark):** Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990) proposes that organizational accidents/quality failures occur when *holes* (weaknesses) in multiple defensive layers align momentarily, allowing a hazard to pass through.
>
> **Four Layers (1 mark):**
> 1. **Organizational Influences** — Quality policy, budgeting, shift planning.
> 2. **Unsafe Supervision** — Inadequate WPS approval, poor task briefing.
> 3. **Preconditions** — Worker fatigue, poor lighting, missing PPE.
> 4. **Unsafe Acts** — Skipping preheat, misreading drawing revision.
>
> **Shipyard Application (1 mark):** A lack-of-penetration defect in a side-shell weld is missed at MPI inspection. Root-cause analysis reveals *all four holes aligned*: management cut inspection time, supervisor skipped WPS verification, welder was fatigued after overtime, and the WPS revision was not communicated. SQM must address all four layers, not blame the welder alone.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark SQM/HFQ questions, students frequently lose marks by (i) writing only definitions without shipbuilding-specific context, (ii) skipping diagrams (which carry 2–3 marks), and (iii) not presenting numerical calculations in clean aligned steps. Always end a 14-mark answer with a **strategic conclusion** linking back to the shipyard's competitive position.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern]**

**Q (a)** Explain the strategic role of Total Quality Management (TQM) in a modern shipyard. Discuss Deming's 14 Points with specific shipbuilding examples. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A shipbuilding firm recorded the following quality cost data for the year 2023:
- Internal Failure: ₹ 30 crore
- External Failure: ₹ 25 crore
- Appraisal: ₹ 18 crore
- Prevention: ₹ 7 crore

Calculate the **Total COPQ**, the **failure-cost share**, and recommend strategic actions. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — TQM and Deming's 14 Points (7 marks)

**(1 mark) Definition of TQM in shipbuilding:**
Total Quality Management is a management philosophy that pursues **continuous improvement** in every shipyard process — from steel stockyard to sea-trial — by engaging all employees, customers, and suppliers, with the goal of achieving long-term success through customer satisfaction.

**(3 marks) Deming's 14 Points applied to shipbuilding:**

| Point | Shipyard Application |
|---|---|
| 1. Constancy of purpose | Long-term plan to build *zero-defect, fuel-efficient* ships, beyond short-term profit. |
| 2. Adopt new philosophy | Move from *find-and-fix* QC to *design-in quality* via QFD. |
| 3. Cease dependence on inspection | Build weld quality *into* the procedure (qualified WPS), not just inspect afterwards. |
| 4. End lowest-tender contracts | Source AH36 steel from ISO 9001 + IACS-certified mills only. |
| 5. Improve constantly | Kaizen events in panel line every quarter. |
| 6. Institute training | Annual recertification of all welders, NDT inspectors. |
| 7. Institute leadership | Supervisors act as coaches, not enforcers. |
| 8. Drive out fear | Encourage near-miss reporting without punitive action. |
| 9. Break down silos | Concurrent engineering — design, production, quality work in parallel. |
| 10. Eliminate slogans | Replace "Zero Defects!" with concrete process changes. |
| 11. Eliminate quotas | Avoid piece-rate welding incentives (which cause undercut). |
| 12. Pride of workmanship | Clean, well-lit welding bays with calibrated tools. |
| 13. Education | Cross-functional training in robotics, coatings, electrical. |
| 14. Transformation | Top-management-led, yard-wide TQM deployment. |

**(1 mark) Strategic role:**
- Reduces COPQ by shifting the balance toward prevention.
- Strengthens class-society relationships through documented QMS.
- Builds a culture of accountability — a key differentiator in global shipbuilding tenders.

**(1 mark) Shipbuilding example:**
At Hyundai Heavy Industries and Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders, TQM has been institutionalized through **Six-Sigma Black-Belt programmes**, where each Black-Belt engineer leads 4–6 kaizen projects per year, saving millions of dollars in rework cost.

**(1 mark) Diagram:**
[Student should draw Deming's PDCA cycle — Plan (QFD), Do (production), Check (ITP), Act (Kaizen).]

#### Part (b) — COPQ Calculation (7 marks)

**(0.5 mark) Given data:**

$$
C_{\text{IF}} = 30,\quad C_{\text{EF}} = 25,\quad C_{\text{A}} = 18,\quad C_{\text{P}} = 7 \text{ (all in crore ₹)}
$$

**(2 marks) Total COPQ calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} &= C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}} + C_{\text{A}} + C_{\text{P}} \\
&= 30 + 25 + 18 + 7 \\
&= 80 \text{ crore ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks) Failure-cost share:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Failure}} &= C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}} = 30 + 25 = 55 \text{ crore} \\
\text{Failure Share} &= \frac{C_{\text{Failure}}}{\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{55}{80} \times 100 = 68.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks) Strategic Recommendations:**

1. **Failure share of 68.75% is far above the Juran benchmark of ≤ 40%.** This indicates a *reactive, inspection-driven* culture.
2. **Shift investment toward prevention:** increase training budget from ₹ 7 crore to ₹ 12–15 crore (a 70–110% rise) — *every ₹ 1 spent on prevention saves ₹ 4–10 in failure costs* (Juran's rule of thumb).
3. **Vendor qualification programme** to reduce external failure (warranty claims).
4. **Six-Sigma Green-Belt training** for shop-floor supervisors to attack the top three defect categories identified via Pareto.
5. **Balanced Scorecard review** at quarterly management meetings.

**(0.5 mark) Conclusion:**
The shipyard must transition from an *appraisal-heavy* to a *prevention-driven* cost structure — a textbook TQM strategic shift.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern]**

**Q (a)** What are Human Factors in Quality? Explain the SHELL model and Reason's Swiss Cheese model with shipbuilding examples. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A panel-line welding crew has a **nominal HEP of 0.01** for the task "verify preheat temperature before welding". After a 30-hour refresher training programme, the HEP reduces to **0.003**. Compute:
- (i) the **percentage reduction in error rate**,
- (ii) the **training effectiveness ratio**,
- (iii) the **estimated cost avoidance** if 200 such preheat-skipping errors occur per year with average rework cost of ₹ 35,000 per error. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Human Factors Definitions and Models (7 marks)

**(1 mark) Definition:**
Human Factors in Quality (HFQ) is the discipline that studies how human capabilities, limitations, behaviour, training, motivation, communication, and ergonomics influence quality outcomes in shipbuilding, and applies this knowledge to design jobs, tools, and processes that minimize human error.

**(2.5 marks) SHELL Model (Edwards, 1972):**
The SHELL model is a *human-factors framework* that maps the central human worker against four interfacing components:

- **S — Software:** Drawings, WPS, ITP, ERP. *Example mismatch:* outdated drawing revision in welder's hand causes misaligned cut.
- **H — Hardware:** Cranes, welding sets, NDT equipment. *Mismatch:* uncalibrated UT probe produces false-negative defect report.
- **E — Environment:** Weather, noise, lighting, yard layout. *Mismatch:* poor lighting in double-bottom leads to misread weld symbol.
- **L — Liveware (co-workers/supervisors):** Communication, supervision. *Mismatch:* shift-handover gap causes incomplete task briefing.

The model shows that the **worker is central**; mismatches at any interface generate *latent* failures.

**(2.5 marks) Reason's Swiss Cheese Model:**
This model (Reason, 1990) explains that accidents/quality failures occur when **holes in multiple defensive layers align**. The four layers are:

1. **Organizational Influences** (quality policy, budgeting)
2. **Unsafe Supervision** (poor WPS approval)
3. **Preconditions** (fatigue, poor lighting)
4. **Unsafe Acts** (skipped inspection)

**Shipyard Example:** A 2018 incident at a European yard where a propeller-shaft alignment was missed at sea-trial. Root cause: *management cut the trial budget* (Layer 1), *supervisor skipped the alignment check* (Layer 2), *engineer was fatigued from overtime* (Layer 3), and *the alignment SOP was outdated* (Layer 4) — all four holes aligned.

**(1 mark) Strategic significance:**
Both models argue that **blaming the frontline worker alone is misguided**; SQM must address systemic layers. This aligns with Deming's Point 8 (*drive out fear*) and Juran's prevention philosophy.

#### Part (b) — HEP Numerical (7 marks)

**(0.5 mark) Given data:**

$$
\text{HEP}_{\text{before}} = 0.01,\quad \text{HEP}_{\text{after}} = 0.003,\quad T_{\text{train}} = 30 \text{ hr},\quad N_{\text{errors}} = 200,\quad C_{\text{rework}} = 35{,}000
$$

**(2 marks) Part (i) — Percentage reduction in error rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{\text{HEP}} &= 1 - \frac{\text{HEP}_{\text{after}}}{\text{HEP}_{\text{before}}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.003}{0.01} \\
&= 1 - 0.30 \\
&= 0.70 = 70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks) Part (ii) — Training Effectiveness Ratio (TER):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TER} &= \frac{\Delta_{\text{HEP}}}{T_{\text{train}}} = \frac{0.70}{30} \\
&\approx 0.0233 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks) Part (iii) — Estimated cost avoidance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Errors avoided per year} &= N_{\text{errors}} \times \Delta_{\text{HEP}} = 200 \times 0.70 = 140 \\
\text{Cost avoidance} &= 140 \times 35{,}000 = 49{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ 49 lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(0.5 mark) Strategic conclusion:**
A 30-hour training programme costing approximately ₹ 3–5 lakh (per crew) avoids ₹ 49 lakh in rework — a **ROI exceeding 10×**, justifying a yard-wide human-factors investment under SQM.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read before entering the exam hall):**

### Strategic Quality Management
- ☐ SQM = alignment of quality with **corporate strategy**, not just shop-floor QC.
- ☐ Deming's 14 Points — memorize **at least 8** with shipbuilding examples.
- ☐ Juran Trilogy = **Plan → Control → Improve** (PQP, ITP, Kaizen).
- ☐ Crosby = **Conformance + Zero Defects + Prevention + PONC**.
- ☐ Ishikawa 6M = **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature**.
- ☐ QFD House of Quality = translate *WHATS* (customer) into *HOWS* (engineering).
- ☐ BSC = **Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth** — equal weighting required.
- ☐ Six Sigma DMAIC = **D-M-A-I-C** with continuous feedback to next project.
- ☐ COPQ = **Internal + External + Appraisal + Prevention**; aim for failure share ≤ 40%.
- ☐ RTY = product of stage yields; even 95% stages yield < 80% overall.
- ☐ DPMO of 3.4 = **6σ world-class** benchmark.

### Human Factors in Quality
- ☐ HFQ addresses **why humans err**, not just *that* they err.
- ☐ **Reason's Swiss Cheese** has 4 layers; address all, not only the frontline.
- ☐ **SHELL Model** centres on the *worker* interfacing with S, H, E, and other L.
- ☐ Heinrich's Domino: **Ancestry → Fault → Act → Incident → Loss** — break the chain early.
- ☐ **Quality Circles** = 6–12 workers, weekly meetings, frontline-driven improvement.
- ☐ **Ergonomics** (MATCH principle) reduces fatigue-induced defects.
- ☐ **HEP (THERP)** ranges from 0.0002 (routine) to 0.5 (novel); training reduces it.
- ☐ Hofstede's **High Power Distance** suppresses NCR reporting — a culture risk.
- ☐ Strategic conclusion: **Prevention is cheaper than detection** (every ₹ 1 saves ₹ 4–10).

### KTU Exam-Specific Tips
- ☐ Always include a **shipbuilding example** for every theory point (otherwise −1 to −2 marks).
- ☐ Draw at least **one diagram per 14-mark answer** (PDCA, House of Quality, Swiss Cheese, or SHELL).
- ☐ For numerical problems, show **each algebraic step** in aligned format — partial marks are awarded.
- ☐ End every 14-mark answer with a **strategic recommendation or conclusion** linking back to the shipyard.
- ☐ Memorize the **6 key formulas**: COPQ, RTY, DPMO, σ, Cpk, HEP-reduction.
- ☐ Watch the **valuation pitfall**: writing generic management theory without shipbuilding context is the #1 reason for losing 3–5 marks in KTU 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Assurance (QA) in Shipbuilding** is the comprehensive, planned, and systematic set of activities, documentation controls, technical inspections, and surveillance procedures implemented throughout the entire ship construction lifecycle — from design approval and material procurement to fabrication, assembly, sea trials, and delivery — to provide credible confidence that the vessel will satisfy specified design intent, Flag State (Maritime Administration) regulations, Classification Society Rules, International Maritime Organization (IMO) conventions, and the Owner's contractual requirements.

In simpler engineering terms: **QA is the engineering discipline of "doing it right the first time, proving it on paper, and verifying it on the hull."**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Hospital" Intuition

Imagine you are constructing a 300-bed floating hospital that must:

- Survive a Category-12 cyclone at sea.
- Carry 1500 people safely for 30 days without resupply.
- Pass inspection from 50 different international doctors (regulators).

You cannot "open the wall" once it is built to check what is inside. You must:

1. **Approve the blueprint** before pouring concrete (steel).
2. **Test every batch of cement** (steel plates, welding consumables).
3. **Photograph every rebar joint** (weld inspection records).
4. **Pressure-test every pipe** before sealing walls (hydrostatic tests, NDT).
5. **Run the generator for 24 hours** before moving in (sea trials).

That whole chain — from blueprint approval to final sea trial — is **Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology Used in KTU Valuation

| Term | Definition (Board-Standard) |
|------|----------------------------|
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Operational techniques and activities used to fulfil quality requirements — the "inspection" arm. |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | All planned and systematic activities implemented within the quality system that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product/service will satisfy quality requirements. |
| **Quality Management (QM)** | Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to quality (ISO 9000). |
| **TQM** | Total Quality Management — management philosophy centred on customer satisfaction, continuous improvement, and全员 participation. |
| **Classification Society** | Non-governmental organization that establishes and applies technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of ships (e.g., **LR, DNV, BV, ABS, IRS, KR, RINA, CCS**). |
| **Flag State** | The country where the ship is registered; enforces international maritime regulations (e.g., India → DG Shipping). |
| **IACS** | International Association of Classification Societies — umbrella body of major class societies. |
| **QSCS** | IACS Quality System Certification Scheme — audits class societies' own QA systems. |
| **AQL** | Acceptable Quality Level — the worst tolerable process average when a continuing series of lots is submitted for acceptance sampling (ISO 2859-1). |
| **NDT** | Non-Destructive Testing — UT, RT, MT, PT, VT, ET — the "five senses" of QA. |
| **SMD** | Steel Mill Document — traceability certificate issued by steel manufacturer. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever you write "QA in shipbuilding," immediately link it to **three stakeholders**: the **Owner (customer)**, the **Classification Society (technical certifier)**, and the **Flag State (legal regulator)**. Examiners award marks for showing this tri-party relationship.

### 1.4 Why QA is Non-Negotiable in Shipbuilding

1. **Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** — A single weld failure can sink a vessel with 1000+ lives.
2. **Harsh Marine Environment** — Corrosion, cyclic loading, fatigue, and temperature extremes demand zero-defect critical joints.
3. **Irreversibility** — Once a plate is welded, you cannot "re-do" without cutting and re-welding, costing lakhs of rupees per joint.
4. **Legal & Insurance Liability** — Unclassified or uncertified vessels cannot be insured or commercially operated.
5. **International Reputation** — A single incident in one yard can blacklist an entire national shipbuilding industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Shipbuilding QA

The QA framework rests on three interconnected pillars:

**Pillar A — Documented Quality Management System (QMS)**
- Based on **ISO 9001:2015** principles: Customer focus, Leadership, Engagement of people, Process approach, Improvement, Evidence-based decision-making, Relationship management.
- Documented through a Quality Manual, Procedures, Work Instructions, and Records.
- The shipyard's QMS is itself **certified by an accredited body** (e.g., IACS QSCS auditors).

**Pillar B — Plan Approval (Design Verification)**
- Conducted by the **Classification Society** (and Flag State where statutory).
- Every drawing, calculation, and material specification is reviewed and stamped.
- Includes: General Arrangement, Midship Section, Hull Structural Details, Machinery Plans, Electrical Diagrams, Piping Schematics, Stability Booklet.
- Iterative loop: Yard submits → Society reviews → Returns with comments (RFI/CLN) → Yard revises → Final Approval.

**Pillar C — Survey During Construction (Execution Verification)**
- Continuous on-site inspection by **Class Surveyors** at predefined "hold points."
- Each hold point requires surveyor attendance before work proceeds.
- Material certificates, welder qualifications, NDT reports, dimensional records are all filed and verified.

### 2.2 The PDCA (Deming) Cycle in a Shipyard

Every QA process follows the **Plan–Do–Check–Act** cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{PLAN:} \quad &\text{Class Rules + Owner Spec} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Quality Plan, ITP (Inspection \& Test Plan)} \\
\textbf{DO:} \quad &\text{Welding, Assembly, Outfitting per approved procedures} \\
\textbf{CHECK:} \quad &\text{NDT, Dimensional checks, Hydrostatic tests, Stage surveys} \\
\textbf{ACT:} \quad &\text{NCR (Non-Conformance Report) closure, CAPA, Lessons Learned}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3 Cost of Quality (CoQ) Model

The classical quality cost model splits costs into four categories:

$$
C_{Q} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

Where:
- $C_{P}$ = **Prevention costs** (training, procedure writing, supplier audits).
- $C_{A}$ = **Appraisal costs** (inspection, NDT, surveyor fees).
- $C_{IF}$ = **Internal Failure costs** (rework, scrap, NCR processing).
- $C_{EF}$ = **External Failure costs** (warranty claims, recalls, liability suits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (often tested):** As $C_P$ increases, $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ fall faster than the rise in prevention cost — leading to a **minimum total quality cost** at an optimal point. This is the economic justification for QA investment.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $C_{Q} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Currency (₹ / \$) |
| 2 | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $COPQ = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Internal + External Failure |
| 3 | Defect Rate (DPMO) | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N}$ | Defects per million opportunities |
| 4 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | $FPY = \dfrac{N - D}{N} \times 100\,\%$ | \% of units passing without rework |
| 5 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{k} FPY_i$ | Product of stage yields |
| 6 | Process Capability Index | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Centered process potential |
| 7 | Process Capability (shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Real-world capability |
| 8 | Six-Sigma Level | $DPMO = 3.4$ at $6\sigma$ | World-class benchmark |
| 9 | AQL Sampling (single) | $n$ from ISO 2859-1 tables | Depends on lot size $\&$ AQL |
| 10 | Acceptance Probability | $P_{a} = \displaystyle\sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Hypergeometric (binomial approx. if $N \gg n$) |

> **Notation note:** In every KTU exam, use $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit, $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit, $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = process standard deviation, $N$ = lot size, $n$ = sample size, $D$ = defective count, $c$ = acceptance number.

### 2.5 The 14 Stages of Ship Construction QA

A typical ship passes through **14 numbered stages** (per IACS Common Structural Rules & most yard practice):

1. Steel stock receipt (Stage 100)
2. Cutting and forming
3. Sub-assembly (small panels)
4. Grand block assembly
5. Block joining on berth
6. Launch
7. Outfitting (pre- and post-launch)
8. Machinery installation
9. Piping system completion
10. Electrical installation
11. Painting and coating
12. Mooring trials (Stage 800)
13. Dock/Sea trials (Stage 900)
14. Delivery and Class endorsement

> Each stage has a **Stage Quality Plan** listing hold points, responsible parties, acceptance criteria, and reference documents.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Welding QA** is the heart of shipbuilding — about **40–60 %** of all yard man-hours go into welding.
- The **weld traceability chain** runs from SMD (Steel Mill Document) → Plate Cutting Plan → Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) → Procedure Qualification Record (PQR) → Welder Qualification Test (WQT) → Actual Weld → NDT Report → As-Built Drawing.
- Any break in this chain is an **NCR** that halts the build until closed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ from First Principles

**Given:**
- Specification limits: $USL$, $LSL$
- Process mean: $\mu$
- Process standard deviation: $\sigma$

**Step 1:** Define the "distance from mean to each specification limit."

$$
d_{U} = USL - \mu \qquad \text{(upper distance)}
$$
$$
d_{L} = \mu - LSL \qquad \text{(lower distance)}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that for a **normal distribution**, ±$3\sigma$ from $\mu$ captures **99.73 %** of process output. So the one-sided capability is:

$$
C_{pU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}
$$
$$
C_{pL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 3:** Real processes drift from center. The actual capability is the **worst-side** capability:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\bigl(C_{pU},\; C_{pL}\bigr) = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 4 (Centering check):** When $\mu = (USL + LSL)/2$, then $C_{pk} = C_p$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Typical Problem

**Problem:** A shipyard machines a shaft with $USL = 52.00$ mm, $LSL = 50.00$ mm. From 25 samples, $\mu = 50.80$ mm and $\sigma = 0.30$ mm. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and interpret.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{p} &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{52.00 - 50.00}{6 \times 0.30} = \frac{2.00}{1.80} \\[4pt]
      &= 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= \min\!\left(\frac{52.00 - 50.80}{0.90},\; \frac{50.80 - 50.00}{0.90}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= \min\!\left(\frac{1.20}{0.90},\; \frac{0.80}{0.90}\right) \\[4pt]
       &= \min\!\left(1.333,\; 0.889\right) = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (for KTU answer):**
- $C_p > 1.00$ indicates adequate **potential** capability.
- $C_{pk} = 0.889 < 1.00$ indicates **process is not capable** because of off-centering.
- Action: Re-center the lathe to bring $\mu$ closer to the **mid-spec value of 51.00 mm**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Cost of Quality with Numerical Optimization

A shipyard incurs:
- $C_{P} = 0.5\,x^{2}$ (₹ lakhs), where $x$ = % of revenue spent on prevention.
- $C_{IF} = 80\,x^{-0.8}$ (₹ lakhs).

**Find the $x$ that minimizes $C_{Q} = C_{P} + C_{IF}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{Q}}{dx} &= \frac{d}{dx}\bigl(0.5x^{2}\bigr) + \frac{d}{dx}\bigl(80\,x^{-0.8}\bigr) \\[4pt]
                  &= 1.0\,x - 0.8 \times 80\,x^{-1.8} \\[4pt]
                  &= x - 64\,x^{-1.8}
\end{aligned}
$$

Set derivative to zero:

$$
x = 64\,x^{-1.8} \;\Rightarrow\; x^{2.8} = 64
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln x &= \frac{\ln 64}{2.8} = \frac{4.1589}{2.8} = 1.4853 \\[4pt]
x &= e^{1.4853} \approx 4.41\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Verify by second derivative (positive confirms minimum):

$$
\frac{d^{2}C_{Q}}{dx^{2}} = 1.0 + 64 \times 1.8 \times x^{-2.8} > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Interpretation:** Spend approximately **4.4 % of revenue** on prevention to minimize total quality cost.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing NDT Method

| Defect Type | Recommended NDT | Why |
|-------------|----------------|-----|
| Surface cracks in welds | **MT (Magnetic Particle)** or **PT (Penetrant)** | Fast, cheap, surface-only |
| Subsurface weld defects | **UT (Ultrasonic)** | Volumetric, deep penetration |
| Volumetric defects (porosity, slag) | **RT (Radiographic)** | Permanent record, full cross-section |
| In-service fatigue cracks | **TOFD / PAUT** | Time-of-flight diffraction, phased array |
| Coating thickness | **DFT gauge** (destructive or electronic) | Quick field check |
| Material identification | **PMI (Positive Material Identification)** | Avoids mix-ups of stainless / carbon steel |

### 3.5 Acceptance Sampling — AQL Logic (Derivation of OC Curve Concept)

For **single sampling plan by attributes** (per ISO 2859-1):

$$
P_{a}(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{\,d}\,(1-p)^{n-d}
$$

Where:
- $p$ = true fraction defective in the lot.
- $n$ = sample size.
- $c$ = acceptance number (max defects allowed).
- $P_{a}$ = probability of acceptance.

When $n \ll N$ (infinite lot approximation), binomial is used; for finite $N$, hypergeometric is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $p$ (true fraction defective) with $AQL$ (specified acceptable quality level). They are **different**: $AQL$ is the *worst tolerable* $p$, while $p$ is the *actual* incoming $p$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 QA Stakeholder Hierarchy in Shipbuilding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Owner / Ship Operator]:::owner -->|Contract Specs| B[Shipyard QA Department]:::yard
    B -->|Plan Approval Request| C[Classification Society e.g. LR, DNV, BV]:::class
    B -->|Statutory Drawings| D[Flag State e.g. DG Shipping India]:::flag
    B -->|Material Orders| E[Steel Mill & Vendors]:::vendor
    E -->|Mill Cert + SMD| B
    C -->|Approved Drawings + Hold Points| B
    D -->|Statutory Approval| B
    B -->|Stage Surveys + NDT Reports| C
    C -->|Class Certificate upon Delivery| A
    D -->|Flag Certificate upon Delivery| A
    B -->|Final Documentation Pack| F[Ship's Technical File]:::file

    classDef owner fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef yard fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
    classDef class fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef flag fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef vendor fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Material & Fabrication Flow with QA Gates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Stage 100 - Steel Stock Receipt] --> g1{QA Gate: MTC + Visual + PMI Check}
    g1 -->|Accepted| s2[Stage 200 - Cutting & Forming]
    g1 -->|Rejected| ncr1[NCR + Return to Vendor]
    s2 --> g2{QA Gate: Dimension + Edge Prep}
    g2 --> s3[Stage 300 - Sub-Assembly Welding]
    s3 --> g3{QA Gate: WPS Compliance + NDT}
    g3 --> s4[Stage 400 - Block Assembly]
    s4 --> g4{QA Gate: Class Surveyor Stage Survey}
    g4 --> s5[Stage 500 - Erection on Berth]
    s5 --> g5{QA Gate: Alignment + Tack Weld QC}
    g5 --> s6[Stage 600 - Launch]
    s6 --> s7[Stage 700 - Outfitting]
    s7 --> g6{QA Gate: System Hydrostatic + Electrical Loop Test}
    g6 --> s8[Stage 800 - Mooring Trials]
    s8 --> s9[Stage 900 - Sea Trials + Class Delivery Survey]

    classDef gate fill:#fef08a,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    classDef ncr fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    class g1,g2,g3,g4,g5,g6 gate
    class ncr1 ncr
    class s1,s2,s3,s4,s5,s6,s7,s8,s9 stage
```

### 4.3 PDCA Cycle in a Shipyard's QA Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PLAN - Class Rules + Quality Plan + ITP] --> D[DO - Welding, Assembly, Outfitting]
    D --> C[CHECK - NDT, Dimensional, Hydrotest]
    C --> Q{NCR Detected?}
    Q -->|Yes| A1[ACT - Root Cause + CAPA + Re-train]
    Q -->|No| A2[ACT - Standardise Best Practice]
    A1 --> P
    A2 --> P

    classDef p fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef d fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
    classDef chk fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
    class P p
    class D d
    class C chk
    class A1,A2 act
    class Q q
```

### 4.4 Cost of Quality Optimisation Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Prevention Spend] -->|Under-investing| B(High Internal Failure Cost)
    B --> C{High Total Quality Cost}
    A --> C
    D[High Prevention Spend] -->|Over-investing| E(High Prevention Cost itself)
    E --> C2{High Total Quality Cost}
    D --> C2
    F[Optimal Prevention Spend 4 to 6 percent of Revenue] --> G(Minimum Total Quality Cost)
    G --> H[Maximum Profit + Customer Satisfaction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Quality Assurance (QA) as applied to shipbuilding. List any four stakeholders involved in shipbuilding QA.*

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

Quality Assurance in shipbuilding is the **planned and systematic set of activities** implemented to provide confidence that a constructed vessel will conform to design specifications, Classification Society Rules, Flag State regulations, and the Owner's requirements throughout the entire construction lifecycle (from design approval to sea trials and delivery).

**Four stakeholders:**
1. **Ship Owner / Customer** — defines contractual quality requirements.
2. **Shipyard (Builder)** — executes and documents QA per approved Quality Plan.
3. **Classification Society** — independent technical certifier (e.g., LR, DNV, BV).
4. **Flag State Administration** — enforces statutory rules (e.g., DG Shipping for Indian flag).

**(Marks distribution: Definition 1.5 m + Stakeholders 1.5 m)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA) in shipbuilding with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Focus** | Operational, inspection-oriented | System-oriented, prevention-focused |
| **Scope** | Product / weld / component | Entire process from design to delivery |
| **Tools** | Gauges, NDT, dimensional checks | Quality Manual, ITP, audits, CAPA |
| **Example** | Performing **UT** on a butt weld joint between two plates. | Establishing a **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** and qualifying welders **before** any fabrication begins. |
| **Mind-set** | "Detect defects" | "Prevent defects and prove conformance" |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently interchange QC and QA in definitions. Remember the **proactive vs. reactive** distinction: QA = proactive system; QC = reactive inspection. A single sentence showing this contrast scores 2 out of 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module-end questions carry **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three pillars of shipbuilding QA** as per IACS framework. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The IACS-aligned QA framework rests on three interconnected pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Plan Approval (Design Verification) — (2.5 marks)**
The Classification Society reviews and approves every engineering drawing, calculation, and material specification **before** construction begins. This includes:
- General Arrangement, Midship Section, Shell Expansion.
- Hull structural drawings, Machinery foundation plans.
- Piping system diagrams, Electrical single-line diagrams.
- Stability booklet, Load line calculations.
The iterative process involves **submission → review → comments (CLN) → revision → final approval stamp**.

**Pillar 2 — Survey During Construction (Execution Verification) — (2.5 marks)**
Class Surveyors conduct **scheduled and unscheduled inspections** at predefined hold points. The yard cannot proceed past a hold point without surveyor attendance and signature. Records filed include:
- Welder qualifications, WPS/PQR traceability.
- NDT reports (UT, RT, MT, PT).
- Dimensional check sheets, alignment records.
- Material certificates (SMD 3.1 / 3.2).

**Pillar 3 — Quality Management System Certification (QSCS) — (2 marks)**
The shipyard itself must hold an **ISO 9001** or **IACS QSCS** certified QMS. Annual surveillance audits verify that procedures are followed and continually improved (PDCA cycle). Without QMS certification, a yard loses credibility for major naval and commercial contracts.

**(b)** A shipyard is machining propeller shaft bearings. The specification gives $USL = 105.00$ mm and $LSL = 95.00$ mm. From a sample of 30 bearings, the mean diameter is $102.50$ mm with a standard deviation of $1.20$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and comment on the process capability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{p} &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{105.00 - 95.00}{6 \times 1.20} = \frac{10.00}{7.20} \\[4pt]
      &= 1.389
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pU} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{105.00 - 102.50}{3 \times 1.20} = \frac{2.50}{3.60} = 0.694 \\[4pt]
C_{pL} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{102.50 - 95.00}{3.60} = \frac{7.50}{3.60} = 2.083 \\[4pt]
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pU},\,C_{pL}) = \min(0.694,\, 2.083) = 0.694
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (3 marks):**
- $C_p = 1.389 > 1.33$ → potential capability is **excellent** (automotive-grade).
- $C_{pk} = 0.694 < 1.00$ → **actual capability is poor** due to off-centering.
- **Root cause:** Process mean $\mu = 102.50$ mm is biased toward the upper limit instead of sitting at the mid-spec of **100.00 mm**.
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the CNC lathe by **2.50 mm downward** (reduce offset); aim for $\mu = 100.00$ mm. After re-centering, $C_{pk}$ should approach $C_p \approx 1.389$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **PDCA (Deming) cycle** as applied to welding quality assurance in a shipyard. Give one shipbuilding-specific example for each phase. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **PDCA cycle** is the cornerstone of continual improvement in any ISO 9001-certified shipyard QA system. Each phase has specific deliverables in welding QA:

**PLAN (1.5 marks)**
- Develop a **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** based on the Classification Society Rules (e.g., IACS UR W17 for steel hull welding).
- Prepare the **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** listing every weld joint, its NDT requirement, acceptance criteria, and hold point.
- Example: For the bilge strake plate, plan to use SAW (Submerged Arc Welding), grade E-grade electrodes, with 100 % UT after welding.

**DO (2 marks)**
- Execute the welding as per the approved WPS by qualified welders (holding valid WQT certificates).
- Maintain traceability records linking welder → weld → plate heat number.
- Example: A welder certified for 1G position performs the bilge strake butt weld, recording the actual heat input, travel speed, and preheat temperature on the **weld log sheet**.

**CHECK (2 marks)**
- Perform NDT as per the ITP: typically 100 % Visual + 20 % MT + 100 % UT for critical joints.
- Verify dimensions against tolerance (gap, misalignment, cap height).
- Example: UT scan reveals a **2 mm slag inclusion** at chainage 12 m — an NCR is raised.

**ACT (1.5 marks)**
- **Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** Identify cause (e.g., improper inter-pass cleaning).
- **Corrective Action:** Issue a **Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA)** note; re-train welder; update WPS with stricter cleaning instructions.
- **Preventive Action:** Issue a yard-wide **Quality Alert**; audit similar joints in other blocks.

**(b)** A shipyard experiences the following annual quality costs (₹ lakhs):
$C_P = 50$, $C_A = 30$, $C_{IF} = 120$, $C_{EF} = 40$.
Compute (i) Total Cost of Quality, (ii) Cost of Poor Quality, (iii) suggest two strategies to optimise. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total Cost of Quality (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Q} &= C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\[4pt]
      &= 50 + 30 + 120 + 40 \\[4pt]
      &= 240 \;\text{₹ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cost of Poor Quality (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\[4pt]
      &= 120 + 40 \\[4pt]
      &= 160 \;\text{₹ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

COPQ constitutes **66.67 %** of $C_Q$ — indicating **failure-driven** cost dominance.

**(iii) Optimisation Strategies (3 marks):**

1. **Increase Prevention Spend:** Since $COPQ$ is very high (₹160 lakhs), even doubling $C_P$ (₹50 → ₹100 lakhs) is justified if it can cut $C_{IF}$ by ~30 % (₹120 → ₹84 lakhs), yielding a net saving of approximately **₹14 lakhs**. Strategies: invest in **welder skill-upgradation**, **automated NDT**, and **PQR refresh programs**.

2. **Reduce External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Conduct **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** at the design stage to anticipate in-service failures, especially in **sea-trial critical systems** (steering, main engine, bilge). Implement **Supplier Quality Assurance (SQA)** audits for purchased equipment to reduce warranty claims.

3. **(Bonus) Statistical Process Control (SPC):** Install SPC charts on critical processes (welding, machining) to detect drift early, reducing scrap and rework.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When the question gives a numerical CoQ breakdown, students often forget to compute the **percentage contribution of COPQ to total CQ**. Examiners award **1 mark** for this interpretive insight. Always follow numbers with a **comment sentence**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Examination Focus**

- **Definition Anchor:** QA = planned, systematic, confidence-providing activities across **design → build → deliver**.
- **Tri-party rule:** Every QA scenario involves **Owner ↔ Yard ↔ Class Society (+ Flag State)**.
- **Three Pillars:** **Plan Approval + Survey during Construction + QMS Certification (QSCS)**.
- **PDCA** is the operational backbone; map every QA action into one of its four phases.
- **Cost of Quality** has 4 components; **COPQ = internal + external failure costs** is the lever to optimise.
- **$C_{pk} < C_{p}$** always when process is **off-center**; minimum of two one-sided indices decides.
- **Six Sigma** = 3.4 DPMO; benchmark for world-class shipyards.
- **14 construction stages** from steel stock to sea trials; each has a **Stage Quality Plan**.
- **AQL (ISO 2859-1)** governs attribute-based sampling; **c** = acceptance number, **n** = sample size.
- **Weld traceability chain** (SMD → WPS → PQR → WQT → Weld → NDT) is the **single most-tested** topic in KTU shipbuilding QA papers.
- **NDT matrix:** Surface → MT/PT; Subsurface → UT; Volumetric → RT.
- **IACS** = 12 member societies; **QSCS** audits class societies themselves.
- **TQM** is a philosophy; **ISO 9001** is the certifiable system that operationalises TQM.

> **Closing Tip for KTU 2024:** Always end any 14-mark answer with a **practical shipbuilding example** (e.g., referencing a hull plate, propeller shaft, or steering gear). Examiners reward **applied engineering thinking**, not rote definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel Process Quality Assurance (Hull and Painting) and Outfit Process Quality Assurance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Steel Process Quality Assurance (Hull and Painting) and Outfit Process Quality Assurance # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Steel Process Quality Assurance (Hull) **Formal KTU Definition:** Steel Process Quality Assurance (QA) in shipbuilding refers to the systematic, documented set of planned activities, inspections, tests, and verifications implemented throughout the entire steel-based hull construction cycle—from raw material receipt, cutting, forming, welding, assembly, to final hull inspection—ensuring compliance with classification society rules (IACS members such as ABS, DNV, LR, BV, KR), ISO 9001:2015 quality management principles, and contractual technical specifications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Steel Process QA is the **backbone** of shipbuilding QA because approximately **60–70% of a ship's structural mass** is fabricated from steel. Any defect introduced at the steel stage propagates downstream and becomes exponentially costlier to rectify. ### Intuitive Analogy — The "Human Skeleton" of a Ship Think of a ship's hull as the **human skeleton**. Just as a doctor: 1. Checks bone density before accepting a donor bone (Material Certification), 2. Ensures surgical cuts are precise and clean (Steel Cutting QA), 3. Verifies bone alignment and joint fusion (Welding QA), 4. Inspects the final skeletal structure under load (Hull Inspection & NDT), the shipbuilder performs **Quality Assurance at every stage** of the steel hull. A misaligned bone (weld defect) causes lifelong pain (fatigue crack in service). Hence, QA is not a one-time event but a **continuous, staged gatekeeping process**. --- ## 1.2 Steel Process Quality Assurance (Painting & Coating) **Formal KTU Definition:** Painting QA in shipbuilding is the controlled execution and verification of surface preparation, paint application, and inspection activities designed to protect the steel hull from **corrosion** (anti-corrosive systems) and **marine biofouling** (anti-fouling systems), governed by standards such as **ISO 8501** (surface preparation), **ISO 19840** (coating thickness measurement), **NACE/SSPC**, and paint manufacturers' technical data sheets (TDS). > [!NOTE] > **Key Principle:** Coating life is governed by the *"90/10 rule"* — **90% of coating failures are attributable to inadequate surface preparation, and only 10% to the coating itself.** This is why QA emphasis is heaviest at the preparation stage. ### Intuitive Analogy — "Sunscreen for a Steel Giant" Painting a ship is like applying **layered sunscreen to a person who lives permanently in the ocean**. Without proper skin cleaning (surface preparation to **Sa 2½**), even the most expensive sunscreen (epoxy coating) will peel off in weeks. The shipyard's painting QA ensures: - The "skin" (steel) is **clean, dry, and roughened** to a defined profile, - The "lotion" (primer) is applied at the **correct wet film thickness (WFT)**, - The "top coat" (finish) is **uniform, defect-free, and cured** before immersion. --- ## 1.3 Outfit Process Quality Assurance **Formal KTU Definition:** Outfit Process QA encompasses the planning, execution, inspection, and verification of all post-hull-construction activities, including **machinery outfitting, piping, electrical, HVAC, insulation, joinery, deck fittings, life-saving appliances, and navigation equipment**, ensuring functional readiness, regulatory compliance (SOLAS, MARPOL, Classification Society rules), and customer acceptance prior to delivery. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Outfitting is split into two strategic phases:** > 1. **Pre-outfitting (Pre-erection outfitting):** Fitted onto sub-assemblies and blocks *before* erection on the slipway or in the dry dock. > 2. **Post-outfitting (Dock/Quayside outfitting):** Fitted *after* the hull is launched, during the **fitting-out quay period**. ### Intuitive Analogy — "Furnishing a House After the Bricks are Laid" Once the steel "house" (hull) is launched, every system inside must be installed, tested, and verified. Outfit QA ensures that the **plumbing** (piping), **electrical wiring** (cables), **air-conditioning** (HVAC), **furniture** (joinery), and **appliances** (machinery) are correctly installed and tested before the "family" (crew and cargo) moves in. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Benchmark:** Modern shipyards target **70–80% outfitting completion before launch (Pre-Outfitting)** to reduce the costly quayside fitting-out period by **40–50%**, directly improving shipbuilding productivity measured in **Compensation Hours per CGT (Compensated Gross Tonnage)**. --- ## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Steel + Outfit QA | Pillar | Description | Governing Standard | |---|---|---| | **Material QA** | Mill certificates, chemical & mechanical tests | EN 10025, ASTM A131, IACS UR S1–S31 | | **Dimensional QA** | Tolerances on cutting, forming, assembly | Class Society Hull Rules, ISO 2768 | | **Welding QA** | WPS/PQR, welder qualification, NDT | **IACS UR W**, AWS D1.1, ISO 3834 | | **Coating QA** | Surface prep, application, inspection | **ISO 8501, ISO 19840**, NACE SP0178 | --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Stages of Steel Process QA — The Sequential Gatekeeping Logic The steel hull process follows a **linear-dependency chain**, where each stage's output is the next stage's input. QA must therefore be **stage-gated** — no stage advances until the previous stage's quality is verified and signed off. ### Stage 1: Material Receipt and Identification - **Why:** Ensures the steel supplied matches the design grade (e.g., **AH36**, **DH36**, **EH36** for high-strength plates). - **How:** Visual marking check, **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** verification, third-party **Independent Lab Testing** for critical grades, traceability tagging. - **Critical Document:** **3.1 / 3.2 Certificate** as per **EN 10204**. ### Stage 2: Steel Cutting - **Why:** Dimensional accuracy of cut plates/Profiles is the foundation of fit-up accuracy downstream. - **How:** CNC cutting (plasma, laser, oxy-fuel), template traceability, **dimensional inspection** with calibrated tools. - **Tolerance Reference:** Generally **±2 mm** for plates up to 12 m length as per IACS UR S31. ### Stage 3: Forming (Bending, Rolling, Pressing) - **Why:** Improper forming induces **residual stresses**, **wrinkles**, and **thinning** that compromise structural integrity. - **How:** CNC plate bending rolls, line heating (for thick plates), die forming, with **radius verification** and **UT post-forming check** for grades susceptible to **strain-age cracking**. ### Stage 4: Edge Preparation and Fit-Up - **Why:** Weld joint geometry (groove angle, root gap, root face) directly determines weld quality. - **How:** Machining, grinding, edge cleaning, fit-up gauges, **fit-up inspection** before welding. ### Stage 5: Welding - **Why:** Welds are the most defect-prone region of any steel structure. - **How:** Qualified **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** per **ISO 15614-1** or **AWS D1.1**, qualified welders per **ISO 9606-1**, controlled preheat/interpass temperatures, **production test coupons**. ### Stage 6: Nondestructive Testing (NDT) - **Why:** Detects subsurface and surface flaws invisible to the naked eye. - **How:** **UT (Ultrasonic Testing), RT (Radiographic Testing), MT (Magnetic Particle Testing), PT (Penetrant Testing)** as per acceptance criteria of **IACS UR W11 / W13 / W17**. ### Stage 7: Dimensional and Alignment Control - **Why:** Hull form accuracy governs vessel performance (speed, resistance, propulsion efficiency). - **How:** **Total Station / Laser Survey** at key erection milestones (keel-lay, block erection, launch). ### Stage 8: Final Hull Inspection and Trials Readiness - **Why:** Sign-off is required before launching and sea trials. - **How:** Class Surveyor attendance, **as-built documentation**, **trim & stability booklet** update. --- ## 2.2 Painting Process QA — Layered Defense Theory Painting QA follows the **"Layered Defense"** philosophy — each layer provides a different protective function, and the cumulative Dry Film Thickness (DFT) determines service life. ### Layer Architecture of a Typical Marine Coating System | Layer | Function | Typical DFT (µm) | Inspection Standard | |---|---|---|---| | **Shop Primer** | Temporary protection during storage/fabrication | 15–25 | **ISO 19840** | | **Anti-Corrosive Primer** | Primary corrosion barrier (epoxy) | 100–200 | **ISO 19840** | | **Tie Coat / Anti-Corrosive Build Coat** | Builds DFT barrier | 100–150 | **ISO 19840** | | **Anti-Fouling (Bottom)** | Prevents marine growth (only underwater) | 100–300 | **ISO 19840** | | **Top Coat (Boot-top/Topside)** | UV resistance, aesthetics | 50–75 | **ISO 19840** | > [!NOTE] > **Verification Principle:** DFT is measured using a **magnetic induction or eddy current gauge** at a statistically determined number of points. The **mean DFT must equal or exceed the nominal DFT (NDFT)**, with no individual reading below **80% of NDFT**. ### Surface Preparation Standards (ISO 8501-1) - **Sa 1 — Light Blast Cleaning:** Light contamination removed. - **Sa 2 — Thorough Blast Cleaning:** ~67% of surface free from visible contaminants. - **Sa 2½ — Very Thorough Blast Cleaning:** ~95% of surface free — **most common shipbuilding standard.** - **Sa 3 — White Metal Blast Cleaning:** ~100% of surface free — **highest purity.** --- ## 2.3 Outfit Process QA — Functional Verification Logic Outfit QA is verified through a structured **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** that maps each system to specific hold points, witness points, and review points. ### Major Outfit Systems and Their QA Focus | System | QA Focus | Critical Standard/Test | |---|---|---| | **Main Propulsion** | Shaft alignment, vibration levels | ISO 484/1, Class vibration limits | | **Piping (Sea, Bilge, Fuel, Oil)** | Hydrostatic test (1.5× design pressure), leak test | **IACS UR P2.11**, Class Rules | | **Electrical (Generator, Switchboard, Cable)** | Insulation resistance, earth continuity, generator load test | **IEC 60092**, Class Rules | | **HVAC** | Airflow balance, duct leakage test | **SMACNA**, Class Rules | | **Fire-Fighting (CO₂, Foam, Sprinkler)** | System integrity, nozzle coverage, alarms | **SOLAS Ch. II-2**, **FSS Code** | | **Navigation & Communication** | GMDSS, gyro, radar, ECDIS verification | **SOLAS Ch. V**, **IMO Resolutions** | | **Life-Saving (Lifeboats, Lifejackets)** | Load test, release mechanism test | **SOLAS Ch. III**, **LSA Code** | | **Sea Trial** | Speed, maneuverability, vibration, noise, all systems functional | **IACS UR S31**, Class attendance | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam relevant. Memorize the variables, units, and boundary conditions.** ### Formula Sheet — Steel & Painting QA | # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Limit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $DFT = \dfrac{WFT \times \text{Volume Solids \%}}{100}$ | Wet-to-Dry Film Thickness conversion | µm | Solids content from TDS | | 2 | $\text{Spreading Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Volume Solids \%} \times 10}{DFT(\mu m)}$ | Theoretical coverage per litre | m²/L | Theoretical; practical = × 0.7 | | 3 | $A_{plate} = L \times B$ (flat), $A_{cyl} = \pi D L$ (curved) | Surface area for paint estimation | m² | Add 10–15% for wastage | | 4 | $\text{Paint Required} = \dfrac{A_{total}}{\text{Spreading Rate}}$ | Total paint quantity | Litres | Round up to nearest pack size | | 5 | $\eta_{weld} = \dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Welding deposition efficiency | % | Typically **60–70%** | | 6 | $P_{preheat} = f(C_{eq}, t, H)$ | Preheat temp. (carbon equivalent & thickness) | °C | **IIW** formula | | 7 | $C_{eq} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent (weldability) | wt% | If $C_{eq} \leq 0.45$, low risk | | 8 | $C_{eq}^{PCM} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn+Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$ | PCM Carbon Equivalent (modern) | wt% | Used for high-strength TMCP steels | | 9 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{n \cdot SF}$ | Allowable stress with safety factor | MPa | $n \geq 1.0$ partial, $SF \geq 1.5$ for steel | | 10 | $\Delta_{hull} = \sqrt{\Delta_{x}^{2} + \Delta_{y}^{2} + \Delta_{z}^{2}}$ | Hull tolerance vector deviation | mm | Compare with **IACS S31** tolerances | ### Formula Sheet — Outfit QA | # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Limit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 11 | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$ | Hydrostatic test pressure | bar | Hold **30 minutes** minimum | | 12 | $P_{test,pneumatic} = 1.25 \times P_{design}$ | Pneumatic test pressure | bar | Where water test unsafe | | 13 | $R_{insulation} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{leak}} \quad [M\Omega]$ | Insulation resistance test | MΩ | **Min 1 MΩ per kV** (IEC 60092) | | 14 | $S_{trial} = \dfrac{V_{trial}}{V_{contract}} \times 100\%$ | Speed trial satisfaction index | % | Must be **≥ 100%** of contract speed | | 15 | $T_{noise} = T_{base} + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{0}}\right)$ | Noise level addition (decibel sum) | dB | Per octave band analysis | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Formula 6 (preheat) and Formula 7 (carbon equivalent) are the **most frequently tested** in Part A questions on welding QA. Formula 1 (WFT to DFT) is the most asked in painting QA. --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Wet Film Thickness to Dry Film Thickness Conversion > **Scenario:** A shipyard applies an epoxy primer with **60% Volume Solids** at a Wet Film Thickness (WFT) of **200 µm**. Determine the Dry Film Thickness (DFT) obtained. ### Step 1: Identify Governing Principle When a liquid coating is applied, it contains both **solid (non-volatile) components** that remain as the dry film and **solvent (volatile) components** that evaporate during curing. The Volume Solids percentage (provided in the manufacturer's TDS) defines the fraction of liquid coating that becomes the solid dry film. ### Step 2: State the Formula $$ DFT = \frac{WFT \times V_{s}}{100} $$ where: - $DFT$ = Dry Film Thickness (in µm), - $WFT$ = Wet Film Thickness applied (in µm), - $V_{s}$ = Volume Solids percentage from TDS. ### Step 3: Substitute the Values $$ DFT = \frac{200 \times 60}{100} $$ ### Step 4: Perform the Calculation $$ DFT = \frac{12000}{100} = 120 \;\mu m $$ ### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation The actual DFT achieved is **120 µm**, which can be **verified in the field** using a calibrated magnetic gauge at multiple points per ISO 19840. > **Inverse Check — Spreading Rate:** If the surface area to paint is **1000 m²**, then the theoretical paint required is: $$ \text{Spreading Rate} = \frac{V_{s} \times 10}{DFT} = \frac{60 \times 10}{120} = 5 \; m^{2}/L $$ $$ \text{Paint Volume} = \frac{A_{total}}{SR} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200 \; L $$ > With practical wastage factor **0.7**, **Actual Paint Required:** $$ V_{actual} = \frac{200}{0.7} \approx 285.71 \;\text{L} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Carbon Equivalent (CE) Calculation and Preheat Determination > **Scenario:** A hull plate has the following ladle analysis (wt%): **C = 0.16, Mn = 1.40, Si = 0.30, Cr = 0.05, Mo = 0.02, V = 0.01, Ni = 0.03, Cu = 0.04.** Determine the IIW Carbon Equivalent and the recommended preheat temperature using the **IIW/ASM Preheat Rule** for plate thickness **25 mm**. ### Step 1: Apply the IIW Carbon Equivalent Formula $$ C_{eq}^{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute Component Values $$ \begin{aligned} C_{eq}^{IIW} &= 0.16 + \frac{1.40}{6} + \frac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.01}{5} + \frac{0.03 + 0.04}{15} \\[6pt] &= 0.16 + 0.2333 + \frac{0.08}{5} + \frac{0.07}{15} \\[6pt] &= 0.16 + 0.2333 + 0.0160 + 0.00467 \\[6pt] &\approx 0.414 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Interpret the Result A $C_{eq} \leq 0.40$ is generally considered **low weldability risk**. A $C_{eq} = 0.414$ is in the **medium risk zone**, especially for thicker plates. ### Step 4: Apply the IIW Preheat Guidance (ASM Rule of Thumb) The preheat temperature can be estimated using the simplified rule: $$ T_{preheat} \;\text{(°C)} \approx 350 \times \sqrt{C_{eq} - 0.25} \quad \text{for } C_{eq} > 0.25 $$ Wait — this formula applies when $C_{eq}$ is on the **higher side**. For $C_{eq} \approx 0.414$ and combined thickness of plate + weld metal ~ 30 mm, the **IACS / AWS guidance** is: $$ T_{preheat} \geq 75 \;°C \quad \text{to} \quad 150 \;°C $$ Let us apply the **AWS D1.1 (Table 3.2) minimum preheat based on $C_{eq}$ and combined thickness:** > For $C_{eq} = 0.41$ and combined thickness $\leq 40$ mm, **minimum preheat = 75 °C**. ### Step 5: Final QA Recommendation - Preheat to **≥ 75 °C** and maintain interpass temperature **≤ 230 °C**. - Use **low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., AWS E7016 / E7018) to avoid **hydrogen-assisted cracking (HAC)**. - Conduct **post-weld NDT** with **UT** after **48 hours cooling** to detect any delayed HAC. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Hydrostatic Test Pressure for an Outfit Piping System > **Scenario:** A ship's ballast line has a **design pressure of 10 bar**. Determine the hydrostatic test pressure per IACS UR P2.11, the minimum hold time, and the acceptance criteria. ### Step 1: Apply the Test Pressure Rule $$ P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute and Calculate $$ P_{test} = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \; \text{bar} $$ ### Step 3: Hold Time and Acceptance - **Hold time:** Pressure of 15 bar must be held for **30 minutes minimum**. - **Visual inspection:** No leaks, no pressure drop > **2%** during hold. - **Dye penetrant or visual** at joints. ### Step 4: Safety Note Pneumatic testing is permitted **only when hydrostatic is impractical** (e.g., compressed air systems), and then: $$ P_{test,pneumatic} = 1.25 \times P_{design} = 12.5 \; \text{bar} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of NDT Method for Steel Hull Welds | Defect Type | Best NDT Method | Reason | Standard Reference | |---|---|---|---| | **Slag inclusion** | RT (X-ray) or UT | Volumetric defect visible on radiograph; UT detects by reflection | **IACS UR W11** | | **Lack of fusion** | UT (ToFD / Phased Array) | Planar defect, UT gives sharp echoes | **IACS UR W13** | | **Surface crack** | MT (Magnetic Particle) or PT (Dye Penetrant) | Open to surface, MT/PT are surface methods | **IACS UR W17** | | **Porosity** | RT | Distributed volumetric, visible as dark spots | **IACS UR W11** | | **Root undercut** | Visual + PT | Surface groove at weld root | **AWS D1.1** | | **Lamellar tearing** | UT pre- and post-weld | Through-thickness weakness detected by UT | **BS 7264** | --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Acceptance Criteria for Welds (IACS UR W11) | Defect Category | Acceptance Limit (Butt Welds, Class I) | |---|---| | **Porosity (cumulative)** | ≤ **3% of weld length** per 100 mm | | **Solid inclusions** | Length ≤ 3 mm, total ≤ 10 mm per 100 mm | | **Lack of fusion** | **Not acceptable** | | **Cracks** | **Not acceptable** | | **Undercut** | Depth ≤ **0.5 mm**, length ≤ 50 mm in 300 mm | | **Root concavity** | ≤ **2 mm** depth | --- # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Steel Process QA Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Steel Receipt
Grade A B D E AH36 etc] --> B{MTC Verification
EN 10204 3.1 3.2} B -- Pass --> C[Steel Storage
Traceability Tagging] B -- Fail --> B1[Rejection NCR Raised] C --> D[Cutting
CNC Plasma Laser Oxy] D --> E[Edge Preparation
Groove per WPS] E --> F[Fit Up
Gap Root Face Alignment] F --> G{Welding
Qualified WPS Welder} G --> H[NDT Inspection
UT RT MT PT] H -- Pass --> I[Dimensional Check
Total Station Survey] H -- Fail --> I1[Rework Repair per WPQR] I1 --> G I --> J[Block Assembly
Erection on Slipway] J --> K[Final Hull Survey
Class Surveyor Sign Off] K --> L[Ready for Painting] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Painting Process QA Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Steel Surface
Post Fabrication] --> P2{Inspection of
Surface Condition} P2 -- Mill Scale Rust Oil --> P3[Surface Preparation
ISO 8501] P3 --> P3a{Achieved Grade} P3a -- Sa 2.5 --> P4[Dust and Cleanliness Check
ISO 8502] P3a -- Sa 1 or Below --> P2b[Re Blast] P4 --> P5[Shop Primer Application
WFT Gauge Check] P5 --> P6[DFT Verification
ISO 19840] P6 -- 80 percent NDFT min --> P7[Anti Corrosive Primer
Epoxy 100 to 200 micron] P7 --> P8[Build Coat Application] P8 --> P9[Top Coat or Anti Fouling] P9 --> P10[Final DFT Audit
Holiday Detection] P10 --> P11[Class Sign Off
Coating Record Book] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Outfit Process QA Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BlockA[Pre Outfit Stage] A1[Machinery Pre Installation] --> A2[Piping Spool Pre Fit] A2 --> A3[Electrical Cable Tray Pre Lay] A3 --> A4[Outfit Block Inspection] end BlockA --> B[Launch Event] subgraph BlockB[Quayside Outfit Stage] B --> C1[Machinery Alignment
Shaft Laser] C1 --> C2[Piping System Hydro Test
1.5 x Design] C2 --> C3[Electrical Load Test
Generator Switchboard] C3 --> C4[HVAC Balancing] C4 --> C5[Safety Systems
SOLAS Verification] end BlockB --> D[Mooring and Dockside Trials] D --> E[Sea Trials
Speed Maneuver Vibration Noise] E --> F[Class Survey Final
Delivery to Owner] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Defect Disposition in Welding QA ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[NDT Result Obtained] --> D2{Defect Found?} D2 -- No --> D3[Accept Weld
Mark on Weld Map] D2 -- Yes --> D4{Surface or
Subsurface?} D4 -- Surface --> D5{MT or PT
Verification} D5 -- Reject --> D6[Grind Out
Repair Weld] D5 -- Accept --> D3 D4 -- Subsurface --> D7{UT RT
Evaluation} D7 -- Above Limit --> D6 D7 -- Critical Crack --> D8[Cut Out and Re weld
Procedure Re qualification] D8 --> D9[Re NDT
Full Cycle] D6 --> D9 D9 --> D3 ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Hierarchy — Quality Document Pyramid in Shipbuilding ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Class Rules
IACS UR Series] --> Q2[Shipyard QMS Manual
ISO 9001] Q2 --> Q3[Project Quality Plan
PQP] Q3 --> Q4[Inspection and Test Plan
ITP] Q4 --> Q5[Work Instructions
WPS NDT Procedures] Q5 --> Q6[Records
MTCs NDT Reports MTRs] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern] **"List and briefly explain the three mandatory documents required before any welding begins on a hull plate in a class-approved shipyard."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three mandatory documents are: 1. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** — A documented procedure qualified per **ISO 15614-1** (or ASME Section IX / AWS D1.1) specifying the welding process, joint type, filler metal, preheat, interpass, and post-weld heat treatment. 2. **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)** — The test record proving the WPS meets mechanical and metallurgical requirements (tensile, bend, impact, hardness). 3. **Welder Qualification Certificate** — Issued per **ISO 9606-1**, valid for the process, material group, and weld type the welder is certified for. Without these, **IACS UR W11 requires immediate stop-work**. > **Marking split:** 1 mark each for naming + 1 mark for the standard. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern] **"What is Carbon Equivalent (CE), and why is it a critical parameter in welding QA?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** Carbon Equivalent is a numerical index expressing the combined effect of alloying elements (C, Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni, V, Cu) on weldability, calculated by the **IIW formula**: $$ C_{eq} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15} $$ - **Significance (2 Marks):** A higher $C_{eq}$ increases **hardness in the HAZ**, raising susceptibility to **hydrogen-assisted cracking (HAC)** and **cold cracking**. For $C_{eq} > 0.45$, **preheat** becomes mandatory to slow the cooling rate and allow hydrogen to diffuse out, ensuring weld integrity in service. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern] **(a)** Describe in detail the **sequential stages of Steel Process Quality Assurance** from raw material receipt to final hull inspection. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **painting QA process for the underwater hull**, including surface preparation grades, coating layers, DFT verification, and acceptance criteria per **ISO 8501 and ISO 19840**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Stage 1 – Material Receipt (1 Mark):** Verification of steel plates and sections against purchase order, including visual marking, **3.1 / 3.2 MTC per EN 10204**, dimensional check, and traceability tagging. For grades **AH36 / DH36 / EH36**, independent lab testing for **Charpy impact at -40°C** is mandatory per **IACS UR S1**. **Stage 2 – Cutting (1 Mark):** CNC cutting (plasma, laser, oxy-fuel) with edge quality and dimensional tolerance check. Tolerance as per IACS UR S31 generally ±2 mm. **Stage 3 – Forming (1 Mark):** Plate bending, line heating, and rolling. Verify radius, check for wrinkles, and conduct **post-forming UT** for plates susceptible to **lamellar tearing**. **Stage 4 – Fit-Up and Welding (2 Marks):** Edge preparation per WPS, fit-up inspection (root gap, misalignment ≤ 3 mm for butt welds), welding by **qualified welders per ISO 9606-1** using **qualified WPS per ISO 15614-1**, with controlled preheat and interpass. **Stage 5 – NDT (1 Mark):** Mandatory **UT for butt welds** (≥ 100% coverage for critical joints), **MT/PT for fillet welds**, **RT for selected critical joints**. Acceptance per **IACS UR W11**. **Stage 6 – Dimensional and Survey (1 Mark):** Total Station survey at each erection milestone (keel-lay, block erection) to ensure hull form matches the lines plan within IACS S31 tolerances. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Surface Preparation (2 Marks):** The underwater hull requires **blast cleaning to Sa 2½** per **ISO 8501-1**, achieving ~95% surface cleanliness. Surface roughness profile measured using **Comparator (ISO 8503)** — typical requirement: **50–100 µm Rz**. **Coating Layers (3 Marks):** 1. **Shop Primer** (15–25 µm) — applied in the fabrication shop for temporary corrosion protection. 2. **Anti-Corrosive Epoxy Primer** (100–200 µm DFT) — primary barrier. 3. **Anti-Corrosive Build Coat** (100–150 µm DFT) — increases barrier thickness. 4. **Anti-Fouling Coating** (100–300 µm DFT) — biocide release to prevent marine growth. **DFT Verification (1 Mark):** Per **ISO 19840**, DFT measured at **5 readings per 10 m² (minimum)**; **mean DFT ≥ NDFT**, **no individual reading < 80% NDFT**, **no more than 1 reading < 90% NDFT per area**. **Acceptance (1 Mark):** - **Holiday Detection Test** at **67.5 V per µm NDFT** (e.g., 200 µm → 13500 V) per **NACE SP0188** to find pinholes. - Adhesion test (cross-cut) per **ISO 2409**. - Final **Coating Record Book** signed by **Class Surveyor**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the concept of **Outfit Process Quality Assurance**, with a clear distinction between **pre-outfitting and post-outfitting**. Discuss the role of the **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A ballast piping system is designed for **8 bar working pressure**. As a QA Engineer, determine the **hydrostatic test pressure**, the **minimum hold time**, the **NDT method**, and the **acceptance criteria**. Justify each value with the governing standard. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Definition (1 Mark):** Outfit Process QA encompasses the systematic quality management of all activities **after steel hull completion**, including machinery, piping, electrical, HVAC, and safety systems. **Pre-Outfitting vs Post-Outfitting (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Pre-Outfitting | Post-Outfitting | |---|---|---| | **When** | During block fabrication (before launch) | After launch, on quayside | | **Scope** | Piping spools, cable trays, foundation plates, machinery skids | Final hook-ups, alignment, commissioning | | **Advantage** | Reduces quayside time, improves productivity | Full system integration testing | | **QA Focus** | Dimensional, fit, traceability | Functional, hydrostatic, electrical, sea trial | **Role of ITP (3 Marks):** The ITP is a matrix document listing every activity, its associated inspection type (**H = Hold**, **W = Witness**, **R = Review**), the responsible party (yard / class / owner), and the acceptance standard. It serves as the **roadmap of QA** throughout the project, ensuring no inspection is missed. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Hydrostatic Test Pressure (3 Marks):** Per **IACS UR P2.11**: $$ P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design} = 1.5 \times 8 = 12 \;\text{bar} $$ **Hold Time (1 Mark):** Pressure held for **30 minutes** minimum with no visible leakage and pressure drop ≤ **2%**. **NDT Method (2 Marks):** - **Visual inspection** at all joints during and after test. - **Dye Penetrant Test (PT)** on welded joints before pressurization. - **Ultrasonic Thickness gauging (UT)** for verification of pipe wall conformity. - **Hydrostatic test** itself serves as a **leak-tightness test** validating weld integrity. **Acceptance Criteria (1 Mark):** - No leakage at joints, fittings, or flanges. - No permanent deformation or pressure drop. - Final certification by **Class Surveyor** and entry in the **Piping Test Record Book**. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Areas (based on KTU valuation trends):** > 1. **Confusing WPS and PQR** — WPS is the *procedure to be used*; PQR is the *record proving it works*. Many students swap these and lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the "80% rule"** in DFT — stating "DFT must be ≥ NDFT" is **incomplete**. The correct statement is mean ≥ NDFT **AND** individual readings ≥ 80% NDFT. > 3. **Using Sa 3 instead of Sa 2½** — Sa 3 is *white metal* and rarely used except for special tanks. Default for ship hull is **Sa 2½**. > 4. **Wrong hydrostatic multiplier** — Hydrostatic = **1.5×**, Pneumatic = **1.25×**. Many students write 2× or interchange them. > 5. **Skipping standards citation** — Always quote **ISO / IACS / AWS** standard numbers. Examiners award 0.5–1 mark for **correct standard citation**. > 6. **Conflating pre- and post-outfit** — A clear table or distinction earns full 3 marks; vague single-line answers earn 1 mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steel Process QA (Hull & Painting) + Outfit Process QA** - ✅ **Hull Steel Grades:** Ordinary strength (A, B, D, E) and High-strength (AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, EH36). - ✅ **MTC:** EN 10204 type **3.1 / 3.2** — non-negotiable document per IACS. - ✅ **Welding Documents:** WPS (per ISO 15614-1), PQR, and **Welder Qualification (ISO 9606-1)**. - ✅ **Carbon Equivalent (CE / IIW):** Threshold **0.45** — above this, **preheat mandatory**. - ✅ **NDT Selection Rule of Thumb:** **UT = subsurface, RT = volumetric, MT/PT = surface**. - ✅ **IACS UR W11:** Governing acceptance standard for hull welds. - ✅ **Surface Prep:** Default to **Sa 2½** (ISO 8501-1); roughness **50–100 µm Rz**. - ✅ **DFT Rule (ISO 19840):** Mean ≥ NDFT; individual readings ≥ **80% NDFT**. - ✅ **Anti-fouling:** Only on underwater hull; biocide-based. - ✅ **Outfit Split:** **Pre-outfit (in block) + Post-outfit (quayside)**. - ✅ **ITP:** Hold (H) / Witness (W) / Review (R) — defines class attendance. - ✅ **Hydrostatic Test:** **1.5 × Design Pressure**, hold **30 minutes**. - ✅ **Pneumatic Test:** **1.25 × Design Pressure** — only when hydro not feasible. - ✅ **Insulation Resistance (Electrical):** Minimum **1 MΩ per kV** (IEC 60092). - ✅ **Sea Trial Verification:** Speed ≥ contract speed; vibration within class limits; all systems functional. - ✅ **Class Sign-off:** Mandatory at every QA milestone — Class Surveyor acts as **independent third-party QA**. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Steel Process QA (Hull and Painting) and Outfit Process QA**

topics.title: Various Quality Standards and Auditing in Shipyards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various Quality Standards and Auditing in Shipyards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Standards** in the context of shipbuilding are a set of documented, internationally recognized, and legally binding specifications, regulations, and procedural frameworks that govern the design, material selection, fabrication, welding, assembly, inspection, and sea-trials of marine vessels. These standards ensure that the built ship conforms to safety, performance, environmental, and statutory requirements prescribed by **Classification Societies (IACS members)**, **Flag State Administrations**, **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, and various **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** frameworks.

**Shipyard Auditing** is the systematic, independent, and documented evaluation process performed to determine whether a shipyard's Quality Management System (QMS), processes, procedures, and outcomes comply with the planned arrangements, regulatory standards, and the effectiveness of these arrangements in achieving quality objectives. Audits may be **First Party (Internal)**, **Second Party (Customer/Class Society)**, or **Third Party (Independent Certification Body)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Quality standards in shipyards are NOT limited to ISO 9001. They span across marine-class rules (IACS UR series), statutory codes (SOLAS, MARPOL), welding standards (AWS D1.1, ASME IX), and NDT standards (ASNT, EN 473). Students must be able to distinguish between *voluntary* (ISO) and *mandatory* (Class/Flag) standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a shipyard as a **commercial kitchen preparing meals for thousands of paying customers** at sea. Without quality standards:
- The chef (welder) might use rotten ingredients (defective steel plates).
- The cook (fitter) may forget hygiene protocols.
- The food (ship) might look fine but sink the restaurant (vessel) mid-ocean.

**Quality Standards** are like the **FSSAI food safety regulations + the MasterChef judging criteria** — they define what is acceptable. **Auditing** is the **mystery diner (health inspector)** who walks in unannounced, checks the kitchen, verifies the logs, and gives a report card.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **IACS** | International Association of Classification Societies – sets unified technical standards (UR series) |
| **QMS** | Quality Management System – the documented framework of policies & procedures |
| **Class Survey** | Mandatory survey by Classification Society to maintain class notation |
| **NC / NCR** | Non-Conformance / Non-Conformance Report – deviation from a defined standard |
| **CAPA** | Corrective and Preventive Action – the response mechanism for NCs |
| **OHSMS** | Occupational Health & Safety Management System |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard Reference:** The **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** requires every IACS member society to certify shipyards against ISO 9001 + specific IACS requirements, ensuring globally uniform quality in ship construction and repair.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Quality Standards in Shipbuilding

Quality standards in shipyards operate on **four interconnected layers**:

**Layer 1 – International Regulatory Framework**
- **IMO Conventions**: SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea), MARPOL (Marine Pollution), Load Line Convention, COLREG.
- **ISO Management System Standards**: ISO 9001 (Quality), ISO 14001 (Environment), ISO 45001 (Occupational H&S).

**Layer 2 – Classification Society Rules (IACS Members)**
- Lloyd's Register (LR)
- American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)
- Bureau Veritas (BV)
- DNV (Det Norske Veritas)
- ClassNK (Nippon Kaiji Kyokai)
- Korean Register (KR)
- Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)
- Russian Maritime Register of Shipping (RS)
- These publish **Rules and Regulations** for hull, machinery, electrical, and welding.

**Layer 3 – Industry & Welding Standards**
- **AWS D1.1** – Structural Welding Code (Steel, American Welding Society).
- **ASME Section IX** – Welding and Brazing Qualifications.
- **EN 10160** – Ultrasonic testing of flat steel products.
- **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** – Personnel qualification for NDT.

**Layer 4 – Shipyard Internal Standards**
- Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
- Work Instructions (WIs).
- Quality Plans, ITPs (Inspection and Test Plans).
- Project-specific specifications.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Layer
