- **Why Layer 1?** IMO is the *legislator* of the seas. Conventions become law once a flag state ratifies them.
- **Why Layer 2?** Classification societies are *technical policemen* of the marine industry. A ship without class cannot be insured, financed, or chartered.
- **Why Layer 3?** Welding and NDT are the *make-or-break activities* in shipbuilding. A defective weld is a structural time-bomb.
- **Why Layer 4?** Internal standards operationalize all the above into shop-floor reality.

### 2.3 Auditing Mechanism in Shipyards

A shipyard audit typically follows the **PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** of ISO 9001:

1. **Plan** – Establish audit programme, define scope, criteria, frequency.
2. **Do** – Conduct the audit: opening meeting → document review → on-site observation → interviews → evidence collection.
3. **Check** – Compare findings against audit criteria; classify as Conformity, Observation, Minor NC, or Major NC.
4. **Act** – Issue audit report; ensure CAPA; verify effectiveness; feed into Management Review.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Application |
|---------|------------------|-------------|
| Audit Coverage Ratio | $\text{CR} = \frac{N_{\text{audits conducted}}}{N_{\text{audits planned}}}$ | Should be **≥ 1.0** (100%) |
| Non-Conformance Density | $\text{NCD} = \frac{N_{\text{NC}}}{N_{\text{activities inspected}}}$ | Lower is better; tracks shop-floor quality |
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \frac{N_{\text{passed first time}}}{N_{\text{total inspected}}} \times 100$ | Weld FPY is a critical shipyard KPI |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{rework}} + C_{\text{scrap}} + C_{\text{warranty}} + C_{\text{liability}}$ | Reported in Management Review |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min\left\{\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right\}$ | Used in CNC plate cutting, pipe bending tolerance |
| Audit Cycle Time | $T_{\text{audit}} = T_{\text{open}} + T_{\text{field}} + T_{\text{report}}$ | KPI for audit efficiency |
| Auditor-to-Staff Ratio | $R_{a/s} = \frac{N_{\text{auditors}}}{N_{\text{production staff}}}$ | Typically 1:80 to 1:120 in large yards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to differentiate between *Quality Assurance (QA)* and *Quality Control (QC)* in shipyard context, always state: **QA = process-oriented (the system)**; **QC = product-oriented (the inspection of welds, coatings, dimensions)**. This is a 3-mark guaranteed question in Part A.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above framework is the **backbone of modern shipyard operations** in Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Hyundai Heavy Industries, and Daewoo. Without these standards, a single defective longitudinal weld could cause catastrophic hull failure — a real risk illustrated by the **MOL Comfort (2013)** and **MV Stellar Daisy (2019)** casualties, both of which had audit-related lapses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: First Pass Yield (FPY) for a Shipyard Welding Block

The FPY metric is central to shipbuilding because **welding constitutes 30–40% of total man-hours** in hull construction. Let us derive FPY for a typical welded panel block.

**Given Data (Hypothetical KTU Example):**
- Total weld joints in a forward cargo block: $N_{\text{total}} = 500$ joints
- Welds passed first inspection (visual + NDT): $N_{\text{pass1}} = 425$ joints
- Welds requiring rework: $N_{\text{rework}} = 75$ joints

**Step 1: Define the formula for First Pass Yield.**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{N_{\text{pass1}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the known values.**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{425}{500} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3: Perform the division.**

$$
\frac{425}{500} = 0.85
$$

**Step 4: Convert to percentage.**

$$
\text{FPY} = 0.85 \times 100\% = 85\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A FPY of 85% means 15% of welds needed rework — a reasonable but improvable rate. Best-in-class shipyards target **≥ 92%** FPY.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) for a Shipyard

A shipbuilding project has a tight margin. Let us calculate COPQ for a single ship under construction.

**Given Data:**
- Rework labour cost: $C_{\text{rework}} = \text{₹} 1.5 \text{ Crore}$
- Material scrap cost: $C_{\text{scrap}} = \text{₹} 0.8 \text{ Crore}$
- Warranty / defect liability cost: $C_{\text{warranty}} = \text{₹} 0.6 \text{ Crore}$
- Third-party audit penalty cost: $C_{\text{liability}} = \text{₹} 0.3 \text{ Crore}$

**Step 1: COPQ formula.**

$$
\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{rework}} + C_{\text{scrap}} + C_{\text{warranty}} + C_{\text{liability}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\text{COPQ} = 1.5 + 0.8 + 0.6 + 0.3
$$

**Step 3: Sum the components.**

$$
\text{COPQ} = 3.2 \text{ Crore INR}
$$

**Step 4: Express as a percentage of ship value.**

If the ship contract value is $V = \text{₹} 80 \text{ Crore}$:

$$
\text{COPQ \%} = \frac{3.2}{80} \times 100\% = 4.0\%
$$

**Industry Benchmark:** COPQ of **3–5% of contract value** is typical; world-class yards (Samsung Heavy, Hyundai) operate at **< 2%**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Auditing Standards in Shipyards

| Parameter | **ISO 9001** | **IACS QSCS** | **Class Society Audit** | **OHSAS / ISO 45001** |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Scope | Generic QMS for any industry | QMS specific to shipyards | Hull/machinery technical compliance | Worker safety in shipyard |
| Issuing Body | ISO (Geneva) | IACS (London) | LR, ABS, BV, DNV, etc. | ISO / National OSH bodies |
| Voluntary or Mandatory? | Voluntary (often contractual) | **Mandatory** for IACS members' approval | **Mandatory** to obtain/maintain class | Mandatory in most jurisdictions |
| Audit Frequency | Annual surveillance + 3-yearly recertification | Annual + ad-hoc | During construction + periodic class surveys | Annual + incident-triggered |
| Outcome | ISO 9001 Certificate | QSCS Certificate | Class Notation (e.g., ✠100A1) | OHSAS/ISO 45001 Cert. |
| Focus | Customer satisfaction, process approach | Welding, NDT, testing capability | Structural integrity, machinery performance | Accident prevention, hazard control |
| Failure Consequence | Loss of certification | Loss of IACS-approved status | **Detention / de-classing of ship** | Prosecution, shutdown orders |

### 3.4 Audit Classification Decision Matrix

Auditors classify findings on a four-tier scale. The matrix below is a standard KTU/PECB-style decision tool:

| Finding Type | Definition | Required Action | Time to Close |
|--------------|------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Conformity (C)** | Process meets all criteria | None | — |
| **Observation (OFI)** | Minor concern; risk of future NC | Suggest improvement | Voluntary |
| **Minor NC** | Isolated lapse; no systemic impact | Documented CAPA within 30 days | 30 days |
| **Major NC** | Systemic failure, absence of control, or pattern of NCs | Immediate containment + root-cause analysis + CAPA | 15–30 days; may trigger audit re-visit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Quality Standards in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REGULATORY TIER IMO CONVENTIONS] --> A1[SOLAS]
    A --> A2[MARPOL]
    A --> A3[LOAD LINE]
    A --> A4[COLREG]

    B[CLASSIFICATION TIER IACS MEMBERS] --> B1[LLOYDS REGISTER LR]
    B --> B2[ABS]
    B --> B3[DNV]
    B --> B4[BV]
    B --> B5[CLASSNK]
    B --> B6[INDIAN REGISTER IRS]

    C[INDUSTRY AND TECHNICAL TIER] --> C1[AWS D1 1 WELDING]
    C --> C2[ASME SECTION IX]
    C --> C3[ASNT SNT TC 1A NDT]
    C --> C4[EN 10160 UT TESTING]

    D[ISO MANAGEMENT TIER] --> D1[ISO 9001 QMS]
    D --> D2[ISO 14001 EMS]
    D --> D3[ISO 45001 OHSMS]

    E[SHIPYARD INTERNAL TIER] --> E1[QUALITY PLAN QP]
    E --> E2[INSPECTION AND TEST PLAN ITP]
    E --> E3[STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE SOP]
    E --> E4[WORK INSTRUCTION WI]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
```

### 4.2 Shipyard Audit Process Flow (PDCA-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PLAN AUDIT PROGRAMME] --> P1[DEFINE SCOPE]
    P1 --> P2[SELECT AUDIT TEAM]
    P2 --> P3[ISSUE AUDIT PLAN]

    D[DO CONDUCT AUDIT] --> D1[OPENING MEETING]
    D1 --> D2[DOCUMENT REVIEW]
    D2 --> D3[SHOP FLOOR OBSERVATION]
    D3 --> D4[INTERVIEWS WITH WELDERS FITTERS INSPECTORS]
    D4 --> D5[EVIDENCE COLLECTION]

    C[CHECK EVALUATE] --> C1[CLASSIFY FINDINGS]
    C1 --> C2[CONFORMITY]
    C1 --> C3[OBSERVATION]
    C1 --> C4[MINOR NC]
    C1 --> C5[MAJOR NC]

    A[ACT CLOSE OUT] --> A1[ISSUE AUDIT REPORT]
    A1 --> A2[ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS]
    A2 --> A3[CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    A3 --> A4[PREVENTIVE ACTION]
    A4 --> A5[EFFECTIVENESS VERIFICATION]
    A5 --> A6[FEED TO MANAGEMENT REVIEW]
    A6 --> P
```

### 4.3 Corrective Action Cycle for Non-Conformance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NC[NC IDENTIFIED DURING AUDIT] --> R1[ISSUE NCR NON CONFORMANCE REPORT]
    R1 --> R2[CONTAINMENT IMMEDIATE ACTION]
    R2 --> R3[ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS 5 WHY OR FISHBONE]
    R3 --> R4[DEFINE CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    R4 --> R5[IMPLEMENT CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    R5 --> R6[VERIFY EFFECTIVENESS]
    R6 --> R7{EFFECTIVE?}
    R7 -->|YES| R8[CLOSE NCR]
    R7 -->|NO| R3
    R8 --> R9[UPDATE QMS DOCUMENTATION]
    R9 --> R10[PREVENT RECURRENCE THROUGH SYSTEMIC FIX]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Quality Audit Information Flow in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QMR[QUALITY MANAGEMENT REPRESENTATIVE QMR] --> QA[QA DEPARTMENT]
    QMR --> QC[QC DEPARTMENT]
    QMR --> NDT[NDT SECTION]
    QMR --> WELD[WELDING COORDINATOR]

    QA --> QA1[DOCUMENT CONTROL]
    QA --> QA2[INTERNAL AUDIT CELL]
    QA --> QA3[CALIBRATION CELL]

    QC --> QC1[INCOMING MATERIAL INSPECTION]
    QC --> QC2[IN PROCESS INSPECTION]
    QC --> QC3[FINAL HULL INSPECTION]

    NDT --> NDT1[ULTRASONIC TESTING UT]
    NDT --> NDT2[RADIOGRAPHIC TESTING RT]
    NDT --> NDT3[MAGNETIC PARTICLE TESTING MT]
    NDT --> NDT4[DYE PENETRANT TESTING PT]

    WELD --> WELD1[WELDING PROCEDURE SPECIFICATION WPS]
    WELD --> WELD2[PROCEDURE QUALIFICATION RECORD PQR]
    WELD --> WELD3[WELDER QUALIFICATION TEST WQT]

    QA1 --> EXT[EXTERNAL AUDITORS CLASS SOCIETY ISO CB]
    QA2 --> EXT
    EXT --> CLAS[CLASS SOCIETY SURVEYOR]
    EXT --> ISO[ISO CERTIFICATION BODY]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a 'Non-Conformance Report (NCR)' in the context of a shipyard quality audit. List the four standard classifications of audit findings.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Non-Conformance Report (NCR)** is a formal document raised when a process, product, or service in the shipyard deviates from the specified requirement (e.g., AWS D1.1 weld tolerance, IACS UR W11, or ISO 9001 clause). It records the deviation, the responsible department, and triggers the CAPA cycle.

**Four classifications of audit findings:**
1. **Conformity (C)** – Process fully meets the criteria.
2. **Observation / Opportunity for Improvement (OFI)** – Minor concern; no NC, but a suggestion.
3. **Minor NC** – Isolated non-fulfilment of a requirement.
4. **Major NC** – Systemic failure or total absence of a required control.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six major Classification Societies that are members of IACS.*

**Model Answer:**

The six major IACS member classification societies are:
1. Lloyd's Register (LR), UK
2. American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), USA
3. Bureau Veritas (BV), France
4. DNV (Det Norske Veritas), Norway
5. ClassNK (Nippon Kaiji Kyokai), Japan
6. Korean Register of Shipping (KR), South Korea

*(Other valid members: Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), Russian Maritime Register of Shipping (RS), RINA, CCS, CRS, PRS, IRClass.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **DO NOT** mix up ISO standards with Class Society rules. ISO 9001 is a *management* standard; LR/ABS/DNV are *technical* rules.
> - **DO NOT** write "IACS audits shipyards directly." IACS audits its *member societies*; the member society audits the shipyard.
> - When drawing the audit cycle, always include the **closing meeting** and **management review** steps. Skipping them costs 2 marks.
> - Students lose marks by confusing **QA (system)** with **QC (product)**. Memorize: *QA builds it right; QC checks it.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **ISO 9001:2015 Quality Management System** framework as applied to a shipbuilding organization. Discuss the **PDCA cycle**, **risk-based thinking**, and the **seven quality management principles** in the shipyard context.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **First-Party, Second-Party, and Third-Party audits** in a shipyard. Give two specific examples of each from a real shipyard.

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Introduction:** ISO 9001:2015 is the globally recognized QMS standard. A shipyard that holds ISO 9001 certification demonstrates its ability to consistently provide products (ships) and services (repairs, conversions) that meet customer, class, and regulatory requirements.

**PDCA Cycle in Shipyard Context:**

$$
\text{PLAN} \rightarrow \text{Establish QMS scope, quality objectives, risk register, and audit calendar}
$$

$$
\text{DO} \rightarrow \text{Implement SOPs, ITPs, WPSs, training, and production}
$$

$$
\text{CHECK} \rightarrow \text{Conduct internal audits, class surveys, NDT, customer feedback}
$$

$$
\text{ACT} \rightarrow \text{Management review, CAPA, continual improvement (Kaizen)}
$$

**Seven Quality Management Principles (applicable to shipyard):**

1. **Customer Focus** – Building to owner's specification and class rules.
2. **Leadership** – Top management commitment (e.g., MD's Quality Policy).
3. **Engagement of People** – Skill-matrix of welders; competency mapping.
4. **Process Approach** – Block-construction process flow from steel stock to launch.
5. **Improvement** – Kaizen events to reduce rework.
6. **Evidence-based Decision Making** – FPY, COPQ, NCR trends.
7. **Relationship Management** – Vendor (steel supplier) evaluation; class society liaison.

**Risk-Based Thinking:** ISO 9001:2015 requires the shipyard to identify risks (e.g., welding defects, supply-chain delays) and opportunities (e.g., modular construction automation) at the process level.

##### Model Solution to (b):

| Audit Type | Description | Shipyard Example 1 | Shipyard Example 2 |
|------------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **First-Party (Internal)** | Conducted by the shipyard's own trained auditors on its own QMS. | Internal audit of the welding shop against AWS D1.1. | Internal audit of the painting and corrosion-protection section. |
| **Second-Party (Customer/Class)** | Conducted by an external party having a contractual interest (e.g., ship owner or classification society). | Classification surveyor (e.g., LR) attending shop-floor thickness measurements. | Ship owner representative inspecting accommodation block during construction. |
| **Third-Party (Independent CB)** | Conducted by an independent certification body for ISO/regulatory certification. | ISO 9001 surveillance audit by TÜV SÜD. | ISO 45001 certification audit by Bureau Veritas Certification. |

**Significance:** All three layers ensure that the shipyard's QMS is robust, customer-aligned, and externally validated.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe in detail the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**. How does it integrate ISO 9001 with class-specific requirements for shipyards?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **role of a Classification Society Surveyor** during the construction of a new ship. List at least **six specific hold points** at which the surveyor must inspect or witness the work.

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition:** IACS QSCS is a unified scheme under which IACS member classification societies audit and certify the quality systems of shipyards, ensuring a common global benchmark for ship construction and repair quality.

**Integration with ISO 9001:**

QSCS = ISO 9001 + IACS-specific additional requirements. The IACS additional requirements cover:

- **Welding Procedure Qualification Records (PQR)** and **Welder Qualification Tests (WQT)** per UR W17 and UR W18.
- **NDT capability and personnel certification** per UR Z17, UR Z23.
- **Material traceability** from receipt of steel plates to erected block.
- **Process control** in cutting, forming, fit-up, welding, NDT, and surface protection.
- **Calibration of measuring and testing equipment**.
- **Documented quality plans aligned with class rules**.

**Audit Cycle:** Initial certification audit → Annual surveillance audit → Re-certification audit every 3 years. Loss of QSCS approval means the yard cannot take new construction contracts requiring that class.

##### Model Solution to (b):

The **Classification Society Surveyor** acts as the technical representative of the class society throughout ship construction. The surveyor has the authority to **withhold, suspend, or withdraw the ship's class** if non-conformities are found.

**Six Specific Hold Points (UR S21 / S22 / Z23 compliant):**

1. **Approval of steel mill test certificates** and verification of material grade before cutting.
2. **Inspection of plate preparation and edge preparation** before fit-up welding.
3. **Fit-up inspection** of butts, seams, and cruciform joints (gap, alignment, root face).
4. **Stage-by-stage welding inspection** (visual + dimensional) and witnessing of NDT (UT/RT of critical joints).
5. **Erection survey at the slipway / dock** – hull alignment, line-shaft alignment, hull penetrations.
6. **Sea trial attendance** – verification of machinery, steering, navigation systems, and emergency procedures.

Additional hold points include **launch, mooring trials, and tilt test**, depending on vessel type.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Quality Standards in shipyards are multi-tiered**: IMO → IACS/Class → Industry (AWS/ASME/ASNT) → ISO Management → Internal SOPs.
- **ISO 9001:2015** is process-based, risk-based, and uses the **PDCA cycle**.
- **IACS** comprises 12 member societies (LR, ABS, BV, DNV, KR, ClassNK, IRS, RS, RINA, CCS, CRS, PRS); it sets **Unified Requirements (UR)**.
- **IACS QSCS** = ISO 9001 + class-specific additional requirements for shipyards.
- **Three audit types**: First Party (Internal), Second Party (Customer/Class), Third Party (Independent CB).
- **Audit findings**: Conformity, Observation, Minor NC, Major NC.
- **NCR triggers CAPA** → Root Cause Analysis (5-Why / Fishbone) → Corrective Action → Verification → Closure.
- **Hold Points** are mandatory inspection gates; passing them is essential to proceed to the next construction phase.
- **Welding standards** (AWS D1.1, ASME IX) and **NDT standards** (ASNT SNT-TC-1A, EN 473) are non-negotiable in modern shipyards.
- **Key KPIs**: FPY (First Pass Yield), COPQ, NCR closure time, audit coverage ratio.
- **Difference**: **QA** = process/system; **QC** = product/inspection.
- **Class notation** (e.g., ✠100A1, ✠1A1) is the formal mark of compliance on the hull.
- **Indian context**: Cochin Shipyard Limited (CSL), Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders (MDL), GRSE are ISO 9001 + IACS QSCS certified yards.
- **Loss of class** = ship cannot trade; this is the ultimate consequence of poor quality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview on ISO Standard in Ship Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview on ISO Standard in Ship Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ISO Standards in Shipbuilding** refer to the family of internationally recognized standards developed by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that govern the design, construction, repair, operation, recycling, and quality management processes within the marine and shipbuilding industry. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding*, these standards form the **backbone of the documented management system** that a shipyard must adopt to deliver vessels that are safe, seaworthy, environmentally compliant, and built to a globally auditable benchmark.

The most operationally critical ISO standards applied across modern shipyards are:

- **ISO 9001:2015** – Quality Management Systems (QMS)
- **ISO 14001:2015** – Environmental Management Systems (EMS)
- **ISO 45001:2018** – Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems (OH&S)
- **ISO 30000:2009** – Risk management and decision-making principles for ships and marine technology
- **ISO 10005:2018** – Quality plans
- **ISO 19011:2018** – Guidelines for auditing management systems
- **ISO 3834** – Quality requirements for fusion welding of metallic materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4, the focus is *not* on each clause of ISO 9001, but on the **overview** — what ISO standards exist for shipbuilding, why they exist, and how they integrate with classification society rules (IACS members like Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV) and statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL).

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Universal Language" Analogy

Imagine a ship as a **collaborative novel** being written simultaneously by **ten different authors in five different countries** — Korean hull fabricators, German engine suppliers, Indian outfitting teams, Norwegian system integrators, and Brazilian steel workers. Without a *common grammar and editorial rulebook*, the final "novel" (the ship) would be incoherent and unsafe.

**ISO standards are that universal grammar.** They tell every contributor:

- *What* the expected output must look like (quality criteria),
- *How* work must be documented (procedures, records),
- *Who* is responsible for what (roles and authorities),
- *When* a check must happen (inspection and test plans), and
- *Why* a process exists (risk-based thinking).

In a shipyard, ISO 9001 acts like the **editor-in-chief's handbook**, ISO 14001 like the **environmental chapter**, ISO 45001 like the **safety foreword**, and ISO 30000 like the **risk-analysis appendix**. Together they ensure the vessel — a multi-decade asset costing hundreds of millions of dollars — is delivered with **traceable quality, documented safety, and verifiable compliance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** Modern shipbuilding projects can involve **over 2 million components** sourced from **30+ countries**. ISO standards provide the only practical mechanism to maintain **traceability, consistency, and auditability** at this scale.

### 1.3 Why ISO Standards are Mandatory in Modern Shipyards

| Stakeholder Driver | ISO Standard Response |
|---|---|
| Customer (Shipowner) demands defect-free delivery | ISO 9001:2015 — QMS |
| Regulatory pressure on emissions & pollution | ISO 14001:2015 — EMS |
| Worker safety on hazardous yard operations | ISO 45001:2018 — OH&S |
| Complex subcontractor networks need uniformity | ISO 10005:2018 — Quality Plans |
| Welding is the *critical-path* activity in hull construction | ISO 3834 — Welding Quality |
| Audits of suppliers and internal processes | ISO 19011:2018 — Audit Guidelines |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The High-Level Structure (HLS) of Modern ISO Management Standards

Since 2012, ISO has harmonized its management system standards under a **common 10-clause framework** known as the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** — also called **Annex SL**. This means ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and ISO 45001 share the same skeleton, allowing shipyards to build a single **Integrated Management System (IMS)**.

The 10-clause architecture is:

1. **Scope** — Defines boundaries of the management system.
2. **Normative References** — Identifies supporting standards.
3. **Terms and Definitions** — Vocabulary used throughout the standard.
4. **Context of the Organization** — Understanding the shipyard and its issues.
5. **Leadership** — Top management commitment and responsibilities.
6. **Planning** — Actions to address risks and opportunities.
7. **Support** — Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information.
8. **Operation** — Operational planning and control of shipbuilding processes.
9. **Performance Evaluation** — Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review.
10. **Improvement** — Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HLS matters for KTU students:** Examiners frequently ask how ISO 9001 integrates with ISO 14001/45001 in an Integrated Management System. The answer is *because they share the same HLS* — they can be audited together by a single combined audit team.

### 2.2 The PDCA Cycle Embedded in ISO Standards

Every ISO management standard is built around the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle, originally articulated by **Walter Shewhart** and popularized by **W. Edwards Deming**.

Applied to shipbuilding:

- **Plan** — Establish quality objectives, ITPs (Inspection and Test Plans), WPSs (Welding Procedure Specifications), and risk registers for the new vessel project.
- **Do** — Execute the production process: cutting, forming, welding, blasting, painting, outfitting, launching.
- **Check** — Monitor NDT results, dimensional surveys, coating thickness, torque audits; perform internal audits.
- **Act** — Issue **Non-Conformance Reports (NCRs)**, root-cause analysis, corrective actions, lessons learned — feed back into the next block or next vessel.

### 2.3 Mapping ISO Standards to Shipbuilding Phases

| Shipbuilding Phase | Applicable ISO Standard(s) | Quality Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Contract & Design | ISO 9001 (Cl. 8.3), ISO 30000 | Design review, FMEA |
| Material Procurement | ISO 9001 (Cl. 8.4) | Mill certificate traceability |
| Steel Cutting & Forming | ISO 9001 (Cl. 8.5.1) | Process capability indices |
| Welding (Hull & Sub-assemblies) | ISO 3834-2 / 3834-3, ISO 15614-1 | WPS, PQR, Welder qualification |
| Surface Preparation & Coating | ISO 8501, ISO 19840 | DFT measurement, blast profile |
| Pipe & Outfitting Fabrication | ISO 9001, ISO 3834 | Hydrostatic tests, traceability |
| Engine & Propulsion Installation | ISO 9001, Maker standards | Maker's ITP, FAT/SAT |
| Sea Trials & Delivery | ISO 9001, ISO 19011 | Customer acceptance protocol |
| Ship Recycling / End-of-Life | ISO 30005, ISO 30000-2 | IHM (Inventory of Hazardous Materials) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Process Capability (Cp, Cpk)

Although ISO standards are *management* standards (not engineering calculation standards), they demand **statistical evidence** that shipbuilding processes are in control. The most tested metrics in KTU papers are the **Process Capability Indices**.

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

Where:
- $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit (e.g., maximum allowable weld undercut depth in mm)
- $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit (e.g., minimum coating DFT in microns)
- $\mu$ = Process mean
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of the process

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A shipbuilding question may give you a USL, LSL, $\mu$, and $\sigma$ and ask you to compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Always state the interpretation: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is the industry-accepted threshold for a *capable* process; $C_{pk} \lt 1.00$ means the process is *not capable* and requires corrective action.

### 2.5 ISO 3834 — Quality Levels for Fusion Welding (Critical for Hull Fabrication)

ISO 3834 classifies welding quality into four descending levels:

| Part | Quality Level | Typical Application in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 3834-2** | Comprehensive Quality Requirements | Warships, LNG carriers, critical hull structures |
| **ISO 3834-3** | Standard Quality Requirements | Commercial cargo ships — general hull fabrication |
| **ISO 3834-4** | Elementary Quality Requirements | Non-critical secondary structures |
| **ISO 3834-5** | Claims Conforming to ISO 3834-2/3/4 | Contractual declaration of compliance |

A shipyard certified to **ISO 3834-2** demonstrates the highest level of welding process control — directly impacting hull integrity and classification society acceptance.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

- **Integrated Audit Savings:** A shipyard with a tri-certified IMS (ISO 9001 + 14001 + 45001) saves up to **30–40% of audit cost** vs. running three separate audits.
- **Bid Eligibility:** Most global shipowners (Maersk, MSC, Shell, BP) and navies now *mandate* ISO 9001 and ISO 45001 certification as a **pre-qualification** for tender participation.
- **Insurance Premiums:** Certified shipyards receive **lower hull-insurance premiums** because insurers recognize documented process control.
- **Cross-Border Recognition:** ISO acceptance eliminates the need for country-specific quality approvals in 160+ member nations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ from First Principles

In shipbuilding, suppose a **coating applicator** measures **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** in microns on a hull plate. The specification is: $USL = 400 \mu m$, $LSL = 200 \mu m$. From a sample of $n = 25$ measurements, we obtain $\mu = 310 \mu m$ and $\sigma = 25 \mu m$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Upper Capability:**

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{400 - 310}{3 \times 25}
$$

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{90}{75}
$$

$$
C_{pk,upper} = 1.20
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Lower Capability:**

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{310 - 200}{3 \times 25}
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{110}{75}
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = 1.4667
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Minimum (the process is judged by its weakest side):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.20, \; 1.4667)
$$

$$
C_{pk} = 1.20
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret against ISO/ts industry benchmarks:**

| $C_{pk}$ Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | Excellent (Six-Sigma class) |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} \lt 1.67$ | Capable (Industry acceptable) |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} \lt 1.33$ | Marginally capable — improvement needed |
| $C_{pk} \lt 1.00$ | Not capable — process re-engineering required |

> **Conclusion:** The coating process with $C_{pk} = 1.20$ is **marginally capable** — acceptable but requires corrective action to push it above the **1.33 threshold** demanded by most classification societies for critical hull coatings.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Non-Conformance Rate (NCR) Tracking Index

A yard records the following across one vessel project:
- Total weld joints inspected: $N = 12{,}500$
- Rejected joints: $R = 75$

**Step 1 — Compute the NCR rate per 10,000 joints:**

$$
\text{NCR Rate} = \frac{R}{N} \times 10{,}000
$$

$$
\text{NCR Rate} = \frac{75}{12{,}500} \times 10{,}000
$$

$$
\text{NCR Rate} = 60 \text{ NCRs per 10,000 joints}
$$

**Step 2 — Benchmark against industry:**

| Yard Tier | Acceptable NCR Rate (per 10,000 joints) |
|---|---|
| World-class (Korean majors) | $\leq 30$ |
| Tier-1 commercial | $30$ – $60$ |
| Tier-2 / smaller yards | $60$ – $100$ |
| Below standard | $> 100$ |

> **Conclusion:** With **60 NCRs per 10,000**, the yard sits at the *upper boundary of Tier-1*. ISO 9001 Cl. 10.2 requires documented corrective action; the yard must perform root-cause analysis on the 75 rejections (typically: 40% welder qualification gaps, 35% parameter drift, 25% material traceability lapses).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Pillars of an Integrated Management System

| Dimension | ISO 9001:2015 (Quality) | ISO 14001:2015 (Environment) | ISO 45001:2018 (Safety) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Intent** | Customer satisfaction & product conformity | Environmental protection & pollution prevention | Worker safety & health |
| **Key Process Metric** | $C_{pk}$, NCR rate, On-Time Delivery | Waste per tonne, VOC emissions | TRIR (Total Recordable Incident Rate), LTI |
| **Shipyard Application** | Hull tolerance, welding quality, sea-trial results | Ballast-water management, paint VOC, scrap steel | Confined-space entry, working at height, hot work |
| **Risk-Based Thinking Focus** | Product non-conformance risk | Environmental aspect impact | Hazard identification |
| **Top Management Role** | Quality policy, QMS review | Environmental policy, compliance obligations | OH&S policy, worker consultation |
| **Documentation Level** | Quality Manual, ITPs, Procedures | Environmental Manual, Aspect Register | OH&S Manual, Hazard Register |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** When ISO 9001 requires "documented information," the same physical document (e.g., a Hot Work Permit) also satisfies ISO 45001's requirement for safe-work procedure control — this is the *integration* benefit of the High-Level Structure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ISO Management System Architecture in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Context of Organization] --> B1[Leadership and Commitment]
    B1 --> C1[Planning Risks and Objectives]
    C1 --> D1[Support Resources Competence Awareness]
    D1 --> E1[Operation Planning and Control]
    E1 --> F1[Performance Evaluation Monitoring and Audit]
    F1 --> G1[Improvement NCR and Corrective Action]
    G1 -. feedback loop .-> C1

    subgraph standards
        S1[ISO 9001 Quality]
        S2[ISO 14001 Environment]
        S3[ISO 45001 Safety]
    end

    S1 --> A1
    S2 --> A1
    S3 --> A1

    E1 --> H1[Hull Fabrication ISO 3834]
    E1 --> H2[Outfitting ISO 9001 Cl 8.5]
    E1 --> H3[Sea Trials ISO 9001 Cl 8.5]
    E1 --> H4[Recycling ISO 30005]

    F1 --> I1[Internal Audit ISO 19011]
    F1 --> I2[Management Review]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — PDCA Cycle Mapped to a Hull Block Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan ITP WPS Risk Register] --> D1[Do Cut Form Weld Blast Paint]
    D1 --> C1[Check NDT Dimensional DFT Survey]
    C1 --> A1[Act NCR Root Cause Lessons Learned]
    A1 --> P1

    P1 --> P2[Block 001]
    P2 --> P3[Block 002]
    P3 --> P4[Block N]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — ISO Standard Family for Shipbuilding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding] --> M1[Management System Standards]
    Q --> M2[Technical Process Standards]
    Q --> M3[Audit and Risk Standards]

    M1 --> M1A[ISO 9001 QMS]
    M1 --> M1B[ISO 14001 EMS]
    M1 --> M1C[ISO 45001 OH and S]

    M2 --> M2A[ISO 3834 Welding]
    M2 --> M2B[ISO 8501 Surface Prep]
    M2 --> M2C[ISO 30000 Risk]
    M2 --> M2D[ISO 30005 Recycling]

    M3 --> M3A[ISO 19011 Auditing]
    M3 --> M3B[ISO 31000 Risk Management]
    M3 --> M3C[ISO 10005 Quality Plans]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Certification Audit Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Yard as Shipyard
    participant CB as Certification Body
    participant IAT as IACS Society

    Yard->>CB: Apply for ISO 9001 Audit
    CB->>Yard: Stage 1 Document Review
    Yard->>CB: Submit Quality Manual
    CB->>Yard: Stage 2 On-site Audit
    Yard->>CB: Address NCRs Raised
    CB->>Yard: Issue ISO 9001 Certificate
    Yard->>IAT: Submit for Class Survey
    IAT->>Yard: Conduct Hull and Machinery Survey
    IAT->>Yard: Issue Class Certificate
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*Define ISO 9001:2015 and explain its significance in the shipbuilding industry.*

**Model Answer:**
ISO 9001:2015 is the international standard for **Quality Management Systems (QMS)** published by the International Organization for Standardization. It specifies requirements for a documented, risk-based, process-oriented management system focused on **customer satisfaction, product conformity, and continual improvement**. In shipbuilding, ISO 9001 is significant because it provides a globally recognized framework to manage the extreme complexity of vessel construction — involving **multi-million components, multi-national supply chains, and decades-long operational life**. It enables shipyards to demonstrate quality assurance to shipowners, classification societies, and regulatory bodies, and is often a **mandatory pre-qualification** for tender participation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**
*State the High-Level Structure (HLS) of ISO management system standards and list its first three clauses.*

**Model Answer:**
The **High-Level Structure (HLS)**, also known as **Annex SL**, is the harmonized 10-clause framework adopted by ISO since 2012 to align all management system standards (ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001). The first three clauses are:

1. **Clause 1 – Scope:** Defines the boundaries and applicability of the standard.
2. **Clause 2 – Normative References:** Lists the referenced documents indispensable for application.
3. **Clause 3 – Terms and Definitions:** Provides the vocabulary used throughout the standard.

The HLS allows shipyards to integrate quality, environmental, and safety systems into a single **Integrated Management System (IMS)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q3. (a)** Explain in detail the **PDCA cycle** and describe how each phase is implemented in a typical shipbuilding project (hull block construction). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **ISO 3834**, classify the welding quality requirements applicable to a commercial cargo vessel, and justify why **ISO 3834-2** is required for critical naval hull structures. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer to Q3(a)

The **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle is the operational backbone of every ISO management system and provides the four-phase framework for process control in shipbuilding.

**PLAN Phase:**
- The shipyard's Quality Control department prepares an **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** for each hull block.
- **Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS)** and **Procedure Qualification Records (PQR)** are developed in line with ISO 15614-1.
- A **risk register** is prepared using **ISO 30000** principles — identifying risks such as distortion, lamellar tearing, and welder unavailability.
- Quality objectives are set: target $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for critical weld parameters.

**DO Phase:**
- The block is cut, formed, and welded using qualified welders.
- Subcontractor work is controlled through **ISO 10005 Quality Plans**.
- Production is executed exactly as planned in the ITP.

**CHECK Phase:**
- Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — ultrasonic, radiographic, magnetic particle — is conducted.
- **Dimensional surveys** verify block geometry against the 3D model.
- Internal audits follow **ISO 19011** guidelines.
- Process capability ($C_{pk}$) is computed from collected data.

**ACT Phase:**
- Any deviation triggers a **Non-Conformance Report (NCR)**.
- Root-cause analysis is performed (e.g., 5-Why or Fishbone).
- Corrective actions are implemented and verified.
- Lessons learned are documented for the next block and the next vessel.

> The PDCA cycle ensures **continual improvement**, which is the central philosophy of ISO 9001:2015.

#### Model Answer to Q3(b)

**ISO 3834** is the family of standards governing **quality requirements for fusion welding of metallic materials**. It has four parts:

- **ISO 3834-2** – Comprehensive Quality Requirements
- **ISO 3834-3** – Standard Quality Requirements
- **ISO 3834-4** – Elementary Quality Requirements
- **ISO 3834-5** – Documentation claims for conformity to 3834-2, -3, or -4

For a **commercial cargo vessel** (e.g., a 50,000 DWT bulk carrier), the general hull fabrication typically follows **ISO 3834-3 (Standard Quality Requirements)** — covering welder qualification, WPS/PQR control, production monitoring, and inspection frequency.

For **critical naval hull structures** — such as the **side shell, deck plating, missile launch foundations, and combat-system support structures** — **ISO 3834-2 (Comprehensive Quality Requirements)** is mandatory because:

1. **Higher weld integrity is required** due to shock loading, blast resistance, and high-cycle fatigue from combat operations.
2. **Full traceability** of every consumable, welder, and procedure must be documented.
3. **100% NDT coverage** of critical welds is required, beyond the sampling of ISO 3834-3.
4. **Independent third-party surveillance** of the welding quality system is mandated.
5. Naval authorities (e.g., the Indian Navy, US Navy) require **ISO 3834-2 certification** as a tender pre-qualification, mirroring classification society expectations for warships.

> Therefore, while a commercial yard operates at **ISO 3834-3**, a warship-building yard must invest in the **higher infrastructure, traceability, and audit regime** of **ISO 3834-2**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q4. (a)** Discuss the role of **ISO 14001:2015** and **ISO 45001:2018** in modern shipyard operations. Explain the concept of an **Integrated Management System (IMS)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A welding process in a shipyard has the following data: $USL = 30 mm$ (weld leg length upper), $LSL = 22 mm$, process mean $\mu = 25 mm$, standard deviation $\sigma = 1.5 mm$. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and interpret whether the process is capable. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer to Q4(a)

**ISO 14001:2015 – Environmental Management System (EMS):**
Shipyards generate significant environmental impacts — welding fumes, paint VOC emissions, ballast-water discharge, scrap steel, hazardous waste from blasting operations. **ISO 14001:2015** requires shipyards to:
- Identify **environmental aspects** (emissions, waste, spills).
- Comply with **legal obligations** (MARPOL, local pollution control norms).
- Set **environmental objectives** (e.g., 20% reduction in VOC emissions).
- Operate an **emergency preparedness and response** system for oil spills and chemical releases.

**ISO 45001:2018 – Occupational Health & Safety Management System:**
Shipyard work is inherently hazardous — working at height, confined spaces, hot work near fuel tanks, heavy lifts. **ISO 45001:2018** requires:
- **Hazard identification** and risk assessment for every task.
- **Worker participation and consultation** at all levels.
- Control of **permitted work** (confined-space entry, hot work, lifting).
- Reporting and investigation of **incidents, near-misses, and fatalities**.
- Reduction of the **Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR)**.

**Integrated Management System (IMS):**
Because **ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and ISO 45001** share the **High-Level Structure (HLS)**, a shipyard can merge them into a single **Integrated Management System (IMS)**. Benefits include:
- One **Quality, Environment, and Safety (QES) Manual** instead of three.
- **Single internal audit** covering all three standards.
- **Common management review** chaired by top management.
- **Reduced documentation burden** — one Hot Work Permit, one Risk Assessment, one Corrective Action form serves all three standards.
- **Lower audit cost** and **higher organizational coherence**.

---

#### Model Answer to Q4(b)

**Given:**
- $USL = 30 \, mm$
- $LSL = 22 \, mm$
- $\mu = 25 \, mm$
- $\sigma = 1.5 \, mm$

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{30 - 22}{6 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{8}{9}
$$

$$
C_p = 0.889
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk,upper}$:**

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{30 - 25}{3 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
C_{pk,upper} = \frac{5}{4.5}
$$

$$
C_{pk,upper} = 1.111
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk,lower}$:**

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{25 - 22}{3 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = \frac{3}{4.5}
$$

$$
C_{pk,lower} = 0.667
$$

**Step 4 — Final $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.111, \; 0.667) = 0.667
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

| Metric | Value | Industry Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| $C_p$ | 0.889 | Process **spread is too wide** — even a perfectly centered process would fail |
| $C_{pk}$ | 0.667 | Process is **not capable** ($C_{pk} \lt 1.00$) |

**Conclusion:**
The welding process is **not capable** of meeting the specification. The mean (25 mm) is below the target midpoint of 26 mm, and the variability ($\sigma = 1.5 \, mm$) is too high. Recommended actions under ISO 9001 Cl. 10.2:
- **Reduce process variability** by tighter control of welding current, voltage, and travel speed.
- **Re-center the process** by adjusting parameter settings.
- After improvement, **re-validate** $C_{pk}$ to confirm it reaches $\geq 1.33$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Marks Lost:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ measures only **process spread**; $C_{pk}$ measures **spread AND centering**. Many students compute $C_p$ and stop. KTU requires **both** values plus the minimum-based $C_{pk}$ selection.
> 2. **Writing the units wrong:** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are **dimensionless numbers**, not mm. Never write "$C_p = 0.889 \, mm$".
> 3. **Skipping the interpretation:** A numerical answer without a verdict (capable / not capable) costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing ISO 3834-2 and ISO 9001:** ISO 3834-2 is a **welding quality** standard, *not* a general QMS. Students often blur the two and lose marks on the welding-specific question.
> 5. **Forgetting the HLS concept:** When asked "how ISO 9001 and ISO 14001 integrate," many students write separate paragraphs instead of recognizing the **Annex SL common framework**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ISO = International Organization for Standardization**, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland — *not* an acronym for "International Standards Organization."
- **ISO 9001:2015** is the **7th edition** of the quality management standard and the most widely used ISO standard globally.
- All modern ISO management standards (post-2012) use the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** with **10 clauses**, enabling **Integrated Management Systems (IMS)**.
- **PDCA** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) is the operational engine of every ISO management system.
- The three pillars of a shipyard IMS are **ISO 9001 (Quality), ISO 14001 (Environment), ISO 45001 (Safety)**.
- **ISO 3834-2** is mandatory for **warships and critical hull structures**; **ISO 3834-3** suffices for **commercial vessel hulls**.
- **ISO 19011** provides audit guidelines used during internal, second-party, and third-party audits.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the industry-accepted threshold for a **capable** shipbuilding process.
- ISO certification is often a **tender pre-qualification** — shipyards without ISO 9001 may be barred from major contracts.
- ISO standards **complement** classification society rules (IACS) and statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL); they **do not replace** them.
- A single **NCR** triggers a documented corrective action cycle under ISO 9001 Clause 10.2.
- The trend in shipbuilding is toward **digital twins and Industry 4.0**, where ISO standards provide the **data structure and audit trail** for digital quality management.
- For Module 4 of PENST862, focus on the **overview and integration logic** rather than clause-by-clause memorization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Qualification Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Qualification Standards ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Qualification Standards** in shipbuilding are the formally codified sets of technical, procedural, and personnel competency requirements established by regulatory bodies, classification societies, and international standardization organizations that a shipyard, its workforce, its processes, and its products **must demonstrably satisfy** before being authorized to design, fabricate, weld, inspect, or commission marine vessels and offshore structures. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, qualification standards sit at the intersection of **Quality Assurance (QA)** and **Regulatory Compliance**, forming the gateway that proves "fitness for purpose" in maritime construction. The qualification hierarchy typically cascades across four levels: | Level | Entity Being Qualified | Governing Document Family | |---|---|---| | **Level 1** | Shipyard / Manufacturing Facility | ISO 9001, ISO 45001, IACS QSCS | | **Level 2** | Welding Procedure & Materials | EN ISO 15614-1, AWS D1.1, ASME IX | | **Level 3** | Welders & Operators | EN ISO 9606-1, ASME IX, AWS D1.1 | | **Level 4** | Inspection & NDT Personnel | ISO 9712, ASNT SNT-TC-1A, PCN | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Driving License" System of Shipbuilding** > > Imagine you cannot legally drive a car without a driving license. The license proves you have: > - Passed a written test (theory / procedure qualification). > - Passed a road test (practical skill / welder performance test). > - Driven an approved vehicle type (material/process qualification). > - The license is renewed periodically (re-qualification / recertification). > > Now scale this to shipbuilding: > - The **car owner** is the **Shipyard** → needs ISO 9001 certification. > - The **type of car** is the **Welding Procedure** → needs WPS/PQR qualification. > - The **driver** is the **Welder** → needs welder qualification certificate. > - The **traffic police** is the **NDT Inspector** → needs ISO 9712 / ASNT certification. > > Without qualification at every level, insurance (classification) and regulatory approval (Flag State, SOLAS) is denied — the ship simply cannot sail. ### 1.3 Why Qualification Standards Matter - **Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)**: Mandated by IMO under Chapter II-1 and II-2. - **Hull & Machinery Insurance**: Classification societies (IACS members) require documented qualification before underwriting. - **Legal Liability**: Unqualified work voids contractual warranties and statutory protections. - **Inter-port Acceptance**: A ship qualified by one IACS society is globally accepted under the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**. - **Defense Contracts**: Naval shipbuilding requires additional **military qualification standards** (e.g., NAVSEA, MIL-STD, Defence Standard 02-781). ### 1.4 Classification Societies — The Custodians of Qualification The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**, founded in 1968, is the global body of classification societies. The current top-tier members include: - **LR** – Lloyd's Register (UK, 1760) - **DNV** – DNV (Norway/Germany, merged 2024) - **ABS** – American Bureau of Shipping (USA, 1862) - **BV** – Bureau Veritas (France, 1828) - **CCS** – China Classification Society - **KR** – Korean Register - **NK** – Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (Japan) - **RINA** – Registro Italiano Navale - **IRS** – Indian Register of Shipping - **PRS** – Polski Rejestr Statków Each society publishes its own **Rules and Regulations**, but under the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR)**, they maintain harmonized qualification criteria. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Qualification Pyramid (Tiered Framework) Qualification is not a single event — it is a **layered verification architecture**. KTU examiners frequently frame questions around this hierarchy. **Tier 1 — System Qualification (Quality Management)** - Standard: **ISO 9001:2015** (Quality Management Systems). - IACS equivalent: **QSCS — Quality System Certification Scheme**. - Audited annually by accredited certification bodies. - Establishes the yard's documented quality manual, procedures, work instructions, and records. **Tier 2 — Process Qualification (Welding Procedure Qualification Record — PQR)** - Standards: **EN ISO 15614-1** (steels), **EN ISO 15614-2** (Aluminium), **ASME Section IX**, **AWS D1.1**. - A **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** is written first. - A **PQR (Procedure Qualification Record)** is generated by testing a sample welded per the WPS. - Mechanical tests on the test coupon: tensile, bend, impact (Charpy V-notch), hardness, macro-examination. - Validity: typically **unlimited** for the range qualified, provided the base material group and process remain unchanged. **Tier 3 — Personnel Qualification (Welder / Operator)** - Standards: **EN ISO 9606-1** (steels), **EN ISO 9606-2** (Aluminium), **ASME Section IX**, **AWS D1.1**. - Welder performs a **test coupon** within a defined **range of qualification**. - Validity: **2 years** (EN ISO 9606-1) or **6 months** with continuity, max **3 years** (ASME IX). - Qualification is tied to: material group, thickness range, welding process, position, type of joint. **Tier 4 — Inspection Personnel Qualification (NDT)** - Standards: **ISO 9712:2021** (Non-destructive testing — Qualification and certification of NDT personnel), **ASNT SNT-TC-1A**, **PCN** (Personnel Certification in Non-Destructive Testing, UK). - Three levels: **Level 1** (operate), **Level 2** (set up, execute, interpret, supervise), **Level 3** (establish procedures, certify personnel). - Validity: **5 years** for Level 1 & 2 (with annual eye-test and work-hour continuity), **10 years** for Level 3. ### 2.2 Welding Procedure Qualification — Deep Mechanics The PQR is the **backbone** of welding qualification. The qualification range is governed by the principle of **"essential variables"** — if any essential variable is changed outside the qualified range, a new PQR is required. **Essential Variables (per EN ISO 15614-1):** | Variable | Permitted Range Without Requalification | |---|---| | Base material group | Same group (e.g., Group 1.1 covers mild steels up to 460 MPa) | | Thickness $t$ | $0.7t_{\text{test}} \leq t \leq 1.1t_{\text{test}}$ (for butt welds) | | Welding process | Same (e.g., 135 MAG = 136 MAG permitted, but not 111 SMAW) | | Joint type | Butt ↔ fillet covered within limits | | Position | PA → all positions; PC → all except PG; PF → PF only | | Heat input $Q$ | $\pm 25\%$ of qualified value | | Preheat temperature $T_p$ | Equal to or above qualified value | | PWHT | Same or more extensive | **Heat Input (Q) Formula — Essential to Welding Qualification:** $$Q = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \text{kJ/mm} \right]$$ Where: - $V$ = Arc voltage (Volts) - $I$ = Welding current (Amperes) - $v$ = Travel speed (mm/min) - $60/1000$ = Unit conversion factor - $k$ = Thermal efficiency coefficient (**0.8** for MAG/GMAW, **0.7** for SMAW/arc, **1.0** for SAW submerged arc) ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Units / Range | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Heat Input | $Q = \dfrac{kVI \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Limits qualified procedure range | | 2 | Thickness Qualification | $0.7\,t_{\text{test}} \leq t \leq 1.1\,t_{\text{test}}$ | mm | PQR thickness envelope | | 3 | Preheat (Carbon Equivalent) | $CE_{\text{IIW}} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | weight % | Predicts cold-cracking risk | | 4 | Cooling Time t8/5 | Empirical from $Q$, $t$, $T_p$ | seconds | Determines HAZ microstructure | | 5 | Charpy Impact Temp. | Tested at $-40^\circ$C for arctic class | °C | Toughness qualification | | 6 | Tensile Qualification | $R_m^{\text{weld}} \geq R_m^{\text{base (min)}}$ | MPa | Procedure strength envelope | | 7 | Welder Validity (EN) | $\Delta t = 24$ months | months | Re-qualification trigger | | 8 | NDT Validity (ISO 9712) | $\Delta t_{\text{L1/L2}} = 60$ months | months | Recertification cycle | | 9 | Hardness Limit (IACS UR W11) | $\text{HV10}_{\max} = 350$ for strength steels | HV | Cold-crack avoidance | | 10 | Yard ISO 9001 Audit Cycle | $\Delta t = 12$ months | months | QMS surveillance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **Heat Input formula $Q = kVI \cdot 60 / (v \cdot 1000)$** appears in nearly every Part B question related to welding qualification. Memorize the constants $k$ for each process. ### 2.4 The IACS Unified Requirement (UR) Framework The **UR system** consolidates the qualification rules across all member societies: - **UR W11** — Hull Steel Materials (incl. cold-forming, toughness). - **UR W17** — Welding Procedure Qualification Tests. - **UR W18** — Welder Qualification. - **UR W28** — Weld Joint Details (acceptance criteria). - **UR W31** — Non-Destructive Testing of Welded Joints. - **UR Z17** — Quality System Certification (QSCS). ### 2.5 Why Qualification Standards Are Engineering-Critical - **Risk Mitigation**: Each tier verifies a specific failure mode — system (organizational), process (metallurgical), personnel (human error), inspection (defect detection). - **Traceability**: Every qualified entity receives a unique certificate number, linked to the parent WPS/PQR/welder — creating an audit trail. - **Re-Qualification Triggers**: Material change, process change, equipment change, personnel lapsed, regulatory revision. - **Cross-Border Recognition**: A weld qualified to EN ISO 15614-1 by an IACS society is accepted globally without re-testing. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Welder Qualification Thickness Range (EN ISO 9606-1) The logic of thickness qualification stems from the principle that **heat flow geometry** scales with thickness. > [!NOTE] > **Background Theory:** > A welder who successfully welds a **test plate of thickness $t_{\text{test}}$** is deemed competent over a thickness range that brackets $t_{\text{test}}$, because the heat dissipation, restraint, and joint preparation that govern weld quality are similar across the bracket. **Step 1:** Identify the test plate thickness from the welder qualification test certificate. - For example: $t_{\text{test}} = 12 \text{ mm}$. **Step 2:** Apply the qualification range for butt welds (per EN ISO 9606-1, Table 3): $$\text{Butt Qualification Range:} \quad 0.7\,t_{\text{test}} \leq t \leq 1.1\,t_{\text{test}}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $t_{\text{test}} = 12$ mm: $$\text{Lower Bound} = 0.7 \times 12 = 8.4 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{Upper Bound} = 1.1 \times 12 = 13.2 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4:** For **fillet welds**, the rule extends the qualification range: - A welder qualified on butt is qualified on fillet of the **same or greater** throat thickness. - A welder qualified on fillet is qualified on fillet only within the same thickness bracket (separate rules apply). **Step 5:** State the operational conclusion in sentence form: > *"A welder qualified on a 12 mm test plate is authorized to weld butt joints in the thickness range **8.4 mm to 13.2 mm**, provided all other essential variables (process, position, material group) are unchanged."* ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Input Calculation (Process Boundary Verification) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A PQR is qualified at a heat input of 1.6 kJ/mm. The production weld deviates to 1.45 kJ/mm. Verify whether requalification is required. **Step 1:** State the heat input formula: $$Q = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the essential variable rule: - Permitted variation without requalification: $\pm 25\%$ of qualified value. $$\Delta Q_{\text{permitted}} = 0.25 \times 1.6 = 0.40 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the qualified envelope: $$Q_{\text{lower}} = 1.6 - 0.40 = 1.20 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ $$Q_{\text{upper}} = 1.6 + 0.40 = 2.00 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Step 4:** Compare actual production heat input (1.45 kJ/mm) against the envelope: $$1.20 \leq 1.45 \leq 2.00 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5:** Conclusion: > *"Since the production heat input of 1.45 kJ/mm lies within the permitted envelope of 1.20 – 2.00 kJ/mm, **no requalification is required**. The procedure remains valid."* ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Carbon Equivalent & Preheat Determination **Step 1:** Compute the **IIW Carbon Equivalent** for a typical shipbuilding steel: $$\text{Composition (wt\%):} \quad C=0.16,\; Mn=1.30,\; Cr=0.05,\; Mo=0.02,\; V=0.01,\; Ni=0.10,\; Cu=0.15$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the IIW formula: $$CE_{\text{IIW}} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$ $$CE_{\text{IIW}} = 0.16 + \frac{1.30}{6} + \frac{0.05+0.02+0.01}{5} + \frac{0.10+0.15}{15}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate each fraction: $$\frac{1.30}{6} = 0.2167$$ $$\frac{0.08}{5} = 0.0160$$ $$\frac{0.25}{15} = 0.0167$$ **Step 4:** Sum the components: $$CE_{\text{IIW}} = 0.16 + 0.2167 + 0.0160 + 0.0167$$ $$CE_{\text{IIW}} = 0.4094 \; \text{wt\%}$$ **Step 5:** Interpret against the IACS UR W11 threshold: > *"With $CE_{\text{IIW}} = 0.41\%$, the steel falls into the **Class B** category of IACS UR W11 (threshold 0.36–0.45%). The mandatory **preheat temperature** is **75 °C** minimum, to avoid hydrogen-induced cold cracking in the heat-affected zone."* ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Standards Table | Attribute | EN ISO Standard | ASME Standard | AWS Standard | IACS UR | |---|---|---|---|---| | PQR Basis | EN ISO 15614-1 | ASME Section IX | AWS D1.1 | UR W17 | | Welder Qualification | EN ISO 9606-1 | ASME Section IX | AWS D1.1 | UR W18 | | NDT Personnel | EN ISO 9712 | (Refer to ASNT) | ASNT SNT-TC-1A | UR W31 | | Material Toughness | EN 10025 series | ASME SA-36/SA-516 | AWS D1.1 Table 3.2 | UR W11 | | Hardness Limit | 350 HV10 | 350 HV10 | 350 HV10 | UR W11 | | Welder Validity | 24 months | 6 months + 24 mo continuity | 6 months continuity | 24 months | | Preheat Guidance | EN 1011-2 | ASME IX QW-406 | AWS D1.1 Table 4.2 | UR W11 | ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Flow — Requalification Trigger Check A yard must initiate requalification when **any one** of the following is true: $$ \text{Requalification Required} = \bigcup_{i} \mathbb{1}\big[ V_i \notin \text{Range}_{i,\text{qualified}} \big] $$ Where $V_i$ represents each essential variable $i$ (material, thickness, process, position, heat input, preheat, PWHT). This is a logical OR — violation of any single essential variable invalidates the entire qualification. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Qualification Pyramid (Hierarchical Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Regulatory Framework
IMO SOLAS, Flag State] --> B[Classification Society Rules
IACS Unified Requirements] B --> C[Quality Management System
ISO 9001 / IACS QSCS] C --> D[Shipyard Qualification
Annual Audit Cycle 12 mo] D --> E[Welding Procedure Qualification
WPS plus PQR via EN ISO 15614-1] E --> F[Welder Performance Qualification
EN ISO 9606-1 Test Coupon] F --> G[Production Welding
Continuous Traceability] G --> H[NDT Inspection
ISO 9712 Level 1 2 3] H --> I[Acceptance Review
Class Surveyor Sign Off] I --> J[Ship Delivery
Class Certificate Issued] ``` ### 4.2 Welding Procedure Qualification Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1 Draft WPS] --> S2[Step 2 Select Test Assembly Geometry] S2 --> S3[Step 3 Weld Test Coupon per WPS] S3 --> S4[Step 4 NDT Examination VT RT UT] S4 --> S5{Step 5 All Defects Below Acceptance Limit} S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 6 Mechanical Testing Tensile Bend Impact Hardness Macro] S6 --> S7{Step 7 All Tests Meet Criteria} S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 8 Issue PQR Document] S8 --> S9[Step 9 WPS Approved by Classification Society] S5 -- No --> S10[Step 10 Revise WPS and Retest] S7 -- No --> S10 S9 --> S11[Step 11 Production Authorization Valid] ``` ### 4.3 NDT Personnel Certification Structure (ISO 9712) ```mermaid flowchart TD L0[Candidate NDT Operator] --> L1A[Training Minimum 40 hours] L1A --> L1B[Level 1 Examination Theory plus Practical] L1B --> L1C[Level 1 Certificate Validity 5 Years] L1C --> L2A[Experience Minimum 12 months at L1 or 2100 hours] L2A --> L2B[Level 2 Examination Theory plus Practical] L2B --> L2C[Level 2 Certificate Validity 5 Years] L2C --> L3A[Experience Minimum 36 months at L2] L3A --> L3B[Level 3 Examination plus Procedure Approval] L3B --> L3C[Level 3 Certificate Validity 10 Years] L3C --> L3D[Authority to Certify Other Candidates and Author NDT Procedures] ``` ### 4.4 Essential Variables Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Qualification Valid] --> C{Check Essential Variables} C --> V1{Material Group Same} V1 -- No --> R1[Requalify PQR] V1 -- Yes --> V2{Thickness within 0.7 to 1.1 Range} V2 -- No --> R2[Requalify PQR] V2 -- Yes --> V3{Process Same or Equivalent} V3 -- No --> R3[New PQR Required] V3 -- Yes --> V4{Heat Input within plus minus 25 percent} V4 -- No --> R4[Revise PQR or New PQR] V4 -- Yes --> V5{Position Permitted} V5 -- No --> R5[Welder Requalification] V5 -- Yes --> OK[Qualification Valid for Production] ``` ### 4.5 Interrelationship of Qualification Documents ```mermaid flowchart TB WPQ[Welder Qualification Certificate
EN ISO 9606-1
Valid 24 Months] --> PROD[Production Weld Authorization] WPS[Welding Procedure Specification
WPS Document] --> PQR[Procedure Qualification Record
PQR Test Results] PQR --> WPS WPS --> PROD QMS[Quality Manual ISO 9001] --> WPS QMS --> WPQ CLASS[Classification Society Approval
LR DNV ABS BV] --> PQR CLASS --> WPQ NDT[NDT Personnel Certificate
ISO 9712] --> INSP[Inspection Authorization] INSP --> ACC[Final Acceptance Survey] PROD --> ACC ACC --> DEL[Class Certificate Issued] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define "Qualification Standards" in the context of shipbuilding quality assurance. List the four hierarchical levels at which qualification is enforced.* **Model Answer:** Qualification Standards in shipbuilding are codified technical and competency requirements established by regulatory bodies, classification societies, and international standards organizations that a shipyard, its workforce, its processes, and its products must demonstrably satisfy before being authorized to design, fabricate, weld, inspect, or commission marine vessels. The four hierarchical levels are: 1. **System Level** — Shipyard / facility qualification (ISO 9001, IACS QSCS). 2. **Process Level** — Welding procedure qualification (EN ISO 15614-1, ASME IX, AWS D1.1). 3. **Personnel Level** — Welder / operator qualification (EN ISO 9606-1). 4. **Inspection Level** — NDT personnel qualification (ISO 9712, ASNT SNT-TC-1A). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the validity period of a welder qualification certificate as per EN ISO 9606-1 and the validity period of an ISO 9712 Level 2 NDT certificate.* **Model Answer:** - **Welder Qualification Certificate (EN ISO 9606-1):** Valid for **24 months** from the date of issue, provided continuity of work is maintained (welding activity in the qualified process at least every 6 months). - **ISO 9712 Level 2 NDT Certificate:** Valid for **5 years** (60 months), subject to annual eye-test confirmation and documented work-hour continuity in the method. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Welding Procedure Qualification** process as per EN ISO 15614-1. Discuss the roles of the WPS and the PQR, list the essential mechanical tests required, and outline the heat input envelope concept. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A welder has been qualified on a **20 mm test plate** for butt joints in steel Grade A using process **135 (MAG)** in position **PA**. Determine the **thickness range, material group coverage, process coverage, and position coverage** that this welder is authorized to weld in production. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Welding Procedure Qualification — EN ISO 15614-1:** The process follows a structured sequence: 1. **Drafting the WPS (Welding Procedure Specification):** A written document specifying all relevant welding parameters — joint type, base material, process, filler metal, shielding gas, preheat, interpass, heat input limits, PWHT, and position. 2. **Preparation of Test Assembly:** A test coupon is prepared per the WPS, with full traceability of materials, consumables, and equipment. 3. **Welding of Test Coupon:** Executed under controlled conditions; parameters logged. 4. **Non-Destructive Testing:** Visual (VT), radiographic (RT), or ultrasonic (UT) examination to detect surface and sub-surface defects. Acceptance per EN ISO 5817. 5. **Mechanical Testing:** - **Transverse Tensile Test** — verifies weld strength $\geq$ base metal minimum. - **Transverse Bend Test** — verifies ductility (mandrel bend to 180° or 120°). - **Charpy V-Notch Impact Test** — verifies toughness at specified temperature (e.g., $-40°C$ for arctic). - **Hardness Testing (HV10)** — verifies HAZ hardness $\leq 350$ HV10 for strength steels (IACS UR W11). - **Macro-examination** — verifies fusion, penetration, and absence of defects. 6. **Documentation into PQR:** All test results compiled into the **Procedure Qualification Record**, which is then cross-referenced back into the WPS, locking in the qualified parameter envelope. 7. **Heat Input Envelope Concept:** The qualified $Q$ value (kJ/mm) defines a permitted range of $\pm 25\%$. Production welds within this envelope do not require requalification. **(b) Welder Authorization Calculation:** **Step 1 — Thickness Range (Butt Joint):** $$t_{\text{low}} = 0.7 \times 20 = 14 \text{ mm}$$ $$t_{\text{high}} = 1.1 \times 20 = 22 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Material Group Coverage:** - Grade A steel falls under **ISO/TR 15608 Material Group 1.1** (mild steels with specified minimum yield strength $\leq 460$ MPa). - The welder is qualified for **all steels in Group 1.1** (including Grades B, D, AH, DH, EH up to grade limit). **Step 3 — Process Coverage:** - Qualified on **Process 135 (MAG with solid wire)**. - Authorised to weld using **Process 135 and 136 (MAG with metal-cored wire)** in production. - **Not** authorized for Process 111 (SMAW) or 141 (TIG) without separate qualification. **Step 4 — Position Coverage (PA → All Positions):** - Qualified in **PA (Flat)** is the most permissive qualification. - Authorized for **PA, PC, PF, PE, PG** (i.e., all positions — flat, horizontal, vertical-up, vertical-down, overhead). **Final Summary Table:** | Attribute | Qualified Value | Authorized Range | |---|---|---| | Thickness | 20 mm | 14 mm to 22 mm | | Material Group | 1.1 | All of Group 1.1 | | Process | 135 | 135, 136 | | Position | PA | PA, PC, PF, PE, PG | --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Describe the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**. Explain its scope, audit cycle, and significance in ensuring shipyard competence. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A welding procedure is qualified at a heat input of **1.8 kJ/mm** using process **121 (SAW)**. During production, the welder operates at $V = 32$ V, $I = 480$ A, and $v = 720$ mm/min. The thermal efficiency for SAW is $k = 1.0$. Verify whether the production heat input lies within the qualified envelope. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) IACS QSCS — Quality System Certification Scheme:** - **Origin:** Established by IACS in 1991 (UR Z17) to harmonize quality system requirements across all member classification societies. - **Scope:** Applies to shipyards, engine manufacturers, and material manufacturers supplying marine equipment. - **Standard:** Based on **ISO 9001:2015** with marine-sector-specific add-ons (welding, NDT, traceability). - **Audit Cycle:** Initial certification audit → **3-year certification validity** → annual surveillance audits → re-certification audit at end of cycle. - **Significance:** - Eliminates redundant audits when a yard supplies multiple class societies. - Creates a globally recognized baseline of quality competence. - Reduces commercial risk for shipowners and class surveyors. - Linked directly to **welding**, **NDT**, and **material qualification** sub-systems. - **Issuing Bodies:** Only IACS member societies can issue QSCS certificates. **(b) Heat Input Verification — Production vs Qualified Envelope:** **Step 1 — Calculate Production Heat Input:** $$Q_{\text{prod}} = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given values** ($k=1.0$, $V=32$, $I=480$, $v=720$): $$Q_{\text{prod}} = \frac{1.0 \times 32 \times 480 \times 60}{720 \times 1000}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:** $$32 \times 480 = 15{,}360$$ $$15{,}360 \times 60 = 921{,}600$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:** $$720 \times 1000 = 720{,}000$$ **Step 5 — Compute the ratio:** $$Q_{\text{prod}} = \frac{921{,}600}{720{,}000} = 1.28 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Step 6 — Define the Qualified Envelope (Q ± 25%):** $$\Delta Q = 0.25 \times 1.8 = 0.45 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ $$Q_{\text{low}} = 1.8 - 0.45 = 1.35 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ $$Q_{\text{high}} = 1.8 + 0.45 = 2.25 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Step 7 — Compare:** $$Q_{\text{prod}} = 1.28 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \text{vs} \quad [1.35,\; 2.25] \text{ kJ/mm}$$ Since $1.28 < 1.35$, the production heat input **falls below the qualified envelope**. **Step 8 — Conclusion:** > *"The production heat input of 1.28 kJ/mm violates the lower bound (1.35 kJ/mm) of the qualified envelope. The welding procedure **must be revised and requalified**, or the production parameters must be adjusted upward (e.g., lower travel speed or lower current) to bring the heat input within 1.35–2.25 kJ/mm."* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Pitfall 1 (Heat Input Constants):** Students often write $k = 0.6$ for SAW. The correct value is **$k = 1.0$** for submerged arc welding (highest thermal efficiency of all arc processes). Misusing $k$ will cost 1–2 marks. > - **Pitfall 2 (Welder Qualification Position Logic):** Students often assume the test position determines only that position. In fact, **PA qualifies all positions, but PF qualifies only PF and PG-related near-flat positions** (per EN ISO 96006-1 Table 4). Reverse-direction qualification does not apply to position. > - **Pitfall 3 (Thickness Range Direction):** Students sometimes compute $1.1t$ to $0.7t$ (incorrect direction). The rule is always **lower bound = 0.7**, **upper bound = 1.1**. > - **Pitfall 4 (NDT Level Scope):** Many students confuse Level 1 with Level 3 authority. Level 1 cannot interpret results; **only Level 2 and above** can interpret and certify. > - **Pitfall 5 (QSCS vs ISO 9001):** QSCS is **not** identical to ISO 9001 — it is ISO 9001 plus IACS marine add-ons. Examiners reward students who clarify this distinction. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Qualification Standards** in shipbuilding exist at **four hierarchical levels**: System (ISO 9001/QSCS), Process (WPS/PQR), Personnel (Welder), and Inspection (NDT). - **EN ISO 15614-1** governs welding procedure qualification for steels; the **PQR + WPS** pair forms the backbone of procedure authorization. - **Heat Input formula:** $Q = kVI \cdot 60 / (v \cdot 1000)$, with $k = 1.0$ (SAW), $0.8$ (MAG/GMAW), $0.7$ (SMAW). - **Heat Input Permitted Variation:** $\pm 25\%$ of the qualified value without requalification. - **Welder Thickness Range:** $0.7\,t_{\text{test}} \leq t \leq 1.1\,t_{\text{test}}$ for butt welds (EN ISO 9606-1). - **Welder Validity:** 24 months (EN ISO 9606-1), requiring 6-monthly continuity proof. - **NDT Personnel Validity:** 60 months for Level 1/2, 120 months for Level 3 (ISO 9712:2021). - **IACS UR W11** sets the **350 HV10 hardness cap** for strength-steel welds to mitigate hydrogen cracking. - **Carbon Equivalent (IIW):** $CE = C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15$. - **Position Qualification:** PA (flat) qualifies all positions; PC qualifies all except PG; PF qualifies only PF. - **IACS QSCS** is built on ISO 9001 with marine-sector overlays; cycle is **3 years certification + annual surveillance**. - **Heat Input violation = requalification trigger.** Always verify the envelope before production release. - **IACS member societies (LR, DNV, ABS, BV, CCS, KR, NK, RINA, IRS, PRS)** operate under the **Unified Requirements (UR)** for harmonized qualification acceptance. - **Hardness Test (HV10)**, **Charpy Impact**, **Bend Test**, **Tensile Test**, and **Macro-examination** are the five mandatory mechanical tests of a PQR. - **Naval/defence contracts** may additionally require **MIL-STD**, **NAVSEA**, or **Defence Standard 02-781** qualification — these are **stricter overlays** to commercial class rules.

topics.title: Training and Certification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Training and Certification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training and Certification** in shipbuilding refers to the formalized, competency-based process of imparting specialized technical knowledge, practical skills, and safety awareness to personnel (welders, NDT inspectors, QC engineers, supervisors) followed by a structured evaluation against national/international standards to ensure they possess the minimum required competency to perform quality-critical tasks in ship construction and repair.

In the context of Quality Assurance (QA) under **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** unified requirements and **ISO 9001:2015** quality management systems, training and certification forms the human competency pillar of the QA framework. The shipyard is obligated to ensure that personnel performing activities affecting product quality are competent on the basis of appropriate education, training, skills, and experience (Clause 7.2 of ISO 9001:2015).

### Key Entities in the Certification Chain

- **The Training Body** — Shipyard in-house training centers, maritime academies (e.g., MERI Mumbai, IMU Visakhapatnam), specialized institutes (IIW, TWI).
- **The Certification Body** — Independent organizations (Classification Societies like Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS; or bodies like ASNT, BINDT) that issue certificates of competence.
- **The Qualified Person** — The individual who has demonstrated the required skill and holds a valid certificate.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Driving License" Analogy for Shipbuilding Skills**
>
> Imagine you need to transport 1,000 passengers safely across the ocean in a ship weighing 50,000 tonnes. Would you let just "anyone" drive the vessel, weld the hull, or inspect the engines? **Absolutely not.** A driving license guarantees that a person has passed a minimum standard test before being allowed to operate a vehicle on public roads.
>
> Similarly, **Training and Certification in shipbuilding is the "driving license" for welders, NDT inspectors, and QC engineers**.
