It legally and technically proves that the person performing the work is competent. Just as a doctor's license is renewed and updated, shipbuilding certifications (e.g., welder qualifications) have **validity periods** (typically 2–3 years) and require **re-certification** to ensure the person has not become rusty. Without this system, a single incompetent welder could cause a crack in a critical hull joint, leading to the **MV Prestige (2002)** or **MOL Comfort (2013)** style catastrophic failures.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Certification Frameworks:**
> - **Welder Qualification:** EN ISO 9606-1 (Steels), ISO 9606-2 (Aluminium), ASME Section IX, AWS D1.1/D1.6.
> - **NDT Personnel:** ISO 9712 (Non-Destructive Testing – Qualification and Certification), ASNT SNT-TC-1A, EN 4179 (NDT for aerospace, often referenced).
> - **Quality Management System (QMS):** ISO 9001:2015 (Clause 7.2 – Competence).
> - **Welding Quality:** ISO 3834 (Quality requirements for fusion welding of metallic materials), IACS UR W17 (Welders' qualification).
> - **Safety & Environment:** STCW (Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping) for seafarers; OSHA / IMO for shore-based personnel.

The **primary metric** for human competency is the **Certification Validity Period** (typically **2 years** for welders, **5 years** for NDT Level 2/3 with eye test every 1 year, and **3 years** for QMS lead auditors).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Training and Certification

The competency hierarchy in shipbuilding is structured into **3-Tier Layered Model** for any technical function (e.g., welding, NDT):

1. **Tier 1 — Foundation (Education):** Theoretical knowledge imparted through lectures, textbooks, and e-learning (e.g., metallurgy of steels, principles of ultrasonic waves).
2. **Tier 2 — Skill (Training):** Hands-on practical training under the supervision of certified instructors using approved procedures (e.g., actual welding of test coupons, scanning calibration blocks with UT).
3. **Tier 3 — Competence (Certification):** Independent, third-party or second-party assessment where the candidate demonstrates the skill without assistance, resulting in a certificate of approval.

### The Logic Flow of Certification

> **Why** is it structured this way?
> - **Skill is perishable** — welders who haven't welded in 6 months show defect rates 2–3× higher.
> - **Standards evolve** — new steel grades (e.g., EH36, DH36 for high-strength hull plates) require updated procedures.
> - **Liability protection** — under product liability law, the shipyard must prove due diligence in personnel qualification.
> - **Audit compliance** — Class surveyors and IACS auditors will reject a ship if uncertified personnel performed the work.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Personnel Competence

| Pillar | Description | Example in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge** | Theoretical understanding of the process and material. | A welder understanding HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) transformation in ASTM A131 Grade A steel. |
| **Skill** | Ability to execute the process manually. | A welder producing a defect-free butt joint in 12 mm plate. |
| **Experience** | Accumulation of practical exposure over time. | A QC engineer who has witnessed 20+ production welds and defect analyses. |
| **Attitude** | Safety and quality consciousness. | Stopping work when a WPS deviation is noticed. |

## 2.3 The Role of ISO 9606-1 in Welder Qualification (Deep Dive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 9606-1:2012 / EN ISO 9606-1** is the most widely accepted welder qualification standard in European and Indian shipyards. It supersedes the older EN 287-1 standard. A welder is qualified for a specific combination called the **"Qualification Range"** or **"Essential Variables."**

The certification is valid for the following ranges (the exam qualifies the welder, but the actual production work must be done within the qualification limits):

| Essential Variable | Range Permitted |
|---|---|
| **Material Group** | Group 1 (Steels with Rm ≤ 460 MPa, e.g., mild steel, AH36) |
| **Plate Thickness (t)** | Qualified for $0.5t \le t \le 2t$ (e.g., test on 10 mm → qualified for 5–20 mm) |
| **Welding Process** | 135 (MAG/MIG with solid wire), 111 (Manual Metal Arc / SMAW) |
| **Joint Type** | Butt (BW) or Fillet (FW) |
| **Welding Position** | PA, PB, PC, PF, PE, PG, H-L045 etc. per ISO 6947 |
| **Filler Metal Type** | Solid wire, flux-cored, etc. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Standard / Parameter | Symbol | Definition / Equation | Validity / Range | Governing Body |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Welder Test Plate Thickness | $t_{test}$ | Thickness of the qualification test coupon | 3 mm to 40 mm typical | ISO 9606-1 |
| Production Thickness Range | $t_{prod}$ | $0.5 \cdot t_{test} \le t_{prod} \le 2 \cdot t_{test}$ | Mid-range of standard | ISO 9606-1 |
| NDT Validity (Level 2) | $T_{NDT}$ | $T_{NDT} = 5$ years + annual eye test | Eye test every 12 months | ISO 9712 |
| Welder Certificate Validity | $T_{weld}$ | $T_{weld} = 2$ years (can extend to 3 with continuity proof) | Continuity of work evidence | IACS UR W17 |
| QMS Auditor Validity | $T_{audit}$ | $T_{audit} = 3$ years + 20 audit days/yr | Re-certification mandatory | ISO 17021 / 9001 |
| Hardness Test for Welder Re-qualification | $HV_{max}$ | $HV_{max} \le 380$ for most shipbuilding steels | For re-qualification coupon | ISO 9606-1 |
| Bending Test Mandrel Diameter | $D$ | $D = 4t$ (for transverse root/face bend in steels) | Ductility proof | ISO 9606-1 |
| Visual Acuity (NDT Personnel) | $V$ | Jaeger No. 1 or near vision $\ge$ N4.5 | Annual test | ISO 9712 |

> **Real-World Utility:** This competency framework is audited by classification society surveyors during newbuild (Keel-to-Delivery) and during the ship's operational life (5-yearly and annual surveys). A non-conformity in personnel qualification is a **Major Non-Conformity (NCR)** that halts production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Welder Qualification Range Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A welder is qualified by passing a butt-joint test on a **12 mm thick** plate using the **MAG (135) process** in the **PC (horizontal-vertical) position**. Determine the range of production plate thicknesses for which the welder is now approved, assuming the test is on mild steel (Group 1 per ISO/TR 15608).

**Step 1 — Recall the governing equation from ISO 9606-1, Clause 6.3:**

$$
t_{qualified} = f(t_{test}, \text{joint type, material group})
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thickness range rule for butt welds in steels (Group 1):**

$$
0.5 \cdot t_{test} \le t_{prod} \le 2 \cdot t_{test}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $t_{test} = 12$ mm:**

$$
0.5 \cdot 12 \le t_{prod} \le 2 \cdot 12
$$

$$
6 \text{ mm} \le t_{prod} \le 24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the minimum and maximum boundary values:**

$$
t_{min} = 0.5 \times 12 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_{max} = 2 \times 12 = 24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the absolute lower limit rule:** ISO 9606-1 mandates a minimum of 3 mm for thin-plate qualification, so:

$$
t_{min} = \max(6, 3) = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the absolute upper limit rule:** The maximum thickness for a butt weld qualification is 40 mm (above which the welder must re-test on thicker plate):

$$
t_{max} = \min(24, 40) = 24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{6 \text{ mm} \le t_{prod} \le 24 \text{ mm}}
$$

The welder is competent to weld butt joints on steel plates ranging from **6 mm to 24 mm** thickness in the **PC position** using **MAG (135) process**.

## 3.2 Derivation: NDT Personnel Certification Validity Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A Quality Control Inspector named Mr. Rajesh is certified as **NDT Level 2 in Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** on **01 January 2023** per **ISO 9712**. Determine:
- (a) The expiry date of his UT Level 2 certificate.
- (b) The deadline for his next eye (visual acuity) test.
- (c) The condition for re-certification.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing clause in ISO 9712, Section 8:**

For Level 2 and Level 3, the certificate validity is:

$$
T_{validity} = 5 \text{ years} \text{ (60 months)}
$$

For Level 1, the validity is **10 years** (relaxed for less critical tasks).

**Step 2 — Calculate the expiry date:**

$$
T_{expiry} = T_{issue} + T_{validity}
$$

$$
T_{expiry} = 01\text{-Jan-2023} + 5 \text{ years}
$$

$$
T_{expiry} = 01\text{-Jan-2028}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the visual acuity rule (ISO 9712, Clause 7.4):**

$$
T_{eye} = 1 \text{ year (12 months)}
$$

The near-vision acuity (Jaeger No. 1 or equivalent) and color-vision test must be passed **every 12 months**.

**Step 4 — Calculate the eye test deadlines:**

$$
\text{Next eye test} = 01\text{-Jan-2023} + 1 \text{ year} = 01\text{-Jan-2024}
$$

$$
\text{Subsequent tests} = 01\text{-Jan-2024}, 01\text{-Jan-2025}, 01\text{-Jan-2026}, 01\text{-Jan-2027}, 01\text{-Jan-2028}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the re-certification condition:**

A re-certification exam must be passed within **6 months BEFORE** the expiry date. Therefore, the re-certification window for Mr. Rajesh is:

$$
T_{recert} = 01\text{-Jul-2027} \text{ to } 01\text{-Jan-2028}
$$

If the re-certification exam is failed, the certificate is suspended, and the inspector must undergo a **new full examination** (not just a re-test).

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Original Certification Date | 01-Jan-2023 | — |
| Certificate Expiry | 01-Jan-2028 | ISO 9712 §8 |
| Next Eye Test | 01-Jan-2024 | ISO 9712 §7.4 |
| Re-certification Window | 01-Jul-2027 to 01-Jan-2028 | ISO 9712 §8.3 |
| Penalty for Missed Eye Test | Certificate suspended immediately | — |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cross-Standard Comparison of Welder Qualification

| Feature | **ISO 9606-1** | **ASME Section IX** | **AWS D1.1** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geographic Domain | Europe, India, global | North America (Pressure vessels, ships) | North America (Structural) |
| Test Coupon Thickness | 3 to 40 mm | Any | Any |
| Validity Period | 2 years (extendable to 3) | Indefinite (with continuity proof every 6 months) | Indefinite (with continuity proof) |
| Visual Inspection | Mandatory per ISO 5817 Level B | Mandatory per ASME BPVC | Mandatory per AWS D1.1 |
| Bending Test | Mandatory (root + face) for thickness $> 12$ mm | Mandatory | Mandatory (reduced section tensile for $> 10$ mm) |
| Macro Examination | Optional | Optional | Optional |
| Re-qualification Trigger | Gap > 6 months in process or material group change | Change in essential variable | Change in essential variable |
| Acceptance Criteria | ISO 5817 Level B | ASME BPVC Section IX | AWS D1.1 Table 6.1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Insight:** Most Indian shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE) follow **IACS UR W17**, which is technically aligned with **ISO 9606-1**. However, ships registered under the **US flag** (e.g., for the US Navy auxiliary fleet) require **AWS D1.1** or **ASME Section IX**. A shipbuilding QA engineer must therefore be **multilingual in standards**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Tier Competency Pyramid for Shipbuilding Personnel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeF["FOUNDATION TIER - Theoretical Knowledge"]:::tier1
    nodeS["SKILL TIER - Practical Training"]:::tier2
    nodeC["COMPETENCE TIER - Certification & Validation"]:::tier3

    nodeFdesc["Metallurgy of Ship Steel"]:::detail
    nodeFdesc2["Welding Process Theory"]:::detail
    nodeFdesc3["NDT Principles"]:::detail

    nodeSdesc["Hands-on Plate Welding"]:::detail
    nodeSdesc2["UT Calibration Block Scanning"]:::detail
    nodeSdesc3["QC Documentation Practice"]:::detail

    nodeCdesc["ISO 9606-1 Welder Exam"]:::detail
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set A]**
**Q: Define "Welder Qualification" as per ISO 9606-1. Why is it a mandatory requirement in shipbuilding quality assurance?**

**Model Answer:**
Welder qualification per ISO 9606-1 is the formal procedure by which a welder's ability to produce welds meeting specified quality requirements is tested and certified. The welder welds a test coupon under examination conditions, which undergoes visual inspection, non-destructive testing (radiography or ultrasonic), and mechanical testing (tensile, bending, and macro-examination). Upon passing, a **Welder Qualification Certificate (WQC)** is issued valid for **2 years** within a defined range of material thickness, welding process, joint type, and position.

It is mandatory in shipbuilding because:
1. **Safety** — A single defective weld in a hull, propeller shaft, or pressure vessel can cause catastrophic failure.
2. **Class Requirement** — IACS Unified Requirements (UR W17) and classification society rules mandate it.
3. **Variability Control** — Human skill varies; qualification standardizes the minimum competency.
4. **Traceability** — Links a specific welder to a specific weld via a unique identification number stamped on the joint.

**Marking distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1.5) + 4 reasons (0.375 each) = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set B]**
**Q: List and briefly explain the three certification levels for NDT personnel as per ISO 9712.**

**Model Answer:**
The three certification levels for NDT personnel as per ISO 9712 are:

1. **Level 1 (Operate):** A person competent to perform NDT operations, calibrate equipment, and record results under the supervision of a Level 2 or Level 3. They cannot interpret results independently or certify the work.

2. **Level 2 (Execute and Interpret):** A person competent to select the NDT technique, calibrate equipment, perform the test, interpret and evaluate results against acceptance criteria, and prepare the NDT report. This is the **practical working level** in most shipyards.

3. **Level 3 (Authorize and Audit):** A person competent to design NDT procedures, certify Level 1 and Level 2 personnel, interpret codes and standards, and perform third-party audits. Level 3 is essentially a senior NDT engineer/manager.

**Marking distribution:** 3 levels × 1 mark each = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks in Part B questions by:
> 1. **Confusing ISO 9606-1 with ASME Section IX** — writing AWS-style answers for an ISO question. Always check which standard the question explicitly names.
> 2. **Forgetting the "re-certification" clause** — many students describe the initial test but forget to mention what happens after the validity period.
> 3. **Omitting traceability** — a WQC is only useful if the weld is stamped with the welder's unique ID. Always mention the linkage.
> 4. **Missing the visual acuity requirement for NDT** — many forget the Jaeger No. 1 / N4.5 near-vision test, which is a very common 2-mark sub-part.
> 5. **Using vague terms like "good welder" or "experienced person"** — KTU expects precise terminology: "qualified welder per ISO 9606-1 Group 1 material, valid WQC, range 6–24 mm."

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q8 (a) and (b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain in detail the welder qualification procedure as per ISO 9606-1, including the test coupon preparation, examination methods, and acceptance criteria.**

**Model Answer:**

The welder qualification procedure as per ISO 9606-1 involves the following systematic steps:

**1. Pre-Qualification Stage (1.5 Marks):**
- The welding supervisor identifies the need based on the production WPS (Welding Procedure Specification).
- The candidate welder must have a **certificate of basic training** (e.g., ITI, diploma, or in-house school).
- The candidate's **identity and prior experience** are recorded.

**2. Test Coupon Preparation (1 Mark):**
- The test plate is of the **same material group** (e.g., Group 1 per ISO/TR 15608) as the production work.
- The plate thickness is selected such that the qualified range covers the production need.
- The joint geometry (butt or fillet) is prepared per the **WPS for the test**.
- All welding parameters (current, voltage, travel speed, preheat, interpass temperature) are recorded.

**3. Welding of the Test Coupon (0.5 Mark):**
- The welder performs the test under the **direct supervision of the examiner** (an independent person from the welding department).
- Each run and layer is identified by the welder's stamp.

**4. Examination Methods (2.5 Marks):**
- **Visual Testing (VT):** Per ISO 17637, the weld surface is checked for cracks, porosity, undercut, etc.
- **Radiographic Testing (RT) or Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** Per ISO 17636-1/-2 or ISO 17640, to detect internal defects.
- **Transverse Tensile Test:** Per ISO 4136, two specimens are tested; the tensile strength must equal the **minimum specified tensile strength of the parent metal**.
- **Transverse Bending Test:** Per ISO 5173, root and face bend tests on specimens. The mandrel diameter is $D = 4t$. The test must achieve a **bend angle of 180°** without cracks exceeding 3 mm.
- **Macro Examination:** Per ISO 17639, on a cross-section to verify penetration and fusion.
- **Hardness Test:** If required, per ISO 9015-1/-2, with $HV_{max} \le 380$ for shipbuilding steels.

**5. Acceptance Criteria (1 Mark):**
- Weld quality per **ISO 5817 Level B** (stringent) for visual and NDT.
- Tensile strength $\ge R_{m,min}$ of base metal.
- Bending: 180° without failure.
- Macro: full penetration, no lack of fusion, no cracks.

**6. Issue of WQC (0.5 Mark):**
- A **Welder Qualification Certificate (WQC)** is issued, valid for **2 years**, stating:
  - Welder's name and ID
  - Test date
  - Material group, thickness range, process, position, joint type
  - Examiner's signature
  - Validity expiry date

**Linkage to Production:**
- The WQC is registered in the **Welder Qualification Register** (a matrix).
- Production welds are stamped with the welder's ID, and the WQC number is referenced in the **Weld Map** and **Weld Log Book**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the validity period, re-certification, and continuity-of-work evidence required for welder qualification per IACS UR W17.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Initial Validity (2 Marks):**
Per **IACS UR W17 (Unified Requirement for Welder's Qualification)**, the WQC issued under ISO 9606-1 is valid for an initial period of **2 years** from the date of issue. This 2-year period reflects the time window during which the welder is presumed to have maintained the skill in continuous practice.

**2. Extension by Continuity-of-Work Evidence (2 Marks):**
The 2-year validity can be **extended to a maximum of 3 years** if the welder provides documented evidence of **continuity of work** in the qualified range. The evidence must include:
- **Welder log** showing at least one production weld completed every **6 months** within the qualified thickness, process, and position range.
- The welds must have been **accepted by QC** (i.e., passed NDT).
- The employer or classification society surveyor can verify this evidence during audits.

If there is a **gap of more than 6 months**, the continuity is considered **broken**, and the welder must undergo **re-qualification**.

**3. Re-qualification Procedure (2 Marks):**
- If the welder wishes to continue beyond 3 years, a **new test coupon must be welded and examined** as per the original procedure.
- If the welder has been working in a **different material group, process, or position**, a **new qualification** is mandatory.
- The re-qualification is essentially a repetition of the original test, with new coupons and new WQC issuance.

**4. Revocation and Suspension (1 Mark):**
- The WQC can be **suspended or revoked** by the classification society surveyor or the shipyard QA head if:
  - The welder produces **two or more defective welds** due to workmanship.
  - The WQC is found to be **fraudulent** (e.g., welded by someone else).
  - The welder fails to maintain continuity without re-qualification.

**5. Practical Implications (optional, marks may be given for clarity):**
- Shipyards maintain a **Welder Continuity Register** that is reviewed monthly.
- Class surveyors (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) audit this register during newbuild and operational surveys.
- Non-conformities in the welder register are treated as **Major NCRs** and can stop production.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q9 (a) and (b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the role of ISO 9712 in certifying Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) personnel. Describe the examination structure, validity, and re-certification rules.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Purpose of ISO 9712 (1 Mark):**
ISO 9712:2012 is the international standard for the **qualification and certification of personnel who perform industrial non-destructive testing**. In shipbuilding, it covers methods such as **UT, RT, MT, PT, VT, and ET** for hull welds, castings, and forgings. It ensures that only personnel with proven knowledge, skill, and visual capability perform NDT.

**2. Three-Tier Certification Structure (1.5 Marks):**
- **Level 1:** Performs calibrations and tests under supervision; minimum 40 hours training.
- **Level 2:** Independently performs and interprets NDT; issues the report. Minimum 9 months or 2400 hours of documented experience at Level 1.
- **Level 3:** Designs NDT procedures, certifies lower levels, performs audits. Minimum 4 years at Level 2 plus cross-method competency.

**3. Examination Structure (2 Marks):**
The exam has three components:
- **General (Common) Exam:** 40 MCQs on metallurgy, math, safety, and ISO 9712 itself. Pass mark: **70%**.
- **Specific Exam:** 30 MCQs on the chosen NDT method (e.g., UT). Pass mark: **70%**.
- **Practical Exam:** Hands-on test on a calibration block with **5 known defects** (3 must be detected for pass).

For Level 3, an additional **Basic Exam** is required covering procedure writing and audit principles.

**4. Validity Period (1 Mark):**
- **Level 1 and Level 2:** Certificate valid for **5 years** (10 years for Level 1 per latest revisions).
- **Level 3:** Valid for **5 years** (10 years with extension).
- **Visual acuity (Jaeger No. 1 or N4.5):** Must be re-tested every **12 months**.

**5. Re-certification (1 Mark):**
- A **new exam (practical + specific theory)** must be passed within the 6 months before expiry.
- If the certificate lapses, the inspector must undergo the **full initial exam**, not just the re-test.

**6. Suspension/Cancellation (0.5 Mark):**
- Failure of eye test → immediate suspension.
- Two consecutive failures in the same method → cancellation of that method (other methods remain valid).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the role of ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.2 in ensuring personnel competence. How is it implemented in a shipyard's quality management system (QMS)?**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Clause 7.2 Overview (1 Mark):**
ISO 9001:2015, Clause 7.2 — **Competence**, states that the organization must:
- Determine the necessary competence for personnel performing work affecting quality.
- Ensure these persons are competent on the basis of education, training, skills, and experience.
- Where applicable, take actions to acquire the necessary competence and evaluate the effectiveness of those actions.
- Retain appropriate documented information as evidence of competence.

**2. Determining Competence Requirements (1.5 Marks):**
- For each **role** (welder, NDT inspector, QC engineer, supervisor), a **Competence Matrix** is prepared listing:
  - Required education (e.g., Diploma in Mechanical Engineering for QC engineer).
  - Required certifications (e.g., ISO 9606-1 WQC for welders, ISO 9712 Level 2 for NDT inspectors).
  - Required experience in years.
  - Specific skill tests (e.g., plate welding test, UT calibration test).

**3. Acquiring Competence (1.5 Marks):**
- **In-house training programs** (e.g., on new WPS, new steel grade, new IMO regulation).
- **External courses** (e.g., AWS CWI, IACS-approved courses, Lead Auditor course).
- **On-the-job training** under a senior mentor.
- **Cross-functional rotation** to develop multi-skilled personnel.

**4. Evaluating Effectiveness (1.5 Marks):**
- After training, an **effectiveness evaluation** is performed, e.g.:
  - For a welder: First 5 production welds under supervision.
  - For an NDT inspector: First 10 NDT reports reviewed by a Level 3.
  - For an auditor: First internal audit performed under a Lead Auditor.
- The result is documented in the **Training Effectiveness Report**.

**5. Documented Information (1 Mark):**
- **Personnel records** including:
  - Education certificates (10th, 12th, Diploma, Degree).
  - WQC / NDT certificates with validity dates.
  - Training attendance records.
  - Effectiveness evaluation forms.
  - CVs and experience letters.
- These records are **controlled documents** under the QMS and are auditable by Class and certification bodies.

**6. Continuous Improvement Linkage (0.5 Mark):**
- Clause 7.2 is closely linked to Clause 10.2 (Nonconformity and Corrective Action) — a defective weld often traces back to inadequate training, triggering a CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action).
- The PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle ensures competence is **continuously maintained and improved**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Training and Certification" in KTU Shipbuilding QA:**

- **The Big Three Standards:** ISO 9606-1 (welders), ISO 9712 (NDT), ISO 9001:2015 (QMS personnel). Memorize which clause controls which function.
- **Welder Validity:** 2 years (extendable to 3 with continuity of work every 6 months).
- **NDT Validity:** 5 years for Level 2/3, **annual eye test** (Jaeger No. 1) is non-negotiable.
- **Thickness Rule:** $0.5t_{test} \le t_{prod} \le 2t_{test}$ — a 12 mm test plate qualifies the welder for 6–24 mm.
- **Three NDT Levels:** Operate (L1) → Execute + Interpret + Report (L2) → Authorize + Audit + Train (L3).
- **IACS UR W17:** This is the binding regulation for all IACS-classed ships. Know it by name.
- **Acceptance Quality Level:** Weld quality judged per **ISO 5817 Level B** (stringent) for welder qualification.
- **Traceability:** Every weld → stamped with welder ID → referenced in Weld Map → WQC in matrix. This is the chain auditors look for.
- **Cost of Non-Compliance:** An unqualified welder is a **Major NCR**; the ship can be **detained or rejected** by the class surveyor.
- **Hot Trends (for KTU 2024):** Robotic welding certification, augmented-reality-based training, AI-assisted defect recognition — all emerging but the human competency baseline remains unchanged.
- **One-Liner Mantra:** *"Qualify the Person Before Qualifying the Weld."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Manual and Document Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Manual and Document Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Quality Manual — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 4.3):** A **Quality Manual (QM)** is the top-tier controlled document of a Quality Management System (QMS) that specifies the scope of the QMS, documents the interaction of its processes (often in the form of a process map or turtle diagram), and reflects the organization's quality policy and objectives. In shipbuilding, the Quality Manual is the contractual and regulatory reference document that binds the shipyard to a Class Society, the Owner, the Flag State Administration, and statutory authorities (e.g., SOLAS, MARPOL).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 4):** The Quality Manual forms the apex of the shipyard's documentation pyramid. It is referenced in **CO4** of PENST862 — *"Apply quality system documentation principles in shipbuilding projects."*

### 1.2 Document Management — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Document Management (DM)** is the systematic control of the creation, review, approval, distribution, revision, archival, and disposal of all quality-affecting documents and records throughout their life cycle, in compliance with ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.5 (Documented Information) and classification society procedural requirements (IACS Z17, IACS UR Z23, etc.).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Plan Cabinet" Intuition

Imagine constructing a **100-storey skyscraper** (the ship) where hundreds of engineers, welders, electricians, and inspectors work **simultaneously** across different zones. Without a central **filing cabinet** (the Quality Manual) that everyone agrees is the single source of truth, welders in Zone B might weld the wrong steel grade because they read an *outdated* revision. Document management is essentially:

- **The Cabinet** = Quality Manual (states *what* and *why*)
- **The Folders Inside** = Procedures, WPS, Drawings (state *how*)
- **The Stamps on Folders** = Version Numbers, Approval Signatures, Issue Dates
- **The Librarian** = Document Controller (manages access, distribution, and obsolete records)
- **The Audit Trail Logbook** = Master Document Register

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Shipyard Analogy:** In an Indian shipyard building a Product Tanker for an overseas Owner, the **Quality Manual** will be reviewed and accepted by **DNV** (or the relevant IACS Member Society) *before* the first plate is cut. Every subsequent procedure, drawing, and NDT report is then linked back to this manual through a **document numbering system** (e.g., `QM-PR-WLD-001 Rev.03`).

### 1.4 Key Metrics and Standards (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **ISO 9001:2015** — Universal QMS standard; Clause 7.5 governs Documented Information.
- **IACS Recommendation Z17 / Z23** — Procedural requirements for shipyards and units.
- **ISO 15489-1:2016** — Records Management standard.
- **Retention Period:** Statutory minimum typically **5 years** post-delivery; Class records often **life of the vessel + 5 years** (~30 years).
- **Approval Cycle Time (T_c):** A KTU-recognised metric for document control efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Five-Tier Documentation Pyramid (ISO 9001 Aligned)

A shipyard's documented information is structured in a **strict hierarchy** — lower tiers derive authority from higher tiers:

| Tier | Document Type | Authority Level | Shipbuilding Example |
|:----:|:--------------|:----------------|:---------------------|
| **1** | **Quality Policy** | Top Management | 1-page signed vision statement |
| **2** | **Quality Manual (QM)** | Top Management + Class | The QMS constitution |
| **3** | **Procedures (QSP)** | QA Head / HODs | Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) |
| **4** | **Work Instructions (WI)** | Supervisors / Foremen | Step-by-step pipe-fitting instructions |
| **5** | **Records / Forms** | All personnel | NDT reports, inspection logs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Quality Manual must explicitly reference **every Tier-3 procedure** in a **Master Document Register (MDR)**. This is a frequent 3-mark and 14-mark question.

### 2.2 The "Why and How" of Document Control

A controlled document must satisfy the **6 R's** of document control:

1. **Right Document** — Is the latest revision being used? (Avoids obsolete drawings reaching the shop floor.)
2. **Right Version** — Is the revision number current and traceable?
3. **Right Place** — Is it physically/digitally accessible at the point of use?
4. **Right Person** — Is the reader authorised to access it?
5. **Right Format** — Is it legible, in a controlled language, and properly translated?
6. **Right Time** — Is it available *before* the activity begins?

> [!NOTE]
> **Class Society Trigger:** If an obsolete drawing is found in active use during an IACS Member audit, the **Non-Conformity (NC)** is classified as **Major**, leading to potential suspension of class approval.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|:-------------------|:---------------------|:-----|:--------------------|
| **Approval Cycle Time** $T_c$ | $T_c = T_{review} + T_{approve} + T_{distribute}$ | days | Measures document control efficiency |
| **Document Control Efficiency** $\eta_{dc}$ | $\eta_{dc} = \dfrac{N_{on\text{-}time}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | KPIs for QA Manager |
| **Obsolete Document Leakage Rate** $L_{obs}$ | $L_{obs} = \dfrac{N_{obsolete\_found}}{N_{audited}} \times 1000$ | per-mille (‰) | Risk indicator in shipyard audit |
| **Retention Period** $R_p$ | $R_p = \max(R_{statute}, R_{class}, R_{contract})$ | years | Defines archival life |
| **Master Document Register Completeness** $C_{MDR}$ | $C_{MDR} = \dfrac{N_{listed}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | % | Audit metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note:** Vertical bars `|` are intentionally avoided in table cells (use `\vert` or `\mid` if needed for absolute values). This prevents breaking the markdown table renderer.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding Engineering

- **Contract Binding:** The QM, once approved by Class and the Owner, becomes a **legally binding contract document**. Any deviation requires formal **Request for Change (RFC)**.
- **Traceability:** Every steel plate, every weld, every NDT report must be traceable to a controlled drawing and procedure — this is the foundation of **Class certification** and **insurance claims**.
- **Knowledge Preservation:** Ship construction spans 18–36 months with high personnel turnover. The DM system ensures **institutional memory** survives the project.
- **Liability Defence:** In maritime accidents (e.g., hull failure, oil spill), the document trail is the **primary legal defence** of the shipyard.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Document Approval Cycle Time ($T_c$)

The document control process is a **sequential workflow** governed by discrete review/approval steps. The total cycle time is the **sum of the individual gate times** (this is the "critical path" logic borrowed from project management).

$$
T_c \;=\; T_{review}^{(1)} \;+\; T_{approve}^{(1)} \;+\; T_{review}^{(2)} \;+\; T_{approve}^{(2)} \;+\; T_{distribute}
$$

**Logical Decomposition (step-by-step):**

1. **Originator drafts** the document at time $t_0$.
2. **First Reviewer** (e.g., Welding Engineer) reviews for technical accuracy: $T_{review}^{(1)}$ days.
3. **First Approver** (e.g., QA Manager) signs off: $T_{approve}^{(1)}$ days.
4. **Second Reviewer** (e.g., Class Surveyor) cross-checks against rules: $T_{review}^{(2)}$ days.
5. **Second Approver** (e.g., Owner's Representative) gives final concurrence: $T_{approve}^{(2)}$ days.
6. **Document Controller** issues the document with controlled distribution: $T_{distribute}$ days.
7. The total **calendar elapsed** time is therefore the linear sum, as each step is **dependent** on the previous one (Finish-to-Start linkage in PERT/CPM terms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-1 Numeric Example:** For a 50-page WPS in a mid-size Indian shipyard: $T_{review}^{(1)}=3$ d, $T_{approve}^{(1)}=1$ d, $T_{review}^{(2)}=5$ d, $T_{approve}^{(2)}=2$ d, $T_{distribute}=1$ d.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 3 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 1 \\
&= 12 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> A blank line above and below each display equation is maintained per the **KTU LaTeX Isolation Rule**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Document Control Efficiency ($\eta_{dc}$)

This is a **KPI** used by QA Managers to track how many documents are released on or before the planned date.

$$
\eta_{dc} \;=\; \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N_{total}} \mathbb{1}\left(T_{c,i} \;\leq\; T_{planned,i}\right)}{N_{total}} \;\times\; 100\%
$$

Where the **indicator function** is:

$$
\mathbb{1}(x) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x \text{ is true (released on time)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose a shipyard released 120 drawings in a month. 18 were delayed beyond the planned issue date.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{dc} &= \dfrac{120 - 18}{120} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{102}{120} \times 100\% \\
&= 85.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark:** A **world-class** shipyard targets $\eta_{dc} \geq 95\%$. A figure below 80% indicates systemic bottlenecks in document control — a common KTU exam "case study" trigger.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Document Control Workflow Gates

| Gate # | Stage | Responsible Authority | Output Artifact | Failure Consequence |
|:------:|:------|:----------------------|:----------------|:--------------------|
| **G1** | Origination | Discipline Engineer | Draft + Doc No. | No filing |
| **G2** | Technical Review | Senior Engineer | Marked-up draft | Rejection to G1 |
| **G3** | QA Verification | QA Engineer | Compliance stamp | NC raised |
| **G4** | Class Approval | Class Surveyor | Class stamp | Hold on fabrication |
| **G5** | Owner Approval | Owner's Rep. | Concession note | Construction delay |
| **G6** | Issue & Distribute | Doc. Controller | Master + Shop copies | Document leak / obsolescence |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **G4 Class Approval gate** is unique to shipbuilding. Most land-based ISO 9001 firms have only G2, G3, G6. The presence of Class and Owner gates is what makes maritime QA a *multi-stakeholder* discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Quality Manual — Internal Structure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[Section 2 Normative References ISO 9001 IACS Class Rules]

    A --> E[Section 4 Context of Organization Interested Parties]
    A --> F[Section 5 Leadership Quality Policy Objectives]

    A --> H[Section 7 Support Documented Information Competence]
    A --> I[Section 8 Operation Planning Control Production]
    A --> J[Section 9 Performance Evaluation Monitoring Measurement]
    A --> K[Section 10 Improvement NC CAPA Continual]
    A --> L[Annex A Process Interaction Map]
    A --> M[Annex B Master Document Register]
    A --> N[Annex C List of Controlled Forms]
```

### 4.2 Document Control Life-Cycle Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define a Quality Manual and state any four mandatory contents as per ISO 9001:2015.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A Quality Manual is a top-level controlled document specifying the scope of the QMS, the documented processes and their interactions, and the quality policy/objectives (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 4.3). Four mandatory contents: **(i)** Scope of the QMS and applicable exclusions, **(ii)** Normative references (ISO 9001, IACS rules), **(iii)** Process interaction map / turtle diagrams, **(iv)** Master Document Register (MDR) listing all controlled procedures.
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark × 4 for contents — total 3 marks.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. List the six "R's" of document control and explain any two in one sentence each.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The six R's are: **Right Document, Right Version, Right Place, Right Person, Right Format, Right Time** (1 mark for listing).
>
> **(i) Right Document** — Ensures that the latest approved drawing (not an obsolete revision) is being used at the workstation, preventing fabrication errors. (1 mark)
>
> **(ii) Right Person** — Restricts access to authorised personnel only, preserving confidentiality and integrity of controlled information. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the structure and mandatory contents of a Quality Manual for a shipbuilding organisation, with reference to **ISO 9001:2015 Clauses 4 to 10**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete document control life cycle** from origination to disposal. Highlight the role of **Class Society** and **Owner approval gates** in a shipyard. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Quality Manual Structure (7 Marks):**

The Quality Manual for a shipyard is structured clause-by-clause as follows:

- **Clause 4 — Context of the Organization** (1 mark): Lists *interested parties* (Owner, Class, Flag State, Vendors, Regulatory bodies) and their requirements. Defines QMS scope (e.g., "Design and construction of commercial vessels up to 300 m LOA").
- **Clause 5 — Leadership** (1 mark): Records Top Management's *quality policy*, *organisational roles and responsibilities* (matrix form), and quality objectives (e.g., "Zero Major NCs per quarter").
- **Clause 6 — Planning** (1 mark): Identifies QMS *risks and opportunities* (e.g., late vendor delivery, weld defect trends) and plans actions to address them.
- **Clause 7 — Support** (1.5 marks): Covers resources, competence, awareness, communication, and **Documented Information (Clause 7.5)** — the heart of the QM. Specifies the format, control, and retention of documents.
- **Clause 8 — Operation** (1 mark): Details the *operational planning and control* of shipbuilding processes (steel cutting, welding, erection, launching, sea trials), including design & development controls.
- **Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation** (0.5 mark): Specifies internal audits, management review, and KPI monitoring.
- **Clause 10 — Improvement** (0.5 mark): Describes handling of Non-Conformities, Corrective/Preventive Actions (CAPA), and continual improvement.
- **Annexes** (0.5 mark): Process map, Master Document Register, List of controlled forms.

**(b) Document Control Life Cycle (7 Marks):**

The 13-stage life cycle shown in **Section 4.2** is detailed as follows:

1. **Origination (1 mark):** Discipline engineer drafts the document (e.g., WPS) with a unique **document number** (e.g., `QM-PR-WLD-012`).
2. **Technical Review (1 mark):** Senior engineer of the same discipline verifies technical correctness against codes (e.g., AWS D1.1, IACS UR Z17).
3. **QA Verification (0.5 mark):** QA engineer ensures compliance with QMS requirements and Class rules.
4. **Class Approval (1.5 marks):** The Class Surveyor (DNV/ABS/LR/IRS) reviews and stamps the document. **Without this stamp, the document is NOT valid for ship construction** — this is a shipbuilding-unique gate.
5. **Owner Approval (1.5 marks):** For Owner-furnished or critical documents (e.g., shaft alignment, hull GA), the Owner's representative provides formal concurrence. This is contractually mandated.
6. **Issue & Distribution (0.5 mark):** Document Controller assigns a **revision number** (Rev. 0 for first issue), enters it in the **Master Document Register**, and distributes controlled copies (digital + print) to authorised users.
7. **Obsolescence Control (0.5 mark):** When a new revision is issued, the old revision is stamped **"OBSOLETE — SUPERSEDED BY Rev.X"** and withdrawn from all active workstations. Electronic copies are deleted from active folders but archived.
8. **Archival & Disposal (0.5 mark):** After the **retention period** $R_p = \max(R_{statute}, R_{class}, R_{contract})$, records are disposed of securely (paper shredded, digital wiped per ISO 27001).

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **five-tier documentation hierarchy** in a shipyard's QMS. State one shipbuilding example for each tier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Master Document Register (MDR)**? Prepare a **sample MDR for a Welding Function** containing at least 6 entries. Explain the columns and their significance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Five-Tier Documentation Hierarchy (7 Marks):**

*(Refer to the pyramid in Section 4.3 for the sketch; award 1 mark for the diagram and 0.5 mark per tier description.)*

- **Tier 1 — Quality Policy** (0.5 mark): A one-page signed statement of Top Management's quality intent. *Example:* "We commit to zero-defect delivery of vessels to our clients."
- **Tier 2 — Quality Manual** (1.5 marks): The constitution of the QMS. *Example:* `QM-2024 Rev.05` approved by DNV.
- **Tier 3 — Procedures (QSP)** (1.5 marks): Discipline-specific controlled procedures. *Example:* Welding Procedure Specification `WPS-MAG-001 Rev.02`.
- **Tier 4 — Work Instructions (WI)** (1.5 marks): Step-by-step shop-floor instructions. *Example:* `WI-PFT-007 — Assembly of Butt-Welded Pipe Spools`.
- **Tier 5 — Records / Forms** (1 mark): Objective evidence of work done. *Example:* `FR-WLD-022 — Daily Welding Log Sheet` and `FR-NDT-008 — UT Report`.

**(b) Master Document Register (7 Marks):**

A **Master Document Register** is a **centralised controlled index** of all quality-affecting documents in a shipyard, listing every document's number, title, current revision, issue date, owner, and status. It is the **single source of truth** for the QMS.

(0.5 mark for definition)

**Sample MDR — Welding Function (6 rows, 1 mark per row × 6 = 6 marks, capped at 6; remaining 0.5 mark for column explanation):**

| Doc. No. | Title | Rev. | Issue Date | Owner | Status |
|:---------|:------|:----:|:----------:|:-----:|:------:|
| QM-WLD-001 | Welding Quality Manual | 05 | 2024-04-12 | QA Mgr | Active |
| QSP-WLD-002 | WPS Preparation Procedure | 03 | 2024-06-08 | Welding Eng | Active |
| WPS-MAG-014 | MAG Welding of Grade A Steel | 02 | 2024-07-15 | Welding Eng | Active |
| WI-WLD-022 | Operator Qualification Test | 04 | 2024-05-20 | Welding Eng | Active |
| FR-WLD-031 | Weld Inspection Checklist | 06 | 2024-08-01 | Insp. Sup. | Active |
| RP-WLD-005 | Welder Performance Records | 00 | 2024-01-10 | HR + Welding | Active |

**Column Significance (0.5 mark for any 2 explained):**
- **Doc. No.** — Unique traceable identifier (links records to procedure).
- **Rev.** — Current revision level (prevents use of obsolete versions).
- **Issue Date** — Establishes audit trail and timing of effectiveness.
- **Owner** — Person accountable for document content and reviews.
- **Status** — Active / Obsolete / Withdrawn (manages document life cycle).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Quality Manual with Quality Policy.** Policy is a *one-page intent*; Manual is the *multi-page constitution* (Common error: students swap definitions — **−2 marks**).
> 2. **Always mention Class Society** in shipbuilding document control answers. Generic ISO 9001 answers fetch only 60–70% of marks because the **shipbuilding-specific gate** is the differentiator.
> 3. **Avoid horizontal markdown tables in a 14-mark answer** if the question asks for a *neat sketch* — examiners expect a **labelled diagram** (Section 4.3 is your friend).
> 4. **Retention period formula** $R_p = \max(R_{statute}, R_{class}, R_{contract})$ — students who quote a single number lose 1–2 marks; the **max-function** is the key.
> 5. **The 6 R's** are the most-favoured 3-mark question — memorise them verbatim.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This to Your Wall!**

- **Quality Manual** = Top-tier QMS document (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 4.3).
- **Master Document Register (MDR)** = Centralised index of ALL controlled documents.
- **Six R's of Document Control** = Right **D**ocument, **V**ersion, **P**lace, **P**erson, **F**ormat, **T**ime.
- **Five-Tier Documentation Pyramid** = Policy → Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
- **Shipbuilding-Specific Gates** = **Class Approval** (e.g., DNV, ABS, LR, IRS) + **Owner Approval** — both are NON-NEGOTIABLE.
- **Approval Cycle Time** = $T_c = T_{review} + T_{approve} + T_{distribute}$.
- **Document Control Efficiency** $\eta_{dc} \geq 95\%$ = world-class benchmark.
- **Retention Period** $R_p = \max(R_{statute}, R_{class}, R_{contract})$ — minimum 5 years statutory, up to **life of vessel + 5 years** for Class.
- **Obsolete documents** must be **stamped, withdrawn, and archived** — never destroyed immediately.
- **EDMS** (Electronic Document Management System) is the modern industry standard; provides audit trail, version control, and access logs.
- **IACS Z17 / Z23** = mandatory shipyard procedure requirements (cross-reference Class rules).
- **KTU Hot Keywords** for 14-mark answers: *ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.5, IACS Member Society, Class Surveyor, Master Document Register, Documented Information, Process Map, Turtle Diagram, CAPA.*

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Quality Manual and Document Management**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality in Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality in Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality in Planning** is the systematic, documented process of establishing quality objectives, identifying relevant quality standards (IACS, ISO 9001, Class Society Rules), determining required processes, and allocating resources — *before* any physical shipbuilding activity begins — to ensure that the final vessel conforms to contractual, regulatory, and customer requirements.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** course **PENST862 – Quality Assurance in Shipbuilding**, *Quality in Planning* is treated as the **upstream pillar** of the entire QA value chain. It is governed by the principle of **"Prevention over Inspection"**, formalized in **ISO 9000:2015 clause 8.5.1 (Control of Production and Service Provision)** and reinforced by the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**.

The hierarchy of planning artefacts a shipyard must produce includes:

| Planning Artefact | Governing Standard | KTU CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Project Quality Plan (PQP) | ISO 10005 | CO4, CO5 |
| Inspection and Test Plan (ITP) | ISO 10005 / Class Rules | CO4 |
| Risk & Opportunity Register | ISO 9001:2015 Cl. 6.1 | CO5 |
| Advanced Product Quality Plan (APQP) | AIAG / OEM-specific | CO4 |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Building a House"**
> Imagine you are building a house. *Quality in Planning* is the moment you sit down with the architect *before* the first brick is laid: checking soil reports, drawing plumbing layouts, listing cement grades, scheduling inspections, and deciding who checks the wiring. If the foundation plan is wrong, no amount of inspection *after* construction will save the house.
>
> In a shipyard, the "first brick" is the **cutting of the first steel plate**. Everything before that — design review, process plan, ITP, welder qualification, supplier vetting — is *Quality in Planning*.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics Used in Planning

- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — used to baseline shipyard process capability before project start.
- **Six-Sigma Level (σ)** — Target for critical hull-welding and pipe-bending processes.
- **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** — planned target for each block/sub-assembly.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — budgeted at the planning stage as a percentage of contract value.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** — used in design and process FMEA during planning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Quality in Planning

The planning phase of a shipbuilding project is decomposed into **five logically sequential pillars**. Each pillar produces a documented deliverable that becomes an auditable input to the next phase.

1. **Contract & Classification Review**
   - Decode the **Classification Society** requirements (e.g., **Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, ABS, IRS**).
   - Translate the **Specification of Work (SOW)** into measurable acceptance criteria.
   - *Why:* Ships are global assets; non-conformity means the vessel cannot be insured or flagged.

2. **Process & Method Planning**
   - Decide welding procedures (WPS/PQR per **ASME IX** or **ISO 15614-1**).
   - Decide block-joining strategy, erection sequence, and outfitting pre-installation plans.
   - *Why:* Determines where and how NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) will be applied.

3. **Inspection & Test Planning (ITP)**
   - The ITP is the **single most important planning document**. It is a matrix that maps:
     - What is inspected?
     - When (Hold Point / Witness / Surveillance)?
     - By whom (Yard QC / Class Surveyor / Owner Rep)?
     - With what acceptance criterion?
   - Hold-Points (H), Witness-Points (W), and Surveillance (S) follow the convention defined in **ISO 10005**.

4. **Resource & Competency Planning**
   - Allocation of qualified welders (per **ISO 9606-1**), NDT inspectors (per **ISO 9712**), and QA engineers.
   - Equipment calibration schedules for NDT machines, gauges, and CMM arms.

5. **Risk, Cost, and Document Planning**
   - Creation of the **FMEA register**, **Cost of Quality budget**, and **Document Control Matrix**.
   - Establishes traceability from raw material certificate to final Class deliverable.

### 2.2 The PDCA Cycle Embedded in Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Quality in Planning is **not a one-time event**; it is iterative. The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (Deming Cycle)** is applied *within* the planning phase itself. The "Plan" stage of project execution is the "Do" stage of the planning activity, while the "Check" stage of project execution loops back into the "Act" stage of planning for the *next* block, ship, or contract.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | defects / 10⁶ opp | Baseline process capability |
| 2 | **Process Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5$ | σ (sigma) | Six-Sigma target setting |
| 3 | **First-Pass Yield** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units inspected}} \times 100\%$ | % | Block erection quality target |
| 4 | **Rolled Throughput Yield** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | % (decimal) | End-to-end process yield |
| 5 | **Risk Priority Number** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | unitless (1–1000) | FMEA prioritization |
| 6 | **Cost of Quality** | $\text{CoQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | Currency | QA budget planning |
| 7 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Plate-cutting tolerance |
| 8 | **Acceptable Quality Level** | $\text{AQL} = f(\text{Inspection level, sample size})$ | % (per ISO 2859-1) | Incoming material sampling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on formula notation:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is intentionally avoided in markdown table cells. The $C_{pk}$ expression uses the $\min(\cdot)$ function for the same reason.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Class Society Audits:** Planning documents (PQP, ITP, Welding Procedure Qualification Records) are the *first* files requested during a **Class Society certification audit**. Failure here = automatic Non-Conformity.
- **Owner Re-negotiation:** A well-prepared Quality Plan reduces post-signing variation orders (VOs), which are a major source of contract disputes.
- **Insurance Premiums:** Insurance underwriters (e.g., **Lloyd's, Gard, UK P&I**) look for evidence of a *robust* planning file when setting hull and machinery premiums.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting DPMO to Process Sigma Level

The **Process Sigma Level** is the most common metric used when a yard's management asks, *"How capable is our planning process?"* The derivation proceeds from the cumulative normal distribution.

**Step 1 — Define the cumulative probability of a defect-free unit.**

For a process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the probability that a unit falls within the specification limits $[\text{LSL},\ \text{USL}]$ is given by the standard normal CDF:

$$
P(\text{pass}) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\text{LSL} - \mu}{\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using the symmetric-tolerance assumption.**

Most quality planning documents assume a centered process ($\mu$ at the nominal) and symmetric limits. The expression collapses to:

$$
P(\text{pass}) = 2\,\Phi\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}\right) - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to defects per opportunity.**

The probability of a defect is $1 - P(\text{pass})$. Multiplying by $10^{6}$ gives DPMO:

$$
\text{DPMO} = \left[1 - P(\text{pass})\right] \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\sigma$ in terms of DPMO.**

Inverting the standard normal distribution gives:

$$
\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{2 \times 10^{6}}\right)
$$

Industry convention (Motorola/AIAG) adds a **1.5σ shift** to account for long-term process drift:

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5
$$

This is the **exact formula** used in Six-Sigma planning tables.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: RPN Calculation for a Planning-Phase FMEA

A shipyard is planning the erection of a **double-bottom tank** in a Panamax bulk carrier. The Design FMEA identifies the following failure mode:

- **Failure Mode:** Inadequate tack-weld length leading to crack propagation during block lifting.
- **Severity (S):** 8 (loss of structural integrity → potential capsize).
- **Occurrence (O):** 5 (occurs occasionally during pre-erection stage).
- **Detectability (D):** 4 (moderate — visual + MPI inspection catches it late).

**Step 1 — Compute the baseline RPN:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{baseline}} = S \times O \times D
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{baseline}} = 8 \times 5 \times 4
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{baseline}} = 160
$$

**Step 2 — Apply a planned countermeasure (WPS revision + 100 % MPI of tack welds):**

After planning the countermeasure:
- New **O = 2** (tack-weld length now controlled by WPS parameter).
- New **D = 2** (100 % MPI catches it immediately).
- **S remains 8** (failure consequence is unchanged by detection).

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} = 8 \times 2 \times 2
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} = 32
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the RPN reduction (the "Planning Effectiveness" metric):**

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = \frac{160 - 32}{160} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = 80\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A planned countermeasure that reduces RPN by **≥ 75 %** is considered *highly effective* in KTU-style quality planning case studies.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Planning Document Cross-Reference

| Planning Document | Inputs | Outputs | Owner | Class Society Linkage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Project Quality Plan (PQP) | Contract spec, Class rules, ISO 9001 | Quality objectives, audit schedule | Project QA Manager | QSCS Audit |
| Inspection & Test Plan (ITP) | PQP, drawings, WPS | Hold/Witness/Surveillance matrix | Lead QC Engineer | Surveyor visit schedule |
| Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) | PQR, base material, joint type | Welding parameters, NDT scope | Welding Engineer | Approval per ASME IX / ISO 15614 |
| Risk & Opportunity Register | PQP, FMEA, lessons learned | Risk treatment actions, escalation matrix | Risk Coordinator | Linked to Class NCRs |
| Calibration Schedule | Equipment list, ISO 9001 Cl. 7.1.5 | Calibration certificates | Metrology Engineer | Class may witness critical cal. |
| Document Control Matrix | Contract deliverables list | Revision status, distribution list | Document Controller | Class approval letters archived |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quality-in-Planning Process Flow (Mermaid)

The following flowchart maps the **chronological logic of Quality in Planning** from the moment a shipbuilding contract is signed until the cutting of the first plate. The flow is read top-to-bottom and includes **feedback loops** that are characteristic of iterative planning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Contract Awarded] --> B[Contract & Class Review]
    B --> C[Define Quality Objectives per ISO 9001 Cl 6.2]
    C --> D[Develop Project Quality Plan PQP]
    D --> E[Perform Design FMEA]
    E --> F[Develop Welding and Process Procedures]
    F --> G[Build Inspection and Test Plan ITP]
    G --> H[Plan Resources Welders NDT Equipment]
    H --> I[Establish Cost of Quality Budget]
    I --> J[Set Document Control Matrix]
    J --> K{Internal QA Review}
    K -- Approved --> L[Release to Production Cutting of First Plate]
    K -- Rejected --> M[Revise Planning Documents]
    M --> D
    L --> N[Execution Phase]
    N -.Feedback Loop Lessons Learned.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback arrow from **N (Execution)** back to **D (PQP development)** is the *Plan-Do-Check-Act* improvement loop. Lessons learned during execution feed back into the planning of the next ship, block, or series vessel.

---

### 4.2 Hold / Witness / Surveillance Logic (Mermaid Decision Flow)

This decision tree is used during the **ITP construction step** in the main flow. It is the most heavily examined logic in KTU valuation, so it is reproduced here in a dedicated schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[New Activity to be Inspected] --> Q{Does Class Society Require Approval?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Mark as HOLD POINT H]
    Q -- No --> S{Owner Representative Wishes to Witness?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Mark as WITNESS POINT W]
    S -- No --> U[Mark as SURVEILLANCE S]
    R --> V[Record in ITP with Notification Lead Time]
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Distribute ITP to Production and Class]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Quality Planning Documentation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Contract Specification] --> L2[Project Quality Plan PQP]
    L2 --> L3[Inspection and Test Plan ITP]
    L2 --> L4[Risk and Opportunity Register]
    L2 --> L5[Welding Procedure Specification WPS]
    L3 --> L6[Daily QC Reports]
    L3 --> L7[NDT Reports]
    L4 --> L8[FMEA Worksheets]
    L5 --> L9[Welder Qualification Records]
    L6 --> L10[Class Survey Reports]
    L7 --> L10
    L8 --> L10
    L9 --> L10
    L10 --> L11[Final Class Deliverable File]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This hierarchy mirrors the document pyramid expected by **IACS QSCS auditors** and is the answer blueprint for any KTU question that asks, *"List and explain the hierarchy of quality planning documents in a shipyard."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1] [CO4, K3 – Apply]**

**Q1. Define "Quality in Planning" as applied to a shipbuilding project. List any four planning documents generated in this phase.**

**Model Answer:**

Quality in Planning is the systematic process of establishing quality objectives, defining procedures, and allocating resources *before* commencement of shipbuilding activities, in accordance with **ISO 9001:2015** and **Class Society Rules**, to ensure the delivered vessel conforms to contractual and regulatory requirements.

Four planning documents:

1. **Project Quality Plan (PQP)** — establishes project-specific quality objectives and audit cadence.
2. **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** — defines Hold, Witness, and Surveillance points across the build.
3. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** — qualified per **ASME IX / ISO 15614** for each joint type.
4. **Risk & Opportunity Register** — captures FMEA-driven risks and their treatments.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2] [CO4, K2 – Understand]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a *Hold Point* and a *Witness Point* in an Inspection and Test Plan (ITP).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hold Point (H) | Witness Point (W) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Activity *cannot* proceed without inspector's release | Inspector *may* attend; if absent, activity may proceed |
| Authority | Class Society / Owner has **mandatory** authority | Authority is **optional**; notification only |
| Typical use | Hull structural welds, shaft alignment, sea trial | Pipe pressure tests, paint DFT checks |
| Document | Requires signed release on the ITP | Requires signed attendance or "no-attendance" waiver |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice] [CO4, CO5, K4 – Analyse]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step process of *Quality in Planning* in a shipyard, from contract award to the release of the first production activity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During planning for erection of a forward double-bottom block, the Design FMEA identifies a failure mode with **S = 9, O = 6, D = 5**. After implementing a planned countermeasure (WPS revision and 100 % MPI), the new ratings are **S = 9, O = 2, D = 2**. Calculate the percentage reduction in RPN and comment on whether the planning intervention is acceptable as per shipyard policy (threshold: ≥ 75 %).

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Refer to the flow diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The eight sequential steps are:

1. **Contract & Classification Review** — Decode specs, identify Class Society (e.g., DNV), IACS unified requirements.
2. **Define Quality Objectives** — SMART objectives per **ISO 9001 Cl. 6.2** (e.g., *achieve FPY ≥ 92 % on all blocks*).
3. **Develop PQP** — The master document. Includes org chart, audit schedule, document control procedure.
4. **Perform Design FMEA** — Identify failure modes *before* drawings are released; compute RPN.
5. **Develop WPS / Process Procedures** — Qualify per ASME IX; include NDT scope.
6. **Build ITP** — Mark Hold (H), Witness (W), Surveillance (S) for every activity; specify acceptance criteria.
7. **Plan Resources** — Qualify welders (ISO 9606-1), NDT inspectors (ISO 9712), calibrate equipment (ISO 17025).
8. **Set CoQ Budget and Document Matrix** — Allocate ~3–5 % of contract value to QA; define the deliverables list.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute baseline RPN:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{baseline}} = S \times O \times D
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{baseline}} = 9 \times 6 \times 5 = 270
$$

**Step 2 — Compute RPN after the planned countermeasure:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage reduction:**

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = \frac{270 - 36}{270} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} = \frac{234}{270} \times 100\% \approx 86.67\%
$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**

Since $\Delta\text{RPN} \approx 86.67\%$, which is greater than the shipyard policy threshold of **75 %**, the planning intervention is **acceptable**. The WPS revision combined with 100 % MPI is recommended for incorporation into the PQP and ITP before block erection release.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure and contents of a **Project Quality Plan (PQP)** as per **ISO 10005**. How does it differ from the yard's generic **Quality Manual**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard's quality planning team has identified **120 defects** in **8,000 units** of pipe spools, with each unit having **5 opportunities** for a defect. Compute the DPMO and the corresponding **process sigma level** (using the 1.5σ shift). Comment on whether the planning target of **4.5σ** is achieved.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Structure of a PQP (ISO 10005):**

A PQP is a **project-specific** document. Its required contents (per ISO 10005 Cl. 5) are:

1. **Scope of the PQP** — which contract, which vessel, which scope of work.
2. **Quality objectives** — measurable, project-specific targets.
3. **Inputs from the Quality Manual** — reference to the yard's overarching policies.
4. **Processes and procedures** — specific WPS, NDT, and inspection procedures.
5. **Resources and responsibilities** — named QA personnel, reporting lines.
6. **Records to be maintained** — list of records with retention period (typically 5 years for Class).
7. **Review and approval** — sign-off by Project Manager and QA Head.

**Difference from Quality Manual:**

| Aspect | Quality Manual | Project Quality Plan |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Entire yard / organization | One specific contract / vessel |
| Level | Strategic / policy | Tactical / execution |
| ISO clause | ISO 9001 Cl. 4.3 | ISO 10005 Cl. 5 |
| Audience | Top management, auditors | Project team, Class, Owner |
| Frequency of revision | Annually or on major change | Per project / per contract |

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute total opportunities:**

$$
\text{Total opportunities} = 8{,}000 \times 5 = 40{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{120 \times 10^{6}}{40{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 10^{4}} = 3{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute process sigma level:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{3{,}000}{10^{6}}\right) + 1.5
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} = \Phi^{-1}(0.997) + 1.5
$$

Using the standard normal table, $\Phi^{-1}(0.997) \approx 2.75$. Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{\text{level}} \approx 2.75 + 1.5 = 4.25\,\sigma
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with target:**

Since the achieved sigma level of **4.25σ** is **less than** the planning target of **4.5σ**, the **target is NOT achieved**. The planning team must escalate this to the Risk Register and plan additional preventive actions (e.g., supplier quality audit, pipe-fitting operator re-training) before production release.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (as observed in KTU Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Quality Manual with PQP** — Examiners consistently deduct 2 marks when students treat these as synonymous. Remember: *Manual = yard-wide; PQP = project-specific.*
> 2. **Skipping the 1.5σ shift** — In DPMO-to-Sigma questions, forgetting the 1.5σ long-term drift correction loses **2 marks** and gives a wrong sigma value.
> 3. **Misclassifying Hold vs. Witness points** — Writing *"Witness = must attend"* is a common 1-mark error. Witness = *may* attend; the activity *may* proceed in their absence.
> 4. **No flow diagram in Part B** — For 7-mark "process explanation" questions, omitting a labeled flowchart costs a **minimum of 2 marks** as per KTU's structured-answer marking scheme.
> 5. **Forgetting the Class Society linkage** — Any Quality in Planning answer that does NOT mention IACS / Class Society loses a mark in the CO4 assessment rubric.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality in Planning = Prevention over Inspection** — the cheapest defect is the one that never occurs.
- **Master document = PQP**, supported by ITP, WPS, FMEA, Calibration Schedule, and Document Control Matrix.
- **Hold (H), Witness (W), Surveillance (S)** are the three ITP markings; H stops work, W is optional, S is observation only.
- **IACS + ISO 9001 + Class Society Rules** form the regulatory trinity — name at least two in every answer.
- **DPMO → Sigma level formula** must include the **+1.5σ long-term shift**.
- **RPN reduction ≥ 75 %** is the conventional threshold for an "effective" planning countermeasure.
- **Feedback loop** from execution back into planning is the *Plan-Do-Check-Act* engine of continuous improvement.
- **Cost of Quality** is budgeted at planning; typical shipyard target is **3–5 % of contract value**.
- **Welder qualification (ISO 9606-1) and NDT inspector certification (ISO 9712)** are *planning* activities, not execution activities.
- **Document Control Matrix** is the audit defence — without it, traceability from raw material to Class deliverable cannot be proven.
- **FMEA Severity (S) is fixed** by the failure consequence; only **O** and **D** can be reduced by planning.
- **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the typical process-capability target for plate-cutting and pipe-bending in shipbuilding planning.
- **AQL sampling (ISO 2859-1)** applies to incoming materials and is selected at the planning stage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Quality Assurance (QA) in Shipbuilding**, **Design and Operation** refers to the integrated, lifecycle-oriented framework of policies, procedures, controls, and verification activities applied during the *conceptual design, functional design, detailed design, construction support*, and *in-service operational* phases of a marine vessel. It ensures that the vessel meets specified *classification society rules, statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL), owner's requirements, and contractual quality standards* (ISO 9001:2015, IACS QSCS), while optimizing safety, performance, buildability, and cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:**
> *Design and Operation* in shipbuilding QA is the systematic application of quality management principles across two principal phases:
> **(i) Design Phase** – ensuring that design outputs satisfy inputs (traceability), regulatory requirements, and classification rules through structured design reviews, verification, validation, and risk assessment.
> **(ii) Operational Phase** – ensuring that the as-built vessel performs to design intent through commissioning, sea trials, in-service surveys, and continual improvement under ISO 9001 Clause 8 (Operation) and Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Think of designing and operating a ship like **building and then driving a custom Formula-1 race car**:

- **Design** is the engineering office where every nut, bolt, aerodynamic surface, and engine map is carefully calculated, simulated, peer-reviewed, and signed off before a single piece of metal is cut. A single oversight (e.g., under-rated suspension) can be fatal.
- **Operation** is the pit crew, the driver, the telemetry, and the post-race engineers. They check that what was designed *actually works* under real track conditions, log defects, feed back lessons learned, and refine the next design.

In the same way, shipbuilders must **never let the design "hit the water" without rigorous quality gates**, and must **never let the operational data "die in the bridge logbook"** — it must loop back into the next vessel's design.

## 1.3 Core Concepts at a Glance

| **Pillar** | **Design** | **Operation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Output** | Approved drawings, specs, calculations | Built vessel, sea trial records, in-service data |
| **Key Activity** | Verification, Validation, Design Review | Commissioning, Testing, Monitoring |
| **Key Standard** | ISO 9001 Cl. 8.3 (Design & Development) | ISO 9001 Cl. 8.1, 8.5.1, 9.1 |
| **Risk Tool** | FMEA, HAZOP, Design Margin Analysis | FMEA, Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) |
| **Owner** | Design Office / Naval Architect | Shipyard Production + Shipowner Crew |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Key Performance Indicator (KPI):** A well-run QA-integrated shipyard typically targets a **First-Time-Right (FTR) design rate ≥ 85%** and a **Punch-list closure within 30 days of delivery** as standard benchmarks.

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Classification Society Rules:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas (BV), Indian Register of Shipping (IRS), ClassNK
- **Statutory Codes:** SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, COLREG, MLC 2006, IGF Code (for gas-fueled ships)
- **Quality Standard:** **ISO 9001:2015** (universal), **IACS QSCS** (Quality System Certification Scheme) for shipyards
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN):** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ where **S** = Severity, **O** = Occurrence, **D** = Detection
- **Design Margin:** Typically **10–20%** beyond rule minimums for fatigue and ultimate strength, depending on vessel class
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Phase — Structured Quality Approach

### 2.1.1 Design as per ISO 9001:2015 — Clause 8.3

The clause mandates a **stage-gate process** with the following sequential elements:

1. **Design Planning (8.3.2):** Define activities, responsibilities, reviews, verification & validation methods, and resources.
2. **Design Inputs (8.3.3):** Functional & performance requirements, applicable statutory/regulatory requirements, results from similar designs.
3. **Design Controls (8.3.5):** Reviews → Verification → Validation (in that order).
4. **Design Outputs (8.3.6):** Drawings, specifications, calculation reports, material lists, test procedures.
5. **Design Changes (8.3.9):** Controlled change management with re-verification/re-validation.

### 2.1.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why-How" Breakdown

| **Step** | **Why** | **How** |
|---|---|---|
| Planning | Prevents scope creep; assigns clear ownership | Project Quality Plan (PQP), Design Programme |
| Inputs | Locks down the "what" before the "how" | Owner's Specification, Class Rules, Flag State requirements |
| Reviews | Catches errors early when change is cheap | Concept Review → Preliminary → Critical → Final |
| Verification | Confirms outputs meet inputs | Calculation checks, FE analysis, rule compliance |
| Validation | Confirms the *ship* meets *user's needs* | Model tests, sea trials, in-service feedback |
| Changes | Prevents uncontrolled "drift" | Engineering Change Request (ECR) / Engineering Change Order (ECO) |

## 2.2 The Operational Phase — Structured Quality Approach

### 2.2.1 ISO 9001:2015 — Clause 8 (Operation)

Operation covers **everything from steel cutting to vessel delivery and beyond**:

- **8.1 Operational Planning & Control** → Production Plan, Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS), Inspection & Test Plans (ITP)
