- **8.5.1 Control of Production** → Controlled work environment, calibrated equipment, traceability (steel heat number → plate → block → hull)
- **8.7 Control of Nonconforming Outputs** → NCR, Rework, Repair, Concession, Scrap
- **9.1 Monitoring & Measurement** → KPIs, internal audits, management review
- **10.2 Nonconformity & Corrective Action** → Root Cause Analysis (8D, 5-Why, Fishbone)

### 2.2.2 Key Operational Quality Gates (Shipyard Sequence)

$$
\text{Steel Cutting} \rightarrow \text{Block Fabrication} \rightarrow \text{Hull Assembly} \rightarrow \text{Erection} \rightarrow \text{Outfitting} \rightarrow \text{Launch} \rightarrow \text{Fitting-Out} \rightarrow \text{Sea Trials} \rightarrow \text{Delivery}
$$

Each gate is preceded by a **Hold Point (H)** and **Witness Point (W)** in the ITP.

## 2.3 Risk-Based Design Thinking

Quality in design is fundamentally about **eliminating or mitigating risk before construction**. The most widely used tool is **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**.

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Severity** (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic loss of life/vessel)
- $O$ = **Occurrence** (1 = very rare, 10 = very frequent)
- $D$ = **Detection difficulty** (1 = almost certain, 10 = impossible to detect pre-failure)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *SOD* — *Severity* × *Occurrence* × *Detection*. Critical design items should target **RPN < 100** after mitigation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | dimensionless (1–1000) | FMEA ranking |
| Design Margin | $M_d = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{allowable}} - \sigma_{\text{applied}}}{\sigma_{\text{applied}}} \times 100\%$ | **10–20%** | Safety factor verification |
| First-Time-Right Rate | $\text{FTR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{first-time accepted}}}{N_{\text{total items}}} \times 100\%$ | **≥ 85%** yard target | Design effectiveness KPI |
| Quality Cost Ratio (CoPQ) | $\text{CoPQ} = \dfrac{C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}}}{C_{\text{failure}}}$ | $C_{\text{failure}}$ dominates | Cost-of-quality trade-off |
| Schedule Slip | $\Delta t = t_{\text{actual}} - t_{\text{planned}}$ | days / weeks | Operational monitoring |
| Defect Density | $\rho_d = \dfrac{N_{\text{defects}}}{N_{\text{tons of steel}}}$ | defects / 1000 t | Production QA KPI |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | currency / vessel | Quality cost reporting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **RPN formula** with the correct interpretation of S, O, D. Confusing *Detection* with *Occurrence* is one of the most common 2-mark errors in KTU Part A.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Risk-based design** prevents catastrophic failures such as hull girder collapse (e.g., historical MOL Comfort, 2013) by enforcing margin checks.
- **Design reviews** catch ~70% of errors before construction — far cheaper than cutting steel twice.
- **Operational QA via sea trials** validates the integrated system (propulsion, navigation, safety) before the owner accepts the vessel.
- **Feedback loops** from in-service defects (e.g., propeller cavitation, coating breakdown) feed **Design for Reliability (DfR)** into the next ship class.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN) on a Shipboard Fuel Oil Purifier

> **Scenario:** A shipboard fuel oil purifier is being assessed during detailed design. The QA team has identified 3 potential failure modes. Compute the RPN for each and rank them.

### Given Data

| **Failure Mode** | **Severity (S)** | **Occurrence (O)** | **Detection (D)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| FM-1: Bowl cracked, separator down | 8 | 4 | 3 |
| FM-2: Gear oil leak, fire risk | 9 | 3 | 5 |
| FM-3: Sludge discharge blocked, low throughput | 5 | 6 | 4 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Apply the FMEA formula**

$$
\text{RPN}_i = S_i \times O_i \times D_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute RPN for FM-1**

$$
\text{RPN}_1 = 8 \times 4 \times 3
$$

Multiplying left to right:

$$
8 \times 4 = 32
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_1 = 32 \times 3 = \mathbf{96}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute RPN for FM-2**

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = 9 \times 3 \times 5
$$

$$
9 \times 3 = 27
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = 27 \times 5 = \mathbf{135}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute RPN for FM-3**

$$
\text{RPN}_3 = 5 \times 6 \times 4
$$

$$
5 \times 6 = 30
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_3 = 30 \times 4 = \mathbf{120}
$$

**Step 5 — Rank the failure modes (highest risk first)**

$$
\text{FM-2 (RPN = 135)} > \text{FM-3 (RPN = 120)} > \text{FM-1 (RPN = 96)}
$$

**Step 6 — Mitigation recommendation**

For FM-2, the *high severity* and *difficult detection* dominate. Recommended actions: add a **vibration sensor (lower D)**, **leak detection probe**, and **routine 5-year gear inspection (lower O)**. Target post-mitigation RPN:

$$
\text{RPN}_{2,\text{new}} = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36 \quad (\text{Acceptable, } < 100)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Multiplication is associative, but KTU valuation prefers *left-to-right sequencing* in textual explanation so the examiner can follow the arithmetic line-by-line.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) for a Block Fabrication Phase

> **Scenario:** A shipyard's block fabrication stage for a bulk carrier reported the following quality costs in one quarter:
> - Prevention (training, design reviews): **₹12,00,000**
> - Appraisal (NDT, inspection): **₹18,00,000**
> - Internal failure (rework, scrap): **₹45,00,000**
> - External failure (warranty, sea-trial defects): **₹25,00,000**

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Identify the components**

$$
C_P = 12{,}00{,}000, \quad C_A = 18{,}00{,}000, \quad C_{IF} = 45{,}00{,}000, \quad C_{EF} = 25{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Prevention + Appraisal (conformance cost)**

$$
C_{\text{conform}} = C_P + C_A
$$

$$
C_{\text{conform}} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{30{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Internal + External Failure (non-conformance cost)**

$$
C_{\text{fail}} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

$$
C_{\text{fail}} = 45{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{70{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total COPQ**

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{conform}} + C_{\text{fail}}
$$

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{total}} = 30{,}00{,}000 + 70{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Failure-to-Conformance ratio**

$$
R_{f/c} = \dfrac{C_{\text{fail}}}{C_{\text{conform}}}
$$

$$
R_{f/c} = \dfrac{70{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{2.33}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**

A ratio of **2.33** means the yard is spending **2.33× more on fixing failures than on preventing them**. Industry benchmark (Juran) suggests the ratio should ideally be **< 1.0**. Recommended action: **invest more in prevention (C_P)** to drive C_fail down — typically every ₹1 extra in prevention saves ₹3–10 in failure cost.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When the question asks for *COPQ*, show the **breakdown of conformance vs. non-conformance**. Most students lose 2 marks for skipping the categorization.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Design Review Levels

| **Review Level** | **Stage** | **Participants** | **Output** | **QA Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R0 — Concept Review** | After feasibility study | Owner, Design Mgr | Approved concept | Locks down *what* is to be built |
| **R1 — Preliminary Review** | ~30% design | Owner, Class, Design | Approved general arrangement | Locks down *where* things go |
| **R2 — Critical Review** | ~60% design | All stakeholders | Approved structural details | Locks down *how* structure works |
| **R3 — Final Review** | 100% design | Design + Yard + Class | Issued-for-construction (IFC) | Locks down *as-built* intent |
| **R4 — Post-Delivery Review** | 6–12 months in service | Owner + Yard | Lessons learned | Closes feedback loop to next class |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Design Phase V-Model for Shipbuilding QA

This is the canonical **V-Model** showing the design-build-verify symmetry that shipyards follow. Each design level on the left has a corresponding verification activity on the right.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Owner Requirements] --> B[Concept Design]
    B --> C[Preliminary Design]
    C --> D[Contract Design]
    D --> E[Functional Design]
    E --> F[Detailed Design IFC]
    F --> G[Production / Construction]
    G --> H[Block Fabrication]
    H --> I[Hull Assembly]
    I --> J[Outfitting]
    J --> K[Launch]
    K --> L[Sea Trials]
    L --> M[Delivery to Owner]
    M --> N[In-Service Operation]
    N --> O[Feedback to Next Class]

    A -.verification.-> REV1
    B -.verification.-> REV2
    C -.verification.-> REV3
    D -.verification.-> REV4
    E -.verification.-> REV5
    F -.verification.-> REV6
    G -.verification.-> REV7
    H -.verification.-> REV8
    I -.verification.-> REV9
    J -.verification.-> REV10
    K -.verification.-> REV11
    L -.verification.-> REV12
    M -.verification.-> REV13

    REV1[Design Review R0]
    REV2[Design Review R1]
    REV3[Design Review R2]
    REV4[Design Review R3]
    REV5[Rule Compliance Check]
    REV6[Drawing Approval by Class]
    REV7[ITP Hold Points]
    REV8[NDT and Dimensional Checks]
    REV9[Hull Alignment Survey]
    REV10[System Integration Tests]
    REV11[Dock and Basin Trials]
    REV12[Speed Manoeuvrability Endurance]
    REV13[Owner Acceptance]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Operational QA Control Loop (Plan-Do-Check-Act)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PLAN: Quality Plan and ITP] --> D1[DO: Execute Production]
    D1 --> C1[CHECK: Inspect and Test]
    C1 --> A1[ACT: Corrective Action or Accept]
    A1 --> P1
    C1 --> NCR[Non-Conformance Report Issued]
    NCR --> RCA[Root Cause Analysis 5-Why]
    RCA --> CAPA[Corrective and Preventive Action]
    CAPA --> P1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Risk-Based Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Design Input Received] --> IDENT[Identify Potential Failure Modes]
    IDENT --> SCORE[Score S O D for Each Mode]
    SCORE --> CALC[Compute RPN]
    CALC --> DECIDE{RPN Greater Than 100?}
    DECIDE -->|YES| MITIGATE[Apply Mitigation Lower S O D]
    DECIDE -->|NO| ACCEPT[Acceptable Risk Log to Risk Register]
    MITIGATE --> RESCORE[Re-compute RPN]
    RESCORE --> DECIDE
    ACCEPT --> CLOSE[Document in FMEA Register]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The decision threshold of **RPN > 100** is the most common yard-internal convention; some classification societies use a stricter threshold of **75** for safety-critical items (e.g., emergency generators, fire pumps).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Design Review" as applied to shipbuilding quality assurance. List any TWO design review levels commonly followed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A *Design Review* is a **systematic, documented examination of design outputs** at defined stages to evaluate their adequacy, identify problems, and propose corrective actions, ensuring the design meets input requirements, classification rules, and statutory regulations as per ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.3.5.
- **Two Levels (1 mark):** *(any two)*
  - R1 — Preliminary Design Review
  - R2 — Critical Design Review
  - R3 — Final Design Review (IFC approval)
  - R0 — Concept Review

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Write the formula for Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA and explain the term "Detection".**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Formula (1 mark):** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$
- **Severity (S) (1 mark):** The *consequence* of failure on system, personnel, or environment (1 = minor, 10 = catastrophic loss of life/vessel).
- **Occurrence (O) (1 mark – partial credit):** The *likelihood* of the failure happening within the design life.
- **Detection (D) (1 mark):** The *relative difficulty* of detecting the failure **before** it reaches the customer, using current design controls. A score of **1** = almost certain detection, **10** = impossible to detect.

> *(Examiner note: ask any two of S/O/D for full 2 marks. Detection is often mis-defined as "likelihood of failure" — that is Occurrence.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, with choice. Each question typically has **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**, sometimes with sub-parts (i)/(ii).

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **design and development process as per ISO 9001:2015 (Clause 8.3)**, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the purpose of **design verification** and **design validation** with a shipbuilding example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipboard **main engine lubrication oil system** is being analysed using FMEA. The QA team records the following data for three failure modes:

| FM | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| FM-A: L.O. pump failure, blackout | 9 | 3 | 4 |
| FM-B: L.O. cooler fouling, high temp | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| FM-C: L.O. tank low level, scavenge fire | 10 | 2 | 6 |

Compute the **RPN for each failure mode**, rank them, and recommend mitigation actions for the highest-RPN mode. **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Answer — (a) 7 marks

1. **ISO 9001:2015 Cl. 8.3 Stages (3 marks):**
   - **8.3.1 General** – The organization shall establish, implement, and maintain a design and development process.
   - **8.3.2 Planning** – Define stages, reviews, verification, validation, responsibilities, resources.
   - **8.3.3 Inputs** – Functional requirements, statutory & regulatory requirements, information from previous designs.
   - **8.3.5 Controls** – Reviews, **Verification**, **Validation**.
   - **8.3.6 Outputs** – Drawings, specifications, calculation reports, material lists, test procedures.
   - **8.3.9 Design Changes** – Controlled review and re-verification.

2. **Verification vs. Validation (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Question asked | "Are we building the product **right**?" | "Are we building the **right** product?" |
| When | After each design stage | At the end of design, on the product |
| Method | Calculation check, FE analysis, rule check | Model tests, sea trials, in-service trials |
| Shipbuilding example | Verifying hull girder bending stress against IACS S21 rules | Confirming the ship actually achieves 14-knot service speed in sea trials |

3. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** *Refer SECTION 4.1 Mermaid V-Model — full marks for a labelled left-right V-Model showing design on left and verification/validation on right.*

#### 📘 Model Answer — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 — Apply RPN formula (1 mark):** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$

**Step 2 — Compute FM-A (1 mark):**

$$
\text{RPN}_A = 9 \times 3 \times 4 = \mathbf{108}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute FM-B (1 mark):**

$$
\text{RPN}_B = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = \mathbf{210}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute FM-C (1 mark):**

$$
\text{RPN}_C = 10 \times 2 \times 6 = \mathbf{120}
$$

**Step 5 — Ranking (1 mark):**

$$
\text{FM-B (210)} > \text{FM-C (120)} > \text{FM-A (108)}
$$

**Step 6 — Mitigation for FM-B (2 marks):**
- **Lower O (occurrence):** Schedule quarterly cooler cleaning during routine docking; install automatic back-flush system.
- **Lower D (detection):** Fit a **temperature differential sensor** across the cooler with alarm at 10°C rise; include trend monitoring in IAS (Integrated Automation System).
- **Lower S (severity):** Add a **stand-by pump** with auto-changeover to ensure continuity.

**Post-mitigation RPN target:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{B,\text{new}} = 7 \times 3 \times 2 = 42 \quad (\text{Acceptable})
$$

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **operational phase quality assurance** activities in a shipyard from **block fabrication to sea trials**. Highlight the role of the **Inspection & Test Plan (ITP)** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard incurred the following **quality costs in a quarter**:
- Prevention: **₹15,00,000**
- Appraisal: **₹20,00,000**
- Internal Failure: **₹60,00,000**
- External Failure: **₹30,00,000**

Calculate the **total Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**, the **conformance-to-non-conformance ratio**, and comment on what management should do. **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Answer — (a) 7 marks

1. **Block Fabrication QA (1.5 marks):**
   - Material traceability (heat number, certificate 3.1/3.2).
   - Welding procedure qualification (WPS, PQR, Welder Qualification).
   - Dimensional control of panels and sub-assemblies.
   - NDT (UT, RT, MPI, DPI) at hold points.

2. **Hull Assembly & Erection QA (1.5 marks):**
   - Hull alignment survey (laser, total station).
   - Block-to-block joint inspection before welding.
   - Penetration tests for oil/water-tight boundaries.
   - Class surveyor attendance at key hold points.

3. **Outfitting QA (1 mark):**
   - Pipe pressure tests (hydrostatic, pneumatic).
   - Cable continuity and insulation tests.
   - Equipment maker's shop tests witnessed by yard QA.

4. **Pre-Commissioning & Sea Trials QA (1.5 marks):**
   - Dock trial (machinery, steering, emergency systems).
   - Basin trial (manoeuvring, anchoring).
   - Sea trial (speed, fuel consumption, vibration, noise, lifesaving appliances).

5. **Role of ITP with examples (1.5 marks):**
   - ITP is a **matrix of activities, hold points (H), witness points (W), and acceptance criteria**.
   - **Example 1:** For a shell plate butt weld → WPS review (W) → Visual (H) → 10% RT (H) → Final visual (H).
   - **Example 2:** For main engine trial → Pre-trial checklist (H) → Dock trial 4h (H) → Sea trial 8h (H) → Performance curve acceptance (H).

#### 📘 Model Answer — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify components (1 mark):**

$$
C_P = 15L, \quad C_A = 20L, \quad C_{IF} = 60L, \quad C_{EF} = 30L
$$

**Step 2 — Conformance cost (1 mark):**

$$
C_{\text{conform}} = C_P + C_A = 15L + 20L = \mathbf{35L}
$$

**Step 3 — Non-conformance cost (1 mark):**

$$
C_{\text{fail}} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 60L + 30L = \mathbf{90L}
$$

**Step 4 — Total COPQ (1 mark):**

$$
\text{COPQ} = 35L + 90L = \mathbf{1{,}25{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}}
$$

**Step 5 — Conformance / Non-conformance ratio (1 mark):**

$$
R_{c/f} = \dfrac{C_{\text{conform}}}{C_{\text{fail}}} = \dfrac{35L}{90L} = \mathbf{0.39}
$$

(Or equivalently $R_{f/c} = 90/35 = 2.57$, meaning failures cost **2.57×** what prevention does.)

**Step 6 — Management comment (2 marks):**
- The yard is *over-spending on failure costs* and *under-spending on prevention*. Industry best practice: spend **2–3× more on prevention+appraisal** than on failure.
- **Action items:** increase training budget (C_P ↑), invest in 3D laser scanning and FEA-driven tolerance control (C_P ↑), automate NDT to reduce human-error rework (C_A & C_IF ↓).
- Expected outcome: for every **₹1 increase in C_P**, an estimated **₹3 to ₹10 decrease in C_fail** (Juran's rule).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Verification and Validation:** Verification = "build the product right"; Validation = "build the right product." Swapping them costs **2–3 marks** directly.
> 2. **Wrong RPN scale:** RPN ranges from **1 to 1000**, not 1 to 100. Students often cap at 100 and lose a mark.
> 3. **Skipping block diagram:** Part (a) questions carry an explicit **1-mark for a neat diagram**. Drawing only text forfeits that mark.
> 4. **Conflating COPQ with total quality cost:** COPQ = **Prevention + Appraisal + Failure** (i.e., cost of conformance + cost of non-conformance), NOT just the failure cost. Defining COPQ only as failure costs loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **No units in the final answer:** Always write the unit (₹, $, days, RPN) — KTU examiners are instructed to deduct **0.5 mark** for missing units in numerical answers.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Design and Operation = ISO 9001 Cl. 8.3 + Cl. 8.1–8.5 + Cl. 9.1** — the spine of shipbuilding QA.
- **The V-Model is the standard mental image** — design on the left, verification/validation on the right, construction at the bottom apex.
- **RPN = S × O × D** — rank failure modes; target RPN < 100 (or < 75 for safety-critical).
- **Verification** checks "build it right"; **Validation** checks "build the right ship."
- **ITP** is the operational QA backbone — every activity has a Hold (H) or Witness (W) point.
- **Design reviews** are R0 (Concept) → R1 (Preliminary) → R2 (Critical) → R3 (Final / IFC) → R4 (Post-delivery lessons learned).
- **COPQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Failure** — healthy yards minimize failure cost relative to prevention.
- **Configuration Management** (ECR/ECO) prevents design drift after IFC.
- **Feedback loop** from in-service defects is non-negotiable for continual improvement (Cl. 10.3).
- **Common 14-mark structure:** (a) Conceptual theory (3) + Diagram (1) + Example (3) / (b) Computation with formula (1) + Step-by-step (4) + Engineering interpretation (2).
- **Always show units, always show the formula, always end with an interpretation sentence** — KTU examiners are explicitly trained to reward this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Policy of Shipyards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST862 - QUALITY ASSURANCE IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Quality Policy of Shipyards # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Policy (ISO 9001:2015 Definition):** A Quality Policy is a formal statement issued by top management that expresses the organization's overall intentions and direction with respect to quality, formally aligned with the **Quality Management System (QMS)** requirements of **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2** and the **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**. In the context of **shipbuilding**, a Quality Policy of a Shipyard is a **top-management-authored, documented, and communicated declaration** that outlines the shipyard's commitment to: 1. Meeting **classification society standards** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK, RINA). 2. Complying with **international maritime regulations** (SOLAS, MARPOL, LOAD LINES, COLREG). 3. Satisfying **customer (shipowner) contractual specifications**. 4. Continually improving the effectiveness of the **Quality Management System (QMS)**. 5. Providing a framework for establishing and reviewing **quality objectives**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Regulatory Anchor:** A shipyard's Quality Policy is the *statutory gateway* through which the shipbuilder demonstrates its **fitness for classification** and **fitness for purpose** before any steel is cut. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a shipyard's Quality Policy as the **"Constitution of the Shipyard."** Just as a nation's constitution sets the supreme guiding principles (without listing every traffic rule), the Quality Policy sets the supreme guiding principles of quality — without listing every welding procedure or paint test. **Real-world analogy — Building a House:** - The **Quality Policy** is the homeowner's written promise: *"We shall build a house that does not leak, lasts 50 years, and meets the city building code."* - The **Quality Manual** is the architect's blueprint of the house itself. - The **Quality Procedures (SOPs)** are the plumber's and electrician's step-by-step work instructions. - The **Quality Records** are the inspection tickets, test reports, and completion certificates. If the shipowner asks: *"Why should I trust your shipyard?"* — the Quality Policy is the **first formal document** the yard presents. --- ## 1.3 Highlighted Standard Metrics and Constants > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Framework Metrics** in a Shipyard Quality Policy: > - **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** measured on a **1–10 scale** or **% satisfaction**. > - **First-Time-Right (FTR) Ratio** in steel fabrication (Target ≥ **95%**). > - **Rework Cost Ratio (RCR)** expressed as % of total project cost (Target ≤ **2%**). > - **Non-Conformance Report (NCR) Closure Time** measured in working days. > - **Audit Non-Conformity (NC) Closure Cycle** within **90 days** (IACS standard). > - **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %** of vessels against contracted delivery date. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hard-coded International Standard:** Any shipyard seeking **IACS QSCS certification** must demonstrate a documented Quality Policy as a **prerequisite**, not an option. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Shipyard Quality Policy A compliant shipyard Quality Policy must contain **six mandatory pillars** as per ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2 and IACS UR Z17: | Pillar | Component | Engineering Significance in Shipbuilding | |:------:|:---------|:----------------------------------------| | **P1** | Top Management Commitment | Signed by CEO / MD / President of the shipyard | | **P2** | Customer & Regulatory Focus | Covers SOLAS, MARPOL, Class Rules, Flag State | | **P3** | Quality Objectives Framework | Linked to KPI dashboard (OTD, FTR, NCR) | | **P4** | Communication & Awareness | Displayed in all workshops, onboard vessels, intranet | | **P5** | Continual Improvement | PDCA cycle, corrective action system (CAR/PAR) | | **P6** | Periodic Review | Annual management review meeting (MRM) | --- ## 2.2 Hierarchical Placement in the QMS Pyramid The Quality Policy sits at the **apex of the QMS documentation pyramid**: $$ \text{Quality Policy} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Quality Manual} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Procedures (SOPs)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Work Instructions} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Quality Records} $$ **Logical Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. **Step 1 — Strategic Declaration:** Top management (CEO, Board of Directors) drafts the Quality Policy aligned with the shipyard's *business vision* (e.g., *"To be the world's most reliable shipbuilder of LNG carriers"*). 2. **Step 2 — Regulatory Mapping:** The policy is cross-referenced against **IACS Unified Requirements (UR)**, **ISO 9001:2015**, and **flag state administrations** (e.g., Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands). 3. **Step 3 — Objective Cascade:** High-level objectives (e.g., *"Zero major non-conformities in next audit"*) are cascaded into departmental KPIs (e.g., Hull Shop, Outfitting Shop, Paint Shop, Electrical, Machinery). 4. **Step 4 — Communication:** The policy is communicated at **induction training**, posted in English + local language on shop-floor notice boards, and uploaded to the yard's intranet/ERP. 5. **Step 5 — Review Cycle:** Reviewed at least **once per year** during the Management Review Meeting (MRM) or whenever there is a change in classification rules / major customer requirements. 6. **Step 6 — External Verification:** During surveillance audits by certification bodies (DNV, LR, BV), the auditor checks that the policy is **implemented, understood, and maintained** at the functional level. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **No pure mathematical formula applies here, but the following derived indices are calculated and reported under the Quality Policy framework:** | S.No. | Index / Metric | Formula | Target | Reporting Frequency | |:-----:|:--------------|:--------|:-------|:-------------------| | **1** | First-Time-Right (FTR) Ratio | $FTR = \dfrac{N_{accepted}}{N_{inspected}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 95\%$ | Weekly | | **2** | Rework Cost Ratio (RCR) | $RCR = \dfrac{C_{rework}}{C_{total\,project}} \times 100\%$ | $\leq 2\%$ | Monthly | | **3** | Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum (R_{i} \times W_{i})}{\sum W_{i}}$ where $R_i$ = rating, $W_i$ = weight | $\geq 8.5 / 10$ | Per vessel delivery | | **4** | NCR Closure Efficiency | $NCE = \dfrac{NCR_{closed\,<\,90d}}{NCR_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 98\%$ | Monthly | | **5** | On-Time Delivery (OTD) | $OTD = \dfrac{V_{delivered\,on\,time}}{V_{total\,contracted}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 90\%$ | Per vessel | | **6** | Audit Non-Conformity Density | $ANCD = \dfrac{NC_{count}}{Audit\,hours}$ | $\leq 0.5$ NC / audit day | Per audit | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These metrics translate the abstract language of the Quality Policy into **measurable, auditable, and contractually defensible** Key Performance Indicators. Shipowners and classification societies increasingly demand these metrics as **pre-qualification criteria** for awarding newbuilding contracts. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in the Shipbuilding Industry 1. **Tender Qualification:** Major shipowners (Maersk, MSC, BW LNG, Shell) require a copy of the Quality Policy along with ISO 9001 and IACS QSCS certificates at the Request for Proposal (RFP) stage. 2. **Flag State Inspection:** Port State Control officers and flag administration surveyors may request the Quality Policy during **initial safety construction surveys**. 3. **Litigation Defence:** In the event of an insurance claim (e.g., hull loss, machinery failure), the Quality Policy is used as **legal evidence** that the shipbuilder maintained due diligence. 4. **Continuous Improvement (PDCA):** The policy itself is the **Plan** phase of the Deming cycle — feeding the **Do** (implementation), **Check** (audit), **Act** (review and update). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Document Architecture ## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Construction of a Shipyard Quality Policy Because this is a **policy/management topic** (not a numerical derivation), the "exhaustive derivation" is replaced with a **complete document-construction walkthrough** that mirrors how a KTU examiner would evaluate a 14-mark descriptive answer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A complete 14-mark answer on Quality Policy must include — Definition → Regulatory basis → Mandatory contents → Communication → Review → Practical shipyard example. Skipping any of these = -3 to -5 marks. ### Step 1 — Header Section **Content:** Shipyard logo, document number (e.g., *HYU-QP-001 Rev. 05*), issue date, effective date, page number, classification (e.g., *Unrestricted / Internal*). **Why:** Establishes **document control** under ISO 9001 Clause 7.5. ### Step 2 — Top Management Endorsement Block **Content:** Signature of the **CEO / Managing Director / President**, with date of approval. **Why:** Demonstrates **Clause 5.1 Leadership & Commitment** of ISO 9001:2015. ### Step 3 — Vision & Mission Alignment **Content:** One-line statement showing how the Quality Policy supports the yard's vision (e.g., *"To be the global No. 1 in eco-friendly shipbuilding"*). **Why:** Provides **strategic context** — the policy is not a free-floating document. ### Step 4 — Customer & Regulatory Commitment Clause **Content:** Explicit mention of: - Compliance with **customer specifications** and **classification rules** (e.g., *"We shall construct all vessels in accordance with Lloyd's Register Rules and Regulations and the requirements of the flag Administration."*). - Compliance with **international conventions** (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC 2006, COLREG). **Why:** This is the **legally binding core** of the policy. ### Step 5 — Quality Objectives Framework Statement **Content:** Reference to measurable objectives (e.g., *On-Time Delivery ≥ 90%*, *Customer Satisfaction ≥ 8.5/10*). **Why:** Establishes the **Clauses 6.2 and 9.3 alignment** of ISO 9001:2015. ### Step 6 — Continual Improvement Clause **Content:** Statement such as *"The Company is committed to the continual improvement of the QMS through the application of the PDCA cycle, root-cause analysis, and corrective/preventive actions."* **Why:** Mandatory for **Clause 10.1 Continual Improvement**. ### Step 7 — Communication & Awareness Clause **Content:** Statement that the policy is communicated to all employees, subcontractors, and is available to interested parties upon request. **Why:** Mandatory for **Clause 5.2 (c)**. ### Step 8 — Review Clause **Content:** *"This policy shall be reviewed annually during the Management Review Meeting and updated as necessary to ensure its continuing suitability."* **Why:** Mandatory for **Clause 5.2 (d)**. ### Step 9 — Approval and Revision History Table **Content:** A standard document-control table with columns: *Rev. No., Date, Description of Change, Prepared by, Reviewed by, Approved by*. **Why:** Establishes **traceability** and **auditability**. --- ## 3.2 Sample Quality Policy Statement (Model Answer for KTU Examinations) > **"XYZ Shipyard Pvt. Ltd. is committed to designing, building, and delivering sea-going vessels and offshore structures that consistently meet the requirements of our customers, applicable international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC 2006), and the rules of recognized classification societies.** > > **We shall achieve this by:** > - *Establishing and maintaining an effective Quality Management System compliant with ISO 9001:2015 and IACS QSCS.* > - *Setting measurable quality objectives at all functional levels and reviewing them periodically.* > - *Empowering our employees through training, motivation, and a culture of accountability.* > - *Engaging only with approved suppliers and subcontractors who share our quality values.* > - *Continually improving our processes, products, and services through the PDCA cycle.* > > **This Quality Policy is communicated to all employees, made available to interested parties, and is reviewed annually by top management for continuing suitability."** --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Generic vs. Shipyard-Specific Quality Policy | Dimension | Generic Manufacturing Industry | Shipyard-Specific | |:----------|:------------------------------|:------------------| | **Customer** | End consumer / OEM | Shipowner + Charterer + Flag State | | **Regulator** | Local factory inspector | Classification Society + Flag State + Coastal State | | **Standards** | ISO 9001 only | ISO 9001 + **IACS QSCS** + Class Rules | | **Key Risk** | Product recall | **Vessel total loss, oil spill, crew injury** | | **KPIs** | Defect rate, yield | FTR, RCR, OTD, NCR closure, Class remarks | | **Audit Cycle** | 3 years | **Annual surveillance + intermediate audit** | | **Legal Consequence** | Consumer court | **Admiralty court, insurance claim, criminal liability** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matrix matters for KTU answers:** Examiners award full marks (14/14) only when the student shows awareness that a *shipyard's* Quality Policy is **fundamentally different in regulatory weight and risk profile** from a generic factory policy. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Quality Policy Position in the QMS Document Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Top Management
CEO and Board] --> B[Quality Policy
Strategic Apex] B --> C[Quality Manual
Tactical Layer] C --> D[Procedures and SOPs
Operational Layer] D --> E[Work Instructions
Shop Floor Level] E --> F[Quality Records
Evidence Layer] F --> G[Continual Improvement
PDCA Cycle] G --> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The Quality Policy sits at **Level 2**, directly below top management. - Downward arrows = **decomposition of intent** into actionable documents. - The loopback arrow from records back to management = **feedback for review**. --- ## 4.2 Six Pillars of a Shipyard Quality Policy (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pillar 1
Top Management
Commitment] --> P6[Pillar 6
Periodic Review
MRM Annual] P2[Pillar 2
Customer and
Regulatory Focus] --> P6 P3[Pillar 3
Quality Objectives
Framework KPI] --> P6 P4[Pillar 4
Communication
and Awareness] --> P6 P5[Pillar 5
Continual
Improvement PDCA] --> P6 P2 --> P3 P4 --> P3 P5 --> P3 P3 --> P1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - All six pillars **converge into the Periodic Review** (Management Review Meeting). - **Pillar 3 (Quality Objectives)** is the **central node** because it is the only pillar that can be *measured*. - Without Pillar 3, the policy becomes a **decorative wall poster** — a common pitfall in shipyards that fail IACS audits. --- ## 4.3 Communication Flow of Quality Policy (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC[Top Management
Drafts Policy] --> APP[CEO Approval
with Signature] APP --> REG[Document Control
Registration Number] REG --> INT[Internal Communication
Induction and Shop Boards] REG --> EXT[External Communication
Web and RFP Response] INT --> EMP[All Employees
and Subcontractors] EXT --> CL[Classification Society
and Shipowners] EMP --> AUD[Internal Audit
Verification] CL --> AUD AUD --> MR[Management Review
Annual MRM] MR --> SRC ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The policy is a **closed-loop system**: it originates at top management, flows outward, and the audit results **loop back** to top management for refinement. - This is the **Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle** applied to the policy itself. --- ## 4.4 Integration of Quality Policy with IACS QSCS Framework ```mermaid flowchart TB ISO[ISO 9001:2015
Clause 5.2] --> QP[Shipyard Quality Policy] IACS[IACS UR Z17
and QSCS] --> QP FLAG[Flag State
Administration Rules] --> QP OWNER[Shipowner
Contractual Specs] --> QP QP --> QM[Quality Manual] QM --> PRO[Shipyard Procedures
Welding Painting NDT] PRO --> HSE[HSE and
Environmental Policy] PRO --> QP ``` **Reading the diagram:** - A shipyard's Quality Policy is the **fusion point** of *four upstream regulatory forces*: ISO, IACS, Flag State, and Shipowner. - The Quality Policy is **not an independent document** — it must harmonize all four. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Q: Define the term "Quality Policy" as applicable to a shipyard. State TWO mandatory contents of a shipyard Quality Policy as per ISO 9001:2015.** **Model Answer:** > A **Quality Policy** is a formal, top-management-issued statement that expresses the shipyard's overall commitment to quality and its alignment with the **Quality Management System** requirements of **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2** and the **IACS QSCS**. > > **Two mandatory contents:** > 1. **Commitment to comply with applicable requirements** — including classification society rules, international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL), customer specifications, and statutory/regulatory requirements. > 2. **Commitment to continual improvement of the QMS** — through the application of the PDCA cycle, root-cause analysis, and corrective/preventive actions. > > *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two mandatory contents = 3 marks)* --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Q: List FOUR classification societies whose rules a shipyard's Quality Policy must typically comply with. Why is the Quality Policy reviewed annually?** **Model Answer:** > **Four major classification societies:** > 1. **Lloyd's Register (LR)** – United Kingdom. > 2. **DNV (Det Norske Veritas)** – Norway (now DNV). > 3. **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** – United States. > 4. **Bureau Veritas (BV)** – France. > > *(Acceptable alternatives: ClassNK – Japan; RINA – Italy; Korean Register – South Korea; Indian Register of Shipping – India.)* > > **Why annual review?** The Quality Policy is reviewed annually to ensure its **continuing suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness** in light of (a) changes in classification rules, (b) new shipowner requirements, (c) audit findings, and (d) the shipyard's evolving strategic objectives. *(2 marks)* > > *(1 mark for four class societies + 2 marks for the review rationale = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`** **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **mandatory contents of a Quality Policy** for a shipyard as required by **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2**, with specific reference to the **shipbuilding industry context**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **role of the Quality Policy in achieving IACS QSCS certification**, and discuss **how the policy is communicated, implemented, monitored, and reviewed** in a typical shipyard. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks** A shipyard Quality Policy, to be compliant with **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2**, must include the following mandatory contents: **1. Appropriateness to the Purpose and Context of the Shipyard (1 Mark)** The policy must reflect the shipyard's **strategic direction** — e.g., the type of vessels it builds (LNG carriers, bulk carriers, naval ships, offshore platforms), its market segment, and its long-term vision. **2. Framework for Setting Quality Objectives (1 Mark)** The policy must provide a **framework** for establishing and reviewing quality objectives. In a shipyard, these objectives may include *On-Time Delivery ≥ 90%*, *First-Time-Right ≥ 95%*, *Customer Satisfaction ≥ 8.5/10*, and *NCR Closure within 90 days*. **3. Commitment to Comply with Applicable Requirements (1.5 Marks)** This includes: - **Customer contractual specifications** — e.g., the shipowner's building specification and general arrangement drawings. - **Statutory and regulatory requirements** — SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, MLC 2006, COLREG. - **Classification society rules** — e.g., Lloyd's Register Rules and Regulations for Special Service Vessels. - **Flag state administration requirements** — e.g., the Liberian or Panamanian maritime authority. **4. Commitment to Continual Improvement of the QMS (1.5 Marks)** The policy must explicitly commit to **continual improvement** through the **PDCA cycle**, **root-cause analysis**, **corrective and preventive actions (CAR/PAR)**, and **management review**. **5. Communication and Availability (1 Mark)** The policy must be **communicated, understood, and applied** within the shipyard. It must also be **available to relevant interested parties** upon request — e.g., during tender evaluation by shipowners. **6. Periodic Review (1 Mark)** The policy must be **reviewed at planned intervals** (typically annually) during the Management Review Meeting (MRM) to ensure its continuing suitability. --- ### **Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks** **Role of Quality Policy in IACS QSCS Certification (3 Marks):** - The **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** requires every shipyard seeking IACS membership recognition to maintain a **documented, top-management-endorsed Quality Policy** as a **prerequisite**. - The Quality Policy is the **first document** audited by the certification body during the **Stage 1 (Documentation Review)** audit. - It demonstrates the shipyard's **commitment to compliance with IACS Unified Requirements (UR)**, including **UR Z17** (Procedural Requirements for Service Suppliers) and the **QSCS audit scheme**. - Without a compliant Quality Policy, **IACS QSCS certification is not granted**, which directly disqualifies the shipyard from building vessels for many major shipowners. **Communication, Implementation, Monitoring, and Review (4 Marks):** - **Communication (1 Mark):** Policy is published on the shipyard's intranet, displayed in all workshops (welding, painting, outfitting), explained during employee induction training, and translated into the local language for shop-floor workers. - **Implementation (1 Mark):** The policy is cascaded into **department-level procedures** — e.g., the Hull Shop implements it through welding procedure specifications (WPS), the Paint Shop through coating procedures, and the Outfitting Shop through pipe-fitting standards. - **Monitoring (1 Mark):** Internal audits (typically twice a year) verify that the policy is being followed. **KPIs** (FTR, RCR, OTD, NCR closure) are tracked on a **monthly dashboard**. - **Review (1 Mark):** During the **annual Management Review Meeting (MRM)**, top management reviews the policy's effectiveness using audit results, customer feedback, and changing regulatory requirements, and updates the policy as necessary. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice ### **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`** **Q: (a)** Discuss the **relationship between a shipyard's Quality Policy and the requirements of classification societies, international maritime conventions, and shipowner specifications.** **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch/diagram**, explain the **hierarchical placement of the Quality Policy within the QMS documentation pyramid** of a shipyard. Also describe **how the policy is linked to the PDCA cycle for continual improvement.** **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks** The Quality Policy of a shipyard operates at the **intersection of three regulatory and contractual forces**: **1. Classification Society Rules (2.5 Marks):** Classification societies such as **Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, and Bureau Veritas** issue detailed Rules and Regulations for the design, construction, and survey of ships. The Quality Policy must commit the shipyard to **strict adherence** to the class rules under which each vessel is being built. For example, an LNG carrier built to **BV class** must comply with BV's rules for cryogenic containment systems, which the Quality Policy must explicitly reference. **2. International Maritime Conventions (2.5 Marks):** Conventions adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — such as **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea), MARPOL (Marine Pollution), MLC 2006 (Maritime Labour Convention), COLREG (Collision Regulations), and Load Lines** — set mandatory international standards. The Quality Policy must commit the shipyard to **incorporate these conventions** into every design and construction activity. **3. Shipowner Specifications (2 Marks):** The shipowner (e.g., Maersk, MSC, Shell) issues a **Building Specification** and **General Arrangement drawings** that go beyond class and statutory minimums. The Quality Policy must commit the yard to **meeting or exceeding** these customer-specific requirements, since non-compliance may result in **contractual penalties, withholding of stage payments, or refusal of delivery**. > In essence, the Quality Policy is the **single point of integration** where class rules, IMO conventions, and shipowner requirements are harmonized into one operational promise. --- ### **Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks** **Hierarchical Placement (4 Marks):** The Quality Policy sits at the **apex of the QMS documentation pyramid**: $$ \text{Quality Policy} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Quality Manual} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Procedures (SOPs)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Work Instructions} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Quality Records} $$ The accompanying **block diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1) illustrates that: - The **Quality Policy** is a **strategic document** (what we promise). - The **Quality Manual** is a **tactical document** (how we organize). - **Procedures/SOPs** are **operational documents** (how we do each task). - **Work Instructions** are **shop-floor-level** documents (step-by-step actions). - **Quality Records** are **objective evidence** (proof that we did it). **Linkage with the PDCA Cycle (3 Marks):** - **Plan (P):** The Quality Policy itself is the **Plan** — it sets the quality intent and objectives. - **Do (D):** Implementation through **procedures, training, and supervision** at all levels. - **Check (C):** Monitoring through **internal audits, KPI dashboards, customer feedback, and classification society surveys**. - **Act (A):** Review and update of the Quality Policy during the **annual Management Review Meeting (MRM)**, based on audit findings, NCR trends, and changes in regulatory requirements. This creates a **closed feedback loop** that ensures the Quality Policy is a **living document**, not a static wall poster. --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks on):** > 1. **Confusing Quality Policy with Quality Manual.** Policy is the *intent*; Manual is the *implementation framework*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Omitting IACS and classification society references.** A *generic* answer loses marks. Always use **shipyard-specific** terminology (IACS UR Z17, class rules, SOLAS, MARPOL). > 3. **Forgetting the annual review clause.** ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2(d) explicitly requires periodic review. Missing it = -1 mark. > 4. **Writing a "decorative" policy.** Examiners want to see **measurable objectives** (KPIs) linked to the policy. A policy without objectives = at most 9/14 marks. > 5. **Skipping the communication clause.** Many students forget that the policy must be **communicated to all employees** and **available to interested parties**. > 6. **Not using a diagram in 14-mark answers.** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for **neat, labelled diagrams** in Part B answers. Always include a **QMS pyramid** or **PDCA cycle** diagram. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Policy of Shipyards:** > - **Definition:** Top-management-issued statement of quality intent, as per **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2**. > - **Mandatory Pillars (6):** Top management commitment, customer/regulatory focus, quality objectives, communication, continual improvement, periodic review. > - **External Drivers:** **IACS QSCS**, classification society rules, **IMO conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC)**, flag state, and shipowner specs. > - **QMS Pyramid:** Quality Policy → Quality Manual → SOPs → Work Instructions → Records. > - **Key KPIs:** FTR (≥ 95%), RCR (≤ 2%), CSI (≥ 8.5/10), OTD (≥ 90%), NCR closure (≤ 90 days). > - **Review Frequency:** **At least once per year** during the **Management Review Meeting (MRM)**. > - **Audit Cycle:** **Annual surveillance audit + intermediate audit** by certification body. > - **Document Control:** Must have a unique **document number, revision number, issue date, and approval signature**. > - **PDCA Linkage:** Policy = **Plan**; Procedures + Training = **Do**; Audits + KPIs = **Check**; MRM Update = **Act**. > - **High-Yield Keywords for KTU Answers:** *IACS QSCS, ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2, PDCA, Continual Improvement, Management Review, Customer Satisfaction, Classification Society, IACS UR Z17, KPI, NCR.* > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** In every 14-mark answer on this topic, structure your answer as: > **Definition (1 mark) → Mandatory Contents (4 marks) → Shipyard-Specific Application (4 marks) → Communication & Review (3 marks) → Diagram (2 marks).** > Following this template consistently will secure full marks across any KTU Part B question on this topic.
